Conclusion: HarmVideoBench enables a more nuanced, explainable assessment of harmful video understanding, and BCR demonstrates that controlled, context-aware reasoning can yield substantial gains, suggesting directions for more robust evaluation and model design.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have recently shown immense potential in automated content moderation, sparking growing interest in developing harmful-video benchmarks. However, we identify two primary limitations in existing works: 1) The multi-layered characteristics of harmful videos are overlooked. Existing benchmarks predominantly formulate evaluation as a binary classification task, failing to capture implicit or deep contextual harms. 2) Explanatory rationales are completely absent. Current frameworks measure exclusively whether a model flags a video correctly rather than explaining why, turning evaluation into a black box where models can succeed through superficial shortcuts. To address these problems, we present HarmVideoBench, a multi-layered diagnostic benchmark comprising 1,379 videos paired with 4,137 multiple-choice questions. HarmVideoBench benchmarks three hierarchical dimensions: Observable Evidence, Clip-Internal Meaning, and Beyond-Clip Reasoning, aiming to evaluate models' deep understanding beyond surface cues with carefully balanced and curated samples. We evaluate 19 leading models on HarmVideoBench to assess their multidimensional understanding of harmful videos. Moreover, we introduce BCR, a benchmark-aligned method that predicts reasoning boundaries and dynamically retrieves context only when needed. Experimental results show that BCR substantially improves the base model's performance in harmful video understanding, raising the macro average from 61.7 percent to a state-of-the-art 84.4 percent.

</details>


### [78] [LISA: Likelihood Score Alignment for Visual-condition Controllable Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27192)
*Yanghao Wang,Hongxu Chen,Jiazhen Liu,Zhenqi He,Rui Liu,Zhen Wang,Long Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LISA regularizes the side network in dual-branch diffusion models by aligning its intermediate features with an approximated likelihood score, improving training efficiency and conditional disentanglement without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underexplored role and training efficiency of the side branch in visual-condition controllable generation, by leveraging a score-based perspective that separates a prior (main network) and a conditional likelihood (side network).

Method: Introduce LIkelihood Score Alignment (LISA): hook a designated layer of the side network, project features into the score latent space via a lightweight decoder, and construct an approximated likelihood score target. Use the distance between the decoder output and this target as an auxiliary regularization loss, jointly training the side network and decoder with the standard diffusion loss.

Result: Across diverse image/video tasks, architectures, and diffusion/flow models, LISA consistently accelerates training convergence, improves final outputs, and yields more disentangled side-network features for conditional modeling, with negligible additional training cost and zero extra inference cost.

Conclusion: LISA offers an effective and efficient regularization framework that aligns side-network features with a likelihood score, enhancing conditional controllability and training efficiency in dual-branch generative pipelines.

Abstract: The prevalent dual-branch paradigm, i.e., training a side network to encode visual conditions and fusing its intermediate-layer features to a frozen pretrained main network, has shown remarkable success in visual-condition controllable generation. Despite its widespread adoption, the role of the side branch and its training efficiency remain underexplored. In this paper, we first revisit this mainstream paradigm through the lens of score-based generative modeling: 1) The main network preserves visual perceptual quality by providing a prior unconditional score. 2) The side network steers conditional control by implicitly contributing a likelihood score. Guided by this perspective, we propose LIkelihood Score Alignment (LISA), an effective regularization method that explicitly aligns the intermediate feature of the side network with an approximated likelihood score. Specifically, we first hook features from a designated layer of the side network and project them into the score latent space by a lightweight decoder. Then, we construct an approximated likelihood score target and calculate the distance between the decoder's output and this target as an additional regularization loss. Finally, we jointly optimize the side network and decoder with both standard diffusion loss and our regularization loss. Experiments across various image/video tasks, architectures, and diffusion/flow models demonstrated that LISA can not only consistently accelerate the training convergence and improve final synthetic results, but also encourage the side network's features to be more disentangled for conditional modeling with negligible additional training cost and zero extra inference cost.

</details>


### [79] [SatSplatDiff: Geometry-preserving generative refinement for high-fidelity satellite Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27223)
*Jiyong Kim,Shuang Song,Ronjgun Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SatSplatDiff adds monocular depth supervision and shadow-guided refinements to Gaussian splatting for satellite 3D reconstruction, addressing geometric degradation from view-limited supervision and generative refinement, yielding state-of-the-art geometry and fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limitations: top-view satellite imagery provides weak facade supervision; independent generative refinement can cause hallucinations and photo-inconsistencies, harming geometry.

Method: Extend SatSplat with DSM-based initialization, monocular depth constraints, multi-scale geometric refinement, and shadow-guided generative refinement that uses shadow maps to align Gaussians with geometry.

Result: State-of-the-art on IARPA2016 and DFC2019; geometric MAE reduced up to 18%; FID-CLIP improved 28-45%; up to 5x resolution with minimal hallucination and strong cross-tile consistency.

Conclusion: SatSplatDiff demonstrates robust, scalable, geometry-preserving photorealistic satellite reconstructions, leveraging shadows to constrain generative refinement; code available.

Abstract: Gaussian Splatting has been recently explored for satellite 3D reconstruction, demonstrating flexibility and efficiency in representing radiometrically diverse satellite scenes. However, the limited top viewpoint of satellite imagery results in insufficient supervision on building facades, leaving surface holes and degraded visual fidelity. Generative refinement, which leverages pretrained generative priors to iteratively refine and update the rendered images used as supervision targets, has recently been investigated to improve the visual fidelity of Gaussian-rendered images. However, since these models refine each view independently, the resulting images can generate hallucinations and break photo-consistency, leading to geometric degradation. To address these limitations, we propose SatSplatDiff, which aims to minimize geometric degradation prevalent in generative refinement. Building on photogrammetric DSM initialization and 2DGS-based shadow casting established in our prior work SatSplat, we first introduce monocular depth supervision and multi-scale geometric refinement to establish a geometrically accurate and well-regularized surface representation. We then apply shadow-guided generative refinement, where geometrically calculated shadow maps guide the Gaussians to maintain consistency with the underlying geometry, improving visual fidelity while reducing geometric degradation. Extensive evaluations on the IARPA2016 and DFC2019 datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, reducing geometric MAE by up to 18% and improving visual fidelity (FID-CLIP) by 28-45% over existing baselines. Our method delivers up to 5x resolution enhancement with minimal hallucination and sensor-consistent appearance, demonstrating seamless cross-tile consistency and strong scalability for large-scale reconstruction. Source code is available at https://github.com/GDAOSU/SatSplatDiff

</details>


### [80] [CORTEX: A Structured Reasoning Benchmark for Trustworthy 3D Chest CT MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27264)
*Hashmat Shadab Malik,Anees Ur Rehman Hashmi,Numan Saeed,Muzammal Naseer,Salman Khan,Christoph Lippert*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CORTEX is a structured reasoning benchmark for 3D chest CT that provides a four-stage diagnostic trace (task understanding, visual observation, diagnostic reasoning, answer synthesis) to enable interpretable, evidence-linked QA and report generation in MLLMs. Built on CT-RATE, it contains 76,177 validated reasoning traces across open-ended VQA, closed-ended VQA, and report generation, with a stage-level evaluation protocol and clinician collaboration; dataset and code to be released publicly after acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D radiology reasoning is typically evaluated only on final answers, ignoring the step-by-step chain linking findings to conclusions. There is a need for structured supervision and verifiable reasoning in 3D chest CT MLLMs, as well as a standard benchmark to train and assess such capabilities.

Method: Generate four-stage reasoning traces using frontier large language models with broad medical and general knowledge. Filter and verify traces with a stage-level evaluation protocol combining automated rubric scoring and expert radiologist review. Collaborate with clinicians to design reasoning structure and rubrics. Build on the CT-RATE chest CT dataset to provide the data scaffold.

Result: CORTEX comprises 76,177 validated reasoning traces across open-ended VQA, closed-ended VQA, and report generation, enabling structured supervision and stage-level evaluation for 3D chest CT reasoning. The dataset and evaluation code will be publicly available upon acceptance.

Conclusion: CORTEX supplies necessary structured supervision and an evaluation protocol to train and evaluate trustworthy reasoning models for 3D chest CT, facilitating interpretable AI in radiology and paving the way for verifiable diagnostic reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: Reasoning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has shown strong promise in medical imaging. However, this reasoning is usually free-form text judged only by its final answer, making it hard to interpret and verify, especially in 3D radiology, where a diagnosis should be traceable to evidence in the scan. Existing chest CT question-answering datasets compound this by reducing expert radiology reports to answer-only pairs, dropping the reasoning that links findings to conclusions and omitting the patient history clinicians rely on. As a result, reasoning-capable 3D chest CT MLLMs remain out of reach, as neither the structured supervision needed to train them nor the protocol needed to verify their reasoning yet exists. We introduce CORTEX (Clinically Organized Reasoning and sTructured EXplanation), a structured reasoning benchmark for 3D chest CT. For each question, CORTEX restores the missing reasoning as a four-stage diagnostic trace mirroring a radiologist's workflow: task understanding, visual observation, diagnostic reasoning, and answer synthesis. We generate these traces using frontier large language models with broad medical and general-domain knowledge, then filter and verify them with a stage-level evaluation protocol combining automated rubric scoring with expert radiologist review. Crucially, both the reasoning structure and evaluation rubrics are designed in close collaboration with clinicians. Built on CT-RATE, a large, publicly available chest CT dataset without reasoning annotations, CORTEX comprises 76,177 validated reasoning traces across open-ended VQA, closed-ended VQA, and report generation, providing both the structured supervision and the stage-level evaluation protocol needed to build and evaluate trustworthy reasoning models for 3D chest CT. Our dataset and evaluation code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [81] [Exact and Deterministic Patch Descriptor Retrieval via Hierarchical Normalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27280)
*Koichi Sato*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Deterministic, exact nearest-neighbor retrieval for patch descriptors using Hierarchical Normalization (HN) that provably matches exhaustive search while pruned by a major/minor component bound, achieving substantial speedups over brute-force with minimal full-vector evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome nondeterminism and potential inaccuracies of approximate NN methods (e.g., HNSW, IVF-PQ) in patch descriptor matching, by enabling provably exact NN results with high efficiency.

Method: Introduce Hierarchical Normalization: split a 128-D feature into K major components with norm sqrt(1-alpha) and (128-K) minor components with norm sqrt(alpha). The minor inner product is bounded by alpha via Cauchy–Schwarz, enabling an admissible upper bound on full similarity (major similarity + alpha). Perform a deterministic, branch-and-bound search: scan K-Dim major component for all entries, then evaluate full 128-Dim only for candidates that cannot be pruned. Use cache-optimized Structure-of-Arrays layout. Train HN-modified HardNet on the Notredame split of the UBC Patch dataset; evaluate on Trevi and Halfdome with settings (K=8, alpha=1/32) and (K=16, alpha=1/8).

Result: Achieves 13.7x (Trevi) / 12.7x (Halfdome) speedups over brute-force 128-Dim search with only 0.4% of entries requiring full evaluation at K=8, alpha=1/32. At K=16, alpha=1/8, FPR@95 rises marginally from 0.0062 to 0.0064 on Trevi at 7.2x speed-up, with 98.8% of entries bypassing full evaluation.

Conclusion: The method provides provably exact NN results with deterministic behavior and substantial practical speedups over brute-force, offering a competitive alternative to ANN methods for patch descriptor retrieval when exactness is essential.

Abstract: We present a patch descriptor retrieval method that returns the exact nearest neighbour -- provably identical to exhaustive full-vector search -- while evaluating only a small fraction of the database, and does so deterministically: the same (database, query) pair always produces the same result, independent of run order, thread count, or hardware. This contrasts with approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) approaches such as HNSW and IVF-PQ, which trade exactness for speed and may return different results across runs. The enabling mechanism is Hierarchical Normalization (HN): a normalisation scheme that splits the pre-normalisation feature vector into a K-dim major component (norm sqrt(1-alpha)) and a (128-K)-dim minor component (norm sqrt(alpha)). Since the minor inner product is bounded by alpha (Cauchy-Schwarz on the prescribed norms), the major similarity plus alpha is an admissible upper bound on the full similarity: the search scans the K-dim major component for all entries, then applies full 128-dim evaluation only to entries that cannot be pruned -- a provably exact branch-and-bound scan. We train HN-modified HardNet on the notredame split of the UBC patch dataset and evaluate on trevi and halfdome. With a cache-optimised Structure-of-Arrays layout and K=8, alpha=1/32, the search achieves 13.7x (trevi) / 12.7x (halfdome) speed-up over brute-force 128-dim search, with only 0.4% of entries requiring full evaluation. At K=16, alpha=1/8, FPR@95 rises from 0.0062 to 0.0064 on trevi at 7.2x speed-up, with 98.8% of entries bypassing full evaluation.

</details>


### [82] [Sculpting NeRF Geometry: Human-Preference Fine-Tuning of a 3D-Aware Face GAN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27305)
*Archer Moore,Mingming Gong,Liam Hodgkinson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RLHF-guided fine-tuning of a 3D-aware NeRF-based generator directly on density-field rewards, avoiding mesh priors and surface supervision; yields geometry improvements with modest quality cost; high user preference for geometry


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliance on explicit surface representations and mesh extraction in 3D generation; leverage reward signals over radiance-field density to steer generation without meshes or text conditioning

Method: Train a reward model from a small set of human preferences; apply it to fine-tune a pretrained 3D-aware generator (EG3D) directly from the NeRF density field (σ); enforce a density-consistency constraint to preserve 2D appearance while reshaping geometry; no pretraining of reward, no mesh extraction, and no multi-view rendering

Result: Geometry improves under RLHF; FID-50k increases from 4.09 to 6.66 (quantifies quality trade-off); the fine-tuned generator, guided by preferences from a single annotator, yields geometries preferred by users in 74.4% of pairwise comparisons

Conclusion: Shows viability of density-field—based RLHF for 3D generative models; can reshape geometry without external shape priors or meshes, with a quantifiable trade-off in 2D appearance

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for 3D generation is now established across a number of works, but most existing pipelines optimise explicit surface representations, often by converting radiance fields into meshes and training heavily on surface-supervised data. We instead fine-tune a pretrained 3D-aware generative model directly from a learned reward over radiance-field density ($σ$) values, with no externally supplied mesh or shape prior. The reward model requires no pretraining, trains easily on a small set of preference samples, and yields robust improvement in 3D geometry. Working on an unconditional 3D-aware face GAN (EG3D), our reward reads the continuous 3D density field of the neural radiance field (NeRF) directly and supplies a geometry-only learning signal, requiring neither text conditioning, mesh extraction, nor multi-view rendering. A density-consistency constraint keeps the 2D appearance qualitatively similar while the geometry is reshaped, at a measurable but bounded distributional cost (FID-50k rises from 4.09 to 6.66): the fine-tuned generator, trained from the preferences of a single annotator as a proof of concept, produces face geometries preferred by users in 74.4% of pairwise comparisons.

</details>


### [83] [See & Sniff: Learning Visuo-Olfactory Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27307)
*Seongyu Kim,Seungwoo Lee,Hyeonggon Ryu,Joon Son Chung,Arda Senocak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SmellNet-V: a scalable visuo-olfactory dataset created by synthetically pairing smell-only samples with semantically aligned web images and a self-supervised framework See & Sniff for joint visuo-olfactory representations, including smell saliency maps and a pixel-level localization benchmark; reports 7% absolute improvement over smell-only baselines in smell classification and generalizes to cross-modal retrieval and localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of paired visuo-olfactory data to study visuo-olfactory perception; leverage odor identity invariance to visual transformations within semantic categories to synthesize cross-modal data without costly data collection.

Method: Create SmellNet-V by pairing smell samples with semantically aligned images; train a self-supervised model See & Sniff that performs dense local alignment to learn joint visuo-olfactory representations; produce smell saliency maps for spatial grounding; introduce a pixel-level smell localization task and an evaluation benchmark.

Result: Outperforms smell-only baselines by 7% in smell classification from smell alone; demonstrates generalization to cross-modal retrieval and smell localization.

Conclusion: Presents visuo-olfactory learning as a viable new direction in multimodal perception and introduces a scalable dataset and evaluation suite to support future research.

Abstract: While modern multimodal models integrate vision with language, audio, or touch, olfaction remains largely unexplored due to the lack of paired visuo-olfactory data. We introduce SmellNet-V, a scalable visuo-olfactory dataset built on the insight that odor identity is largely invariant to visual transformations within a semantic category. This allows us to synthetically pair smell-only samples with semantically aligned in-the-wild web images, converting a unimodal olfactory dataset into a cross-modal benchmark without costly co-collection. Building on this dataset, we propose See & Sniff, a self-supervised framework that learns joint visuo-olfactory representations via dense local alignment and naturally produces smell saliency maps for spatial grounding of odor sources. We further introduce pixel-level smell localization task and a benchmark for evaluation. Our method surpasses smell-only baselines by 7% in smell classification from smell alone and generalizes to cross-modal retrieval and smell localization, establishing visuo-olfactory learning as a new direction in multimodal perception.

</details>


### [84] [ViQ: Text-Aligned Visual Quantized Representations at Any Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27313)
*Xumin Yu,Zuyan Liu,Zhenyu Yang,Yuhao Dong,Shengsheng Qian,Jiwen Lu,Han Hu,Yongming Rao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViQ introduces a two-stage Visual Quantized Representations framework to balance semantics and details in discrete representations, enabling native-resolution inputs and efficient multimodal training, with competitive performance and substantial speedups over traditional continuous features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve a unified, discrete visual representation that preserves both high-level semantic information and low-level details, while supporting inputs at native resolutions for efficient multimodal modeling.

Method: Stage 1: text-aligned pre-training to inject semantic supervision from a pretrained language model and enable native-resolution processing. Stage 2: feature discretization using proximal representation learning to progressively compress the feature space, plus a position-aware head-wise quantization mechanism for handling arbitrary resolutions.

Result: Experiments show ViQ achieves competitive performance versus state-of-the-art multimodal encoders with continuous/high-dimensional features, while preserving high-quality low-level reconstruction. Multimodal training with ViQ yields 20%-70% faster training/inference across different base LLMs and recipes.

Conclusion: ViQ provides a unified discrete representation for arbitrary visual inputs that balances semantics and details, enabling efficient multimodal training and flexible resolution handling, with demonstrated efficiency gains.

Abstract: A unified representation for text and vision is a natural pursuit, as it enables simpler multimodal modeling and more efficient training. However, representing images as discrete signals in the same way as text inevitably introduces severe information loss. Existing work struggles to balance low-level details and high-level semantics in discrete representations: reconstruction-oriented representations often lack semantic information, whereas semantically stronger features typically suffer from severe loss of detail. We present ViQ, a Visual Quantized Representations framework, which is designed to balance semantics and details in discrete representations while supporting inputs at native resolutions, thereby enabling it to serve as a unified and general discrete representation for arbitrary visual inputs. Our approach structures quantization learning into two stages: text-aligned pre-training and feature discretization. With text-aligned pre-training, we enhance the visual encoder semantic-rich supervision from the pretrained language model and enable it to process native-resolution visual inputs. During discretization, we propose a proximal representation learning strategy to progressively compact the feature space, along with a position-aware head-wise quantization mechanism that enables flexible processing of arbitrary resolutions. Extensive experiments on multimodal tasks demonstrate that ViQ achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art multimodal vision encoders with continuous and high-dimensional visual features, while maintaining high precision in low-level reconstruction. We also show that multimodal training with visual quantized representations largely improves efficiency, yielding up to 20\%-70\% acceleration with different base LLMs and training recipes.

</details>


### [85] [Not All Actions Are Equal: Rethinking Conditioning for Dexterous World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27325)
*Zizhao Yuan,Zhengtu Liang,Taowen Wang,Qiwei Liang,Yichi Wang,Yunheng Wang,Yuetong Fang,Lusong Li,Zecui Zeng,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DexAC-WM: a structured, tokenized action-conditioning framework with semantic grounding for high-DoF action-conditioned world models, improving visual-temporal realism and action-following on EgoDex/EgoVerse and generalizing across backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-DoF action-conditioned modeling is underexplored. Uniform action sequence compression creates imbalanced optimization across diverse action components, hindering fine-grained dynamics and action fidelity. Semantic grounding is lacking, limiting dynamic visual detail capture in world models.

Method: DexAC-WM preserves dimension-level semantics via action tokenization, employs local refinement and global modulation to align action signals with visual dynamics. A semantic branch provides rich object-scene priors to improve dynamic visual detail and action grounding. The approach is evaluated on EgoDex and EgoVerse, with analyses across backbones.

Result: Incorporating structured action conditioning plus the semantic branch yields significant gains in visual-temporal realism and action-following consistency, as measured by FID, FVD, and PCK. DexAC scales to other backbones, demonstrating the approach's generality.

Conclusion: Structured action modeling and semantic grounding are essential for scaling world models to high-DoF control, and DexAC-WM offers a scalable, effective design for high-DoF action-conditioned video prediction.

Abstract: Recent advances in action-conditioned world models show promising progress in modeling complex interactions and forecasting future states under diverse action sequences. While these models are often driven by stronger visual representations and model capacity, action conditioning itself remains underexplored. Most existing approaches compress the entire action sequence into a single representation, which works well for low-DoF control but becomes less reliable in high-DoF scenarios. We observe that high-DoF dexterous actions are inherently heterogeneous, spanning multiple orders of magnitude, where large-scale motions coexist with subtle but important signals. When uniformly aggregated, optimization exhibits an imbalance across action components, which hinders the modeling of fine-grained effects and affects action fidelity. We therefore propose DexAC-WM, which treats action conditioning as a structured process rather than global compression. DexAC preserves dimension-level semantics via action tokenization and aligns action signals with visual dynamics through local refinement and global modulation. To address the limited high-level semantic grounding in existing world models, we further introduce a semantic branch that provides rich object-scene priors, which enables world model to capture dynamic visual details while supporting high-DoF action-conditioned video prediction. Experiments on EgoDex and EgoVerse show that combining the semantic branch with DexAC significantly improves FID, FVD, and PCK, demonstrating gains in visual-temporal realism and action-following consistency. We further verify that DexAC extends to other backbones, showing the scalability of our structured action-conditioning design. These results suggest that scaling world models to high-DoF control requires both structured action modeling and semantic grounding.

</details>


### [86] [RoPEMover: Depth-Aware Object Relocation via Positional Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27332)
*Ipek Oztas,Duygu Ceylan,Aybars Bugra Aksoy,Aysegul Dundar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A geometry-aware diffusion-transformer object-motion method uses depth-aware RoPE to enable coherent, geometry-consistent displacement in single images, handling occlusions and scene lighting while preserving object identity; trained with synthetic data plus limited real data, achieving state-of-the-art on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve geometry-consistent object motion in single-image editing, addressing occlusions, disocclusion of hidden regions, and scene-level effects (shadows, illumination) that are not well preserved by existing methods.

Method: Extend 2D Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) into a depth-aware formulation that encodes 3D spatial structure and can be explicitly manipulated to induce controlled object motion within diffusion-transformer representations. Operate directly on positional representations; train on synthetic data with a small real-data subset via parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Result: The approach yields state-of-the-art performance across standard object motion benchmarks, preserving object identity under large displacements, generating plausible content in newly revealed regions, and maintaining scene-dependent effects such as shadows and illumination.

Conclusion: RoPE-defined spatial fields, when extended to depth-aware 3D structure, provide a controllable mechanism for geometry-aware motion in diffusion models, enabling high-quality single-image object motion with minimal real supervision.

Abstract: Moving an object in a single image requires geometry-consistent spatial rearrangement, including handling occlusions, revealing previously unseen regions, and maintaining coherent shadows and reflections. Existing approaches are not well suited to this setting and often fail to preserve such scene-level consistency. We address this problem by introducing a geometry-aware object motion method that operates directly on the positional representations of diffusion transformers. Our key insight is that rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) define a structured spatial field that can be explicitly manipulated to induce controlled motion. We extend 2D RoPE into a depth-aware formulation that encodes 3D spatial structure, enabling consistent object displacement and scene-aware updates. Our model is trained using synthetic data combined with a small set of real images via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Despite minimal real supervision, it preserves object identity under large spatial displacements, generates plausible content in newly revealed regions, and consistently updates scene-dependent effects such as shadows and illumination. Experimental results on standard object motion benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [87] [SAM2Matting: Generalized Image and Video Matting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27339)
*Ruiqi Shen,Guangquan Jie,Chang Liu,Henghui Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAM2Matting converts VOS trackers into high-fidelity video matting by adding a region-proposal bridge and matting heads, trained on images, achieving state-of-the-art video matting with strong temporal consistency and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging the gap between frame-wide tracking and pixel-accurate matting is challenging in video applications; existing video matting datasets are expensive and domain-limited, risking poor generalization and tracking robustness.

Method: Decouples tracking and matting by enhancing a foundational tracker (e.g., SAM2/SAM3) with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads. The tracker maintains temporal consistency while the matting heads handle fine-grained details; trained on image data only.

Result: Sets new state-of-the-art in video matting, supports diverse prompt types, preserves temporal consistency, and generalizes well to both human-centric and in-the-wild scenarios.

Conclusion: SAM2Matting demonstrates that a tracker-to-matting paradigm can deliver high-fidelity video matting without requiring specialized video matting datasets, by augmenting a base tracker with targeted matting components.

Abstract: Despite impressive advances in image matting, video matting remains challenging due to the inherent gap between high-level tracking, which requires frame-wise understanding, and low-level matting, which focuses on extremely fine-grained details. Existing methods attempt this with expensive and narrowly-scoped video matting datasets, which may limit out-of-domain generalization and compromise tracking robustness. We rethink the paradigm with SAM2Matting, a tracker-to-matting framework that advances VOS trackers to high-fidelity video matting. Specifically, it decouples the task by enhancing a foundational tracker (e.g., SAM2, SAM3) with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads, enabling the uncompromised tracker to handle temporal consistency while the matting components resolve fine-grained details. Notably, despite being trained only on images, SAM2Matting establishes new state-of-the-art performance on video matting, supports diverse prompt types, maintains strong temporal consistency, and demonstrates robust generalization across both human-centric and in-the-wild scenarios.

</details>


### [88] [RayPE: Ray-Space Positional Encoding for 3D-Aware Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27345)
*Minghao Yin,Jiahao Lu,Wenbo Hu,Wang Zhao,Shan Ying,Kai Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RayPE is a geometry-aware positional encoding for video diffusion transformers that injects 6D Plücker ray coordinates into self-attention, yielding a bilinear-like geometry term that improves cross-frame 3D consistency with negligible parameter overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RoPE-based position encodings on the (u,v,t) camera sampling grid do not encode the 3D structure of the scene. Exploiting the bilinear Plücker product between rays offers a natural, geometry-aligned alternative to improve multi-view consistency and controllability in video diffusion models.

Method: Introduce RayPE: add 6D Plücker coordinates to queries and keys in self-attention with a symmetric flip arrangement so the identity configuration matches the reciprocal product. The injection is additive; the attention score decomposes into a content term, a geometry term, and cross-terms, all individually necessary per experiments. Stabilize across heterogeneous camera scales by decoupling ray direction from magnitude, gating by a learned function of log-magnitude, and applying RMSNorm to align with the content branch. The module adds <0.1% parameters to a pretrained video DiT and is zero-initialized to preserve pretrained weights.

Result: Experiments indicate each term (content, geometry, cross-terms) is essential. RayPE improves camera controllability, cross-frame 3D consistency, and overall video quality across a four-dataset training mixture, with minimal architectural overhead.

Conclusion: RayPE provides a geometry-aware enhancement for attention in video diffusion transformers by embedding Plücker-based 3D ray information, achieving improved multi-view consistency and controllability while remaining parameter-light and initialization-friendly.

Abstract: Modern video diffusion transformers position their tokens through RoPE on the (u,v,t) axes -- a description of the camera's sampling grid that says nothing about the 3D structure of the scene. We observe that the geometric relation between two camera rays is captured by the Plucker reciprocal product, which is bilinear in the two rays -- the same algebraic form as the dot product in Transformer attention. Building on this analogy, we propose RayPE, a positional-encoding extension that injects per-token 6D Plucker coordinates additively into the queries and keys of self-attention, with a query/key flip arrangement under which the symmetric identity configuration coincides exactly with the reciprocal product. The injection is additive, the resulting attention score decomposes into a content term, a geometry term, and two content and geometry cross-terms -- all of which our experiments find individually necessary. To make the encoding stable across video data with heterogeneous camera-translation scales (SfM, deep SLAM, metric), we further decouple ray direction from moment magnitude, gate the encoding by a learned function of the log-magnitude, and apply RMSNorm to align it with the QKNorm-normalized content branch. The full module adds less than 0.1% parameters to a pretrained video DiT, is zero-initialized to start from the pretrained weights, and improves camera controllability, cross-frame 3D consistency, and overall video quality on a four-dataset training mixture.

</details>


### [89] [PhysiFormer: Learning to Simulate Mechanics in World Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27364)
*Yiming Chen,Yushi Lan,Andrea Vedaldi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion transformer, PhysiFormer, models 3D object motion in world coordinates using vertex trajectories; it yields diverse, physically plausible futures without hand-engineered inductive biases and outperforms autoregressive baselines; generalizes to new geometries, materials, and larger object counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To build view-invariant, geometry-aware world models that operate in world coordinates rather than pixel space, while capturing uncertainty in dynamics and enabling multi-object reasoning without explicit object encodings.

Method: Coordinate-space diffusion implemented as a denoising diffusion process on mesh vertex trajectories. Inputs include initial vertex positions and velocities, and object material type (rigid or elastic). The model uses attention factorised over time, space, and objects to enable permutation-invariant multi-object reasoning and does not rely on explicit object encodings. Trained on ~100k simulated trajectories; capable of handling rigid/elastic dynamics and mixed-material settings, unseen geometries, and larger object counts.

Result: The approach substantially outperforms autoregressive baselines in trajectory accuracy, preserves rigidity more effectively, and maintains momentum-based physical consistency. It produces diverse plausible futures due to the probabilistic diffusion formulation and generalizes beyond training conditions.

Conclusion: Coordinate-space diffusion offers a promising direction for view-invariant, geometry-aware world modeling with potential impact in robotics, graphics, and physical design; the authors provide visualizations, code, and models.

Abstract: We present PhysiFormer, a diffusion transformer for physically-plausible 3D object motion. Unlike video world models that operate in view-dependent pixel space, PhysiFormer represents objects as 3D meshes expressed in world coordinates. Given the initial vertex positions and velocities, as well as object material type, rigid or elastic, the model samples future vertex trajectories. While related neural physics approaches build on ad-hoc latent spaces or explicitly enforce rigidity and causality, PhysiFormer shows that excellent results can be obtained without any such inductive biases, by casting vertex trajectory prediction as a single denoising diffusion process directly in world coordinates. The probabilistic formulation captures uncertainty in the learned dynamics, enabling diverse plausible futures from initial conditions, making this framework potentially useful for applications with unobserved uncertainty. The model features attention factorised over time, space, and objects for efficiency, enabling permutation-invariant multi-object reasoning without needing explicit object encoding. Trained on over 100k simulated trajectories, PhysiFormer generates rigid and elastic mechanics, and generalises to mixed-material settings, unseen real-world geometries, and larger object counts. It substantially outperforms autoregressive baselines in trajectory accuracy, rigidity preservation, and momentum-based physical consistency. Our results position coordinate-space diffusion as a promising step toward view-invariant, geometry-aware world modelling for robotics, graphics, and physical design. Visualisations, code, and models are available at https://yimingc9.github.io/physiformer.

</details>


### [90] [Don't Settle at the Mode! Mitigating Diversity Collapse in Pretrained Flow Models via Feature Self-Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27371)
*Pradhaan S Bhat,Rishubh Parihar,Abhijnya Bhat,R. Venkatesh Babu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free self-guidance mechanism for conditional flow models to mitigate diversity collapse by dispersing internal features and projecting back to the data manifold; plug-and-play with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diversity collapse during sampling in conditional flow-based generative models; existing remedies (latent guidance, external reward-model-based sample selection) are either limited in effectiveness or incur high inference cost.

Method: Introduce feature self-guidance by dispersing internal features during batch generation. Apply a manifold regularization step that projects dispersed features back onto the data manifold to preserve alignment with inputs. The approach is designed as a plug-and-play module that can be added to pretrained flow models with minimal extra cost.

Result: Significant improvements in sample diversity while preserving fidelity across diverse conditional flow models, including multi-step and few-step text-to-image, depth-to-image, and reference-image generation, with only marginal inference overhead.

Conclusion: A training-free, plug-and-play augmentation that reduces diversity collapse in conditional flow models by feature dispersion and manifold projection, maintaining conditional alignment and broad applicability.

Abstract: State-of-the-art flow models generate stunning images from text or image prompts. However, they suffer from diversity collapse when generating multiple samples under the same conditioning. Existing methods address this issue via either latent guidance, which has limited effectiveness, or sample selection, which relies on external reward models that incur significant inference-time overhead. In this work, we introduce an efficient, training-free self-guidance mechanism to mitigate diversity collapse without requiring additional reward models. Specifically, we disperse the internal features of the flow model during batch generation with feature self-guidance. Further, to keep the features close to the manifold, we introduce a manifold regularization step that projects these dispersed features back onto the data manifold, ensuring diverse generation without sacrificing alignment with the input conditions. Our method integrates seamlessly as a plug-and-play module into pretrained flow models, adding only a marginal inference cost. Experiments demonstrate significant improvements in diversity while preserving fidelity across several conditional flow models, including multi-step and few-step text-to-image, depth-to-image, and reference image generation.

</details>


### [91] [DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27372)
*Ron Campos,Subhajit Maity,Xin Li,Srijan Das,Aritra Dutta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DnA introduces a denoising attention mechanism that uses a positive (class-relevant) and a negative (irrelevant but related) query to form two interacting subspaces with larger principal angles, improving discriminability and performance across image and video tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard softmax-based attention can produce noisy patterns that dilute relevant features in visual perception tasks; there is a need to enforce subspace separation to improve discriminability and denoising in attention.

Method: The method deploys a positive query to identify features of the correct class and a negative query to identify closely related but irrelevant features. These interactions are projected into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles to promote separation. Experiments use a ViT-B backbone and evaluate on image and video tasks, with analyses supporting the two-subspace design and denoising effect.

Result: On ImageNet-1K with ViT-B, DnA achieves an absolute gain of 0.8% over the baseline. It also yields improvements in video understanding with video transformers (1.8%) and video LLMs (0.5%). Extensive empirical analyses justify the two-subspace interaction and denoising mechanism.

Conclusion: DnA enhances attention discriminability by denoising via two interacting subspaces, improving performance across image and video understanding tasks, and providing evidence for the effectiveness of the two-subspace design.

Abstract: The softmax activation in multihead attention (MHA) is the de facto standard for attention-based models in visual perception tasks. However, standard softmax can produce noisy attention patterns that dilute relevant features and degrade its performance. In this paper, we propose Denoising Attention or DnA, in which, first, a positive query identifies which image features belong to the correct class, and a negative query identifies closely associated but irrelevant image features. DnA then projects these interactions into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles, promoting subspace separation and improved discriminability. Using a ViT-B backbone, our proposed DnA achieves an absolute gain of 0.8% on ImageNet-1K compared to the baseline. We further show improvements across multiple visual understanding tasks, including video understanding with video transformers (1.8%) and video LLMs (0.5%). Our extensive empirical analyses justify the design choices involving two interacting subspaces and the denoising effect of DnA.

</details>


### [92] [Paying More Attention to Visual Tokens in Self-Evolving Large Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27373)
*Shravan Venkatraman,Ritesh Thawkar,Omkar Thawakar,Rao Muhammad Anwer,Hisham Cholakkal,Salman Khan,Fahad Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISE is an unsupervised framework that fixes visual under-conditioning in self-evolving large multimodal models by enforcing visual conditioning through geometric and semantic invariance rewards, improving vision-language understanding without annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses visual under-conditioning where decoders rely on language priors rather than image content in self-evolving LMMs, leading to poor performance on vision-language tasks like captioning and VQA.

Method: Introduce VISE, which trains a single model with two invariance-based rewards: (1) geometric invariance to enforce spatial consistency under known transformations, and (2) semantic invariance to penalize evidence-agnostic generation by perturbing predicted regions and checking for evidence absence. No external reward models or labeled data; operates on raw unlabeled images.

Result: On 18 benchmarks, using Qwen3-VL-2B as base, VISE improves CIDEr by +16.85 on COCO and +19.66 on TextCaps, reduces object hallucination by 5.0 Chair-I points, and generalizes across four model families and scales.

Conclusion: VISE effectively mitigates visual under-conditioning in self-evolving LMMs, enabling better visual grounding and vision-language understanding in an entirely unsupervised, single-model setting, with strong cross-model generalization.

Abstract: Recently, self-evolving large multimodal models (LMMs) have received attention for improving visual reasoning in a purely unsupervised setting. However, multi-role self-play and self-consistency reward schemes in existing self-evolving LMMs optimize answer agreement without ensuring the decoder attends to visual content, relying instead on statistical language priors to produce self consistent outputs. This leads to a persistent failure mode we term visual under-conditioning, where the decoder relies on language priors rather than the image during generation, manifesting as insufficient attention to visual tokens. As a result, current self-evolving LMMs struggle on vision--language understanding tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering. To address this, we propose VISE (Visual Invariance Self-Evolution), a purely unsupervised self-evolving framework that directly regularizes the model's visual conditioning policy through two complementary invariance-based rewards: a geometric invariance reward that enforces spatial consistency under known transformations, and a semantic invariance reward that penalizes evidence-agnostic generation by requiring the model to recognize the absence of evidence when predicted regions are perturbed. VISE operates within a single model without specialist roles, external reward models, or annotations, and is trained on raw unlabeled images. Experiments on 18 benchmarks demonstrate the efficacy of our approach. Using Qwen3-VL-2B as the base model, VISE achieves gains of $+16.85$ CIDEr on COCO and $+19.66$ CIDEr on TextCaps, reduces object hallucination by $5.0$ Chair-I points, and generalizes across four model families and scales. Our code and models are available at https://mbzuai-oryx.github.io/VISE

</details>


### [93] [Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27376)
*Ritesh Thawkar,Shravan Venkatraman,Omkar Thawakar,Abdelrahman Shaker,Fahad Khan,Hisham Cholakkal,Salman Khan,Rao Muhammad Anwer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Self-evolving, fully self-supervised training for unified LMMs using only unlabeled images, via Proposer-Solver-Generator roles, STE stability signal, and solver-mediated image-generation evaluation; achieves consistent gains across eight tasks and multiple backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce or eliminate reliance on annotated data and external reward models for improving understanding and image generation in unified multimodal models.

Method: Three internal roles (Proposer, Solver, Generator) with self-derived consistency signals; Solver Token Entropy as a continuous difficulty signal; multi-scale internal evaluation combining QA fidelity and cycle-consistent captions; architecture-agnostic framework applied to BLIP3o, BAGEL, VARGPT-v1.1; training via native prompting/generation interfaces.

Result: Consistent improvements across eight understanding metrics; BAGEL MMMU +3.5 percentage points; GenEval image generation improved from 82% to 85%; code and models released.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that autonomous, unlabeled-data training can enhance both understanding and generation in LMMs across architectures, reducing dependence on human supervision and external rewards; scalable, reusable framework.

Abstract: Most unified large multimodal models (LMMs) that support both visual understanding and image generation still rely on curated post-training supervision, such as human annotations, preference labels, or external reward models. We ask whether a unified LMM can improve both abilities autonomously using only unlabeled images. We propose a self-evolving training framework with three internal roles: a Proposer that generates visual questions, a Solver that answers and evaluates them, and a Generator that synthesizes images. Training uses only self-derived consistency signals, without human annotations, preference labels, or task-trained external reward/judge models. To stabilize learning, we introduce Solver Token Entropy (STE), a continuous difficulty signal based on token-level prediction uncertainty that remains useful even when sample-level consistency becomes unreliable. For image generation, we design a multi-scale internal evaluation scheme that combines question-answer fidelity scoring with cycle-consistent captioning. This creates a solver-mediated coupling, where better visual understanding enables more reliable generation assessment and stronger internal training signals. The framework preserves the same role decomposition, reward logic, and training schedule across diffusion-based BLIP3o, rectified-flow BAGEL, and autoregressive VARGPT-v1.1 architectures, requiring only each backbone's native prompting and generation interface. Across eight understanding metrics, our method consistently improves over the corresponding base models. On BAGEL, it achieves a $+3.5\%$ absolute gain on MMMU and improves GenEval image generation performance from $82\%$ to $85\%$. Code and models are publicly released.

</details>


### [94] [DanceOPD: On-Policy Generative Field Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27377)
*Wei Zhou,Xiongwei Zhu,Zelin Xu,Bo Dong,Lixue Gong,Yongyuan Liang,Meng Chu,Leigang Qu,Lingdong Kong,Wei Liu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DanceOPD: an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models that unifies T2I, local editing, and global editing by routing samples to a capability-specific velocity field and training with a velocity MSE on rollout states; it supports absorbing guidance fields and improves multi-capability composition while preserving anchor quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern image generation models struggle to jointly support diverse capabilities (text-to-image, local/global editing) due to misalignment and interference between capabilities. A unified, efficient distillation framework is needed to compose expert capabilities without degrading anchor generation or existing guidance.

Method: Define each capability as a velocity field over a shared flow state space. Route each sample to a selected field, query a low-noise student-induced state, and train with a simple velocity MSE objective on rollout states. The student learns from fields queried along its own rollout, enabling composition of capabilities. The framework can also absorb operator-defined fields such as classifier-free guidance (CFG).

Result: Empirical evaluations across T2I, editing, realism-field absorption, and CFG absorption demonstrate improved multi-capability composition: target capabilities are strengthened while anchor generation quality is preserved. The approach provides a practical route for generative field distillation in flow-matching models.

Conclusion: DanceOPD offers a practical, on-policy distillation paradigm for combining multiple capabilities in flow-matching generative models, enabling robust, scalable composition without sacrificing baseline generation quality.

Abstract: Modern image generation demands a single model that unifies diverse capabilities, including text-to-image (T2I), local editing, and global editing. However, these capabilities are rarely naturally aligned and often conflict. For instance, editing tends to degrade T2I performance, while global and local editing interfere with each other. Consequently, effectively composing these capabilities has become a central challenge for image generation model training. To tackle this, we introduce DanceOPD, an on-policy generative field distillation framework for flow-matching models that routes each sample to one capability field, queries one low-noise student-induced state, and trains with a simple velocity MSE objective. With each capability source defined as a velocity field over the shared flow state space, the student learns from fields queried on its own rollout states to compose expert capabilities. This formulation also absorbs operator-defined fields such as classifier-free guidance. Comprehensive experiments on T2I, editing, realism-field absorption, and CFG absorption show that our approach improves multi-capability composition, strengthening target capabilities while preserving anchor generation quality. We believe this work establishes a practical route for generative field distillation in flow-matching models.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [95] [Detecting and Controlling Sycophancy with Cascading Linear Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26155)
*Maty Bohacek,Rishub Jain,Nicholas Dufour,Thomas Leung,Chris Bregler,Roma Patel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An iterative cascading feature-generation pipeline for activation steering uses graded feature presence to identify linearly separable subspaces that underlie sycophancy, enabling better detection, deterministic scoring, robust steering, and greater interpretability at lower computational cost, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current activation-steering approaches rely on many contrastive, often binary sample pairs, which impedes clean feature disentanglement and reliable steering. By isolating degrees of a feature that scale linearly with behavior, the method seeks to enhance interpretability and steerability, specifically targeting sycophancy in language models.

Method: Propose an iterative data-generation pipeline that collects and analyzes cascading samples showing graded feature strength, isolates linear cascading features, and identifies linearly separable subspaces corresponding to sycophancy. Select model activations aligned with the desired behavior and evaluate detection, scoring, and steering performance against baselines.

Result: Cascading features corresponding to sycophancy form linearly separable subspaces. The approach enables detection, deterministic scoring, and robust steering that match or outperform LLM-as-judge and system-prompting baselines, with lower computational demand and improved interpretability.

Conclusion: Graded, cascading feature data facilitate cleaner feature disentanglement and more reliable, interpretable activation steering for sycophancy. The pipeline provides practical gains in detection and steering efficiency and is supported by code and data resources.

Abstract: Interpreting and controlling model behaviors through activation steering methods requires many pairs of contrastive samples that clearly exhibit desired or undesired behavior. These data pairs determine the degree to which interpretability frameworks can reliably detect model features responsible for a behavior, and therefore the ability to steer models toward or away from such behavior. In this work, we present an iterative data generation pipeline that isolates cascading linear features responsible for a behavior. Specifically, we show how moving beyond simple binary pairs of samples, and instead isolating samples that show degrees of features that scale linearly with behavior, allows for better disentanglement of features. We focus on detecting and steering away from sycophancy -- the tendency of language models to prioritize user validation. We demonstrate that sycophancy features discovered through cascading samples form linearly separable subspaces, and allow for selection of model activations that more clearly correspond to the desired behavior than baseline approaches. We also evaluate their ability to enable detection, deterministic scoring, and robust steering, and see that they either match or outperform LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines while providing lower computational demand and more interpretability guarantees. Code & Data: https://cascading-feats.github.io/

</details>


### [96] [Geometry-Aware MCTS for Extremal Problems in Combinatorial Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26399)
*Luoning Zhang,Xu Zhuang,Tianhao Wang,Nathan Kaplan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for extremal combinatorial geometry on n x n grids, enforcing global geometric constraints incrementally to prune actions. Reduces collinearity-check complexity from O(n^3) to O(n^2); uses canonical pruning and symmetric batch transitions to exploit geometry; achieves new best-known results on 5 of 6 problems, notably Max-N3IL (~1.8n for n=82–119) and Smallest Complete Set (~0.95n); demonstrates adaptability of the framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical exact solvers struggle with combinatorial explosion in strict global geometric constraint problems. Standard RL and transformer models face sparse rewards and high token costs, limiting their effectiveness on these tasks.

Method: Introduce Geometry-Aware MCTS that enforces geometric constraints through incremental updates to the feasible action space. For collinearity constraints (e.g., No-Three-in-Line), reduce constraint checking from O(n^3) to O(n^2). Exploit geometric symmetries via canonical pruning during node expansion and symmetric batch transitions to speed up search.

Result: Extensive experiments yield new best-known results on five of six problems. Max-N3IL configurations reach roughly 1.8n for grids with 82 ≤ n ≤ 119. Smallest Complete Set configurations reach roughly 0.95n within tested grids. The framework is demonstrated as a highly adaptable approach for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry.

Conclusion: Geometry-Aware MCTS is a versatile framework capable of discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry by tightly integrating geometric constraints, symmetry exploitation, and efficient search strategies.

Abstract: We study certain extremal problems in combinatorial geometry that ask about configurations of points in an $n \times n$ grid that satisfy strict, global geometric constraints. Classical exact solvers suffer from combinatorial explosion for these types of problems, and standard reinforcement learning and transformer-based models struggle with the sparse reward "validity cliff" and quadratic token-consumption limits. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose a Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework. Our approach strictly enforces geometric constraints through incremental updates to the feasible action space. For constraints about collections of collinear points, like those that occur in the classic No-Three-in-Line problem (Max-N3IL), this mechanism reduces the constraint checking complexity from $O(n^3)$ to $O(n^2)$. To improve search efficiency, we exploit geometric symmetries in two ways: canonical pruning during node expansion to reduce the branching factor, and symmetric batch transitions to accelerate the discovery of promising configurations. We perform extensive experiments and establish new best-known computational results on five out of six of the problems that we considered. Notably, for Max-N3IL we find configurations of size roughly $1.8 n$ for grids of size $82 \le n \le 119$. For the Smallest Complete Set problem, we find configurations of size roughly $0.95 n$, providing new upper bounds within the tested grids. This work establishes Geometry-Aware MCTS as a highly adaptable framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry.

</details>


### [97] [Life After Benchmark Saturation: A Case Study of CORE-Bench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26158)
*Nitya Nadgir,Sayash Kapoor,Kangheng Liu,Peter Kirgis,Matilda Orona,Stephan Rabanser,Tilman Bayer,Abhishek Shetty,Yue Ling,Derrick Chan-Sew,Rumi Nakagawa,Saiteja Utpala,Zachary S. Siegel,Arvind Narayanan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Accuracy saturation in benchmarks biases evaluation towards a single metric; this work argues for multi-dimensional benchmarking and demonstrates it on CORE-Bench Hard, with v1.1 and an OOD suite, plus a human-agent uplift study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the overreliance on accuracy in benchmark design; explore construct validity, generalization, efficiency, reliability, the model-versus-scaffold dynamic, and human-AI collaboration in evaluating agent performance.

Method: Diagnose CORE-Bench Hard to surface construct-Validity threats; develop CORE-Bench v1.1 and CORE-Bench OOD; measure efficiency, reliability, and scaffold performance; conduct a small randomized human-agent collaboration experiment on reproducibility tasks.

Result: Identified construct validity threats; CORE-Bench v1.1 remains informative for multiple dimensions even after accuracy saturation; CORE-Bench OOD broadens generalization assessment; human collaboration yields about a 2x speedup (likely underestimated due to time limits) and reveals other nuanced findings.

Conclusion: Propose a rigorous, multi-dimensional evaluation framework as an alternative to accuracy-centric benchmarking and demonstrate its value through iterative benchmark improvements and human-AI collaboration assessment.

Abstract: When a benchmark's accuracy saturates, it is often retired and replaced with a more challenging version. We show that this approach privileges accuracy and misses the opportunity to study six other key dimensions of agent performance: construct validity issues such as shortcuts, out-of-distribution generalizability, efficiency, reliability, the relative importance of the model versus the scaffold, and uplift from human-agent collaboration. We use CORE-Bench Hard, a benchmark for computational reproducibility of scientific code, as a case study to demonstrate that measuring agents along these dimensions yields meaningful insights into agent performance even after accuracy saturates. First, we surface threats to construct validity in CORE-Bench Hard that are difficult to anticipate with less capable agents. We introduce an improved benchmark, CORE-Bench v1.1, and an out-of-distribution task suite, CORE-Bench OOD. Second, we find that despite accuracy saturation, CORE-Bench v1.1 remains useful for measuring efficiency, reliability, model performance, and scaffold performance. Finally, we conduct a small-scale randomized experiment to measure uplift from human-agent collaboration on real-world computational reproducibility tasks. We find a statistically significant speedup by about a factor of two -- likely underestimated due to one-fifth of human-only reproductions reaching the time limit before completing -- and describe various other findings. Together, our contributions present a more rigorous alternative to the dominant accuracy-centric evaluation paradigm.

</details>


### [98] [Refusal Lives Downstream of Persona in Chat Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26161)
*Viola Zhong,Qirui Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Compliant persona and refusal directions interact: a persona direction gates refusal; late-layer expression controls refusal; manipulating portraits can restore or remove refusal, showing refusal is not an isolated direction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how alignment signals for refusal and persona interact in instruction-tuned models, and whether treating them as separate mechanisms is valid for safety and alignment.

Method: Identify linear directions in activation space corresponding to 'compliant persona' and 'refusal' in two models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). Intervene on these directions by projecting them out or reintroducing them, at different layers, and measure changes in refusal rates. Use late vs early layer windows and random-direction controls to locate where gating occurs.

Result: Compliant persona direction suppresses refusal substantially (e.g., in Llama, refusal rate drops from 97% to 2%). Reintroducing the refusal direction partially restores refusal at late layers but not early ones. Projecting out the persona direction in a late-layer window restores refusal to baseline; projecting out a random direction has no effect. Refusal is gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed.

Conclusion: Refusal cannot be treated as a single isolated direction; its expression depends on persona. Accurate alignment analysis must consider the joint geometry of persona and refusal and their layer-specific gating.

Abstract: Linear directions in activation space have been identified for both refusal and persona traits in instruction-tuned chat models, but the two have been studied as separate mechanisms. We show they interact: a compliant persona gates refusal. In Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, we extract a compliant model-persona direction and a refusal direction and intervene on both. Compliant persona steering suppresses refusal -- in Llama, the refusal rate falls from 97% to 2%. Reintroducing the refusal direction partially restores refusal at late layers but not at early ones. Projecting out the persona direction in a late-layer window restores it to baseline; projecting out a random direction does not. Refusal is therefore gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed. Treating refusal as a single isolated direction misses its dependence on persona.

</details>


### [99] [AlgoEvolve: LLM-driven Meta-evolution of Algorithmic Trading Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26173)
*Dhruv Sharma,Gautam Shroff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-driven semantic evolution is extended to algorithmic trading, with inner loops generating executable Python trading strategies and rigorous evaluation, and an outer meta-evolution loop optimizing prompts to improve search heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of applying semantic mutation-based program synthesis to dynamic, noisy, non-stationary trading environments, beyond static coding benchmarks.

Method: Inner loop: LLM-generated Python trading strategies are executed and tested under a rigorous protocol. Outer loop: a meta-evolutionary process evolves prompts that guide the inner-loop synthesis to balance exploration and exploitation and reduce zero-trade failures.

Result: The system shows emergent regime-adaptive trading logic, including autonomous shifts in rules. The prompt-evolution outer loop yields improved search heuristics that outperform initial human-designed instructions and reduce zero-trade trades.

Conclusion: LLM-based semantic evolution is a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex, non-stationary domains like algorithmic trading, with outer-loop prompt evolution enhancing search effectiveness.

Abstract: Recent work shows that Large Language Models (LLMs) can act as semantic mutation operators for the evolutionary discovery of programs and proofs. Most current applications focus on static coding benchmarks. We extend this paradigm to algorithmic trading. This domain is uniquely challenging because it is noisy, non-stationary, and highly discontinuous. We present AlgoEvolve, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that generates, evaluates, and iteratively improves executable trading strategies. These strategies are expressed as Python code and evaluated through a rigorous testing protocol. Across multiple experiments, the system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive strategy logic, including autonomous shifts in trading rules. We further introduce a meta-evolutionary outer loop that evolves the prompts guiding program synthesis in the inner loop. This outer loop discovers improved search heuristics. These heuristics balance exploration and exploitation while reducing zero-trade failures. They consistently outperform initial human-designed instructions. The results demonstrate that LLM-based semantic evolution provides a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex environments.

</details>


### [100] [Agentic Analysis for Agentic Infrastructure: An LLM-Powered Pipeline for Comparative Governance of DAO and Corporate AI Protocols](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26203)
*Yutian Wang,Luyao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-powered comparative pipeline analyzes governance discourse of AI interoperability standards ERC-8004 vs Google A2A; 4,323 records analyzed; governance form shapes substantive focus but participation inequality and fragmentation persist; discourse alignment is denser under permissionless/open governance; emphasizes potential of LLM-assisted methods for scalable tech governance research; data and code are openly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To empirically study how governance designs affect interoperability standards for AI agents and to address the scarcity of scalable, data-driven methods in socio-technical governance research.

Method: Integrates automated annotation via LLMs, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis on 4,323 governance participation records for two standards (ERC-8004 and Google A2A) to examine how institutional design shapes discourse and community structure.

Result: Governance form influences substantive priorities; participation inequality and community fragmentation are comparable across regimes; discourse alignment is denser in the permissionless setting, suggesting open governance may promote thematic convergence despite decentralized participation.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of LLM-assisted pipelines for large-scale governance research in technology standards. Provides implications for designing more equitable AI standards; all data and code are openly available for replication and extension.

Abstract: As AI agent protocols proliferate, the governance structures shaping their interoperability standards remain empirically underexamined. We introduce an LLM-powered comparative pipeline for large-scale governance discourse analysis, integrating automated annotation, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to study socio-technical power structures at scale. We validate it on two contrasting standards for agent interoperability: ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led). Analyzing 4,323 governance participation records, we combine LLM-assisted coding, topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to examine how institutional design shapes thematic priorities and community structure. We find that while governance form influences substantive focus, both regimes exhibit comparable levels of participation inequality and community fragmentation. Discourse alignment is denser in the permissionless setting, suggesting that open governance may foster greater thematic convergence despite decentralized participation. These findings illustrate how LLM-assisted methods can advance the empirical study of technology governance, with implications for designing more equitable agentic AI standards. All data and code are openly available.

</details>


### [101] [Knowledge-augmented Agentic AI for Mental Health Medication Information Seeking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26205)
*Huizi Yu,Jian Liu,Wenkong Wang,Lingyao Li,Jiayan Zhou,Zhaoqian Xue,Xiang Li,Xinxin Lin,Zhiying Liang,Zhuoru Wu,Siyuan Ma,Xin Ma,Lizhou Fan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A provenance-aware knowledge-graph framework integrates patient-generated data with regulatory adverse-event records to improve psychiatric drug safety assessment, demonstrating strong NER performance, high concordance among patient sources, and earlier signal emergence than regulatory records for at least one drug.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety knowledge for psychiatric drugs is split between abstract regulatory records and user-generated narratives; integrating them without conflating evidence and anecdote can improve risk communication, reduce nocebo effects, and support better adherence.

Method: Develop a Neo4j-based provenance-preserving, multi-agent knowledge graph unifying 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and 20 years of FDA Adverse Event Reporting System data for nine antidepressants; use a large-language-model entity-recognition pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations; co-annotate with ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies; assess concordance and temporal lead-lag of signals.

Result: LLM-based NER achieved F1 of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions; community sources (Reddit and WebMD) showed greater mutual concordance (up to Jaccard 0.905) than with regulatory reports; in sertraline, adverse events appeared in community sources well before FDA dates; provenance graph preserves traceability and keeps regulatory facts distinct from patient experiences.

Conclusion: Source-aware integration via a provenance-grounded knowledge graph can yield auditable, patient-relevant safety information in psychiatry; the approach warrants prospective testing to assess actual usefulness and patient benefits.

Abstract: Patients increasingly seek medication information online, yet safety knowledge for psychiatric drugs is split between regulatory adverse-event records, which are authoritative but abstract, and patient narratives, which are experience-near but unvalidated. Integrating them without conflating evidence and anecdote is especially consequential in psychiatry, where poorly contextualised information can amplify fear, nocebo responses, and non-adherence. Here we develop a provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based multi-agent framework unifying 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and twenty years of U.S. FDA Adverse Event Reporting System records for nine antidepressants. A large-language-model entity-recognition pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations reached highest F1 scores of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. The two community platforms were far more concordant with each other (overlap up to a Jaccard similarity of 0.905) than with regulatory reports, indicating that patient-generated data form a partly independent safety signal. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before the corresponding FDA date. A Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies preserves provenance, keeping every claim traceable and regulatory facts distinct from patient experience. These results establish source-aware integration as a route to more auditable psychiatric medication information, with usefulness and patient benefit to be tested prospectively.

</details>


### [102] [Accelerating Skill Assessment in Chess: A Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26267)
*Tianyuan Zhou,Zhizheng Fu,Tianming Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DD-Elo extends traditional Elo by incorporating move-level data via a drift-diffusion model, enabling faster and explainable adaptation to changing skill while staying bounded to Elo, preserving backward compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Elo's reliance on match outcomes ignores granular gameplay quality, causing response lag. Move-by-move data is noisy and high-dimensional, but leveraging it could yield faster, more responsive rating updates.

Method: Model skill expression as a decision process using drift-diffusion dynamics and integrate move-level information into rating adjustments. Provide rigorous mathematical proof that DD-Elo's deviation from Elo is bounded. Validate empirically that DD-Elo tracks skill changes faster than Elo and remains backward-compatible.
