Result: DD-Elo demonstrates faster adaptation to skill changes than Elo, while maintaining theoretical alignment and interpretability. Code is publicly available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: DD-Elo offers an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible enhancement to chess rating systems by fusing cognitive modeling with move-by-move data.

Abstract: Rating systems such as Elo serve as the gold standard for matchmaking in competitive chess. However, they inherently suffer from response lag due to their exclusive reliance on match outcomes, neglecting the granular quality of gameplay. Nevertheless, incorporating move-by-move information into rating adjustments presents a significant challenge given the substantial noise and the vastness of the game-state space. To address this, we propose the Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System (DD-Elo), a novel skill assessment framework inspired by the drift diffusion model (DDM) from cognitive neuroscience. By modeling skill expression as a decision-making process, our model integrates move-level data to capture rapid skill fluctuations. We provide a rigorous mathematical derivation proving that DD-Elo maintains a bounded deviation from the traditional Elo system, ensuring theoretical alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than Elo. Our findings suggest that DD-Elo offers an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible solution for chess rating ecosystems. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/Aquila-zhou1/DD-Elo .

</details>


### [103] [Governing Actions, Not Agents: Institutional Attestation as a Governance Model for Autonomous AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26298)
*Jakob Salfeld-Nebgen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Computational governance model for AI agents: execution of high-risk actions requires independently attested preconditions, cryptographic binding to intent, deterministic policy evaluation, and tamper-evident, verifiable logs; demonstrated with software deployment and clinical prescribing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address governance gaps where autonomous agents plan freely but require external, independently verifiable evidence at the point of consequential action, mirroring human institutional controls and accountability mechanisms.

Method: Formalize a governance pattern into a computational model in which execution authority is delegated to external attestations. Each high-risk precondition must be independently attested by an authoritative source, cryptographically bound to the agent’s declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. Decisions are recorded in a tamper-evident log suitable for independent verification. A proof-of-concept implementation and illustrative demonstrations in software deployment and clinical prescribing are provided.

Result: A formal computational governance model is specified and demonstrated via a working prototype, showing how independent attestations and tamper-evident logging can control high-risk AI actions without constraining the agent’s planning autonomy.

Conclusion: The pattern provides a scalable, auditable approach to governing consequential AI actions, balancing agent autonomy with accountability. It offers concrete mechanisms (independent attestations, cryptographic binding, deterministic policy evaluation, verifiable logs) and suggests directions for deployment, security, and further research.

Abstract: Autonomous AI agents may begin to perform consequential, irreversible actions such as clinical prescribing and production software deployment. This paper observes that human institutions have governed powerful autonomous actors not by monitoring their reasoning but by requiring independently attested evidence at the point of consequential action. We formalise this institutional pattern as a computational governance model for AI agent systems. Under the proposed model, an agent retains full autonomy over planning and reasoning but holds no execution authority over designated high-risk actions. Execution is conditional on preconditions that are each independently attested by a separate authoritative source, cryptographically bound to a declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. Decisions are recorded in a tamper-evident log amenable to independent re-verification. We present a proof-of-concept implementation and illustrate the model with examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing.

</details>


### [104] [COrigami: An AI Pipeline for Co-Designing Flat-Foldable Visually Recognisable Origami](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26299)
*Tom Zahavy,Shaobo Hou,Thomas Tumiel,James Doran,Francesco Faccio,Xidong Feng,Alex Havrilla,Igor Khytryi,Chenglei Li,Lisa Schut,Vivek Veeriah,Arijan Abrashi,Michał Kosmulski,Robert J. Lang,Nick Robinson,Brandon Wong,Marcus Chiam,Gloria Fang,Satinder Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An AI-driven pipeline (COrigami) converts natural language into crease patterns for computational origami, combining geometric constraint solving with reinforcement learning-based aesthetic evaluation to support co-creative design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between verifiable geometric constraints and subjective aesthetics in creative folding; provide a mathematically grounded, collaborative tool for origami design.

Method: End-to-end pipeline: NL-to-crease via semantic stick figure, base packing, flat-foldable crease pattern solving, shaping, and RL refinement with an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop; produces starting patterns for human refinement.

Result: Demonstrates that integrating algorithmic optimization with autonomous aesthetic critique can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints, enabling reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity in computational origami.

Conclusion: AI can act as a collaborative assistant in origami design, enabling co-creative workflows that respect mathematical constraints while shaping aesthetic outcomes.

Abstract: While generative AI has achieved remarkable success in solving problems with verifiable solutions, generating physical art that satisfies both strict geometric constraints and subjective visual aesthetics remains a challenge. This paper presents an approach to tackle these difficulties in the domain of computational origami, a mathematically rigid environment that grounds artistic design within the equations of flat foldability. We present COrigami, an end-to-end AI-driven pipeline that assists the design cycle by generating crease patterns from natural language. Our pipeline involves generating a semantic stick figure, computing a base packing, solving for a flat-foldable crease pattern, shaping the flat-folded crease pattern, and refining the generated model using reinforcement learning driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop. Our system acts as a highly effective collaborative assistant, generating structural starting points that human artists can further expand and shape. By integrating algorithmic optimisation with autonomous aesthetic critique, this work demonstrates how AI systems can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints to enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity.

</details>


### [105] [The Verification Horizon: No Silver Bullet for Coding Agent Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26300)
*Binghai Wang,Chenlong Zhang,Dayiheng Liu,Jiajun Zhang,Jiawei Chen,Mouxiang Chen,Rongyao Fang,Siyuan Zhang,Xuwu Wang,Yuheng Jing,Zeyao Ma,Zeyu Cui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reliable verification is the key bottleneck as models become capable; the paper introduces a three-way signal quality framework (scalability, faithfulness, robustness) and analyzes four reward constructions across tasks, showing that targeted verification reduces reward hacking and improves performance; concludes no fixed reward suffices as policy capability grows and verification must co-evolve with the generator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human intent is inherently underspecified and proxy rewards during training can be hacked or saturate, making verification hard but essential for aligning advanced agents with intended goals.

Method: Proposes a framework to evaluate verification signals along three dimensions (scalability, faithfulness, robustness). Empirically studies four reward constructions: (1) test verifier for general coding tasks, (2) rubric verifier for frontend tasks, (3) user as verifier for real-world agent tasks, (4) automated agent verifier for long-horizon tasks. Performs across diverse task types and policy levels with in-depth analysis of reward design and leveraging reward signals.

Result: Across task types and policy levels, targeted verification design suppresses reward hacking, improves task completion quality, and yields significant gains on internal and public benchmarks.

Conclusion: Achieving scalability, faithfulness, and robustness simultaneously is the central challenge; no fixed reward function remains effective as policy capability grows; verification must co-evolve with the generator.

Abstract: A classical intuition holds that verifying a solution is easier than producing one. For today's coding agents, this intuition is being inverted: as foundation models develop stronger reasoning capabilities and engineering harnesses grow more sophisticated, generating complex candidate solutions is no longer difficult -- reliably verifying them has become the harder problem. Every verifier we can build is only a proxy for human intent, never the intent itself. This makes verification subject to a twofold difficulty: first, intent is underspecified by nature, making it inherently hard to faithfully check whether it has been fulfilled; second, during model training, optimization widens the gap between proxy and intent -- manifesting as reward hacking or signal saturation. To address this, we characterize the quality of verification signals along three dimensions -- scalability, faithfulness, and robustness -- and argue that achieving all three simultaneously is the central challenge. We further study four reward constructions: a test verifier for general coding tasks, a rubric verifier for frontend tasks, the user as verifier for real-world agent tasks, and an automated agent verifier for long-horizon tasks. Across different task types and policy capability levels, we conduct in-depth analysis and experiments on the core challenges of reward design and how to more effectively leverage reward signals. Experiments show that targeted verification design can effectively suppress reward hacking, improve task completion quality, and achieve significant gains across multiple internal and public benchmarks. These experiences collectively point to a core observation: no fixed reward function can remain effective as policy capability continues to grow; and verification must co-evolve with the generator.

</details>


### [106] [How Do Tool-Augmented LLM Agents Perform on Real-World Energy Analytics Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26346)
*David Akinpelu,Akintonde Abbas,Rereloluwa Alimi,Ayodeji Lana*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Empirical evaluation of tool-augmented LLM agents for real-world energy market analytics (243 problems across 3 categories), comparing closed- and open-source LLMs using live market tooling; releases artifacts for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Energy markets demand live data retrieval, regulatory knowledge, and multi-step quantitative reasoning under real-world constraints; static recall benchmarks are insufficient.

Method: Evaluation environment with 243 expert-curated problems across Market Data Retrieval and Analysis, Knowledge Retrieval and Interpretation, and Advanced Quantitative Modeling and Decision Analytics; tasks include price/demand analysis, tariff impact modeling, asset revenue/returns estimation, hedging, and optimization; agents use domain tools (live ISO APIs, docket search, tariff databases, asset optimization models, document retrieval); multi-dimensional scoring (approach correctness, answer accuracy, attribute alignment, source validity); category-aware scoring; compare closed-source vs open-source LLMs; key artifacts released.

Result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract; the paper presents a framework and comparative analysis; no quantitative results are provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Tool-enabled LLM agents with live data and domain tooling can address high-stakes energy analytics; findings aim to guide future research; artifacts are publicly released to support reproducibility.

Abstract: Agentic benchmarks have emerged across general-purpose and domain-specific settings, including finance, coding, law, and drug discovery, yet energy-domain evaluations remain largely limited to static knowledge recall. This is a critical gap for a sector that requires live data retrieval, specialized regulatory and market knowledge, and multi-step quantitative reasoning under real-world constraints.
  We present an empirical study of tool-augmented LLM agents on real-world energy market analytics tasks. Our evaluation environment includes 243 expert-curated problems across three categories: (1) Market Data Retrieval and Analysis, (2) Knowledge Retrieval and Interpretation, and (3) Advanced Quantitative Modeling and Decision Analytics. Tasks include price and demand analysis, tariff impact modeling, asset revenue and returns estimation, hedging strategy analysis, and optimization modeling, with problems spanning multiple difficulty levels.
  Agents are equipped with a configurable suite of domain tools, including live electricity market APIs for major U.S. ISOs, regulatory docket search, utility tariff databases, asset optimization models, and retrieval-augmented generation over energy market documents. We assess agent responses using a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol that scores approach correctness, answer accuracy, attribute alignment, and source validity, with category-aware routing to match scoring criteria to question type. We evaluate both closed-source and open-source LLMs, providing a comparative analysis of how model capability and domain tooling interact in a high-stakes professional domain. Key artifacts are publicly released to support reproducibility and future research.

</details>


### [107] [What We are Missing in Multimodal LLM Evaluation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26348)
*Po-han Li,Shenghui Chen,Sandeep Chinchali,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A survey of MLLM evaluation benchmarks identifies gaps in cross-modal integration metrics and calls for benchmarks that assess temporal-spatial coherence, physical reasoning, multimodal consistency, and selective attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To measure real progress in multimodal intelligence by aligning evaluation with cross-modal abilities; existing benchmarks are largely task-specific and fail to test integration across modalities.

Method: Literature survey of current evaluation approaches and benchmark taxonomies for MLLMs, followed by a gap analysis highlighting missing or underrepresented dimensions.

Result: Gaps identified in evaluation benchmarks: temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention. These gaps indicate benchmarks do not adequately test cross-modal integration or boundary of capabilities.

Conclusion: Addressing these gaps is essential to accurately measure progress in multimodal intelligence and to reveal true capability boundaries.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can process diverse inputs, e.g., text, images, audio, and video, and generate textual responses. While their capabilities have advanced rapidly, evaluation of such models has not kept pace. Most existing evaluation benchmarks are limited to isolated tasks and reveal little about whether a model integrates information across modalities. We examine current means for evaluating MLLMs and review the existing benchmark taxonomy to identify gaps, including temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention. Addressing these gaps is essential for measuring real progress in multimodal intelligence and exposing capability boundaries.

</details>


### [108] [OpenFinGym: A Verifiable Multi-Task Gym Environment for Evaluating Quant Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26350)
*Kaicheng Zhang,Wen Ge,Lei Jiang,Weixin Yang,Jordan Langham-Lopez,Jialin Yu,Lukasz Szpruch,Hao Ni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenFinGym is a unified, end-to-end gym environment for quantitative-finance agents that spans forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection, with automated task construction, containerized runtime, a verifier to prevent leakage, a fast paper-trading engine, and support for long-horizon tasks and both SFT/RL post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of LLM agents in quantitative finance is fragmented across isolated tasks, with benchmarks that lack financial relevance. Financial workflows are multi-stage and interdependent, requiring end-to-end evaluation that reflects real-market interaction and risk/decision-making.

Method: OpenFinGym provides a single execution/verification interface for forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection; includes an automated pipeline to convert quantitative finance publications into executable tasks; a containerized runtime with a host-side verifier to prevent runtime train-test leakage; a low-latency paper trading engine; deferred-resolution support for long-horizon/event-driven forecasts; and integration for supervised finetuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) post-training.

Result: Conceptual architectural proposal describing a unified framework; no empirical results are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: The work advocates an end-to-end, multi-task evaluation platform to reduce fragmentation, improve generalization assessment, and enable scalable, leakage-free agent rollouts in quantitative finance.

Abstract: Although large language model agents are increasingly applied to quantitative-finance workflows, their evaluation remains fragmented across isolated tasks, while the financial relevance of benchmark tasks is often overlooked. Yet financial workflows are inherently multi-stage, spanning interdependent tasks such as forecasting, strategy construction, risk management, and trading. Existing platforms typically focus on a single task, and can therefore overstate agent competence and fail to reveal weaknesses in generalization, real-market interaction, and financially meaningful decision-making. We introduce OpenFinGym, a unified gym environment for quantitative-finance agent development that covers forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection under a single execution and verification interface. OpenFinGym additionally provides an automated task-construction pipeline that turns quantitative finance publications into executable task packages; a containerised runtime with a host-side verifier service that supports scalable agent rollouts and prevents runtime train-test leakage; a paper trading engine with a low-latency data-stream design; deferred-resolution support for long-horizon and event-market forecasts; and integration for SFT and RL post-training

</details>


### [109] [Instruction Bleed: Cross-Module Interference in Prompt-Composed Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26356)
*Ching-Yu Lin,Yifan Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formalizes and measures compositional behavioral leakage (CBL) in prompt-based modular agents; uses a three-channel perturbation protocol; content perturbations yield a moderate effect (d=0.63); indicates cross-module interference is real and should be evaluated in deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address a practical failure mode in prompt-composed agent systems where editing one module unintentionally shifts behavior in others, despite no shared dependencies, due to lack of architectural isolation.

Method: Formalize CBL; deploy a job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6) across 144 trials; use a reusable three-channel protocol perturbing non-focal modules along volume, content, and form; measure paired effects, report effect size (Cohen's d) and bootstrap CIs; assess whether any channel induces observable changes in focus modules.

Result: Only the content-channel perturbation produced a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63; bootstrap 95% CI excludes zero); no recommendation flipped; the effect is sub-threshold for standard QA but accumulates over many decisions; CBL is orthogonal to known failure axes.

Conclusion: Cross-module interference is a measurable and integral aspect of prompt-composed agents; an operational definition, reusable protocol, falsifiable predictions, and system-class characterization are proposed, establishing cross-module interference measurement as a required evaluation dimension.

Abstract: Practitioners of prompt-composed agentic systems report a recurring failure mode: editing one prompt module silently shifts the behavior of others despite no shared variable or executable dependency. We formalize this as compositional behavioral leakage (CBL): interference between modules sharing a context window. CBL is enabled by architectural non-isolation: transformer self-attention provides no formal boundary between concatenated modules. We probe CBL on a deployed job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6, 144 trials) through a reusable three-channel protocol that perturbs non-focal modules along volume, content, and form. Only the content channel produces a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63, bootstrap 95% CI excluding zero); no recommendation flipped -- a sub-threshold regime invisible to standard QA but compounding across the thousands of decisions a deployed agent makes. CBL is orthogonal to known agent-failure axes (adversarial injection, cognitive degradation, multi-agent fault propagation, privacy leakage). We contribute an operational definition, a reusable protocol, a falsifiable prediction set, and a system-class characterization, establishing cross-module interference measurement as a requirement for prompt-composed agent evaluation.

</details>


### [110] [Accelerating Returns and the Qualitative Engine for Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26359)
*Guojun Liao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Accelerating returns may speed up quantitative progress, but discovery hinges on qualitative reasoning; the paper proposes the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) to preserve and organize human scientific wisdom, arguing that AGI timing is irrelevant to the core claim.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconcile Kurzweil's accelerating-returns narrative with the enduring challenge of scientific discovery and to address the missing qualitative capacity needed for genuine breakthroughs.

Method: Provide a simple mathematical interpretation of accelerating returns; analyze ARC-AGI-3 results; incorporate Hassabis's perspective on meaning; propose QES as a framework to preserve and facilitate qualitative scientific reasoning.

Result: Finds that acceleration enhances executional and infrastructural capabilities but does not solve discovery; introduces QES to preserve, organize, and make accessible the processes of scientific discovery as a form of human wisdom, independent of when AGI arrives.

Conclusion: Quantitative acceleration and AGI timing do not guarantee breakthroughs; true scientific progress requires qualitative reasoning—QES offers a structured approach to preserve and leverage human discovery processes.

Abstract: Ray Kurzweil described a thesis of accelerating returns, which is the most influential narratives in discussions of technological progress. Its central claim is that advances in multiple technological fields, especially compute, artificial intelligence, brain science, and biotechnology, interact in such a way that progress becomes self-amplifying and approximately exponential. This paper gives a simple mathematical interpretation of that claim and then argues that, even if such acceleration is real, it does not by itself resolve the central problem of scientific discovery. The reason is that accelerating returns apply most naturally to executional and infrastructural capability, whereas genuine discovery often depends on a different capacity: qualitative reasoning about when a current framework is structurally inadequate and what conceptual move is needed next. Recent ARC-AGI-3 results sharpen this distinction: humans solve the benchmark at ceiling, whereas frontier AI systems remain below 1%, indicating that the gap between current AI and human flexible reasoning is still very large. At the same time, Demis Hassabis has emphasized that humans must retain their sense of meaning and what they choose to focus their lives on, a reminder that the future of AI is not only a technical forecast but also a question of what forms of human understanding are worth preserving and transmitting. This paper positions the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) [3] as a response to that missing capacity. In this view, the Kurzweil theory helps explain why quantitative capability may accelerate, while QES addresses the central problem in scientific discovery that acceleration alone does not solve. Its value does not depend on when AGI arrives, but on the fact that the processes of scientific discovery themselves constitute a form of human wisdom worth preserving, organizing, and making accessible.

</details>


### [111] [Narration-of-Thought: Inference-Time Scaffolding for Defeasible Ethical Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26366)
*Patrick Cooper,Alvaro Velasquez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Narration-of-thought (NoT) restructures chain-of-thought prompts into five sections to externalize stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty, reducing two failure modes (stakeholder collapse and uncertainty suppression) in moral-dilemma reasoning across multiple models, and enabling auditable, consensus-driven agentic behavior without training or fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address systematic failures of standard chain-of-thought on moral dilemmas—stakeholder undercounting and premature hedging—by designing a prompt that makes reasoning components explicit and verifiable, improving reliability and auditability for deployment.

Method: Introduce NoT prompt with five sections (protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, commitment). Compare against a verbose-CoT baseline across 100 DailyDilemmas from four generators across three vendors. Include ablations, matched-budget controls, textual-gradient descent initialization, cross-family vs in-family judge, and a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol to assess consensus.

Result: NoT reduces stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to <1% and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24% across models. Cliff’s delta gains of +0.79 to +0.90 (stakeholder count) and +0.65 to +0.93 (uncertainty) on three of four generators. Section ablations attribute shifts to specific sub-instructions. Gradient-descent initialization and cross-family judges enhance performance. Five-round debate yields 95% full consensus on calibration set and 100% convergence on replication. Outputs provide auditable traces of stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty for dependable deployment.

Conclusion: NoT yields auditable, externally grounded traces that improve reliability of moral-dilemma reasoning across models without training. The approach shows strong cross-model robustness, enables debuggable decision traces, and scales to multi-party debates, but relies on prompt design and evaluation in controlled settings.

Abstract: Standard chain-of-thought on moral dilemmas exhibits two failure modes: stakeholder collapse (the trace names at most one party with a stake in the outcome) and uncertainty suppression (no explicit unknowns or hedges before committing to an action). We introduce narration-of-thought (NoT), a system prompt that structures chain-of-thought into five sections: protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, then commitment. NoT adds no training, parameters, or fine-tuning. On 100 DailyDilemmas scenarios across four generators from three vendors, NoT cuts stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to under 1% and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24% on every model. A matched-budget verbose-CoT control rules out token spend as the active ingredient; NoT retains Cliff's delta advantages of +0.79 to +0.90 on stakeholder count and +0.65 to +0.93 on uncertainty score for three of four generators, and a section ablation attributes each shift to its specific sub-instruction. Textual-gradient descent initialised at NoT improves the scaffold further; a cross-family training judge (different vendor from the generator) dominates an in-family one on every measured axis. Extended to a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol, the scaffold converts a 6% standoff into 95% full consensus on a calibration set and 100% combined convergence on a DailyDilemmas replication. The resulting traces externalise the stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty grounding each commitment, providing an auditable substrate for dependable agentic deployment.

</details>


### [112] [When Agents Meet Electric Bus Fleet Operations: Pricing Behavior, Trade-offs, and Policy Implications in an Aggregator Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26400)
*Jônatas Augusto Manzolli,Ali Eslami,Luis Miranda-Moreno,Jiangbo Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic aggregators combine optimization-based electric bus scheduling with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation to maintain feasible schedules, re-optimize selectively, and allocate flexibility values between the aggregator and the PTO; results show adaptive fleet-grid coordination but potential value capture by the aggregator under profit pricing, necessitating transparent coordination and tariff rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manage the complexity of coordinating service reliability, battery state-of-charge, charger availability, electricity prices, route-energy uncertainty, and V2G opportunities in electric bus fleets.

Method: Integrate an optimization core that enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges with an agentic layer comprising supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation; trigger real-time re-optimization as conditions change; compare profit-based vs operation-based (service-level) coordination modes in a realistic depot case study.

Result: Agentic aggregation maintains feasible schedules, enables selective re-optimization, and improves charging and V2G utilization; however, a trade-off exists where profit-oriented pricing can enable the aggregator to capture value from the PTO, potentially reducing PTO value.

Conclusion: Agentic aggregators can effectively manage electric bus V2G operations and fleet-grid coordination, but deployment in public fleets requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules to ensure fair distribution of benefits.

Abstract: Agentic systems are changing how complex operational tasks are coordinated, introducing a new paradigm for connecting heterogeneous data sources and automating processes. Electric bus fleets provide a relevant test case. Their operation requires continuous coordination between service reliability, battery state-of-charge, charger availability, electricity prices, route-energy uncertainty, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) opportunities. This paper proposes an agentic aggregator framework that streamlines this decision environment by coupling an optimization-based electric bus scheduling model with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation. The optimization core enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges, while the agentic layer interprets changing operating conditions, triggers real-time re-optimization when needed, and defines how flexibility value is allocated between the aggregator and the public transport operator (PTO). A realistic depot case study evaluates day-ahead and real-time operations under profit-based and operation-based coordination modes, considering service delays, route-energy deviations, electricity price shocks, and combined disturbances. The results show that agentic aggregation can support adaptive fleet-grid coordination by maintaining feasible schedules, activating re-optimization selectively, and improving the use of charging and V2G flexibility. However, they also reveal a critical trade-off: the same agentic capability that reduces operational complexity can extract value from the PTO when configured around profit-oriented pricing. These findings suggest that agentic aggregators can become useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but their deployment in public-fleet contexts requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules.

</details>


### [113] [Unbiased Canonical Set-Valued Oracles Via Lattice Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26418)
*Jobst Heitzig*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A self-referential probability oracle is modeled via closed credal sets; a lattice-theoretic fixed-point construction (Knaster–Tarski) yields a canonical, nontrivial self-consistent answer. For binary events this canonical answer is an interval under hull factoring; for non-performative questions it collapses to a point probability; the method generalizes to arbitrary random variables X using L(X|A,C). Existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness are proven; many questions remain, including whether interval structure persists in the generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-referential forecasts pose a problem: once the oracle's answer is learned and acted upon, it can alter the probability it reported. Counterfactual questions avoid influence but become irrelevant after learning. The work seeks a self-consistent, nontrivial alternative that remains meaningful after learning.

Method: Model probability as a closed credal set (a set of probability measures). Define an isotone operator capturing how being learned updates beliefs; take the least fixed point of this operator on the complete lattice of closed credal sets (Knaster–Tarski). A variant takes the least fixed point that contains every self-consistent point. Prove existence, self-consistency, nonemptiness; show reduction to point answers for non-performative questions; for binary B, under hull-factoring, the canonical answer is an interval; extend to arbitrary X with conditional law L(X|A,C).

Result: Proves that a canonical self-consistent credal set exists, is nonempty and self-consistent. For non-performative problems it reduces to a classical point probability. For binary events, the canonical answer is an interval (under a natural hull-factoring assumption). The approach generalizes in a model-free lattice-theoretic way from B to X via L(X|A,C).

Conclusion: A purely lattice-theoretic framework yields a principled, nontrivial self-consistent report for self-referential probabilities, with a concrete interval characterization in the binary case under hull factoring. Open questions remain about whether the interval characterization persists under the full generalization to arbitrary random variables.

Abstract: A non-agentic "oracle" AI that estimates probabilities of future events faces a self-reference problem: once its answer is learned and acted upon, it can change the very probability it was asked to report. One response, advocated for the Scientist AI programme, is to ask only counterfactual questions, evaluated as if the answer had no influence. We observe that such answers tend to become irrelevant the moment they are learned, precisely because their premise is then false. We therefore explore a self-referential alternative in which the oracle reports not a single probability but a credal set that is simultaneously unbiased and self-consistent with the consequences of being learned. The naive self-consistency requirement is satisfied by too many sets (including the useless answer $[0,1]$), so the problem is to single out a canonical, nontrivial member. We do so with the Knaster--Tarski fixed-point theorem on the complete lattice of closed credal sets, taking the least fixed point of a suitably defined isotone operator; a variant instead reports the least fixed point that contains every self-consistent point estimate. We prove existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness, show that the construction collapses to the classical point answer for non-performative questions, and that for a binary event the canonical answer is, under a natural hull-factoring assumption, an interval. The development is purely lattice-theoretic and extends unchanged from a binary event $B$ to an arbitrary random variable $X$, with $P(B\mid A,C)$ replaced by the conditional law $\mathcal{L}(X\mid A,C)$. We close with open questions, including whether the interval characterization itself survives that generalization.

</details>


### [114] [Estimating Uncertainty in Classifier Performance with Applications to Large Language Models and Nested Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26422)
*Kylie Anglin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper assesses confidence interval (CI) methods for text classification performance metrics under common social-science conditions (small/moderate samples, rare constructs, nested data). It finds that standard CIs (Wald, basic percentile bootstrap) perform poorly; recommends Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (notably for F1); when data are nested, adjust for effective N and degrees of freedom; hierarchical bootstrap outperforms cluster bootstrap for moderate within-subject texts but is overly conservative with very few texts per subject. The goal is improved CI reporting and more attention to validation sample size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the reliability and transparency of reported uncertainty for text-classification performance metrics in social science applications, addressing small samples, rare constructs, and hierarchical data structures that complicate interval estimation.

Method: Extensive simulation study evaluating multiple CI methods for standard performance metrics (e.g., recall, precision, F1) under conditions typical of social science text classification, including small to moderate sample sizes and texts nested within individuals; comparison across Wald, basic percentile bootstrap, Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, pseudo-count regularized bootstrap; evaluation of bootstrap variants (hierarchical vs cluster) and the effect of adjusting for effective N and degrees of freedom.

Result: Default methods like Wald and basic percentile bootstrap showed poor coverage (often well below 95%). Accuracy improved with Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a new pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (particularly for F1). With nested data, adjusting for effective N and degrees of freedom is needed for accurate CIs. Among bootstrap methods, hierarchical bootstrap is more accurate than cluster bootstrap when there are a moderate number of texts per individual, but becomes overly conservative when individuals contribute few texts.

Conclusion: The study provides practical guidance to improve uncertainty quantification in ML-based text classification within social science settings, advocating for specific CI methods, consideration of data structure (nested data), and emphasis on validating sample sizes at study design time.

Abstract: Researchers increasingly use text classification--supervised models or large language models--to measure constructs from natural language, providing metrics such as recall and precision as evidence of their validity. Yet, though these metrics are point estimates subject to sampling variation, measures of uncertainty are inconsistently reported alongside them. Further, when they are reported, they are often estimated with methods that are not appropriate when relevant labelled datasets are small or performance is high. To increase and improve confidence interval reporting in the field, this paper evaluates confidence interval methods for performance metrics under conditions typical of social science text classification: small to moderate sample sizes, infrequent constructs, and texts nested within individuals. Across simulations, default methods such as the Wald interval and the basic percentile bootstrap are the least accurate, with coverage sometimes far below the nominal 95% level. Accuracy is improved with the use of Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (which is particularly relevant to the calculation of F1). When texts are nested within individuals, we demonstrate that adjustment for both effective N and the appropriate degrees of freedom is necessary for producing accurate analytic intervals. Among bootstrap intervals, the hierarchical bootstrap is more accurate than the cluster bootstrap when individuals produce a moderate number of texts but overly conservative when individuals produce only a few. By providing guidance to the field on appropriate interval estimation, we aim to improve the transparency of machine learning applications, and to encourage greater attention to the validation sample size at the design stage.

</details>


### [115] [Data-driven Machine Learning Cannot Reach Symbolic-level Logical Reasoning -- The Limit of the Scaling Law](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26454)
*Tiansi Dong,Mateja Jamnik,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract argues that data-driven supervised learning cannot reach symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning due to two main limitations: (1) training data cannot cover all 24 valid syllogistic types; (2) end-to-end premises-to-conclusion mapping creates conflicting targets for perception vs. reasoning. The authors show Euler Net fails to achieve rigorous reasoning, test GPT-5-nano and GPT-5 on satisfiability across four surface forms, and find surface form affects reasoning; GPT-5 can reach high accuracy but may provide incorrect explanations. They conclude that supervised machine learning cannot attain the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether scaling data and compute in supervised DL can achieve symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning and assess current LLMs' ability to handle syllogistic satisfiability across surface representations.

Method: Provide a theoretical analysis of two limitations to supervised DL; perform experiments with Euler Net to test rigorous syllogistic reasoning; evaluate GPT-5-nano and GPT-5 on satisfiability tasks across four surface forms (words, double words, simple symbols, long random symbols) and examine explanations.

Result: Two key limitations prevent rigorous syllogistic reasoning in supervised DL. Euler Net fails to achieve it. Surface form affects reasoning performance in LLMs. GPT-5 may achieve near-perfect accuracy but can give incorrect explanations. Stopping training at 100% accuracy does not guarantee logical rigour.

Conclusion: Supervised machine learning is unlikely to reach the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning; achieving robust, provable reasoning may require explicit symbolic or hybrid approaches that encode logical structure rather than relying solely on data-driven learning.

Abstract: Sphere neural networks have achieved symbolic level syllogistic reasoning without training data, raising the question of where the limit of the scaling law for logical reasoning lies, i.e., whether data-driven machine learning systems can achieve the same level by increasing training data and training time. We show two methodological limitations that prevent supervised deep learning from reaching the symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning: (1) training data can not distinguish all 24 types of valid syllogistic reasoning; (2) end-to-end mapping from premises to conclusion introduces contradictory training targets between neural components for pattern recognition and logical reasoning. Beside theoretical analysis, we experimentally illustrate that Euler Net cannot achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning. We further challenge the most recent ChatGPTs (GPT-5-nano and GPT-5) to determine the satisfiability of syllogistic statements in four surface forms (patterns): words, double words, simple symbols, and long random symbols, showing that surface forms affect the reasoning performance and that ChatGPT GPT-5 may reach 100% accuracy but still provide incorrect explanations. As empirical training processes are stopped after achieving 100% accuracy, we conclude that supervised machine learning systems will not attain the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning.

</details>


### [116] [MKG-RAG-Bench: Benchmarking Retrieval in Multimodal Knowledge Graph-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26458)
*Xiaochen Wang,Bao Hoang,Han Liu,Ting Wang,Fenglong Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A cross-domain benchmark (MKG-RAG-Bench) to evaluate retrieval in multimodal knowledge-graph RAG, highlighting retrieval bottlenecks and its impact on generation; provides datasets and an evaluation pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks neglect retrieval challenges in MKG-RAG. Multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous and hard to retrieve, and retrievers built for unstructured text may underperform. A dedicated benchmark is needed to diagnose retrieval quality and guide system development.

Method: Construct two multimodal knowledge graphs (general and medical); develop aligned QA datasets for controlled retrieval+generation evaluation; use an LLM-based curation pipeline to filter low-utility knowledge, generate structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and cover diverse modality configurations; assess representative retriever families across modality settings.

Result: Demonstrates that effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance; retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes, validating the need to treat retrieval as a first-class evaluation target.

Conclusion: MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge-graph RAG systems by isolating retrieval as a core evaluation target.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over knowledge graphs has emerged as a promising approach for grounding large language models, yet existing benchmarks largely overlook the challenges of retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph RAG (MKG-RAG). In practice, retrieval is a critical bottleneck: multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous, difficult to align across modalities, and often poorly served by retrievers designed for unstructured corpora. To address this gap, we introduce MKG-RAG-Bench, a cross-domain benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate retrieval in MKG-RAG. MKG-RAG-Bench is constructed from two multimodal knowledge graphs spanning general and medical domains, and includes carefully aligned question-answering datasets that support controlled evaluation of both retrieval and downstream generation. The benchmark is built using an LLM-based curation pipeline that filters low-utility knowledge, generates structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and systematically covers diverse modality configurations. Through extensive experiments across representative retriever families and modality settings, we show that effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance, and that retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes. By isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target, MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing current limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems.

</details>


### [117] [auto-psych: Automating the science of mind using agent-driven theory discovery and experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26460)
*Ben Prystawski,Kushin Mukherjee,Daniel Wurgaft,Linas Nasvytis,Michael Y. Li,Noah D. Goodman,Michael C. Frank*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Auto-psych demonstrates nested agent-based discovery loops enabling automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science; it recovers ground-truth theories from synthetic data and outperforms literature-based theories in three human experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data collection bottlenecks in AI-driven theory discovery; leverage automated discovery and crowdsourced data to advance computational cognitive science where theories are code.

Method: An inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; an outer loop designs, launches, and analyzes crowdsourced experiments online. The system (auto-psych) tests a classic cognitive psychology case (perceived randomness in coin-flip sequences) using nested agent-based loops to generate and test explanatory theories.

Result: The system can quickly and reliably recover ground-truth theories from synthetic data; the nested structure is essential for performance; in three independent human experiments, it yields theories that fit data better than those derived from the literature.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science, illustrating a scalable path for AI-driven hypothesis generation and empirical testing with crowdsourced data.

Abstract: AI-based scientific automation is increasingly possible by using agents to generate hypotheses, design experiments, and analyze data. Data collection is a major bottleneck in this pipeline, however. Psychology, and computational cognitive science in particular, is well-positioned to benefit from AI experimentation because theories are often represented as code and crowdsourcing platforms enable programmatic human data collection at scale. Here, we apply automated discovery techniques to the project of generating theories in computational cognitive science, with an agent-based system collecting human data independently through crowdsourced survey experiments. As a testbed, we use a classic case study from cognitive psychology: judging which sequences of coin flips seem subjectively more random. Our system, auto-psych, uses nested agent-based discovery loops to generate explanatory theories of human behavior. The inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; the outer loop designs experiments to test these models, launches them online, and analyzes the data. This system can quickly and reliably recover ground-truth theories from synthetic data via systematic experimentation, but the nested structure is critical to model performance. Further, in three independent sequences of human experiments, the system finds theories that fit the data better than theories generated from the scientific literature. This work thus demonstrates the feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science.

</details>


### [118] [Clinical Harness for Governable Medical AI Skill Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26494)
*Tianhan Xu,Lei Bao,Yongxiang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a runtime governance framework, the Clinical Harness, to register, orchestrate, guard, and monitor AI-enabled clinical capabilities; demonstrates with osteoporosis using knowledge-, data-, and physics-driven skills for end-to-end lifecycle care.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical AI is dominated by isolated models; clinical care requires persistent, accountable capabilities across time; governance is needed to ensure safety, provenance, interoperability, and lifecycle management.

Method: Introduce 'clinical AI skills' and a 'Clinical Harness' architecture; describe components for registration, orchestration, guarding, and monitoring; integrate knowledge-driven, data-driven, and physics-enhanced skills; apply osteoporosis exemplar to illustrate lifecycle care under governance.

Result: Conceptual demonstration showing how integrated skills can support lifecycle management under runtime governance; provides a blueprint for implementing AI-enabled clinical capabilities in real workflows.

Conclusion: Runtime governance is essential for clinically trustworthy AI; the Clinical Harness and AI skills enable persistent, accountable AI-enabled care; osteoporosis exemplar validates the approach and highlights directions for real-world implementation and evaluation.

Abstract: Medical AI remains organized around isolated models, whereas clinical care requires accountable capabilities that persist across time. We propose clinical AI skills and the Clinical Harness: a runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, guarding and monitoring AI-enabled clinical capabilities. Using osteoporosis as an exemplar, we show how knowledge-driven, data-driven and physics-enhanced skills can support lifecycle care under runtime governance.

</details>


### [119] [Humans Disengage, Reasoning Models Persist: Separating Difficulty Registration from Deliberation Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26502)
*Han-yu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Across-item registration (difficulty tracking) aligns between humans and five thinking LRMs, but within-item allocation diverges: LRMs lengthen on uncertain/wrong trials while humans shorten on trials they expect to get wrong, revealing two distinct stopping policies under resource-rational metareasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle how humans and large reasoning models allocate time/tokens during problem solving, separating cross-item difficulty tracking (registration) from within-item bias (allocation), and to test whether similar overall difficulty correlations mask different internal control policies.

Method: Use a public matched human-LRM corpus with five thinking LRMs. Decompose deliberation into two levels: registration (response time across items) and allocation (token length when item identity fixed). Measure wrong-vs-right effects (Cohen's d) within items, replicate across datasets, control with item fixed effects, compare with a non-thinking baseline, and interpret using resource-rational metareasoning.

Result: All agents show cross-item alignment in registration, but diverge in allocation. LRMs exhibit a large negative bias on wrong vs right (larger token use when wrong) with Cohen's d = 1.47–3.13 on H-ARC, while humans show the opposite sign. The divergence persists when item identity is fixed and replicates across datasets; absent in a non-thinking baseline. Trace length tracks problem difficulty but does not reflect control policy, so cross-item difficulty correlations are similar across agents but the within-item policy differs.

Conclusion: The data reveal two distinct stopping policies under resource-rational metareasoning: humans engage on items they expect to solve and abandon others, whereas LRMs lengthen chains when uncertain and prone to failure. Standard cross-item correlations with difficulty can disguise this divergence; evaluating reasoning requires separating registration from allocation and adopting policy-aware metrics.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) take longer on harder problems, just as humans do. This surface similarity hides an opposite pattern within items. When an LRM gets a problem wrong, it spends more tokens than when it gets the same problem right; humans do the reverse, spending less time on the trials they get wrong. We separate two levels of deliberation: how response time tracks difficulty across items (registration), and, with item identity held fixed, whether an agent spends more on its own failures or successes (allocation). On a public matched human-LRM corpus, humans and all five thinking LRMs reproduce the known cross-item alignment (registration) but diverge within items (allocation): every LRM shows a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47-3.13 on H-ARC) while humans show the opposite sign. The comparison stays inside each agent's own scale; we never put seconds and tokens on one axis. The dissociation holds under item fixed effects, replicates across datasets, and is absent in a non-thinking baseline. We read the human pattern as engagement versus abandonment: people stay on items they expect to solve and give up on the rest. We read the LRM pattern as length driven by uncertainty: chains grow when the model is unsure, which is exactly when it tends to fail. Both policies produce the same cross-item correlation with difficulty, so they look aligned on the measure prior work has used; the divergence shows up only once item identity is fixed. Under resource-rational metareasoning, the split is between two stopping policies that share a difficulty signal but implement opposite control; trace length captures the signal and misses the control.

</details>


### [120] [NeuraDock Visual Cognitive Load Agent Tutorial: A Quality-Gated Open-Source EEG Workflow for Alpha Dynamics and Real-Time Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26518)
*Zhiyuan Xu,Yueqing Dai,Junling Li,Junwen Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Step-by-step reproducible tutorial showing how to install and run NeuraDock Agent, perform preprocessing and QC, extract Alpha metrics, compare rest vs task visual cognitive-load, run mini-dataset analyses, launch a live online dashboard, call a real-time API from external apps, and use an LLM interpretation layer to explain quality risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge offline EEG toolkits with real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipelines by providing a transparent, reproducible workflow and validation pathway focused on Alpha dynamics.

Method: A reproducible workflow: install NeuraDock Agent; preprocess and quality-control EEG data; compute Alpha dynamics and workload metrics; perform within-subject Rest/Task comparisons; execute the included mini-dataset analyses (18 recordings, 10 contrasts); benchmark local online API latency; start an online dashboard; call the real-time API from external applications; apply an LLM-based interpretation layer to explain data quality risks.

Result: Applied to the mini-dataset: 18 recordings processed; 10 within-subject contrasts generated; 7 of 10 contrasts show task-related posterior Alpha suppression; initial evidence of within-subject repeatability; benchmarked local online API latency.

Conclusion: The tutorial provides a transparent, end-to-end path from EEG data to real-time cognitive-load prototypes, addressing the offline-online gap and targeting researchers, developers, and applied teams.

Abstract: This tutorial paper provides a step-by-step, reproducible walkthrough of NeuraDock Agent, an open-source EEG agent focused on Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis. The goal is practical: a reader should be able to install the agent, run EEG preprocessing and quality control, generate Alpha dynamics figures, perform within-subject Rest/Task visual cognitive-load comparison, run the public mini-dataset analyses and compare them with the reference validation summary, start an online dashboard, call the real-time API from an external application, and use the LLM interpretation layer to explain quality risks. Existing EEG toolkits provide excellent offline analysis, but assembling a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline often requires manually bridging acquisition, custom QC, Alpha feature extraction, and a web API; this tutorial closes that offline-to-online gap. The tutorial uses a quality-gated workflow: downstream Alpha and workload metrics are computed only after preprocessing and QC gating rather than directly from raw EEG. In the included mini-dataset validation, the agent processed 18 recordings, generated 10 within-subject comparisons, observed task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 of 10 contrasts, estimated initial evidence of within-subject repeatability, and benchmarked local online API latency. The tutorial is intended for researchers, developers, and applied teams who want a transparent path from EEG files to real-time visual cognitive-load prototypes.

</details>


### [121] [Boundary-Aware Context Grounding for A Low-Channel EEG Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26519)
*Zhiyuan Xu,Yueqing Dai,Junling Li,Junwen Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A hardware-aware grounding architecture (NeuraDock Agent) separates a deterministic EEG engine from an LLM layer, ensuring only supported measurements, workflows, and results are exposed while keeping raw data local; evaluated for reproducibility and boundary robustness, showing practical calibration benefits but not clinical validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between general LLM reasoning and domain-specific EEG constraints (sensor capabilities, pipeline support, and justified interpretations), particularly for low-channel EEG where misinterpretations are likely.

Method: Architectural separation: a local deterministic EEG engine handles parsing, quality control, spectral workflows, and artifact writing; an LLM layer uses an allowlisted compact summary and a versioned context pack describing the hardware (seven channels), workflows, result fields, boundaries, limits, and reference cases. Raw EEG stays on-device. Evaluation includes: (1) reproducibility across 12 recordings and repeats, (2) request-capture and failure-injection to test data boundary preservation, (3) a boundary-awareness benchmark with 36 questions, four context ablations, and two LLMs (288 outputs).

Result: Demonstrated reproducible structure and artifacts across repetitions; boundary-preserving behavior under HTTP or malformed outputs; robust performance under normal and adversarial queries; supports hardware- and implementation-aware grounding for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses.

Conclusion: Hardware- and implementation-aware grounding is a practical mechanism to calibrate EEG agent behavior, preserving data locality and explicit context, though the work does not establish clinical validity of any cognitive-load index.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can make scientific software easier to use. However, a general model does not automatically know which measurements a particular sensor can support, which algorithms are implemented in the current software, or which conclusions are justified by a computed result. These distinctions are especially important for low-channel electroencephalography (EEG), where sparse spatial coverage and variable signal quality make plausible but unsupported interpretations easy to produce. We present NeuraDock Agent, an open-source architecture that separates a deterministic local EEG engine from a hardware-aware language layer. The numerical engine parses recordings, performs quality control, executes reviewed spectral workflows, and writes machine-readable artifacts. The LLM receives only a compact, allowlisted summary and a versioned context pack. The context describes the seven-channel hardware, reviewed workflows, result fields, implementation boundaries, scientific limits, and reference cases. Raw EEG and dense per-sample arrays remain local
  We evaluate the system at three levels. First, 12 recordings produced identical structured results over ten numerical repetitions, and a complete Rest/Task run produced identical result, report, and figure hashes over three repetitions. Second, request-capture and failure-injection experiments confirmed the tested data boundary and preservation of local artifacts under HTTP, malformed-output, and connection failures. Third, a boundary-awareness benchmark tested 36 ordinary and adversarial questions under four context ablations and two LLMs, yielding 288 outputs.These results support hardware- and implementation-aware grounding as a practical mechanism for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses; they do not establish clinical validity or a validated absolute cognitive-load index.

</details>


### [122] [Radical AI Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26523)
*Daniel A. Herrmann,Benjamin A. Levinstein*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a holistic framework to attribute beliefs, desires, and meanings to AI systems by integrating radical interpretation with mechanistic interpretability, arguing that these attitudes and the underlying propositional structure are mutually constraining and cannot be measured piecemeal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable trustworthy AI by diagnosing system goals and potential deception, and to provide clear criteria for when interpretability tools have succeeded.

Method: Synthesize philosophical radical interpretation with mechanistic interpretability, define a set of representationalist and interpretationist criteria, and propose joint tests that assess beliefs, desires, and propositional structure together; discuss the role of holism and how attitudes constrain structure and vice versa.

Result: A coherent framework with criteria and tests for attributing beliefs, desires, and meanings in AI; clarifies when interpretability tools succeed by enforcing joint constraints and compatibility with the system's internals.

Conclusion: Beliefs, desires, and propositional structure are jointly constrained; holism complicates piecemeal attribution but also provides leverage to constrain and measure attitudes via mechanistic interpretability; the framework can guide safer deployment and evaluation of AI systems.

Abstract: We develop a framework for interpreting AI systems as agents, drawing on the philosophical tradition of radical interpretation and the tools of mechanistic interpretability. The core question is: given the computational facts about a system, how do we solve for its beliefs, desires, and meanings? This matters increasingly for safety. We want to be able to trust the systems we deploy, whether by understanding their goals or, more modestly, by reliably detecting deception. Interpretability researchers are building tools to read beliefs and desires off a model's internals, but there is no settled account of when such a tool has succeeded. This book supplies one. We propose criteria on both representationalist and interpretationist approaches, and tie each to tests current interpretability methods can carry out. A central lesson is that these attributions cannot be made piecemeal. Beliefs, desires, and the propositional structure they presuppose are jointly constrained, and a method that fixes one while measuring the others inherits whatever distortions that introduces. This holism becomes pressing for AI systems, which may not share the interpreter's concepts. However, it also provides leverage: a system's attitudes constrain its propositional structure, that structure constrains which attitudes can be attributed, and mechanistic interpretability can help us measure both.

</details>


### [123] [PMDformer: Patch-Mean Decoupling Information Transformer for Long-term Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26549)
*Ao Hu,Liangjian Wen,Jiang Duan,Yong Dai,He Yan,Dongkai Wang,Jun Wang,Yukun Zhang,Ruoxi Jiang,Zenglin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PMDformer introduces Patch-Mean Decoupling to separate patch-level trend from shape residuals, with Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA) to enhance long-range, cross-variable forecasting; reports state-of-the-art performance on LTSF benchmarks; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-term time series forecasting requires capturing long-range dependencies and cross-variable relationships. Patch-based transformers struggle to model true shape similarities across patches due to scale differences and overemphasis on outdated correlations. Decoupling patch trends and refining attention can improve both stability and accuracy.

Method: Patch-Mean Decoupling (PMD) subtracts the patch mean to separate trend from residual shape. Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) reintegrates the decoupled trend into the attention computation. Proximal Variable Attention (PVA) concentrates cross-variable attention on the most relevant, recent time segments to reduce reliance on outdated correlations. These components form PMDformer.

Result: Extensive experiments show PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks.

Conclusion: PMDformer effectively captures shape similarity in long-term forecasting by decoupling trend and residuals and by focusing attention on recent, cross-variable signals. The approach provides improved performance and robustness; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) plays a crucial role in fields such as energy management, finance, and traffic prediction. Transformer-based models have adopted patch-based strategies to capture long-range dependencies, but accurately modeling shape similarities across patches and variables remains challenging due to scale differences. To address this, we introduce patch-mean decoupling (PMD), which separates the trend and residual shape information by subtracting the mean of each patch, preserving the original structure and ensuring that the attention mechanism captures true shape similarities. Futhermore, to more effectively model long-range dependencies and capture cross-variable relationships, we propose Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA). The former module reintegrates the decoupled trend from PMD while calculating attention output. And the latter focuses cross-variable attention on the most relevant, recent time segments to avoid overfitting on outdated correlations. Combining these components, we propose PMDformer, a model designed to effectively capture shape similarity in long-term forecasting scenarios. Extensive experiments indicate that PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/aohu1105/PMDformer.

</details>


### [124] [Explainable Ensemble-Based Machine Learning Models for Detecting the Presence of Cirrhosis in Hepatitis C Patients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26561)
*Abrar Alotaibi,Lujain Alnajrani,Nawal Alsheikh,Alhatoon Alanazy,Salam Alshammasi,Meshael Almusairii,Shoog Alrassan,Aisha Alansari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ML-based detection of cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients using a dataset of 2038 Egyptian patients; Extra Trees outperformed others with 96.92% accuracy using 16 features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early detection of cirrhosis in HCV patients to prevent complications; gap in applying ML to this specific task.

Method: Dataset: 28 attributes, UCI ML Repository; models: Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, Extra Trees; evaluation metrics: accuracy, recall, precision, AUC; feature subset selection to 16 features.

Result: Extra Trees achieved 96.92% accuracy, 94.00% recall, 99.81% precision, AUC 96%, using 16 of 28 features.

Conclusion: ML can accurately diagnose cirrhosis in HCV patients; feature reduction to 16 features; potential to aid early detection and clinical decision-making.

Abstract: Hepatitis C is a liver infection caused by a virus, which results in mild to severe inflammation of the liver. Over many years, hepatitis C gradually damages the liver, often leading to permanent scarring, known as cirrhosis. Patients sometimes have moderate or no symptoms of liver illness for decades before developing cirrhosis. Cirrhosis typically worsens to the point of liver failure. Patients with cirrhosis may also experience brain and nerve system damage, as well as gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Treatment for cirrhosis focuses on preventing further progression of the disease. Detecting cirrhosis earlier is therefore crucial for avoiding complications. Machine learning (ML) has been shown to be effective at providing precise and accurate information for use in diagnosing several diseases. Despite this, no studies have so far used ML to detect cirrhosis in patients with hepatitis C. This study obtained a dataset consisting of 28 attributes of 2038 Egyptian patients from the ML Repository of the University of California at Irvine. Four ML algorithms were trained on the dataset to diagnose cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients: a Random Forest, a Gradient Boosting Machine, an Extreme Gradient Boosting, and an Extra Trees model. The Extra Trees model outperformed the other models achieving an accuracy of 96.92%, a recall of 94.00%, a precision of 99.81%, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 96% using only 16 of the 28 features.

</details>


### [125] [EvoOptiGraph: Weakness-Driven Coevolution via Graph-Based Structural Generation for Optimization Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26578)
*Qingcan Kang,Mingyang Liu,Xiaojin Fu,Shixiong Kai,Tao Zhong,Mingxuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A co-evolving data-model framework (EvoOptiGraph) for MILP optimization tasks that uses graph representations, evolutionary data generation, and RL with verifiable rewards to improve LLM performance, achieving superior accuracy, executability, and generalization across six datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two core issues: (1) training corpora lack structural diversity for optimization modeling with LLMs, and (2) static data generation pipelines decoupled from model learning. EvoOptiGraph proposes a closed-loop, co-evolutionary setup driven by model weaknesses to enhance data diversity and model capability.

Method: Represent each MILP as an attributed bipartite graph and apply validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate structurally diverse instances. Convert evolved graphs into solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation and verify back-translation. Train in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on an initial dataset, followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where weakness signals guide generation of new instances targeting the model’s failures; this creates a closed loop updating the training distribution.

Result: Empirical evaluation on six public MILP datasets shows EvoOptiGraph outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization.

Conclusion: Targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy for improving LLM performance on optimization modeling tasks.

Abstract: Automating optimization modeling from natural language with large language models (LLMs) faces two key challenges. First, training corpora lack structural diversity. Second, data generation pipelines remain static and decoupled from model learning. To address these challenges, we propose EvoOptiGraph, a novel framework where data and model co-evolve, driven by model weaknesses. EvoOptiGraph represents each mixed-integer linear program (MILP) as an attributed bipartite graph and applies validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate structurally diverse instances. The evolved graphs are converted into solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation and verified back-translation. Training proceeds in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on an initial dataset, followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where graph-derived weakness signals guide the generation of new instances targeting the model's failures. This forms a closed loop that continuously updates the training distribution. Empirical results on six public datasets show that EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization. These results demonstrate that targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy for improving LLMs on optimization modeling tasks.

</details>


### [126] [A Multi-Level Validation and Traceability Framework for AI-Generated Telescope Scheduling Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26585)
*Hengchu Xiao,Chuanjun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework improves reliability and executability of AI-based telescope scheduling by validating data references, ensuring logical consistency, and enforcing constraints, using atomic reasoning units and dependency graphs to enable error localization and post hoc analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes astronomical scheduling requires reliable, executable decisions with traceable reasoning; existing AI decisions suffer from data reference issues, reasoning errors, and non-executable outputs, hindering deployment in high-reliability tasks.

Method: Introduce a framework with data reference validation, logical consistency checks, and observational/instrumental constraint verification. Represent decisions as sequences of atomic reasoning units with dependency relationships, enabling error localization and post hoc analysis. Use validation to filter/correct invalid decisions and enable feedback correction.

Result: Compared with pure AI methods, the framework enhances executability and reliability, reduces loss of transient opportunities, and improves repair/block of erroneous decisions; experiments show improved reliability and executability in complex scenarios.

Conclusion: Provides a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling by delivering a traceable, verifiable decision-making process with better error handling.

Abstract: With the gradual introduction of AI into telescope scheduling, AI-based decision-making has shown advantages in handling complex multi-constraint problems. However, its outputs often suffer from inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable decisions, limiting applicability in high-reliability observational tasks. In this work, we propose a multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework that performs systematic reliability verification of AI-generated decisions prior to execution, and enables explicit representation of the reasoning process to support traceable decision-making. The framework integrates data reference validation, logical consistency checks, and observational and instrumental constraint verification to filter and correct invalid decisions. It also introduces atomic reasoning units and their dependency relationships, representing scheduling decisions as a sequence of interconnected reasoning steps that support error localization and post hoc analysis.
