Experiments show that the framework improves executability and reliability of AI scheduling and reduces loss of transient opportunities. In particular, feedback correction and structured validation of reasoning steps enhance the ability to repair and block erroneous decisions, especially in complex scenarios. Compared with pure AI methods, the framework-enhanced approach maintains flexibility while substantially improving reliability and executability. These results demonstrate a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling.

</details>


### [127] [Content-Based Smart E-Mail Dispatcher Using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26593)
*K. Paramesha,K R Sriram,Sujan Shetty,Shamanth Kishore,R. Tejaswini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automates email-to-WhatsApp group routing using an LLM-driven agent framework, enabling content-based dispatch to student groups without labeled data; aims to reduce email workload and cognitive load while improving information flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Email overload and manual forwarding are error-prone and time-consuming in large organizations. Automating routing to relevant groups can improve productivity and reduce cognitive burden.

Method: An agent-based system with prompts incorporating email content as input and contextual instructions. The LLM analyzes email text to determine the relevant WhatsApp groups (e.g., by semester/program) and dispatches messages accordingly. The design claims to function without labeled datasets.

Result: A system that automatically routes emails to appropriate student WhatsApp groups, purportedly enhancing productivity and reducing cognitive load without requiring labeled data.

Conclusion: The paper presents a practical LLM-driven solution for automatic content-based routing of emails to WhatsApp groups, addressing information flow efficiency; success hinges on prompt design, LLM reliability, and handling privacy/consent considerations.

Abstract: Email communication has become an integral part of personal and professional life, but handling its vast volume is still a significant issue for large organisations. Manual perusal of emails and forwarding their contents and attachments to intended recipients using other instant messaging platforms has proved to be error-prone and time-consuming leading to losses in terms of productivity and creating undue stress. The main objective of this paper is to explore an alternative mechanism that is to automate the task of dispatching emails based on their contents to the respective WhatsApp groups of students of various semesters of programs in an engineering college, facilitating a smooth flow of information from one end to another end in an organisation. The dispatcher system is built using agents querying large language models (LLMs) to enable it to analyze the contents of emails and route them to the relevant groups of students for their information and consumption. The system harnesses the capabilities of LLMs in analysing the textual contents for decision-making. With a well-structured agent framework prompt that includes email content as input with instructions and context, the system figures out the relevant groups to which the email message is dispatched, thus providing the required information on time. The proposed system does not rely on labelled datasets and provides several benefits, including enhanced productivity and a reduction in the cognitive load associated with reading emails.

</details>


### [128] [LLM-based Models for Detecting Emerging Topics in Service Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26595)
*Mahsa Tavakoli,Ruth Bankey,Cristián Bravo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework combining fine-tuned, quantized LLMs, statistical methods, and human-in-the-loop to analyze multilingual public-sector feedback, enabling detection of emerging service quality topics and potential inequities with improved alignment to expert judgments and reduced model fabrication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing volumes of multilingual public feedback hinder timely identification of emerging service quality issues and disparities; traditional manual or static indicator approaches lack scalability and fail to capture complex patterns in text.

Method: Fine-tuned, quantized LLMs integrated with statistical techniques and human oversight (human-in-the-loop). Multilingual analysis with similarity assessments and expert evaluations, emphasizing near-term emergent topics and equity signals.

Result: Evaluation shows stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models; human-in-the-loop reduces LLM fabrication and improves reliability and relevance of insights; demonstrates practicality for scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public-sector service quality analysis.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical, responsible AI approach for multilingual feedback analysis that enhances service quality, fairness, responsiveness, and public trust in tax administrations and similar public-sector contexts.

Abstract: Enhancing the analysis of service feedback is essential for public sector organizations, particularly tax administrations, where trust and compliance depend on fair and effective service delivery. As feedback volumes grow, identifying emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations becomes increasingly challenging. Traditional approaches often rely on manual review or static expert-defined indicators, limiting scalability and the ability to capture complex patterns in textual feedback.
  This paper presents a novel methodology that integrates large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration to improve multilingual customer feedback analysis. The primary objective is to detect emerging service quality topics that may also reveal potential inequities in service delivery. Our framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to produce accurate, computationally efficient, and context-aware analyses.
  The proposed approach was evaluated using similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers, demonstrating stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models. By incorporating a human-in-the-loop framework, the methodology reduces LLM fabrication while improving the reliability and relevance of generated insights.
  The results demonstrate the practicality of combining LLMs with human expertise to support scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations. This work contributes to the development of responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through more effective analysis of multilingual customer feedback.

</details>


### [129] [Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26649)
*Adam Mondl,Matthew Maisel,John H. Brock*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autoformalization pipeline converts agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural-language policies into Cedar policies via an LLM-based generator-critic loop, enabling formal verification; evaluated on MedAgentBench with substantially higher coverage than prior symbolic enforcement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal policy enforcement in high-stakes domains requires guarantees; probabilistic guardrails and hand-coded symbolic rules either lack formal guarantees or suffer from scalability limits.

Method: LLM-based generator-critic loop that autoformalizes prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and NL policy documents into Cedar Policy Language; pipeline outputs formally verifiable policies.

Result: On MedAgentBench, autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than prior hand-coded symbolic enforcement.

Conclusion: The approach scales better and provides formal guarantees by increasing coverage of NL specifications in formal Cedar policies, addressing key limitations of prior methods.

Abstract: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but most existing approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or on hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to the breadth of real policy specifications. We present an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. The resulting policies are written in the Cedar Policy Language. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, our autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.

</details>


### [130] [SKILL-DISCO: Distilling and Compiling Agent Traces into Reusable Procedural Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26669)
*Zhongxin Guo,Danrui Qi,Hanwen Gu,Peng Cheng,Yongqiang Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillDisCo distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into executable, verifiable procedural skills, reducing reasoning cost and execution length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce repeated reasoning over similar task instances by learning reusable, parameterized control-flow components that capture shared structure across successful traces in FSM-defined domains.

Method: Extract and distill parameterized finite-state-machine (PFSM) subgraphs from successful traces; compile them into callable procedural skills that are executable and verifiable; evaluate on ALFWorld and WebArena across model scales; measure success rate and turns.

Result: In experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena, SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces the number of agent turns, demonstrating the benefit of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

Conclusion: Representing shared experience as reusable PFSM subgraphs enables modular, verifiable skills that generalize across tasks and model scales, yielding more efficient and effective problem solving.

Abstract: Agents often repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work has explored workflow reuse and executable skill induction, but it remains unclear which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how the shared procedural structure should be represented across successful traces. We study this problem in FSM-defined scenarios, where successful traces can be viewed as paths in an unknown transition graph, and formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. Based on this view, we introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

</details>


### [131] [NebulaExp-8B: An Empirical Post-Training Pipeline via Full-Scale Ablation Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26671)
*Qiaobo Hao,Yangqian Wu,Shunyi Wang,Zhongjian Zhang,Ziqun Li,Yayin He,Muqing Li,Chen Zhong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NebulaExp is a transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline for Qwen3-8B-base with two branches (general instruct and complex reasoning). It curates large datasets, applies a multi-stage data processing stack, and shows measurable gains through SFT and GRPO RL, including single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD strategies. The work emphasizes reproducibility and analyzes trade-offs across instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reproducibility gaps in post-training alignment and enable lightweight, transparent optimization of LLMs by detailing data construction, filtering, and training recipes.

Method: End-to-end data processing and ranking stack: response distillation, cross-verification filtering, grading of difficulty, task classification, and diversity-aware sampling; two model branches with NebulaExp-Ins-SFT for Instruct and GRPO RL; reasoning branch with medium-difficulty GRPO RL; exploration of OPD with single and multiple teachers (MOPD) using small supervision samples.

Result: Instruct branch: SFT improves from 55.01 to 60.99; GRPO RL raises to 61.85. Reasoning branch: medium-difficulty GRPO RL raises from 73.88 to 75.17. OPD: single-teacher with 4K samples yields +3.26 on IFEval and +4.43 average; MOPD with 10K samples yields +4.18 over base. The report provides a fully reproducible post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs and discusses capability trade-offs.

Conclusion: Presents a transparent, reproducible post-training workflow for 8B-scale LLMs, demonstrates performance gains across instruction following and reasoning, and analyzes trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general knowledge.

Abstract: Post-training alignment determines the reasoning and human preference following capabilities of large language models, yet most existing works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, which hinders community reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work presents NebulaExp, a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline built on Qwen3-8B-base, covering two orthogonal model branches: general instruct model and complex reasoning-specialized model. We curate a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and a 200K verifiable RL candidate pool, and design an end-to-end data processing stack including response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, our three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning approach NebulaExp-Ins-SFT improves the average benchmark score from the 55.01 baseline of Qwen3-8B-nothink to 60.99. GRPO reinforcement learning then further elevates the average score to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. To address RL's dependency on task verifiers, we systematically investigate single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD): utilizing merely 4K instruction-following samples and outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain; MOPD fuses four domain-specialist teachers with merely 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over the base model. This report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, and comprehensively dissects the capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge.

</details>


### [132] [Do Safety Guardrails Need to Reason? LeanGuard: A Fast and Light Approach for Robust Moderation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26686)
*Dongbin Na*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A lightweight label-only encoder can match a larger reasoning guard's moderation performance with far less compute, challenging the assumed necessity of chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CoT increases inference cost and latency, which is undesirable for on-device guardrails. The study questions whether reasoning is actually beneficial for moderation accuracy and robustness.

Method: Train a lightweight bidirectional encoder (LeanGuard) and a reasoning guard on the same data. Remove the reasoning component while keeping other components fixed, enabling a controlled base comparison. Evaluate a 395M parameter label-only encoder against a larger decoder-based reasoning guard on public benchmarks, with input length capped at 512 tokens and a single forward pass.

Result: LeanGuard achieves an average F1 of 82.90 ± 0.26, matching the performance of a heavier reasoning guard while using ~100x less inference compute. It shows robustness to training-label noise and preserves higher recall at a strict false-positive rate limit, outperforming the reasoning guard in this robustness dimension.

Conclusion: Heavier reasoning guards are not necessarily superior for moderation; CoT may be unnecessary for current guardrail tasks, at least under the evaluated benchmarks. LeanGuard offers a more efficient, robust alternative. The work invites reevaluation of benchmark design and prompts further exploration of label-only constraints for guardrails.

Abstract: In order to screen a prompt or a response, the recent guardrail methods generate a chain-of-thought (CoT) before they issue a verdict. This design follows a common belief that step-by-step reasoning improves a decision. However, CoT also makes the guard heavy and slow, because the model must generate many tokens before it decides. This may not match how guardrails are actually deployed. A guardrail sometimes should not be heavy and slow, and it often runs on-device, for example on an embodied robot. In this paper, we pose a question whether a safety guardrail really needs to reason. To answer this question, we train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, and we then remove only the reasoning while we keep everything else fixed. With this controlled same-base comparison, we show that the chain does not improve moderation accuracy. We name the resulting guard LeanGuard. A 395M label-only encoder reaches an average F1 of 82.90 $\pm$ 0.26 over public benchmarks. It matches a reasoning guard that is built on a much larger decoder, while it uses only a single forward pass over an input of at most 512 tokens. This is about a ~100x reduction in inference compute. We further show that this label-only encoder stays robust under training-label noise and retains far more recall at a strict false-positive rate than the reasoning guard, so a heavier reasoning guard is not the more robust choice either. Our finding suggests that the current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and that the necessity of CoT for moderation is still not proven. We release all source codes and models including LeanGuard at https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard.

</details>


### [133] [Kalman Prototypical Networks for Few-shot Fault Detection in Combined Cycle Gas Turbines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26710)
*Mohammed Ayalew Belay,Lucas Ferreira Bernardino,Adil Rasheed,Rubén M. Montañés,Pierluigi Salvo Rossi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A metric-based few-shot learning framework (Kalman Prototypical Network, KPN) for CCGT fault diagnosis that treats class prototypes as latent states to stabilize embeddings and reduce episodic variance; evaluated on Modelica-simulated offshore CCGT leak faults; shows improved accuracy and convergence vs MAML, Matching Networks, and Relation Networks under limited labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fault diagnosis in combined-cycle gas turbines is hampered by scarce labeled fault data and complex thermo-fluid interactions; a robust, data-efficient learning approach is needed to generalize across transient fault scenarios.

Method: Propose Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN) where class prototypes evolve as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system (akin to a Kalman filter) to stabilize embedding representations across episodes. Use metric-based FSL, episodic training, and synthetic data from a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of offshore CCGT to generate normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Compare against standard FSL methods (Matching Networks, Relation Networks, MAML) across varying support/query configurations, assessing accuracy and stability.

Result: KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods in accuracy and stability, with faster training convergence and better generalization under limited labeled data.

Conclusion: Modeling prototype evolution as latent dynamic states improves robustness of embeddings and fault detection performance in data-scarce, clinically realistic CCGT fault scenarios; KPN is a promising approach for real-world fault diagnosis with limited labeled data.

Abstract: Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs) play a key role in modern power generation, offering both high efficiency and reduced environmental impact. However, their complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions complicate fault detection, particularly when labeled fault data are scarce. In this paper, we introduce the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning (FSL) framework specifically tailored for CCGT fault diagnosis. We model the evolution of class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system to reduce episodic variance and improve robustness in embedding representation. Synthetic data sets generated with a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system were used, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Application of the proposed framework on simulated leak fault detection tasks demonstrate that KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods such as Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

</details>


### [134] [LithoDreamer: A Physics-Informed World Model for Multi-Stage Computational Lithography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26713)
*Yuqi Jiang,Yumeng Liu,Zimu Li,Jinyuan Deng,Qian Jin,Yucheng Cui,Yu Li,Xunzhao Yin,Qi Sun,Cheng Zhuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces LithoDreamer, a physics-informed World Model for computational lithography, modeling the full Layout–Mask–Resist Image–After Development Image pipeline as a decision-driven, multi-step evolution with latent-space physics-informed dynamics and a contrastive variational optimization for interpretable intervention planning; achieves state-of-the-art forward evolution and inverse planning; dataset released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lithography models struggle to capture the continuous physical processes across mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development. A physics-informed, controllable, and interpretable model is needed to improve yield and performance.

Method: Formulates the lithography pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution in latent space (World Model). Introduces contrastive variational optimization to guide intervention paths by contrasting latent differences with evolution constraints, enabling interpretable, supervision-free optimization. Uses a physics-informed latent space to track feature changes between adjacent states and controls intervention exploration to drive subsequent transitions.

Result: LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning on lithography tasks, and provides a publicly available lithography dataset.

Conclusion: First physics-informed World Model for computational lithography; demonstrates effective forward evolution and inverse planning via latent-space physics and contrastive optimization; dataset released for public use.

Abstract: As semiconductor technology nodes scale, computational lithography is essential for ensuring yield and performance. However, lithography is a continuous physical process involving mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development, which existing models fail to capture. To overcome this limitation, we present LithoDreamer, the first physics-informed World Model (WM) framework for computational lithography, which formulates the ``Layout-Mask-Resist Image-After Development Image (ADI)'' pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. LithoDreamer captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces, in which it controls process intervention exploration and drives subsequent state transitions. To achieve interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision, we propose a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts the latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, guiding the model to generate evolutions consistent with real lithography physics. Experiments show LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning. Our lithography dataset is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/7jiangyq/lithodreamer.git).

</details>


### [135] [A Latent ODE Approach to Spatiotemporal Modeling of Cine Cardiac MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26718)
*David Brüggemann,Ekaterina Krymova,Firat Özdemir,Jochen von Spiczak,Sebastian Kozerke,Samia Mora,Robert Manka,Mathieu Salzmann,Olga V. Demler*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A continuous latent dynamical model for 3D+t ventricular motion using heart-rate–aware ODE dynamics and graph-based mesh autoencoder improves HF risk prediction beyond conventional CMR markers in UK Biobank.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional risk models rely on a few image indices from selected cardiac phases, potentially missing full-cycle ventricular dynamics; a continuous, interpretable latent representation could capture richer phenotypes and improve prognostic performance.

Method: Develop a latent dynamical model encoding bi-ventricular anatomy and full-cycle cine motion as a continuous latent trajectory. Use heart-rate-aware neural ODE dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder for 3D+t reconstruction. Define a covariate-conditioned prior for the end-diastolic latent state. Use a Cox model to relate latent deviations to incident heart failure. Validate on 72,386 UK Biobank participants without baseline CVD, with 367 HF events. Evaluate prognostic performance (C-index) and reconstruction/generative quality; compare to non-graph/ non-ODE baselines and seven established markers.

Result: The latent score added to refitted pooled cohort equations increases stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785; seven established markers yield 0.764. The proposed model achieves the best balance among reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and prognostic performance compared to non-graph and non-ODE approaches.

Conclusion: Continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion yields informative phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries and improves risk prediction. External validation in more representative cohorts is needed before clinical deployment.

Abstract: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) captures rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion, but conventional risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases. We present a latent dynamical model that encodes bi-ventricular anatomy and full-cycle cine motion as a continuous latent trajectory, using heart-rate-aware neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion. A covariate-conditioned prior defines the expected end-diastolic latent state, and a Cox proportional hazards model tests whether deviations from this prior predict incident heart failure. We studied 72,386 UK Biobank participants without baseline cardiovascular disease, including 367 incident heart failure events. In a held-out evaluation subset, adding the latent score to refitted pooled cohort equations improved the stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785, compared with 0.764 for seven established cardiac markers. Compared with non-graph and non-ODE approaches, the proposed model gave the best trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and downstream prognostic performance. These results suggest that continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion provides informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, while external validation in more representative patient cohorts is required before clinical risk-prediction use.

</details>


### [136] [Socratic agents for autonomous scientific discovery in high-dimensional physical systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26722)
*Xianrui Zeng,Pengfei Liu,Yirui Zang,Yang Shen,Fei Yu,Chunlei Yu,Minghao Liu,Yang Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An autonomous AI scientist, AHOIS, uses Socratic interrogation within a closed-loop experimental framework to construct, test, and revise physical explanations, demonstrated on a multimode-fiber optical system with self-discovered encoding and measurement strategies, yielding competitive MNIST/Fashion-MNIST classification without hand-crafted priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond procedural automation toward epistemic autonomy in scientific discovery, enabling AI agents to generate and falsify hypotheses, check constraints, and revise models in response to evidence within real experimental settings.

Method: A multi-agent architecture comprising a physics-critic agent that interrogates hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation, and falsification criteria; operates in closed-loop experimentation on a real multimode-fiber optical platform; without pre-encoded schemes, it discovers and validates a random-interference encoding hypothesis, learns sparse measurement strategies, diagnoses failure modes, and translates an existing imaging protocol into an executable workflow for a non-original configuration.

Result: Discovered encoding yielding 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9; classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST; identified and interpreted distinct failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination, detector noise); translated a published protocol into an autonomous workflow; ablations show Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration, and experimental-plan validity.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a pathway from workflow automation to evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous discovery in complex physical environments, showcasing epistemic autonomy through Socratic-style hypothesis testing within a real experimental setting.

Abstract: The automation of scientific discovery has reached an inflection point. While AI systems now operate instruments, optimize parameters and generate hypotheses, most remain procedural: they execute workflows fixed by human designers. True autonomous science demands epistemic autonomy--the capacity to construct, challenge and revise physical explanations in response to evidence. Here we introduce AHOIS, a multi-agent AI scientist that embeds Socratic midwifery into closed-loop experimentation. A physics-critic agent interrogates hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation and falsification-criteria formulation. We evaluate AHOIS on a real multimode-fibre optical platform, a high-dimensional system with complex wave transformations, indirect detection, environmental drift and multi-modal acquisition. Without prior encoding schemes, classifiers or speckle models, the system autonomously proposed and validated a random-interference encoding hypothesis, discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies, diagnosed distinct failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination and detector noise) and translated a published imaging protocol into an executable workflow on a non-original configuration. The discovered encoding yielded 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9 and classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST. Ablations show that Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration and experimental-plan validity. These results establish a route from workflow automation towards evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous discovery in complex physical environments.

</details>


### [137] [Scientific discovery as meta-optimization: a combinatorial optimization case study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26728)
*Yuan-Hang Zhang,Chesson Sipling,Massimiliano Di Ventra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Meta-optimization via consensus objective aggregation for LLM-assisted scientific discovery; applied to MemComputing-based 3-SAT solver; improves scaling and speed; a framework for evolving evaluation criteria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the static evaluation criteria in automated scientific discovery by letting the objective function itself be optimized, yielding a self-correcting meta-criterion.

Method: Generate multiple objective functions with LLMs; aggregate via correlation-weighted voting to produce a consensus objective; apply to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT on digital MemComputing machines; compare scaling and runtime.

Result: Changing baseline scaling from ~N^2.51 to ~N^1.33; ~67x speedup on largest instances; demonstration of a problem-agnostic meta-optimization framework for scientific discovery.

Conclusion: Consensus objective aggregation offers a general, self-improving evaluation framework for scientific discovery across domains; potential broad impact, with further work needed on robustness and details.

Abstract: Scientific discovery is fundamentally an optimization problem, defined by a vast "state space" of theories and experiments, and an evaluation criterion based on quality, novelty, and validity. Large language models (LLMs) have enabled automated exploration of this space, but we argue that simultaneous modification of the evaluation criteria is equally important. Here, we propose formalizing research as meta-optimization, where the optimization objective itself is also being optimized. Our key contribution is "consensus objective aggregation," where LLM-generated objective functions are combined via correlation-weighted voting, yielding a stable, self-correcting evaluation criterion that evolves as understanding deepens. We apply this framework to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT problems based on digital MemComputing machines, reducing the baseline scaling with problem size $N$ from $\sim N^{2.51}$ to $\sim N^{1.33}$ and delivering a $\sim 67\times$ speedup on the largest instances tested. As a problem-agnostic framework, we hope this approach will considerably aid scientific discovery.

</details>


### [138] [EGG: An Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26758)
*Yaochen Han,Ke Fan,Hongxu Jiang,Wanqi Xu,Weiyu Xie,Runhua Zhang,Chenhui Zhu,Yixiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EGG introduces an Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation that splits kernel design into algorithmic structure design and hardware-specific tuning, using a stage-aware multi-agent collaboration to guide LLMs toward efficient kernels. It reports 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench and real workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-performance GPU kernels for LLMs require expert-tuned optimization; current LLM-based generation lacks domain-specific optimization guidance, limiting exploration of the optimization space.

Method: A two-stage decomposition: (1) algorithmic structure design to establish a high-quality computational structure; (2) hardware-specific tuning involving parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. A stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism manages inter- and intra-stage context to stabilize optimization trajectories.

Result: EGG achieves 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench and real-world workloads, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.

Conclusion:  staged expert-guided optimization with hierarchical design and context-aware multi-agent collaboration yields substantial performance gains over standard baselines, demonstrating the value of domain-guided prompts and structured exploration for kernel generation.

Abstract: High-performance GPU kernels are critical for reducing the exponentially growing computational costs of large language models (LLMs), but their development heavily relies on manual tuning by domain experts. While recent advances in LLM-based approaches show promise for automating kernel generation, they still struggle to achieve both correctness and high performance. This limitation primarily arises from the lack of domain-specific optimization guidance, hindering effective exploration of the optimization space. We propose EGG, an Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation, which incorporates expert optimization principles to guide LLMs' decisions. Inspired by expert workflows, we decompose kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: 1) algorithmic structure design, which establishes a high-quality computational structure foundation; 2) hardware-specific tuning, which performs targeted adjustments through parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. This staged decomposition defines explicit optimization objectives, structuring the design space to achieve progressive refinement. To this end, a stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism is designed for inter and intra-stage context management, ensuring stable optimization trajectories. Experiments on KernelBench and real-world workloads show that EGG achieves a 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.

</details>


### [139] [ResilPhase: Plug-and-Play Phase Mapping and Noise-Resilient Macro-Trajectory Extrapolation for Diffusion Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26769)
*Qicheng Zhao,Yu Li,Qi Sun,Zheyu Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ResilPhase is a derivative-free, phase-aware extrapolation framework for fast diffusion-model inference that reframes acceleration in ODE space. It uses a barycentric Lagrange extrapolator and a bounded Phase Mapping to bypass derivative instability, achieving high fidelity under aggressive acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce inference latency of diffusion models (DiTs) where prior cache-then-forecast methods rely on unstable discrete extrapolations of misaligned representations, causing quality degradation and error amplification.

Method: Reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ordinary differential equation (ODE) space, aligning forecasting with the Global Drift (end-to-end state evolution) rather than intermediate features. Introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator and a bounded Phase Mapping to regularize the extrapolation domain, forming the ResilPhase framework.

Result: Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo show state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration ratios.

Conclusion: ResilPhase provides a noise-resilient acceleration pipeline by mitigating derivative fallacy and high-order instability, enabling faster diffusion-based generation with minimal quality loss and robust performance across evaluated benchmarks.

Abstract: The adoption of powerful diffusion models is hindered by their significant inference latency. Recent ``cache-then-forecast'' schemes alleviate this issue by accelerating DiTs using derivative-based polynomials, but they suffer from severe quality degradation at high acceleration ratios. Our analysis reveals its root cause: the discrete extrapolation performed on representations that are misaligned with the continuous diffusion trajectory and are numerically unstable. Thus, accelerated DiTs suffer from accumulated spatial errors, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability. We therefore reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ordinary differential equation (ODE) space. Instead of predicting intermediate features, we align forecasting with the model's Global Drift (GD), i.e., the end-to-end state evolution, thereby eliminating feature inconsistency and memory overhead. However, even this smooth macro-trajectory remains vulnerable to the derivative fallacy: its higher-order temporal derivatives are intrinsically noisy. Thus, we introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to effectively bypass derivative instability and approximation error. We further propose a bounded Phase Mapping that regularizes the extrapolation domain, suppressing oscillatory error growth. These elements collectively constitute ResilPhase, a noise-resilient acceleration framework. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo demonstrate state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration ratios.

</details>


### [140] [Memory Depth, Not Memory Access: Selective Parametric Consolidation for Long-Running Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26806)
*Haoliang Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Defines memory depth for long-running LLMs as durable, goal-conditioned tendencies stored in a small parametric store; introduces loop-drift protocol to test persistence under unloading; evaluates EVAF (surprise- and valence-gated LoRA) across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, finding retrieval excels at short factual recall while memory-depth consolidation improves goal persistence; reveals two-factor control (selection and actuation) and model-dependent inner-loop dynamics; suggests memory depth complements retrieval and exposes boundary issues via public Memora streams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation that retrieval alone cannot preserve long-horizon, goal-directed behavior once working context is unloaded. Proposes a parametric memory layer to encode durable tendencies, enabling persistent behavior across long interruption cycles.

Method: Introduce loop-drift protocol: a stress test where retrieval index remains intact while working context is unloaded, enforcing persistence of goal-conditioned behavior under long-loop interference. Develop EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Evaluate on GPT-2, TinyLlama, and Mistral-7B with metrics such as short-fact accuracy (0.956–0.973) and goal persistence/post-unload recovery (0.812–0.904). Use 2–3 parametric writes per 200 events. Analyze via controlled experiments: selection vs actuation, random gates for selection isolation, fixed-inner controls for inner-loop write strength, matched-gate inversion for coupling asymmetry. Use Public Memora streams as external diagnostic for memory invalidation boundaries.

Result: Retrieval dominates shallow factual recall; EVAF-driven consolidation dominates memory depth and persistence after unloading, achieving strong goal persistence and post-unload recovery with minimal writes. The mechanism splits into two controllable dimensions—selection and actuation—where selective consolidation can be isolated, and inner-loop write strength is model-dependent. Unexpected asymmetric coupling emerges under miscalibrated actuation. Public Memora streams reveal stale-memory invalidation as a boundary. Memory depth is shown to be complementary to retrieval and robust across several models.

Conclusion: Selective parametric consolidation provides memory depth that complements retrieval access, enabling durable, goal-conditioned behavior in long-running LLMs. The loop-drift protocol is a useful diagnostic for memory persistence, and EVAF demonstrates how targeted, gated LoRA consolidation can encode durable tendencies with few writes.

Abstract: Long-running language agents need more than memory access. Retrieval systems can fetch past facts at query time, but they do not decide which experiences should continue to shape behavior after the working context is unloaded. We study this separate problem as memory depth: durable goal-conditioned tendencies written into a small parametric store. We introduce the loop-drift protocol, a controlled stress test in which the retrieval index remains intact while working context is unloaded and goal-conditioned behavior must persist under long-loop interference. We evaluate EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, retrieval is strongest on shallow factual recall (short-fact accuracy 0.956--0.973), while EVAF is strongest on goal persistence and post-unload recovery (0.812--0.904) with only 2--3 parametric writes per 200 events. Mechanism controls show that selective consolidation factorizes into two controllable dimensions: selection and actuation. Matched random gates isolate selection beyond sparse writing; fixed-inner controls across GPT-2, TinyLlama, and Mistral-7B show that inner-loop write strength is model-dependent; and a Mistral-7B matched-gate inversion reveals asymmetric selection-actuation coupling under miscalibrated actuation. Public Memora event streams serve as an external diagnostic, exposing stale-memory invalidation as an unresolved boundary. Within this probe, selective parametric consolidation supplies memory depth distinct from and complementary to retrieval access.

</details>


### [141] [KARLA: Knowledge-base Augmented Retrieval for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26807)
*Francois Crespin,Fabian M. Suchanek,Nils Holzenberger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A retrieval-augmented generation method where an LLM emits special tokens to query a knowledge base during generation, enabling up-to-date, traceable facts, parameter-free updates via KB edits, and comparable factual accuracy for smaller models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often generate wrong or outdated factual content and updating them with retraining is expensive. A system that can ground outputs in an external KB would improve accuracy, transparency, and adaptability while reducing model size requirements.

Method: The model is trained to produce special tokens that trigger queries to an external knowledge base during token generation. This setup allows factual content to be retrieved and inserted from the KB in real time, with traceability linking outputs to KB entries. The approach resembles retrieval-augmented generation but focuses on emitting explicit KB-query tokens to control grounding.

Result: Experiments show improved factual grounding for both short and long-form generation. Facts can be revised via knowledge base edits rather than parameter updates, and smaller models can achieve the same factual accuracy as larger models.

Conclusion: The proposed KB-triggered generation mechanism provides up-to-date, transparent, and adjustable factual content without retraining, and can reduce the need for large parameter counts to achieve factual reliability. It also enables post-hoc fact revisions through KB edits, highlighting practical benefits for deployment.

Abstract: We propose a new method that allows an LLM to automatically pull in factual knowledge from a knowledge base during token generation. This means that (1)~factual knowledge in the LLM output can be updated without retraining the LLM, (2)~facts in the LLM output can be traced to the knowledge base for transparency and explainability, and (3)~smaller models can achieve the same factual accuracy as larger models. Our core idea is to train the model to produce special tokens that trigger a query to the knowledge base. Our experiments show that our method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form generation, and allows factual revisions to take effect through KB edits rather than parameter updates.

</details>


### [142] [Computational Analysis of Heart Rate Variability in Healthy Adults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26816)
*María J. Lado,Arturo J. Méndez,Leandro Rodriguez-Liñares,Baltasar García Pérez-Schofield,Pedro Cuesta-Morales,Brais Iglesias-Otero,Xose A. Vila*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HRV analysis in 40 healthy adults evaluates normality, stability, correlations, reproducibility, and cross-study consistency; recommends a minimal, non-redundant set of HRV indices for clinical/research use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of gold standard for HRV parameters in healthy individuals; need to clarify distribution, stability, redundancy, and cross-dataset comparability to improve clinical utility.

Method: Cross-sectional study of 40 healthy adults (20M/20F, age 30-50). Computed time-domain, frequency-domain, and nonlinear HRV indices using computational signal processing. Assessed normality, stability, correlations, reproducibility, and consistency; compared with Fantasia database; evaluated inter-study variability and redundancy.

Result: Time-domain and nonlinear indices tend to be normally distributed; gender differences observed. Most indices are stable; HF-related indices less stable. HF-related indices show high correlations (redundant). Fantasia comparison shows <10% error for most indices; SD2 and SDNN in women exceed 15% error. Time-domain/nonlinear indices show low inter-study variability; frequency-domain indices show high variability limiting cross-study comparisons. Proposed representative indices: ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF).

Conclusion: The study provides a practical set of HRV indices that balance normality, stability, and non-redundancy, improving clinical/research relevance. Highlights need for standardization and careful index selection when comparing across studies in healthy populations.

Abstract: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis is a key indicator of cardiac physiological state and aids in disease diagnosis. However, research on HRV parameters in healthy individuals remains limited, and no gold standard exists. This study evaluates HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50) to improve HRV's clinical utility. Using computational methods for signal processing and data analysis, time, frequency, and nonlinear indices were analyzed to address five questions: (1) normality, (2) stability, (3) correlation, (4) reproducibility, and (5) consistency. Key findings: (1) Time-domain and nonlinear indices, particularly global and LF (low frequency), follow normal distributions, with gender differences noted. (2) Most indices are stable except HF (high frequency)-related ones. (3) High correlations in HF-related indices suggest redundancy, indicating only one is necessary in studies. (4) Comparisons with the Fantasia database revealed less than 10% error for most indices, except SD2 and SDNN in women (greater than 15%). (5) Time-domain and nonlinear indices show low inter-study variability, while frequency-domain indices exhibit high variability, limiting cross-study comparisons. The selected indices-ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF)-are best suited for accurately representing HRV components and enhancing its clinical and research relevance.

</details>


### [143] [The Capability Frontier: Benchmarks Miss 82% of Model Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26836)
*Bradley Fowler,Ryan Smith,Daniel Thi Graviet,William Myers,Joshua Greaves,Narmeen Fatimah Oozeer,Antía García,Philip Quirke,Amirali Abdullah,Fazl Barez,Shriyash Kaustubh Upadhyay*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Capability Frontier, a Pareto frontier across models and generations to quantify best achievable performance at each cost, correcting biases from single-model/single-run benchmarks; extensive empirical and simulation results across 21 LLMs and 16 benchmarks show substantial underestimation of distributed capabilities and strong gains from oracle-based selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarking LLMs under heterogeneous data distributions leads to underestimation of capabilities due to model specialization and sampling across generations. A principled framework is needed to characterize performance under cost constraints.

Method: Define a frontier over models and generations (oracle routing) to maximize accuracy at a given cost. Correct for biases from single-model evaluation and from noisy maxima. Empirically evaluate 21 LLMs across 16 benchmarks spanning coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, and agentic tasks; compare frontier performance at matched cost to benchmark top models. Complement with probabilistic simulations varying query topic entropy.

Result: Correcting for single-model evaluation yields a 54% reduction in error rate; correcting for single runs yields an 82% improvement; state-of-the-art accuracy is matched at 85% cost reduction. Oracle routing performance deteriorates less with higher topic entropy, indicating larger gaps between single models and the frontier as topic diversity increases.

Conclusion: Collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated when using single-model, single-run benchmarks. The Capability Frontier provides a more accurate and actionable framework for evaluation and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks typically report accuracy for a single model on a single run. This systematically understates real-world LLM capabilities, particularly under heterogeneous data distributions: (i) different models get different questions correct according to their specializations, and (ii) given a budget, multiple generations can be sampled and selectively retained. To quantify this gap, we introduce the Capability Frontier: a Pareto frontier over a set of models that characterizes the best achievable performance at each cost level under optimal selection across models and generations (i.e., via an oracle). Our construction corrects for two opposing biases: underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from taking maxima over noisy samples. We study 21 LLMs across 16 widely used benchmarks spanning coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, and agentic tasks, comparing Capability Frontier performance at matched cost to each benchmark's top-performing model. Correcting for single-model evaluation yields a 54% error rate reduction; additionally correcting for single runs yields an 82% improvement, with SOTA accuracy matched at 85% cost reduction. Complementing these empirical results, we use controlled probabilistic simulations to show that higher query topic entropy produces a near-monotonic increase in the performance gap between oracle routing and the best single model. Our findings suggest collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated, with implications for evaluation and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

</details>


### [144] [Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines for Warehouse Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26852)
*Janik Bischoff,Anne Meyer,Uta Mohring,Fabian Dunke,Maximilian Barlang,Özge Nur Subas,Hadi Kutabi,Stefan Nickel,Kai Furmans*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A context-aware framework (CASOP) to automatically synthesize and evaluate order-fulfillment pipelines from modular algorithms, demonstrated by generating over a million pipelines across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decomposed approaches are common due to organizational boundaries and data limits, but there is no general mechanism to select, compose, and evaluate algorithm pipelines for warehouse order fulfillment.

Method: Introduce CASOP with: (1) a modular algorithm repository; (2) semantic data and algorithm cards; (3) a taxonomy of subproblems; (4) a pipeline synthesizer to assemble valid pipelines; (5) a pipeline evaluator to compare them; evaluated on 7 benchmark sets across four problem classes to yield 1,063,044 valid pipelines; open-source implementation.

Result: Generated 1,063,044 valid optimization pipelines across benchmark instances, enabling automatic synthesis and evaluation of context-specific pipelines; demonstrates scalability and practical utility.

Conclusion: CASOP provides a general, extensible platform to design, automatically synthesize, and select high-performing optimization pipelines for warehouse order fulfillment, with open-source resources for researchers and practitioners.

Abstract: Order fulfillment in manual picker-to-goods warehouses involves interconnected decisions such as item assignment, order batching, and picker routing. While integrated models capture interactions between these decisions, practical warehouse systems often require decomposed approaches due to organizational boundaries, differing responsibilities, or limited data availability. Existing studies primarily evaluate algorithms for isolated subproblems or fixed subproblem combinations for specific warehouse settings, but lack a general mechanism to determine applicable algorithm configurations, compose them into valid solution pipelines, and assess their performance.
  With Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines (CASOP), we propose a framework for constructing and evaluating context-specific optimization pipelines and apply these to order fulfillment. The framework comprises: (1) a modular repository of algorithms for common order fulfillment problems; (2) semantic data and algorithm cards to describe warehouse context and algorithm requirements; (3) a taxonomy that structures order fulfillment problems into relevant subproblems; (4) a pipeline synthesizer that identifies applicable algorithms for a given warehouse context and composes all valid optimization pipelines; and (5) a pipeline evaluator that assesses all resulting pipelines. We demonstrate the framework on 7 benchmark instance sets covering four problem classes, resulting in 1,063,044 valid pipelines. The framework supports researchers and practitioners in designing, automatically synthesizing, and selecting valid, high-performing algorithmic pipelines for warehouse operations. The software is open-source and available at https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_pipes and https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_algos.
  Keywords: Warehouse optimization, Algorithm selection, Pipeline synthesis, Order fulfillment

</details>


### [145] [LCAi: Life Cycle Assessment with big data fusion and retrieval-augmented generation-assisted interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26857)
*Georgios Tsironis,Juan D. Medrano-Garcia,Gonzalo Guillen-Gosalbez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for LCA interpretation with multi-perspective retrieval and constrained synthesis; demonstrated on a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple facility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LCA interpretation often lacks structured mechanisms to translate quantified improvement opportunities into actionable strategic pathways under technological, social, and policy uncertainty; AI-assisted interpretation risks hallucination and may miss cross-domain diversity without controlled synthesis.

Method: Develop a perspective fusion RAG architecture spanning academic, industry, public discourse, and EU funding datasets. Three-step workflow: (1) scenario anchor defining system boundaries and decarbonization targets; (2) perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval; (3) neutral synthesis integrating only ledger-stored outputs without further retrieval.

Result: Proof-of-concept demonstration showing structured retrieval and constrained synthesis mitigate hallucination while preserving cross-domain diversity; enables implementation-oriented decision-making and broader AI tools use in LCA studies, especially for technologies scalable in practice.

Conclusion: The framework enables disciplined translation of impact results into strategic pathways and extends AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation for LCA—opening avenues for AI-enabled interpretation in scalable technologies; further validation and broader case studies are anticipated.

Abstract: The interpretation phase of life cycle assessment often lacks structured mechanisms for translating quantified improvement opportunities addressing environmental hotspots into actionable strategic pathways under technological, social, and policy uncertainty. To overcome this limitation, this study introduces a perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation framework for LCA interpretation, where a multi-perspective retrieval and controlled synthesis is incorporated in the artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted LCA. To operationalise large language models in LCA interpretation, a perspective fusion RAG architecture was developed, covering academic, industry, public discourse, and European union (EU) funding datasets. Our approach comprises three steps: (1) a scenario anchor defining system boundaries and decarbonization targets, (2) a set of perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval, and (3) a neutral synthesis step integrating only ledger-stored outputs without further retrieval. The framework is demonstrated through a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple production facility using GPT-5 nano as the reasoning model. Overall, the structured retrieval and constrained synthesis are designed to mitigate the risk of hallucination while preserving cross-domain diversity. The approach presented can support more disciplined translation of impact results into strategic pathways and opens up new avenues for the use of advanced AI tools in LCA studies, particularly those focused on technologies that could be deployed at scale. This proof-of-concept demonstrates how AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation can support implementation-oriented decision-making beyond conventional LCA studies.

</details>


### [146] [AgentX: Towards Agent-Driven Self-Iteration of Industrial Recommender Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26859)
*Changxin Lao,Fei Pan,Guozhuang Ma,Han Li,Huihuang Lin,Jijun Shi,Kangzhi Zhao,Kun Gai,Mo Zhou,Qinqin Zhou,Quan Chen,Ruochen Yang,Shifu Bie,Shuang Yang,Shuo Yang,Wenhao Li,Wentao Xie,Xiao Lv,Xuming Wang,Yijun Wang,Yiming Chen,Yusheng Huang,Zhongyuan Wang,Zibo Zhao,Zijie Zhuang,Baoning Xia,Chao Liu,Chaoyi Ma,Chubo He,Dawei Cong,Feng Jiang,Gang Wang,Guilin Xia,Hanwen Xu,Jiahong Xie,Jiahui Qiao,Jian Liang,Jiangfan Yue,Jing Wang,Jinghan Yang,Jinghui Jia,Kan Qin,Lei Wang,Ming Li,Peilin Song,Pengbo Xu,Qiang Luo,Ruiming Tang,Shiyang Liu,Shuxian Jin,Tao Wang,Tao Zhang,Xiang Gao,Xianghan Li,Yingsong Luo,Yiwen Ning,Yongcheng Liu,Yuan Guo,Zhaojie Liu,Zhenkai Cui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentX introduces a self-evolving production engine for recommender systems, enabling autonomous hypothesis generation, implementation, evaluation, and learning to accelerate iteration beyond human-driven workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current recommendation iteration is bottlenecked by the need for human engineers to generate hypotheses, modify production code, run experiments, and attribute results, causing innovation to scale with headcount rather than evidence and compute.

Method: Four-stage closed loop: Brainstorm Agent synthesizes evidence into executable proposals; Developing Agent translates proposals into production-ready code with repository-grounded generation and reliability checks; Evaluation Agent performs safe online rollout with guardrail-vetoed A/B judgments and converts outcomes into knowledge; Harness Evolution layer (SGPO) distills execution trajectories into semantic-gradient updates to self-improve the agents.

Result: A production-deployed, self-improving system that operates at scale and pace beyond manual workflows, enabling autonomous experimentation and knowledge-driven optimization.

Conclusion: AgentX reframes production optimization as an autonomous, self-improving research loop, transforming recommendation systems into an industrialized, evidence-driven process.

Abstract: Recommendation algorithm iteration is moving from an artisanal, engineer-bound process toward an industrialized research loop, but this transition remains blocked by a structural execution bottleneck: the idea-to-launch cycle still depends on human engineers to generate hypotheses, modify production code, launch A/B experiments, and attribute online results. Innovation therefore scales linearly with headcount rather than compounding with evidence, compute, and accumulated experimental knowledge. We present AgentX, a production-deployed multi-agent system that fundamentally restructures this production function. AgentX operates as a self-evolving development engine: it autonomously generates, implements, evaluates, and learns from recommendation experiments at a scale and pace that no manual workflow can sustain.
  The system orchestrates four tightly coupled stages in a closed loop. A Brainstorm Agent synthesizes evidence from historical experiments, system architecture, data analysis, and external research into ranked, executable proposals. A Developing Agent translates each proposal into production-ready code through repository-grounded generation and multi-dimensional reliability verification. An Evaluation Agent conducts safe online rollout with guardrail-vetoed A/B judgment, converting both successes and failures into structured knowledge assets. A Harness Evolution layer (SGPO) then distills execution trajectories into semantic-gradient updates that continuously sharpen the agents themselves -- making the system not merely automated, but self-improving.

</details>


### [147] [TAVR-VLM: Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding for Hallucination-Resistant Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26874)
*Zhixiang Lu,Xiwei Liu,Sifan Song,Changkai Ji,Anh Nguyen,Jionglong Su,Imran Razzak,Jinfeng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TAVR-VLM introduces Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding Attention (R-CGA) to reduce diagnostic hallucinations in multimodal LLMs for TAVR planning, achieving state-of-the-art on M^3TAVR with improved AUROC, CIDEr, and lower hallucination rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes TAVR planning requires precise, grounded multimodal reasoning. Multimodal LLMs often hallucinate by lacking reliable grounding between visual data and clinical text, necessitating a robust grounding mechanism.

Method: Propose R-CGA framework with a causal 'Risk → Region → Word' grounding pathway. Compress multimodal inputs into a global risk mask via a risk bottleneck; apply a support-projected causal consistency objective during autoregressive generation to constrain token-level grounding within the risk-defined mask; evaluate on M^3TAVR (1,482 patients).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance: AUROC 0.896; CIDEr 0.936; hallucination rate reduced to 8.1%. Demonstrates improved interpretability and evidence-based surgical AI.

Conclusion: Risk-conditioned causal grounding effectively mitigates diagnostic hallucinations in multimodal medical AI, enhancing reliability and interpretability in TAVR planning; suggests a generalizable strategy for grounding in high-stakes domains.

Abstract: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR) planning requires meticulous multimodal reasoning. However, adapting Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to this high-stakes domain is severely impeded by diagnostic hallucinations, where generated text lacks anatomical grounding. To address this, TAVR-VLM is introduced: a novel framework featuring Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding Attention (R-CGA) that instantiates a model-internal ``Risk $\rightarrow$ Region $\rightarrow$ Word'' structural grounding pathway. R-CGA compresses multimodal inputs into a causal risk bottleneck, purifying dense visual features into a global risk mask. During autoregressive generation, a support-projected causal consistency objective constrains token-level grounding within the risk-defined support mask. Evaluated on $\text{M}^3\text{TAVR}$, a comprehensive 1,482-patient cohort, TAVR-VLM establishes a new state-of-the-art. It achieves an AUROC of 0.896, boosts CIDEr to 0.936, and drastically reduces the hallucination rate to 8.1\%, thereby improving interpretability for evidence-based surgical AI.

</details>


### [148] [A Pipeline for Generating Longitudinal Synthetic Clinical Notes Using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26879)
*William Poulett*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A modular synthetic clinical notes pipeline generates a 70-patient, 20–50-note dataset across a hospital journey to enable AI development while preserving patient privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide realistic, longitudinal clinical notes for AI development and evaluation in healthcare without exposing real patient data, addressing privacy and data access barriers.

Method: A modular pipeline combines structured patient generation, semi-structured patient journey simulation, and LLM-based generation of unstructured notes; includes validation/augmentation steps to improve faithfulness, realism, and diversity; outputs 70 synthetic patients with 20–50 notes each, across multiple validation levels.

Result: Release of a synthetic dataset suitable for tasks like summarization, coding, and decision support, enabling development and testing without real patient data; configurable realism/scalability via validation levels.

Conclusion: Synthetic, privacy-preserving clinical notes can support a range of AI applications in healthcare, with an emphasis on cross-Note longitudinal consistency and diversity through LLM-assisted validation.

Abstract: Synthetic data is increasingly used to enable the development and evaluation of AI systems in domains where access to real-world data is restricted. In healthcare, clinical documentation presents particular challenges due to its sensitivity. This work introduces a synthetic clinical notes pipeline and dataset designed to support the development of clinical AI tools while avoiding the privacy risks associated with real patient data. The dataset is generated using a modular pipeline that combines structured patient generation, semi-structured patient journey simulation, and unstructured clinical note generation using large language models. The pipeline is designed to prioritise internal consistency across longitudinal patient records, while also capturing variation in writing style, note structure, and clinical detail. Additional mechanisms, including LLM-based validation and augmentation steps, are used to improve faithfulness, realism, and diversity of the generated notes. We release a dataset of 70 synthetic patients, each associated with 20-50 clinical notes spanning a full hospital journey. The dataset is provided at multiple levels of validation, enabling users to balance realism and scalability depending on their use case. This dataset supports the development, testing, and evaluation of clinical AI systems, including summarisation tools, coding models, and decision support systems, without reliance on real patient data.

</details>


### [149] [Generative Retrieval via Diffusion Transformer with Metric-Ordered Sequence Training and Hybrid-Policy Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26899)
*Chenghao Liu,Yu Zhang,Zhongtao Jiang,Kun Xu,Zhenwei An,Renzhi Wang,Zhao Wang,Jiachen Zhang,Yuxiao Zhang,Kun Xu,Songfang Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A staged, continuous generative retrieval approach (MO-DiT+HPPO) for pattern-preserving attribute retrieval that combines metric-ordered pretraining, tail-centroid fine-tuning, and online-objective aligned optimization to achieve Pareto improvements in intersection metrics across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embedding-based retrieval biased toward high-similarity items often drifts away from a seed-induced pattern; there is a need to retrieve items that satisfy a target attribute while staying within the fine-grained pattern expressed by a seed set.

Method: MO-DiT+HPPO: a staged framework consisting of raw-sequence pretraining, multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining, tail-centroid fine-tuning, and HPPO. Metric-ordered training converts sparse online retrieval labels into ordered in-pattern trajectories by predicted attribute density, teaching the model the metric-improvement direction across domains. Pareto pair filter retains only winner pairs that preserve the same pattern while improving the attribute metric. HPPO labels a hybrid candidate pool with the online intersection metric and applies reference-anchored preference optimization to align the generated query distribution with the online objective. Extensive ablations and validations (metric-predictor validation, order ablations, CPT/SFT comparisons, candidate-policy ablation) identify the sources of gains.

Result: In four attribute domains with item- and pattern-holdout setups, metric-ordered DiT outperforms a pretrained generative retriever on the intersection metric; HPPO provides additional gains, achieving significant improvements on seven of eight domain-split cells and a marginal tie on the hardest split.

Conclusion: The combination yields Pareto improvements by increasing the target attribute density without sacrificing pattern purity, with gains attributable to metric-ordered supervision, policy optimization, and careful candidate selection; results generalize across multiple attribute domains and holdout protocols.

Abstract: Embedding-based retrieval ranks items by their similarity to a query in a shared vector space and usually aims to return the highest-scoring items. In many production settings this is not what is wanted: given a seed set that expresses a fine-grained pattern, one needs more items that both satisfy a target attribute and stay within that pattern. We formalize this as pattern-preserving attribute retrieval. The two goals pull against each other: averaging the seeds preserves the pattern but stays in a low-attribute region, while global attribute retrieval drifts to unrelated patterns. We approach the task with continuous generative retrieval, where a model reads a sequence of item embeddings and generates query embeddings for nearest-neighbor search. We propose MO-DiT+HPPO, a staged framework with raw-sequence pretraining, multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining, tail-centroid fine-tuning, and HPPO. Metric-ordered training turns sparse online retrieval labels into in-pattern trajectories ordered from low to high predicted attribute density, teaching one model the metric-improvement direction across domains. HPPO aligns the generated query distribution with the true online objective by labeling a hybrid candidate pool with the online intersection metric and applying reference-anchored preference optimization. A Pareto pair filter keeps only winner pairs that do not lower same-pattern purity, raising the attribute metric without sacrificing the pattern. Across four attribute domains under item- and pattern-holdout protocols, metric-ordered DiT improves the intersection metric over a pretrained generative retriever, and HPPO improves it further, with significant gains on seven of eight domain-split cells and a marginal tie on the hardest split. Metric-predictor validation, order ablations, CPT/SFT comparisons, and a candidate-policy ablation show where the gains come from.

</details>


### [150] [Learning to Recover Task Experts from a Multi-Task Merged Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26902)
*Jinwook Jung,Taegyu Kim,Kumju Jo,Sungyong Baik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReTeX predicts additive parameter offsets to undo interference in merged multi-task experts, enabling near-expert performance from a single merged checkpoint with a router-free task identifier based on offline SVD subspace signatures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static multi-task model merging causes parameter interference across task-specific experts; dynamic merging trades off performance for storage/loading costs. A storage-efficient method is needed to recover per-task performance without task routing at inference.

Method: Treat inter-expert interference as affine perturbations to each expert during merging and approximate these perturbations as additive offsets. Predict these offsets to recover expert performance from a single merged checkpoint. Use a router-free task identifier based on offline SVD subspace signatures; at inference, select the task whose subspace yields the smallest projection residual for a given input, then apply predicted offsets to recover the corresponding expert behavior.

Result: ReTeX recovers over 95% of the individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP, and enhances generalization to unseen tasks. The offset prediction also enables emergent adaptive interpolation of expert knowledge for OOD tasks.

Conclusion: ReTeX provides a storage-efficient framework to recover task-specific performance from a single merged checkpoint, with a simple router-free identification mechanism and demonstrated gains across vision and NLP domains; code is available online.

Abstract: Multi-task model merging aims to consolidate several task-specific experts into a unified model, yet static merging consistently suffers from parameter interference. While dynamic merging models aim to bridge this gap, many works rely on the costly storage and loading of redundant expert components at inference. In this work, from the perspective of task expert, we view parameter interference as parameter perturbation introduced to each expert during merging process. We show that such parameter perturbations can be modeled as affine transformation, which can be approximated as additive offsets. Motivated by these, we propose Recover Task eXpert (ReTeX), a framework that predicts those offsets, in order to undo parameter interference and recover task-expert performance from a single merged checkpoint. To recover the appropriate expert when task identity is unknown, we introduce a router-free task identifier based on SVD subspace signatures computed offline before inference. At inference, the identifier selects the task whose subspace yields the smallest projection residual for a given input. As a result, ReTeX recovers over 95% of individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP domains, while significantly improving generalization to unseen tasks. Crucially, we also show that the parameter offset prediction leads to emergent adaptive interpolation of expert knowledge for out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks. ReTeX adaptively interpolates seen expert knowledge to handle unseen tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/BAIKLAB/ReTeX

</details>


### [151] [Diagnosing Task Insensitivity in Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26918)
*Jingyu Liu,Xiaopeng Wu,Kehan Chen,Chuan Yu,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Identifies task insensitivity as a key source of out-of-distribution failures in long-horizon LLM agents. Shows that models can ignore task description when instructions are corrupted, and that similar tasks can trigger the same actions. Proposes Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a contrastive regularizer encouraging actions to depend on the task instruction. Empirically, this improves task sensitivity and OOD generalization while preserving stable attention to task tokens.
