<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve robustness of long-horizon LLM agents by strengthening task-conditioned behavior and reducing shortcut optimization that ignores task instructions, enabling better OOD generalization.

Method: Diagnose through analysis of attention drift and task-token dependence; develop Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization (TPNLL), a lightweight contrastive regularizer that penalizes insensitivity to task perturbations and reinforces task-driven actions; integrate into the training/optimization pipeline and assess via diverse benchmarks.

Result: TPNLL enhances task sensitivity and OOD generalization compared to baselines, while maintaining more stable attention to task tokens.

Conclusion: Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization is an effective, lightweight regularizer to align LLM action with task instructions, improving robustness to task perturbations and out-of-distribution generalization; future work could explore broader perturbations and scalability across domains.

Abstract: Large language models can serve as capable long-horizon agents, but their out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains weak. We identify a key source of this failure as task insensitivity: when faced with similar but distinct tasks, models might apply patterns learned during training and fail to solve the task at hand. We show that models often continue with actions aligned with the original task even when the instruction is semantically corrupted and cannot be directly answered. We further find that, when we replace the task description in a trained prompt with another similar but distinct task, the model may still output the same action. This behavior is accompanied by a consistent training-time attention drift away from task tokens and toward local observations, suggesting an optimization bias toward shortcuts. To mitigate this problem, we propose Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a lightweight contrastive regularizer that explicitly encourages action dependence on the task instruction. Extensive evaluations show that our intervention improves task sensitivity and OOD generalization while preserving more stable attention to task tokens.

</details>


### [152] [Where Do CoT Training Gains Land in LLM based Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26935)
*Jingyu Liu,Zhiwen Wang,Yuxin Jing,Huanyu Zhou,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prompt actions improve with training; CoT does not widen its advantage and mainly aids prompt-action quality; masking action-token supervision improves out-of-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle whether CoT helps by changing the model's actions via reasoning or by improving direct action prediction, and to assess generalization and fidelity of CoT.

Method: Compare action quality when predicting actions with and without CoT across checkpoints; analyze revision behavior; selectively mask action-token supervision on a fraction of training examples to test generalization.

Result: Prompts-only action quality improves substantially across checkpoints; CoT actions retain similar relative advantage; later checkpoints revise actions less in response to CoT, implying heavier reliance on prompts; masking action-token supervision improves out-of-domain generalization.

Conclusion: CoT training primarily benefits the quality of prompt actions and generalization; it does not inherently amplify the benefit of CoT reasoning; selective masking is a viable regularization to improve out-of-domain performance.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is widely used in language-model agents, but prior work has shown that verbalized CoT is not always faithful and may instead reflect post-hoc reasoning, which means the model already knows the answer before reasoning. We therefore ask what CoT training is actually improving: is the model getting better at changing its action through generated reasoning, or is it getting better at predicting the action directly from the prompt? We study this question by comparing \emph{prompt actions} (predicting action without CoT) with CoT actions (predicting action with CoT). Across checkpoints, prompt-action quality improves substantially. While interacting with the environment, the relative advantage of CoT actions over prompt actions remains similar, showing that CoT training does not widen the advantage of CoT reasoning, and it helps to improve the quality of prompt actions. We further find that later checkpoints are less likely to revise the action in response to CoT, suggesting greater reliance on the prompt. Motivated by these patterns, we selectively mask action-token supervision on a fraction of training examples. This intervention improves out-of-domain generalization.

</details>


### [153] [Look-Before-Move: Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention in Dynamic 3D Story Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26964)
*Jiaming Bian,Bingliang Li,Yuehao Wu,Pichao Wang,Zhi Wang,Hailan Ma,Huadong Mo,Zhenhong Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Look-Before-Move for narrative-guided camera planning in dynamic 3D story worlds, with Semantic Observation Contract, Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search, and Semantic Trajectory Grounding; introduces a Story World Benchmark; demonstrates improved perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As embodied AI and world models operate in dynamic 3D environments, there is a need to actively decide what to observe rather than passively interpret observations; narrative intent should guide visual attention under 3D constraints.

Method: Propose Look-Before-Move pipeline: Semantic Observation Contract to translate director's intent into executable visual constraints; Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search to select narrative-compliant and geometrically feasible viewpoints; Semantic Trajectory Grounding to connect viewpoints into collision-aware, temporally coherent motion.

Result: Empirical experiments show improved subject perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over baselines; the framework improves visual attention organization prior to motion.

Conclusion: Organizing visual attention before camera motion is beneficial for aligning perception with narrative intent in dynamic 3D story worlds; the proposed framework and benchmark support this direction.

Abstract: As embodied AI and world models increasingly operate in dynamic 3D environments, visual perception must move beyond passively interpreting given observations toward actively deciding what to observe. We study this problem through camera planning in dynamic 3D story worlds, where the camera must not only generate smooth motion, but also decide what visual evidence should be acquired before it moves. We formulate this capability as Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention, where the camera acts as an embodied observer that determines what to observe, how to compose the observation, and how to shift attention over time under narrative intent and physical 3D constraints. To realize this capability, we propose Look-Before-Move, a camera planning framework that separates observation specification from motion execution. It first builds a Semantic Observation Contract to convert directorial intent into executable visual constraints, then performs Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search to find narrative-compliant and geometrically feasible viewpoints, and finally applies Semantic Trajectory Grounding to connect selected viewpoints into continuous, collision-aware, and temporally coherent camera motion. We further construct a dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark based on StoryBlender, covering 50 stories, 457 scenes, and 1585 shots with animated characters, semantic scene configurations, and executable 3D environments. Experiments show that our framework improves subject perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over representative baselines, demonstrating the importance of organizing visual attention before generating camera motion.

</details>


### [154] [Einstein World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26969)
*Munachiso Samuel Nwadike,Zangir Iklassov,Ali Mekky,Zayd M. Kawakibi Zuhri,Kentaro Inui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Einstein World Models (EWMs): an LLM-based reasoning framework that interleaves visual-temporal rollouts from a separate world-module with textual reasoning, treating rollouts as inspectable hypotheses to enhance reasoning beyond language alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of language-only reasoning and to explore whether visual counterfactuals can augment complex thought in LLMs by making visual thought experiments inspectable within the reasoning trace.

Method: Introduce an LLM calling a dedicated world-module that generates short visual rollouts of candidate scenes. The rollouts are not final answers but hypotheses to inform subsequent reasoning, embedded within the LLM's trace. This extends tool-using paradigms to include visual thought experiments.

Result: Conceptual blueprint; outlines architecture and workflow with visually grounded rollouts, but the abstract does not report empirical results or experiments.

Conclusion: EWMs extend LLM reasoning capabilities by incorporating visual thought experiments via a world-module, enabling more expressive reasoning and suggesting a direction for future development and evaluation.

Abstract: Does intelligence require the ability to reason about phenomena beyond direct experience? It is natural to suspect that some complex thought cannot be captured through language alone. However, of particular concern to this work, is whether visualising counterfactual events can complement language as a mechanism for complex thought. We ask whether LLMs can be trained to utilise such visualisation mechanisms, in a way that benefits their reasoning abilities. Motivated by this question, we propose Einstein World Models. EWMs are a blueprint for LLM-based reasoning systems that place visual-temporal rollouts inside the reasoning trace, allowing them to reason in ways that text alone may not support well. In an EWM, the LLM calls a world-module (not to be confused with a world model), to produce short rollouts of scenes under consideration. The returned rollout is treated not as the answer, but as an inspectable hypothesis that can support later reasoning. Einstein World Models extend the capability of LLMs for tool calling (such as web search or code execution), into the domain of visual thought experiments.

</details>


### [155] [Adaptive Utility driven Resource Orchestration for Resilient AI (AURORA-AI)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27005)
*Rahul Umesh Mhapsekar,Ilias Cherkaoui,Lizy Abraham,Indrakshi Dey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AURORA-AI is a principled, adaptive framework that reallocates compute among heterogeneous AI models to maximize a fairness-aware, multi-objective utility under non-stationary conditions, using HJB control, Lyapunov stability, and closed-loop optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static resource allocation under non-stationary conditions degrades predictive performance and human-centric properties; need a unified, theory-backed approach to maintain high utility and fairness while controlling latency and cost.

Method: Integrates Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control with Lyapunov stability monitoring and a fairness-aware composite utility in a closed-loop policy. Dynamically redistributes computational budget across diverse models in a stress-rich, discrete-time simulation with demographic bias shocks, drift, and black-swan disruptions. Evaluated against Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, and PPO baselines.

Result: AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from black-swan disruptions (vs 88 steps for Static; 22 steps for PPO). Elevates alpha-quantile and super-quantile by 29% and 25%. Reduces mean and max demographic parity gap and increases the fraction of Lyapunov-stable steps.

Conclusion: Fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory offers a practical, theoretically motivated path toward resilient, human-centric AI deployment.

Abstract: Modern AI systems are increasingly deployed under non-stationary computational, demographic, and operational conditions in which static resource allocation strategies degrade both predictive performance and human-centric properties such as fairness and explainability. This paper presents AURORA-AI, an Adaptive Utility-driven Resource Orchestration framework for Resilient AI that unifies Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control, Lyapunov-based stability monitoring, and a fairness-aware composite utility into a single closed-loop policy.The framework continuously redistributes computational budget across a population of heterogeneous AI models so that the global utility, defined jointly over predictive performance, demographic parity, cost, latency, robustness, and interpretability, remains maximised under disruption. The framework is evaluated in a stress-rich discrete-time simulation that concurrently injects demographic bias shocks, gradual concept drift, and abrupt black-swan disruptions, and is compared against five established controllers including Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, and a deep reinforcement-learning agent based on Proximal Policy Optimisation. AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from the black-swan event compared to eighty-eight time steps for the Static baseline and twenty-two for Proximal Policy Optimisation, lifts the alpha-quantile and the super-quantile by twenty-nine and twenty-five percent respectively, simultaneously reduces the mean and maximum demographic parity gap, and increases the fraction of Lyapunov-stable operating steps. These results indicate that fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory is a practical and theoretically motivated path toward resilient human-centric AI deployment.

</details>


### [156] [Semantic Early-Stopping for Iterative LLM Agent Loops](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27009)
*Sahil Shrivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Semantic early-stopping halts multi-agent LLM loops when draft embeddings stabilize and quality stops improving, replacing fixed iteration caps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiency and token waste in fixed-cap multi-agent LLM loops by introducing a stopping criterion that reflects meaning stability and answer quality.

Method: Define cosine-distance between consecutive draft embeddings with a patience window; monitor both embedding convergence and quality improvement; provide deterministic termination and a machine-checkable foundation, while treating convergence as an empirically tested conjecture rather than a Banach contraction. Propose a judge-efficient evaluation protocol: replay each question on identical drafts, cache LLM-judge calls, separate operational tokens (policy cost) from evaluation tokens (measurement cost). Introduce an oracle concept to select the best round.

Result: Proves deterministic termination and well-definedness; introduces an efficient evaluation protocol that yields paired efficiency-quality comparisons; on HotpotQA (60-question test split) a judge-free semantic stopper reduces operational tokens by 38% at parity quality (Delta-IS = -0.004, p = 0.81); a full quality-gated variant is costlier due to judging; an oracle selecting the best round achieves +0.115 Information Score (p ~ 4e-11).

Conclusion: Semantic early-stopping improves efficiency without sacrificing quality and reframes stopping as selecting the best round rather than a fixed time bound; practical implications include substantial token savings and a principled evaluation framework, with the oracle result highlighting the value of round-level selection.

Abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) loops, for example a Writer that drafts and a Critic that revises, are almost always terminated by a fixed iteration cap (max_iterations). This is a syntactic kill-switch: it is blind to whether the answer is still improving, so it over-spends tokens on easy inputs and truncates hard ones. We study semantic early-stopping: the loop halts when consecutive draft embeddings stop changing in meaning (cosine distance with a patience window) and the answer's measured quality stops improving. Our work makes three contributions. First, an honest theoretical footing: we prove deterministic termination and well-definedness and machine-check these claims, while treating the convergence of the distance sequence as an empirically tested conjecture rather than a (previously over-claimed) Banach contraction. Second, a judge-efficient evaluation protocol: we generate each question's full trajectory once, replay every stopping policy over the identical drafts, and cache every LLM-judge call, yielding a strictly paired efficiency-versus-quality comparison at low cost; we further separate operational tokens (charged to a policy) from evaluation tokens (a measurement instrument). Third, an empirical study on multi-hop retrieval-augmented question answering (HotpotQA). On the 60-question test split, a judge-free semantic stopper reduces operational tokens by 38% relative to max_iterations at parity quality (Delta-IS = -0.004, p = 0.81), whereas the full quality-gated variant is counter-productive because its per-round judging dominates cost. An oracle that selects the best round attains +0.115 Information Score over every practical policy (p ~ 4e-11), reframing the problem from "when to stop" (easy) to "which round is best" (open).

</details>


### [157] [How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27061)
*Pasi Fränti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Review of external validity indices for clustering, focusing on set-matching measures. Recommends centroid index (CI) as an intuitive, explainable cluster-level measure; for finer, point-level evaluation, PSI offers a normalized score not biased by cluster sizes; if all points should count equally, ACC or other set-matching measures are suitable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess external validity indices when ground truth is available, comparing measures with respect to interpretability, bias, and granularity, to provide practical recommendations for clustering evaluation.

Method: Literature review of common external validity indices, emphasizing set-matching-based measures; analyze properties such as normalization and sensitivity to cluster sizes; compare cluster-level (CI) versus point-level (PSI, ACC) scoring; synthesize recommendations.

Result: CI is recommended for intuitive, explainable cluster-level evaluation. For finer, point-level evaluation, PSI provides a normalized score not biased by cluster sizes; ACC is appropriate when every data point should be treated equally; other set-matching measures are available for different preferences.

Conclusion: Use CI for interpretable cluster-level assessment; opt for PSI or ACC based on desired granularity and weighting of data points; there are additional set-matching options to consider depending on specific evaluation goals.

Abstract: External indexes can be used for cluster evaluation when ground truth is available. We review the most common external validity indexes focusing on set-matching-based measures. We recommend centroid index (CI), because it is an intuitive cluster-level measure with an explainable result. If we need a more fine-tuned, point-level measure, there are more choices. Pair-set index (PSI) provides a normalized score which is not biased by cluster sizes. If all points should matter equally, then clustering accuracy (ACC) or any other set-matching measure is suitable.

</details>


### [158] [Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for Large Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27136)
*Shicheng Ye,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: JERP jointly learns long-term experiential rules and policy from the same interaction trajectories, retrieving rules at decision time to guide actions and updating both rules and policy after each episode, yielding improved performance on AlfWorld and WebShop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-step LLM agents, accumulated interaction experience must be leveraged. Traditional approaches separate usage into either outside-the-model rules (interpretable but potentially stale) or policy updates (broader improvements but limited local correction in sparse rewards). This coupling aims to keep rules aligned with the evolving policy while absorbing stable behaviors into the model.

Method: Maintain a long-term experiential-rule pool. At decision time, retrieve task-relevant rules and condition the agent on them together with its interaction history. After each episode, use collected trajectories to (1) optimize the policy and (2) revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories, thus aligning rules with policy evolution.

Result: Empirical evaluation on AlfWorld and WebShop shows consistent decision-performance gains for complex interactive tasks, indicating the coupling improves both rule relevance and policy effectiveness.

Conclusion: Jointly updating experiential rules and policies from the same data helps keep rules synchronized with policy changes while steadily embedding successful, stable behaviors into the model, improving performance on complex multi-step tasks.

Abstract: For LLM agents in multi-step interactive environments, a key challenge is to make effective use of accumulated interaction experience. Existing work has typically separated two uses of such experience: keeping it outside the model as natural-language rules for later prompting, or using trajectories and feedback to update the model parameters. The former is easy to interpret but can fall out of sync with the evolving policy; the latter improves the policy more broadly but provides only limited correction for local mistakes in sparse-reward settings. We present Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for LLM Agents (JERP), which updates a long-term experiential-rule pool and the policy from the same interaction trajectories. At decision time, JERP retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the agent on them together with the interaction history. After each episode, it uses the collected trajectories both to optimize the policy and to revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories. This coupling keeps the rule pool aligned with the evolving policy while allowing stable and effective behaviors to be gradually absorbed into the model itself. Experiments on AlfWorld and WebShop show that JERP yields consistent gains in decision performance for complex interactive tasks.

</details>


### [159] [OpenRCA 2.0: From Outcome Labels to Causal Process Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27154)
*Aoyang Fang,Yifan Yang,Jin'ao Shang,Qisheng Lu,Junjielung Xu,Rui Wang,Songhan Zhang,Yuzhong Zhang,Boxi Yu,Pinjia He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAVE enables step-wise causal labeling for LLM RCA by forward verification from root causes to symptoms, enabling OpenRCA 2.0 benchmark; across 11 LLMs, exact root-cause recovery is only 20.7%, with 76.0% identifying a correct root-cause and 61.5% grounding it to a verified propagation path; outcome-only eval misses this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RCA datasets label only root causes, ignoring propagation paths; this simplifies evaluation and masks LLM deficiencies in causal reasoning and tool use. A forward, path-grounded protocol is needed for trustworthy RCA agents.

Method: Introduce PAVE protocol leveraging fault-injection interventions to reconstruct causal propagation paths via forward reasoning (cause-to-effect). Build OpenRCA 2.0 with 500 instances, cross-system evaluation across 11 frontier LLMs, and step-wise causal annotations.

Result: Exact root-cause set recovered in 20.7% of cases across models. Ungrounded diagnosis (identifying at least one correct root-cause service) achieved 76.0%, but only 61.5% were grounded in a verified propagation path to the symptom. Indicates outcome-only evaluation undervalues true RCA capability.

Conclusion: Step-wise causal ground truth is essential for trustworthy LLM-based RCA; the current evaluation paradigm (outcome-only) hides significant failure modes. PAVE/OpenRCA 2.0 provide a more rigorous benchmark for RCA in LLM agents.

Abstract: Root cause analysis (RCA) poses a holistic test of LLM agentic capabilities, such as long-context understanding, multi-step reasoning, and tool use. However, existing datasets suffer from a fundamental gap: they label only the root cause, not the propagation path connecting it to the observed symptom, which largely simplifies the task to naive pattern matching. To support rigorous evaluation, we introduce PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that leverages known interventions from fault injection to reconstruct causal propagation paths. The mechanism is forward verification: reasoning from cause to effect rather than inferring backward from symptoms. Applying PAVE yields OpenRCA 2.0 (500 instances), the first cross-system RCA benchmark with step-wise causal annotations for LLM agents. Across 11 frontier LLMs, recovering the exact root-cause set succeeds in only 20.7% of cases on average. To locate where this difficulty lies, we relax the criterion and find what we call the ungrounded diagnosis: agents identify at least one correct root-cause service in 76.0% of cases, but ground that service in a verified causal propagation path to the observed symptom in only 61.5%. Outcome-only evaluation hides this failure mode; step-wise causal ground truth is the missing piece for trustworthy LLM-based RCA agents.

</details>


### [160] [TOPS: First-Principles Visual Token Pruning via Constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets for Efficient MLLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27161)
*Tinghao Wang,Yichen Guo,Rui Huang,Zheng Lu,Qizhe Zhang,Chenxi Li,Yuan Zhang,Jiajun Cao,Zhirong Shen,Yaosong Du,Guangyan Gan,Wenya Wang,Lin William Cong,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TOPS introduces a principled, training-free visual token pruning method for multimodal LLMs, formulating pruning as Token Optimal Preservation Sets guided by information theory. It uses Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity to select tokens, achieving strong pruning with little to no performance loss across multiple backbones and benchmarks, and even reducing hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational overhead in multimodal LLMs caused by excessive visual tokens. Existing pruning methods lack a principled objective and often retain redundant tokens or ignore user instructions. A theory-guided, model-agnostic, training-free approach is needed.

Method: Formulate token pruning as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets. Conduct a top-down information-theoretic analysis to derive three fundamental principles: Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity. Propose TOPS, a training-free and model-agnostic pruning module applicable to various MLLMs.

Result: TOPS outperforms prior pruning methods across 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks. On LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS prunes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% and 100.6% performance on 7B and 13B models, respectively, suggesting pruning can reduce hallucination and inspire lighter MLLM designs.

Conclusion: Pruning redundant visual tokens via a principled, information-theoretic framework yields effective, model-agnostic pruning (TOPS) that maintains or improves performance while reducing computation and potentially mitigating hallucinations.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but their efficiency is limited by the large number of visual tokens, which introduces substantial computational overhead. Visual token pruning offers a natural solution, yet existing methods are imperfect: attention-based criteria tend to retain redundant tokens, while diversity-based criteria are often agnostic to user instructions. Even methods that combine multiple criteria still lack a principled formulation of the intrinsic objective of token pruning. In this paper, we revisit visual token pruning from a first-principles perspective and formulate it as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets. Through a top-down information-theoretic analysis, we identify three fundamental principles for effective token selection: Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity. Based on these principles, we propose TOPS, a training-free and model-agnostic pruning module that can be applied to various MLLMs. Extensive experiments on 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks demonstrate that TOPS outperforms prior methods under diverse pruning settings. Notably, on LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% and 100.6% performance on its 7B and 13B models, respectively, suggesting that pruning redundant visual tokens can sometimes mitigate hallucination and inspire future lightweight MLLM design.

</details>


### [161] [A Process Harness for Uplifting Legacy Workflows to Agentic BPM: Design and Realization in CUGA FLO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27188)
*Fabiana Fournier,Lior Limonad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a policy-governed agentic layer (process harness) atop deterministic BPM to add reasoning, adaptation, and oversight without replacing the engine; introduces the TDF model and CUGA FLO implementation; validated on a loan-approval workflow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uplift legacy, deterministic workflows with agentic LLM capabilities while preserving structural control and compliance, enabling adaptive decision-making and oversight through policy-driven agents.

Method: Introduce the Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model with a data schema and execution semantics, comprising TaskAgent, DecisionAgent, and FlowAgent operating under a unified FRAME policy. Implement CUGA FLO as a realization of TDF and demonstrate on a loan-approval workflow with a hook-driven regulatory override.

Result: Demonstrates how the process harness reconciles imperative, deterministic execution with normative, policy-governed agentic autonomy. Validates all three agent types and hook-driven overrides in a loan-approval scenario.

Conclusion: The process harness offers a rigorous framework to integrate agentic autonomy within existing BPM, balancing structural compliance with policy-guided adaptability and oversight.

Abstract: We introduce the process harness, a new mechanism for uplifting legacy workflows into Agentic Business Process Management (Agentic BPM) without replacing the underlying workflow engine. A process harness places a policy-governed agentic layer around a deterministic workflow engine, intercepting designated control points to contribute reasoning, adaptation, and oversight while the engine retains structural authority over the process. To define the process harness rigorously, we develop the Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model, specifying both its data schema and its execution semantics. TDF decomposes LLM reasoning across three policy-governed agent types: a TaskAgent for knowledge-intensive task execution, a DecisionAgent for per-case gateway routing, and a FlowAgent that governs runtime flow adaptation through a principled hook mechanism. Each agent reasons within an explicit policy drawn from the process FRAME, the aggregate policy set governing all LLM calls in the system. We then present CUGA FLO as the design and implementation realization of the TDF model, and demonstrate it on a loan approval workflow that exercises all three agent types and hook-driven regulatory override. The process harness uniquely reconciles imperative requirements, realized through deterministic workflow execution that enforces structural compliance, with normative requirements, realized through policy-framed agentic autonomy invoked at designated control points wherever the process demands it.

</details>


### [162] [Vulnerability of Natural Language Classifiers to Evolutionary Generated Adversarial Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27215)
*Manjinder Singh,Alexander E. I. Brownlee,Mohamed Elawady*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GAversary is a black-box NLP adversarial attack using a hybrid genetic algorithm with GloVe-based mutations to craft semantically similar word substitutions, achieving dramatically higher attack efficacy than BAE/A2T but perturbing more words and increasing runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NLP models are vulnerable to adversarial inputs; existing methods either rely on white-box access or yield limited semantic similarity. A black-box, semantically aware GA could strike a balance between effectiveness and accessibility.

Method: Hybrid genetic algorithm that treats the model as a black box (logits only). Mutation operator uses GloVe embeddings to propose semantically similar word replacements. Fitness is guided by the model's output logits to drive search toward misclassification while maintaining semantic closeness.

Result: On benchmark datasets and standard targets, GAversary substantially lowers accuracy (e.g., from 76.8% to 5.8%), outperforming BAE and A2T. However, it perturbs nearly twice as many words, yields slightly lower semantic similarity, and incurs ~5% higher run-time.

Conclusion: GAversary demonstrates the effectiveness of semantically informed, black-box genetic attacks in NLP, highlighting a strong vulnerability and the need for robust defenses; future work may optimize perturbation efficiency and runtime without sacrificing attack success.

Abstract: Deep learning models have achieved impressive performance across various fields but remain vulnerable to adversarial inputs, particularly in NLP, where such attacks can have significant real-world consequences. Adversarial attacks often involve small, semantically similar token replacements to fool NLP models, and recent methods have become more precise by targeting specific vulnerable words, often by exploiting some level of access to the model's internal structure. This paper proposes GAversary, a hybrid Genetic Algorithm (GA) to generate adversarial attacks on natural language models. The GA is able to treat the target model as a black box, requiring only the logit value output by the model to guide the search. GAversary differs from GAs previously proposed for this problem by using GloVe embeddings to propose word replacements (the mutation operator) to improve the semantic similarity of the adversarial examples. GAversary is applied to several benchmark data sets and well-known target models. GAversary is able to substantially reduce the target model's accuracy on test data compared to the BAE and A2T attacks compared against (in the best case, reducing a 76.8% accuracy to 5.8%, compared to BAE's 27.6%). The trade-off is that GAversary perturbs just under twice as many words as the other two methods, with a slightly lower semantic similarity to the original text and around a 5% increase in run-time.

</details>


### [163] [Ask, Don't Judge: Binary Questions for Interpretable LLM Evaluation and Self-Improvement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27226)
*Sangwoo Cho,Kushal Chawla,Pengshan Cai,Zefang Liu,Chenyang Zhu,Shi-Xiong Zhang,Sambit Sahu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BINEVAL decomposes evaluation into atomic binary questions answered by LLMs, producing transparent, multi-dimensional scores; it matches or exceeds baselines across several benchmarks, offers strong factual consistency performance, and provides interpretable feedback to guide prompt improvement; it is training-free and task-agnostic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating LLM outputs is bottlenecked by expensive human evaluation, poor correlation of lexical metrics with human judgment, and opaque holistic LLM judgments. A interpretable, diagnostic, and training-free framework is needed.

Method: A meta-prompt generates fine-grained, atomic evaluation questions. An LLM answers each question independently for every output, yielding question-level feedback. The per-question verdicts are aggregated into multi-dimensional scores; the framework is tested across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS. The approach supports iterative prompt optimization and can adapt to self-update and cross-model update settings.

Result: BINEVAL achieves competitive or superior correlation to human judgments compared with UniEval and G-Eval, with particular strength on factual consistency benchmarks (QAGS). It better matches human score distributions and avoids ceiling effects seen in prior LLM judges, enabling improved discrimination between borderline and clearly flawed outputs. It also provides actionable, question-level feedback to improve prompts, both for summarization and generation tasks, under self-update and cross-model update scenarios.

Conclusion: BINEVAL is a task-agnostic, training-free, and interpretable evaluation framework that delivers strong empirical performance while offering practical diagnostic and optimization value for LLM evaluation.

Abstract: Evaluating LLM outputs remains a major bottleneck in NLP: human evaluation is expensive and slow, lexical metrics correlate poorly with human judgments on open-ended generation, and holistic LLM judges often produce opaque scores that are hard to debug. We propose BINEVAL, a framework that decomposes evaluation criteria into atomic binary questions and aggregates the resulting verdicts into interpretable, multi-dimensional scores. Given a task prompt, a meta-prompt generates fine-grained evaluation questions, and an LLM answers them independently for each output, yielding transparent question-level feedback together with calibrated overall scores. This decomposition makes evaluation easier to inspect, easier to diagnose, and directly usable for prompt improvement. Across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines including UniEval and G-Eval, with especially strong results on factual consistency benchmarks such as QAGS. Beyond competitive correlation with human judgments, BINEVAL better matches human score distributions and avoids the ceiling effects common in prior LLM judges, leading to better discrimination between borderline and clearly flawed outputs. We further show that the same question-level feedback supports iterative prompt optimization, improving evaluator prompts on summarization and generation prompts on IFBench under both self-update and cross-model update settings. Overall, BINEVAL provides a task-agnostic, training-free, and interpretable evaluation framework that combines strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic and optimization value.

</details>


### [164] [EO-WM: A Physically Informed World Model for Probabilistic Earth Observation Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27277)
*Junwei Luo,Shuai Yuan,Zhenya Yang,Yansheng Li,Zhe Liu,Hengshuang Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EO-WM: a video diffusion transformer for multispectral Earth observation forecasting that uses a physically informed conditioning framework to model weather-driven dynamics, separating weather baseline and anomalies and accumulating anomalous forcing over time. Introduces two diagnostic benchmarks (Extreme Summer and Seasonal Matched-Pair) to evaluate weather-response beyond reconstruction accuracy. Shows modest improvements in NDVI decline amplitude and directional hit rate, while staying competitive on standard metrics; opensource release planned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting Earth's surface dynamics from satellite data under changing meteorological conditions is partially observed and weather-driven. Existing deterministic models collapse uncertainty and diffusion models treat weather too generically. There is a need to explicitly model distinct weather conditioning and its accumulation over time, and to evaluate forecast responsiveness to weather forcing beyond standard pixel-level accuracy.

Method: EO-WM is a video diffusion transformer for multispectral EO forecasting. It introduces a physically informed conditioning framework with three weather-related conditioning signals: a climatological baseline, weather anomalies, and cumulative physical stress signals. Baseline and anomaly are processed via separate conditioning pathways, and anomalous forcing is accumulated over time to capture sustained heat/drought stress. Two new diagnostic benchmarks assess weather-response behavior: Extreme Summer Benchmark and Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark.

Result: Experiments show EO-WM reduces NDVI decline amplitude error by 5.63% relative and improves directional hit rate by 7.80% relative, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics.

Conclusion: EO-WM effectively models weather-driven dynamics in EO forecasting, improving response fidelity to weather forcing without sacrificing standard reconstruction metrics. The proposed benchmarks enable evaluation of weather-responsiveness, and code will be released open-source.

Abstract: Earth Observation (EO) forecasting aims to predict future Earth surface dynamics from satellite observations under changing meteorological conditions. In this paper, we view this task as a partially observed, weather-driven world modeling problem, in which weather acts as a conditioning signal, while forecasting remains uncertain due to sparse observations and unobserved land-surface states. However, existing methods do not fully capture this setting: deterministic models collapse uncertainty into a single future prediction, while diffusion-based methods typically treat weather variables as undifferentiated conditioning signals, and existing benchmarks focus mainly on reconstruction accuracy rather than whether forecasts respond correctly to changed weather forcing.We introduce EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer for multispectral EO forecasting. EO-WM incorporates a physically informed conditioning framework that represents meteorological forcing through a climatological baseline, weather anomalies, and cumulative physical stress signals. Specifically, it separates baseline and anomaly through distinct conditioning pathways, and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to capture sustained heat and drought stress. To evaluate weather-response behavior beyond standard metrics, we introduce two diagnostic benchmarks: an Extreme Summer Benchmark for severity-aware prediction of vegetation degradation under extreme weather, and a Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark for testing response fidelity under changed weather forcing. Experiments show that EO-WM reduces the error in predicted Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) decline amplitude by a relative 5.63% and improves directional hit rate by a relative 7.80%, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics. The benchmarks and model will be made open-source at https://github.com/Luo-Z13/EO-WM.

</details>


### [165] [Simulation-based inference for rapid Bayesian parameter estimation in epidemiological models: a comparison with MCMC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27286)
*Alina Bazarova,Johann Fredrik Jadebeck,Henrik Zunker,Carolina J. Klett-Tammen,Torben Heinsohn,Wolfgang Wiechert,Katharina Noeh,Stefan Kesselheim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SBI via neural posterior estimation can calibrate a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 ICU data with accuracy comparable to MCMC, but orders of magnitude faster, enabling near-real-time inference across short and long inference windows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MCMC-based calibration is computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear mechanistic models, hindering rapid outbreak analysis; SBI offers a scalable alternative.

Method: Train neural posterior estimators to calibrate the SECIR model to Germany's COVID-19 ICU occupancy data. Evaluate on 31-day windows and a 201-day reconstruction with multiple transmission change points. Compare results to MCMC using Wasserstein distances, KL divergences, and posterior predictive checks. Report runtimes on CPU-only for MCMC and CPU+GPU for SBI.

Result: SBI yields posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC for 31-day windows; in the 201-day setting SBI preserves the dominant posterior structure with higher uncertainty. SBI is substantially faster: ~60-70s on a single GPU (31-day) vs ~1000s on CPU (MCMC); ~157s (201-day) SBI vs >19,000s MCMC.

Conclusion: SBI provides a rapid, scalable Bayesian calibration framework for mechanistic epidemiological models, suitable for repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis.

Abstract: Mechanistic epidemiological models are widely used to support infectious disease forecasting and public-health decision making. Bayesian calibration of such models is commonly performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can become computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. Here, we investigate simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation as a scalable alternative for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 intensive care unit (ICU) occupancy data from Germany during 2020. We compared SBI and MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using both 31-day inference windows and a substantially more challenging 201-day reconstruction problem involving multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated quantitatively using Wasserstein distances and Kullback-Leibler divergences together with posterior predictive checks. Across the 31-day windows, SBI recovered posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC while accurately reproducing observed ICU trajectories. In the 201-day setting, SBI preserved the dominant posterior structure despite increased uncertainty. SBI, by combining CPU and GPU resources, substantially reduced computational runtime compared with MCMC, which was restricted to running on CPUs. Whereas MCMC required approximately 1000 seconds for the 31-day inference problems, SBI achieved comparable posterior and predictive performance in approximately 60-70 seconds on a single GPU. For the 201-day inference problem, SBI required an average of 157 seconds, while the MCMC runs took over 19,000 seconds. Our results demonstrate that SBI provides a rapid and computationally efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, supporting repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis.

</details>


### [166] [Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27287)
*Preet Baxi,Jiannan Xu,Jane Yi Jiang,Stefanus Jasin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prompt injection in LLM-based résumé screening can subtly boost some applicants' rankings, but its impact depends on how many candidates adopt it and how similar their qualifications are; rare injection with similar quality yields higher rankings for manipulators, but widespread manipulation erodes effectiveness; with heterogeneous quality, manipulation is less effective on average and can let lower-quality candidates outrank stronger ones, raising fairness concerns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess risks of strategic manipulation in LLM-driven hiring and quantify conditions under which prompt injection affects ranking and fairness.

Method: Controlled experiments using simulated résumés; manipulate self-promotional text without adding new qualifications; vary candidate quality (homogeneous vs heterogeneous) and prevalence of injection; measure ranking outcomes.

Result: In homogeneous quality with few injectors, prompt injection reliably improves rankings; effectiveness declines as injection prevalence grows and collapses when manipulation is widespread. In heterogeneous quality, average effectiveness is reduced, but injections can sometimes let lower-quality candidates outrank higher-quality ones, creating fairness issues. Overall vulnerability is highest when manipulation is rare and quality differences are small.

Conclusion: LLM-based screening is susceptible to strategic prompt injection under specific conditions; requires defenses and fairness safeguards; publicly available code/resources provided for replication.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to screen and rank job applicants, creating incentives for candidates to strategically manipulate algorithmic hiring systems. We study prompt injection in automated résumé screening, defined as subtle self-promotional text that introduces no new qualifications but is designed to influence LLM evaluations. Using controlled experiments, we show that prompt injection reliably improves applicant rankings when résumé quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject. However, its effectiveness rapidly diminishes as more candidates inject, collapsing when manipulation becomes widespread. When candidate quality is heterogeneous, prompt injection is less effective on average, but can occasionally allow lower-quality candidates to outrank higher-quality ones, raising fairness concerns. Overall, LLM-based screening is most vulnerable when manipulation is rare and candidate quality differences are small. Code and resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/preetb1199/Prompt_Injection_ACL26

</details>


### [167] [When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27288)
*Josef Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ensemble gains in multi-model LLM systems are limited by the probability that all models fail on a query (beta). Traditional rho-based diagnostics misestimate gains; finite-sample Clopper-Pearson bound bounds max possible improvement. Empirically across 67 models, beta is larger than under Gaussian copula approximations, tail behavior depends on task/formulation, and improvements come from complementary failures rather than simply adding models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify theoretical limits of ensemble improvements and provide finite-sample bound; empirically examine tail risk across diverse models and tasks.

Method: Theoretical: derive upper bound on accuracy for single-model outputs as 1 - beta; show rho cannot identify beta; derive Clopper-Pearson bound on beta. Empirical: calibrate 67 models from 21 providers using tetrachoric and Gaussian copula models; measure all-wrong rate beta for open-ended mathematics and execution-graded code; compare question formats (multiple-choice vs free-response); assess ensemble gains under routing signals and checkable tasks.

Result: Beta values observed: open-ended math beta ≈ 0.052 whereas Gaussian copula underpricing gives ≈0.023 (about 2.5× underpricing; 90% CI 1.7–3.4; k = 17). Code task beta ≈ 0.079. Free-response increases tail to beta ≈ 0.127 with a five-judge panel achieving kappa ≈ 0.73–0.92, indicating co-failure linked to answer format rather than subject. At matched quality, low-rho heterogeneous ensembles can beat high-rho Self-MoA, but on checkable tasks the ensemble rarely beats the single best model without a strong query-level routing signal. Gains come from complementary failures, not simply adding models.

Conclusion: Tail risk and co-failure dominate ensemble gains; effective routing signals are essential to surpass the best single model. At practical task formats, focus on reducing co-occurrence of errors and implementing targeted routing rather than naive augmentation with more models.

Abstract: Multi-model LLM systems such as routing, voting, cascades, fusion, and mixture-of-agents are used to beat single-model accuracy. We show that their gain is capped by a quantity the field rarely reports. For any policy whose output is one member model answer, accuracy cannot exceed one minus beta, where beta is the rate at which every model is wrong on the same query. In contrast, the usual diagnostic, average pairwise error correlation rho, cannot identify beta: error laws with identical marginals and pairwise correlations can have different all-wrong rates. A Clopper-Pearson bound on beta gives a finite-sample certificate on the largest gain any router, vote, or cascade could deliver before training a router.
  Across 67 models from 21 providers, a tetrachoric-calibrated single-factor model still underprices the all-wrong tail: on open-ended mathematics, observed beta is 0.052 versus 0.023 under the full 67-model Gaussian copula, about 2.5 times underpricing, with 90 percent CI 1.7 to 3.4 and k equals 17. The effect recurs on execution-graded code, where beta is 0.079. Re-asking the same GPQA-Diamond questions in free-response rather than multiple-choice form reopens the tail, with beta 0.127 and a five-judge panel with kappa 0.73 to 0.92, locating co-failure in answer format rather than subject. At matched quality, low-rho heterogeneous ensembles beat high-rho Self-MoA, but on checkable tasks in our pool, combining models rarely beats the single best model without a strong query-level routing signal. Gains come from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models.

</details>


### [168] [Language-Based Digital Twins for Elderly Cognitive Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27334)
*Mohammad Mehdi Hosseini,Mohammad H. Mahoor,Hiroko H. Dodge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A language-based digital twin uses LLMs to mimic elderly conversational behavior; a multi-head cVAE evaluates fidelity and cognitive scores (MoCA) on I-CONECT, achieving real-data–level reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors, and outperforming GPT baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable non-invasive, scalable, personalized cognitive health monitoring by simulating individual conversational patterns and estimating cognitive status.

Method: Develop a language-based digital twin incorporating stylometric cues and contextual metadata; train a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) to jointly optimize reconstruction quality and MoCA score prediction; evaluate on the I-CONECT dataset comparing with real data and GPT baselines.

Result: The digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics; reconstruction quality and MoCA prediction errors are comparable to real data; outperforms baseline GPT-generated responses.

Conclusion: Language-based digital twins are a promising non-invasive approach for continuous cognitive health monitoring, with potential clinical utility and scalability; further work needed on generalization, privacy, and integration with clinical workflows.

Abstract: Digital twins have emerged as a promising paradigm for personalized healthcare, enabling modeling of individual behavior and health trajectories. In cognitive health, early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) remains challenging, where language and conversational patterns serve as non-invasive biomarkers. In this work, we propose a language-based digital twin framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to mimic the conversational behavior of elderly individuals by incorporating stylometric cues and contextual metadata. To evaluate fidelity and cognitive consistency, we introduce a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) that jointly measures reconstruction quality and predicts cognitive scores. Experiments on the I-CONECT dataset show that the digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics and achieves reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors comparable to real data, while outperforming baseline GPT-generated responses. These results highlight the potential of language-based digital twins as a scalable and non-invasive approach for personalized and continuous cognitive health monitoring.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [169] [A unified cell-merge algorithm for generating diverse Voronoi diagrams and new tessellations based on spatial chromatic model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27235)
*Weining Zhu*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: A new cell-merge (CM) Voronoi generation algorithm using a spatial chromatic model enables rapid generation of multiple Voronoi diagrams by retrieving and merging cells from a unified database, without bespoke VD algorithms; it can also yield new tessellations (e.g., competition intensity, couple-cell diagrams).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There are many VD variants (ordinary, spherical, linear-entity, weighted, higher-order, etc.) because of differences in space, entities, and distance metrics. A unified, reusable generation approach is desirable to quickly produce diverse VDs for computation and analysis, especially when multiple VD types are required.

Method: Introduce a cell-merge Voronoi generation algorithm based on a spatial chromatic model. It retrieves cells from a unified database and merges them to form different VD configurations, avoiding the need for dedicated VD algorithms for each type.

Result: The CM Voronoi algorithm enables rapid generation of diverse VDs by cell retrieval and merging from a single database, and it can generate new tessellations such as competition intensity and couple-cell diagrams beyond classical VDs.

Conclusion: The proposed CM framework offers a flexible, reusable approach to generating a variety of Voronoi diagrams and related tessellations, suitable for applications like location-based spatial analysis, with potential simplifications in implementation and extension to novel diagram types.

Abstract: As a type of spatial tessellation model and an important spatial structure of computational geometry, Voronoi diagrams (VDs) are widely used in many fields. Due to differences in generation spaces, types of spatial entities, distance metrics, and relationships between entities and Voronoi regions, Voronoi diagrams vary into many types, such as the ordinary VD, VD on spheres, VD for linear entities, weighted VD, and ordered higher-order VD. These VDs also have their own generation algorithms. In this study, we propose a new cell-merge (CM) Voronoi generation algorithm based on the spatial chromatic model. The advantage of the CM algorithm is that it can quickly generate diverse VDs by retrieving and merging cells from a unified database, without requiring the development of specific algorithms for each VD. The CM Voronoi algorithm can be particularly applied in cases where a variety of Voronoi diagrams are frequently required for computation and analysis, such as in location-based spatial analysis. Furthermore, the proposed CM method can also generate some new types of spatial tessellations, such as competition intensity and couple-cell diagrams which are different from classical VDs.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [170] [Unsupervised Memory-Enhanced Video Transformers: Obstacle Detection for Autonomous Agricultural Rover](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26151)
*Théo Biardeau,Anne-Sophie Capelle-Laizé,Salwan Alwan,David Helbert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Video Memory Transformers for Anomaly Detection (VMTAD) is a fully unsupervised, real-time anomaly detection method for dynamic agricultural scenes, using a memory-augmented transformer to leverage temporal context. It achieves state-of-the-art AUC on rapeseed rover datasets and includes a lightweight variant with 14 ms inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To close safety gaps for autonomous agricultural rovers by addressing obstacles undetected by LiDAR and the limitations of supervised vision under unseen objects; traditional unsupervised anomaly methods struggle with dynamic rover motion, requiring a real-time, label-free solution.

Method: A transformer-based architecture augmented with a dedicated memory module that processes encoded representations of preceding frames to exploit temporal context, enabling robust anomaly detection in dynamic rover motion. Trained using only normal (non-anomalous) data.

Result: Evaluated on the Grillion agricultural rover with a rapeseed dataset, achieving 0.973 detection AUC and 0.997 segmentation AUC (state-of-the-art). A lightweight variant delivers real-time inference at 14 ms, aligning with stopping-distance safety requirements.

Conclusion: VMTAD provides effective unsupervised anomaly detection in dynamic agricultural environments with real-time performance, addressing both perception gaps and safety constraints for rover deployment.

Abstract: While autonomous rovers have become indispensable to precision farming, achieving consistent operational safety remains a critical challenge. Conventional safety sensors, such as LiDAR, fail to detect obstacles positioned below the plant canopy, posing a significant risk. While camera-based supervised learning methods can detect common objects, they perform poorly when faced with obstacles that were not present in their training data. Actual unsupervised anomaly detection offers a solution by learning the normal visual patterns of an environment, but often fails for the dynamic scenes captured by a moving rover.\\ This paper introduces Video Memory Transformers for Anomaly Detection (VMTAD), a fully unsupervised method designed for real-time obstacle detection in dynamic agricultural scenes. VMTAD utilizes a transformer-driven architecture augmented with a dedicated memory module. This memory module leverages temporal context by processing encoded representations of preceding frames. This approach enables the system to effectively address the dynamic context caused by the robot's movement. The model is trained using only images that represent normal operation, requiring no data labels.\\ VMTAD was rigorously evaluated on the 'Grillion' agricultural rover. On a challenging rapeseed dataset, VMTAD achieved state-of-the-art performance, reaching a 0.973 detection and 0.997 segmentation Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. A lightweight variant provides an optimal balance of high accuracy and real-time inference (14 ms), which is critical for safety, as confirmed by our analysis of the rover's total stopping distance.

</details>


### [171] [Reinforcement Learning Enables Autonomous Microrobot Navigation and Intervention in Simulated Blood Capillaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26154)
*Jannik Drotleff,Samuel Tovey,Paul Hohenberger,Christoph Lohrmann,Julian Hoßbach,Konstantin Nikolaou,Christian Holm*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Deep RL agents trained in a realistic capillary-network simulator learn robust navigation strategies for autonomous microrobots, reveal fundamental limits, and demonstrate counterintuitive flow-blocking interventions that restore throughput without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap between idealized RL studies and biologically realistic environments; enable autonomous microrobotic control for targeted interventions in vascular networks.

Method: Develop a physically grounded capillary network simulator with hydrodynamic flow, explicit red blood cell dynamics, and anatomically derived branching; train deep RL agents to navigate via chemotaxis; map navigation performance across robot size and swimming speed; analyze policy types.

Result: Identify a forbidden regime where Brownian motion and flow dominate propulsion; agents converge on universal strategies (e.g., run-and-rotate, energy-efficient search-and-sit); enable targeted blocking/unblocking of capillary flow restoring throughput to baseline without retraining.

Conclusion: Shows RL is a viable framework for autonomous microrobotic interventions in complex biological environments; strategies generalize across parameters and can inform design of microrobots and control policies.

Abstract: Autonomous microrobots navigating biological vasculature could enable targeted drug delivery and thrombolysis, yet training control policies for realistic environments remains an open challenge. Prior reinforcement learning (RL) studies of microrobotic navigation have been limited to idealized geometries that omit complex hydrodynamic flow fields, confined branching structures, and dense cellular obstacles found in vivo. Here, we develop a physically grounded simulation of a blood capillary network, incorporating realistic hydrodynamic flow fields, explicit red blood cell dynamics, and anatomically derived branching geometry, and train deep RL agents to navigate it via chemotaxis. We systematically map the physical limits of navigation across robot size and swimming speed, revealing a forbidden regime where Brownian motion and flow overcome propulsion. Successful agents independently discover multiple universal strategy types, including run-and-rotate and energy-efficient search-and-sit policies, regardless of robot parameters. Without retraining, these agents perform targeted blocking and unblocking of capillary flow, restoring throughput to healthy baseline levels. These results establish RL as a viable framework for developing autonomous microrobotic intervention strategies in complex biological environments.

</details>


### [172] [RMTL: Reinforced Micro-task Learning for Long-Horizon Manipulation with VLM Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26175)
*Anıl Can Ateş,Orhan Kahraman,Cihan Topal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RMTL decomposes VLM rewards into micro-task prompts, uses multi-view averaging and a hierarchical policy with a reverse curriculum to improve learning in long-horizon robotic manipulation, demonstrated on Fetch tasks with three stage prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global single-prompt VLM rewards are often near-flat for long-horizon tasks under randomized initial conditions, hindering early progress and scalable learning.

Method: Decompose the task into a small set of language-described micro-tasks. At each step, compute a multi-view VLM reward using the active micro-task's prompt, averaged across views to mitigate occlusions. Apply a reverse curriculum to expose harder initial conditions. Use a PPO worker initially guided by a fixed distance-based rule to select the active micro-task, then replace it with a learned hierarchical manager. Instantiate on Fetch with three short stage-specific prompts and no additional prompt tuning.

Result: RMTL yields more informative reward signals than single-prompt VLM rewards, enabling faster learning in robotic manipulation.

Conclusion: Decomposing VLM rewards into micro-task-specific prompts can substantially improve the scalability of language-guided reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for robotic manipulation often requires manually designing a dense reward function, which is difficult to tune and often fragile, or learning a reward from human demonstrations or preferences, which can be expensive. A recent line of work uses pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) as zero-shot reward models, replacing these costs with a single text prompt. However, we argue that a single global prompt is too coarse for long-horizon manipulation tasks with randomized initial conditions. The single-prompt VLM reward is near-flat for much of the trajectory, making early progress hard for the agent to detect. We propose Reinforced Micro-Task Learning (RMTL), an approach that decomposes a manipulation task into a small set of language-described micro-tasks and trains the agent to switch between them. At each step, the agent receives a multi-view VLM reward computed using the prompt of the currently active micro-task and averaged across multiple camera views to reduce the effect of view-specific occlusions. A reverse curriculum gradually exposes the agent to harder initial conditions, while a PPO worker is first trained with a fixed distance-based rule that selects the active micro-task. We then replace this rule with a learned hierarchical manager, turning rule-based phase selection into a fully learned hierarchical policy. We instantiate RMTL on the Fetch manipulation environment using three short stage-specific prompts and without additional prompt tuning. Experiments show that RMTL provides more informative reward signals than single-prompt VLM rewards, enabling faster learning. These results suggest that decomposing VLM rewards into micro-task-specific language prompts can substantially improve the scalability of language-guided reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [173] [LiMoDE: Rethinking Lifelong Robot Manipulation from a Mixture-of-Dynamic-Experts Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26183)
*Zhihao Gu,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LiMoDE introduces a two-stage lifelong mixture-of-dynamic-experts framework for robot manipulation. Stage 1 pre-trains a dynamic MoE with experts activated by motion cues to capture diverse short-term manipulations. Stage 2 adapts lifelong MoE by learning lifelong experts and combining them with frozen ones for new tasks, enabling knowledge transfer with modest extra parameters and computation. It is validated on simulated lifelong benchmarks and real-world tasks, showing strong lifelong adaptation and competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses catastrophic forgetting and poor cross-task skill reuse in lifelong robot manipulation. While prior parameter-efficient fine-tuning handles single-task adaptation, it struggles to extract reusable skills and model interactions. Prompt-based methods exist but an architectural approach (dynamic MoE) is proposed to better capture and transfer knowledge across tasks.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) multi-task pre-training with a dynamic mixture of experts (MoE) where a varying number of heterogeneous experts are activated based on motion information to cover diverse short-term manipulations; (2) lifelong MoE adaptation mechanism (LiMoEAM) that learns lifelong experts and dynamically combines them with frozen ones for new tasks, enabling transfer and continual learning with limited updates.

Result: Empirical evaluation on both simulated lifelong learning benchmarks and real-world tasks demonstrates superior performance and robust lifelong adaptation with a moderate increase in trainable parameters and inference overhead.

Conclusion: LiMoDE provides an architectural solution for lifelong robot manipulation by decoupling prior knowledge acquisition (dynamic MoE pre-training) from lifelong adaptation (LiMoEAM), yielding effective knowledge transfer, mitigated forgetting, and scalable generalist capabilities with modest computational cost.

Abstract: Building a generalist robot that can leverage prior knowledge for continuous task adaptation remains a significant challenge. Previous works alleviate the catastrophic forgetting problem by parameter-efficient fine-tuning for single-task adaptation. However, they fail to extract reusable skills and model the interaction with other skills effectively. Recent works try to address these issues by learning prompts. Differently, this paper presents an architectural perspective on the Lifelong Mixture of Dynamic Experts (\textit{LiMoDE}), a novel two-stage learning scheme for lifelong robot manipulation. Specifically, a dynamic MoE structure is first proposed in the multi-task pre-training stage to learn prior knowledge, where a varied number of heterogeneous experts are activated based on the motion information to address different short-term manipulations. Subsequently, in the task adaptation stage, we design a lifelong MoE adaptation mechanism % (LiMoEAM) that learns lifelong experts and dynamically combines them with frozen ones for new tasks, facilitating the knowledge transfer during adaptation. The proposed \textit{LiMoDE} is evaluated on both the simulated lifelong learning benchmark and real-world tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness in achieving superior performance and strong lifelong adaptation by introducing a moderate number of additional trainable parameters and inference overhead.

</details>


### [174] [Morphology-Specific Closed-Loop Control of Logarithmic-Spiral Continuum Arms via Online Jacobian Error Compensation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26188)
*Partha Datta,Yi Jin,Wei Lin,C. Chase Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A morphology-specific closed-loop task-space control framework for logarithmic-spiral continuum arms, using a segmented tendon-driven MuJoCo model with an analytical Jacobian and online error compensation (Broyden update + Kalman filter), outperforming PCC baselines in simulation and enabling complex manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Logarithmic-spiral morphologies offer scalable fabrication and versatile grasping but previously lacked inverse kinematics and closed-loop control, hindering reliable manipulation of underactuated continuum arms.

Method: Develop a segmented tendon-driven model with a centerline backbone and equilateral tendon routing in MuJoCo to capture tapered compliance and contact dynamics. Derive an analytical task-space Jacobian from the logarithmic-spiral kinematics and augment it with online Jacobian error compensation using a Broyden secant update and Kalman-filter estimation. Validate via planar and spatial simulations including trajectory tracking, attitude regulation, disturbance rejection, 3D position tracking, and position-orientation control. Compare against a PCC baseline and apply to obstacle-assisted reach-wrap-release, adaptive whole-arm grasping, and cooperative multi-arm tasks.

Result: The controller consistently reduces tracking errors, suppresses attitude drift, and maintains a bounded Jacobian estimation error compared to PCC. It enables accurate, robust control of highly underactuated continuum manipulators and demonstrates promising performance on morphology-enabled manipulation tasks in simulation.

Conclusion: A physics-grounded, scalable baseline for hardware implementation and learning-augmented soft robotic control is established by integrating logarithmic-spiral morphology with online Jacobian compensation, validated in simulation and ready for hardware realization.

Abstract: Logarithmic spirals are ubiquitous in biological appendages and provide an attractive morphology for continuum manipulators capable of reaching, wrapping, and grasping. Recently reported logarithmic-spiral robots demonstrated scalable fabrication and versatile grasping but lacked inverse kinematics and closed-loop control. This work presents the first morphology-specific closed-loop task-space control framework for logarithmic-spiral continuum arms. A segmented tendon-driven model with a centerline backbone and equilateral tendon routing is developed in MuJoCo to capture tapered compliance and contact dynamics. An analytical task-space Jacobian is derived directly from the logarithmic-spiral kinematics and combined with online Jacobian error compensation using a Broyden secant update and Kalman-filter estimation. The resulting controller continuously corrects modeling errors arising from nonlinear deformation, contact, and geometric mismatch. The framework is validated through planar and spatial simulations, including trajectory tracking, attitude regulation, disturbance rejection, three-dimensional position tracking, and simultaneous position-orientation control. Compared with a piecewise-constant-curvature (PCC) baseline, the proposed method consistently reduces tracking errors, suppresses attitude drift, and maintains a bounded Jacobian estimation error. The controller is further applied to morphology-enabled manipulation tasks, including obstacle-assisted reach-wrap-release motions, adaptive whole-arm grasping, and cooperative multi-arm object handling. Results demonstrate that combining logarithmic-spiral morphology with online Jacobian compensation enables accurate, robust, and scalable control of highly underactuated continuum manipulators. The proposed framework establishes a physics-grounded baseline for future hardware implementation and learning-augmented soft robotic control.

</details>


### [175] [OmniContact: Chaining Meta-Skills via Contact Flow for Generalizable Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26201)
*Runyi Yu,Xiaoyi Lin,Ji Ma,Yinhuai Wang,Koukou Luo,Jiahao Ji,Huayi Wang,Wenjia Wang,Runhan Zhang,Ping Tan,Ting Wu,Ruoli Dai,Qifeng Chen,Lei Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical framework using contact flow (CF) for long-horizon loco-manipulation: a low-level CF-Track skill library and a high-level CF-Gen planner, trained on OmniContact, enabling robust execution, autonomous recovery, and semantically grounded manipulation via VLMs, with strong meta-skill and skill-chaining performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation requires not only robust, reusable meta-skills but also their reliable, closed-loop chaining with autonomous recovery. Existing explicit humanoid-object interaction representations are precise but hard for high-level planning; implicit skill embeddings are compact but lack interpretability for composition.

Method: Introduce contact flow (CF) as a compact, interpretable interface consisting of key body trajectories and time-series binary contact signals. Train CF-Track as a low-level policy to learn a unified library of loco-manipulation skills; develop CF-Gen at the high level to heuristically synthesize future contact-flow sequences. Collect OmniContact, a MoCap-based HOI dataset for humanoid loco-manipulation, to support learning. The framework enables robust execution, failure recovery, and flexible composition of meta-skills; integrates with vision-language models for semantic task decomposition.

Result: OmniContact achieves 98.7% success on Carry Box and 76.5% on Push-Stack Boxes, outperforming baselines by an average margin of 40.9% in meta-skill and 66.5% in skill chaining. The approach supports semantically grounded loco-manipulation tasks (e.g., arranging scattered boxes into a heart shape) and allows for robust recovery and flexible composition.

Conclusion: CF provides a compact, interpretable interface for long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation, enabling robust execution, autonomous recovery, and flexible meta-skill composition. The OmniContact dataset supports learning; integration with VLMs enables semantically grounded behaviors.

Abstract: Learning long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation poses a dual challenge: it requires not only the robust execution of meta-skills but also their seamless, closed-loop chaining equipped with autonomous recovery. Existing approaches remain limited: explicit humanoid-object interaction representations offer precision but are notoriously difficult for high-level planning, whereas implicit skill embeddings are compact but lack the interpretability required for reliable composition. We propose \ours, a hierarchical framework centered on \textbf{contact flow (CF)}, a compact representation consisting of key body trajectories and time-series binary contact signals.
