Leveraging this shared interface, our low-level policy \textbf{CF-Track} learns a unified library of loco-manipulation skills, while our high-level module \textbf{CF-Gen} heuristically synthesizes future contact-flow sequences. To support this setting, we additionally collect the OmniContact dataset, a MoCap-based HOI corpus for humanoid loco-manipulation (Appendix~\ref{sec:dataset}). Together, they enable robust execution, autonomous failure recovery, and flexible composition of meta-skills for long-horizon tasks. Experiments show that OmniContact achieves \(98.7\%\) success on \textit{Carry Box} and \(76.5\%\) on \textit{Push-Stack Boxes}, outperforming prior baselines by average margins of \(40.9\%\) in meta-skill and \(66.5\%\) in skill chaining. Besides, our framework naturally integrates with VLMs for semantic task decomposition, enabling complex, semantically grounded loco-manipulation behaviors, such as arranging scattered boxes into a heart shape.

</details>


### [176] [RoboTales: ROBOTic Anthropomorphic LEarning Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26213)
*Andrew Chen,Ju-Hung Chen,Phurinat Pinyomit,Alexis E. Block*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboTales uses a low-cost Baxter robot to perform expressive sock-puppet storytelling that synchronizes narration, gestures, and mouth movements; results from a pilot study show puppet-based storytelling improves engagement and recall versus a gesture-only mode; the system is modular and platform-agnostic for screen-free, child-learning applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance engagement and narrative comprehension in children by leveraging embodied storytelling through affordable, reusable robotic puppetry; address limitations of gesture-only interaction and passive media; enable modular adoption across manipulators.

Method: Autonomously implemented puppet storytelling on a Baxter robot using expressive sock puppetry to synchronize narration, gestures, and lip movements. A pilot study compared puppet-based storytelling to a gesture-only condition, measuring engagement (HRIES) and story recall.

Result: Puppet-based storytelling outperformed gesture-only mode, yielding higher HRIES ratings and better recall, indicating that embodied puppetry enhances engagement and narrative understanding.

Conclusion: The approach is modular and platform-agnostic, adaptable to other manipulators, and offers a screen-free alternative to passive media, supporting future deployment in child-centered learning environments.

Abstract: RoboTales is a low-cost robotic storytelling system that animates narratives using expressive sock puppetry. Implemented autonomously on a Baxter robot as a test case, RoboTales synchronizes narration, gestures, and mouth movements to perform character-driven stories. In a pilot study, puppet-based storytelling outperformed a gesture-only mode, producing higher HRIES ratings and improved story recall, suggesting that embodied puppetry enhances engagement and narrative comprehension. Designed to be modular and platform-agnostic, RoboTales can be adapted to other manipulators and offers a screen-free alternative to passive media, supporting future deployment in child-centered learning environments.

</details>


### [177] [TaskNPoint: How to Teach Your Humanoid to Hit a Backhand in Minutes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26215)
*Blake Werner,Ilona Demler,Pietro Perona,Aaron D. Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TaskNPoint is a coach–learner training protocol for learning dynamic robotic skills by focusing on a short, crucial interaction window. A human coach provides a discrete skill set, one demonstration per skill, the interaction window, and the goal. The learner trains in a realistic sim with randomized target sampling to generalize to unseen goals, achieving fast, GPU-efficient learning on a Unitree G1 for forehand/backhand shots, soccer ball kicks, and box picking/placing, with no per-task reward tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic skills in robotics depend on coordinating control, physics, and morphology around a short interaction window. Traditional long-horizon learning is data-inefficient and brittle to unmodeled disturbances. A coach–learner paradigm focusing on the key window promises data efficiency and better transfer from humans to robots.

Method: TaskNPoint protocol: (1) human coach supplies a discrete set of skills, (2) one demonstration per skill, (3) identification of the interaction window (the critical 20 cm during contact), and (4) the goal. Learning occurs in a physically realistic simulation to complete each action trajectory. Randomized target sampling during training enables zero-shot generalization to unseen goal locations. Demonstrations can be short videos; tasks tested on a Unitree G1 performing forehands/backhands against human-thrown balls, soccer-ball kicks, and object manipulation (picking/placing boxes) from novel locations; training time under an hour on a single GPU with no per-task reward tuning.

Result: The approach enables successful learning from short human video demonstrations and under an hour of training on a single GPU, achieving robust performance across diverse dynamic tasks without task-specific reward shaping.

Conclusion: TaskNPoint demonstrates data-efficient, sim-to-real-capable learning of dynamic skills via a coach–learner framework centered on the interaction window. The protocol generalizes to multiple dynamic tasks and suggests a scalable path for teaching complex motions in humanoid robotics, contingent on realistic simulation and accurate identification of the interaction window.

Abstract: How do we learn to hit a tennis backhand? Not from a thousand hours of tennis tournaments on TV - we work with a coach and practice. We argue this is also the right recipe for teaching dynamic skills to humanoid robots. This follows from a structural property of dynamic skills: the outcome is decided by a short, crucial portion of the trajectory - for a backhand, the ~20cm of racket travel around ball contact. Getting this interaction window right requires coordinating the whole motion, so that control, physics, and morphology act in concert. Learning thus reduces to mastering a handful of distinct actions and, for each, practicing until the window comes out right. To this end, we introduce TaskNPoint, a training protocol which makes the coach-learner division of labor explicit. The human coach contributes four inputs: a discrete set of skills (e.g. different shots), one demonstration per skill, identification of the interaction window, and the goal. Learning in a physically realistic simulation environment fills in each action trajectory and provides robustness to unmodeled events. Crucially, randomized target sampling during training lets a single demonstration generalize zero-shot to unseen goal locations. We test this approach on a Unitree G1 humanoid that hits forehands and backhands against balls thrown by a human, kicks incoming soccer balls, and picks and places boxes from novel locations. We find that learning is successful from short human video demonstrations and under an hour of training on a single GPU, with no per-task reward tuning.

</details>


### [178] [NavIsaacLab: Generating Realistic Crowd via Parallel Robot Learning for Benchmarking Human-aware Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26265)
*Bingyi Xia,Han Bao,Jingyu Zhu,Hanjing Ye,Yuhan Pang,Guangcheng Chen,Liang Lin,Wenjun Xu,Jiankun Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NavIsaacLab is a physics-based, photorealistic pedestrian simulation framework built atop Isaac Lab that enables real-time 3D visual feedback and GPU-parallel simulation to benchmark and train human-aware navigation policies using data-driven pedestrian models (trajectory diffusion and adversarial motion learning) across diverse scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current human-aware navigation datasets are scarce and diverse data is hard to obtain; existing simulators rely on handcrafted pedestrian rules and offer limited sensor signals, hindering training and evaluation of realistic policies.

Method: NavIsaacLab integrates photo-realistic scene rendering, GPU-parallel physics simulation, and data-driven pedestrian models (trajectory diffusion for pedestrian trajectories and an adversarial motion learning controller). It supports cross-scale, diverse scenes to provide a robust benchmark and training platform for human-aware navigation.

Result: The framework provides real-time, accurate 3D visual feedback and controllable, physics-based pedestrian behaviors, enabling extensive benchmarking and training of navigation methods with realistic sensor signals.

Conclusion: NavIsaacLab addresses data scarcity and realism gaps in human-aware navigation by offering a scalable, photorealistic, data-driven simulation platform for end-to-end training and evaluation of navigation policies.

Abstract: Robot autonomous navigation that accounts for surrounding human activities is crucial for ensuring both safety and natural human-robot interaction in real-world environments shared by humans and robots. Simulation of complex and diverse navigation scenarios serves as the foundation for training reliable robot navigation policies and accurately evaluating the performance of algorithms, offering an efficient alternative to manual supervision of real data. However, current human-aware navigation research faces significant challenges due to the scarcity of diverse, high-quality scene data. Existing simulation platforms often rely on handcrafted rules to approximate pedestrian behavior and lack the capability to provide extensive sensor signals, typically assuming perfect observations. To address these limitations, this paper presents NavIsaacLab, a comprehensive framework for benchmarking and training human-aware navigation policies through physics-based and photo-realistic simulations of pedestrians and scenes. Based on Isaac Lab, the proposed framework employs photo-realistic scene rendering capabilities and supports parallel simulation on GPU, delivering real-time and accurate 3D visual feedback to robots. To enhance the realism of human behavior, a data-driven approach is employed that incorporates a trajectory diffusion model and an adversarial motion learning controller, enabling controllable, physics-based pedestrian simulation. Furthermore, the integration of diverse cross-scale scenes provides a robust benchmark for state-of-the-art human-aware navigation methods.

</details>


### [179] [Racing a Wheeled Quadruped: Active Load Transfer Mitigation via Model Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26313)
*Marla Eisman,Brian Lam,Samuel Sonnino,Francesco Borrelli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical MPC-RL framework for active roll control in autonomous wheeled quadruped racing; reduces lateral load transfer and improves lap performance on Unitree Go2-W.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve stability and performance of high-speed legged vehicles during aggressive turns by mitigating lateral load transfer (LTR) through a hybrid control stack combining offline planning, online MPC, and a full-body RL policy.

Method: Offline time-optimal raceline generation; online MPC planner minimizes lateral Load Transfer Ratio (LTR) using a bicycle model of the platform; low‑level whole-body RL policy controlling 16 actuators with leg actuators acting as active suspension to generate anti-roll torque; deployment on the Unitree Go2-W platform.

Result: Physical track experiments show mean LTR reduction up to 44%, fastest lap time improvement 8.7%, peak lateral acceleration increase of 21.3% to 1.98 m/s^2, and robust high-speed stability beyond the non-tilting baseline controller.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates a viable hierarchical control approach that exploits MPC and RL to suppress roll-induced stability issues while enhancing performance in autonomous racing; real-time applicability is supported by onboard actuation and links to supplementary code/video.

Abstract: This paper presents a hierarchical control framework using model predictive control (MPC) and reinforcement learning (RL) for active roll control to manage lateral load transfer during autonomous racing of a wheeled quadruped. The framework integrates offline time-optimal raceline generation, an online MPC planner that actively minimizes the lateral Load Transfer Ratio (LTR), and a low-level, whole-body RL policy deployed directly onto the robot's 16 actuators. The MPC is based on a vehicle dynamics bicycle model of the Unitree Go2-W platform. The robot's leg actuators act as active suspension where knee joints generate anti-roll torque to bank into turns. Physical track experiments demonstrate that active roll control reduces mean LTR by up to 44%, improves the fastest lap time by 8.7%, and boosts peak lateral acceleration capability by 21.3% to 1.98 $m/s^2$, maintaining robust high-speed stability beyond the range of a non-tilting baseline controller. Supplementary code and video can be found at https://github.com/meisman-ucb/go2w-roll-control-mpc

</details>


### [180] [Layered Outer-Loop Control for Disturbance-Robust Multi-Waypoint UAV Arrival](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26315)
*Runfeng Ling*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A layered terminal-control architecture for multi-waypoint UAV position regulation is validated across simulation and hardware, showing that separating core controller design from benchmark-specific refinements improves deployability and robustness under harder closed-loop conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve fast, disturbance-robust UAV position control that remains credible beyond a single benchmark, by isolating a core controller from benchmark-specific refinements and validating in increasingly deployment-like settings.

Method: Phase I: PyBullet with stochastic wind for rapid structural selection; identifies a controller core with separate components for smooth approach, persistent-bias compensation, and supervised near-target regulation. Phase II: Apply the core architecture in PX4/Gazebo with a cascaded outer loop, delay-sensitive settling, and strengthened transit-to-hover coupling; evaluate across benchmarked dynamics and deployable supervisory logic. Hardware evaluation on a Vicon-tracked Tello stack in a two-level study.

Result: Phase I bare controller achieves 0.024 m mean late-stage wind error. Phase II final controller selected using a transfer-oriented rule focusing on absence of benchmark priors, cross-scenario balance, and deployable supervisory logic. Grace analysis indicates residual failures are sensitive to completion semantics rather than gross waypoint miss behavior.

Conclusion: Separating core controller design from benchmark-specific refinements yields more informative benchmark success and maintains defensible performance under harder, deployment-like closed-loop evaluation.

Abstract: Disturbance-robust UAV position control is easy to demonstrate in benign simulations but much harder to make fast in approach, well behaved near the target, and credible beyond a single benchmark. This letter presents a layered terminal-control architecture for multi-waypoint UAV position regulation together with a staged evaluation across PyBullet, PX4/Gazebo, and hardware. Phase I uses a PyBullet benchmark with stochastic wind for rapid structural selection, identifying a controller core that separates smooth approach generation, persistent-bias compensation, and supervised near-target terminal regulation. Phase II carries only that main architecture into a more demanding PX4/Gazebo closed loop, where the outer-loop controller acts through a cascaded flight stack with delay-sensitive settling and stronger transit-to-hover coupling. This step exposes which benchmark gains survive autopilot-mediated dynamics and which refinements collapse once the loop becomes more deployment-like. In Phase I, the bare controller attains 0.024 m mean late-stage wind error. In Phase II, the final controller is selected using a transfer-oriented rule emphasizing absence of benchmark priors, cross-scenario balance, and deployable supervisory logic. Strict is used as the primary reporting reference; the supplementary retrospective Grace analysis shows that part of the residual failure set is sensitive to completion semantics rather than gross waypoint-miss behaviour. The evaluation is completed on one Vicon-tracked Tello stack through a two-level hardware study. Taken together, the results suggest that benchmark success becomes more informative when the main controller design is separated from benchmark-specific refinement and remains defensible under harder closed-loop evaluation.

</details>


### [181] [KRVF: A Source-Aware Semantic Voxel World Representation for Edge Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26321)
*Runfeng Ling*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KRVF is a source-aware semantic voxel world model for edge mobile manipulation that encodes occupancy, color, semantic evidence, freshness, and evidence source to support robust, queryable robot memory and object/grasp reasoning, with a ROS 2 implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge/mobile manipulation requires up-to-date, queryable, semantically meaningful world models that operate under edge-compute constraints; traditional reconstruction-centric mapping prioritizes geometric fidelity and can hinder semantic reasoning when depth data is unreliable.

Method: Introduce KRVF, a source-aware semantic voxel representation. Each voxel stores occupancy, color, semantic evidence, temporal freshness, and evidence source. The representation separates measured occupancy from semantic-prior hypotheses, enabling depth-failure-aware object reasoning without corrupting persistent geometry. It closes a feedback loop by rendering map-prior depth for repair and exposes task-level query operators for semantic objects and grasp candidates. Implemented in ROS 2 to convert online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory.

Result: Formalization of the KRVF representation and documentation of a ROS 2 implementation that turns online RGB-D observations into a task-facing memory suitable for edge mobile manipulation.

Conclusion: KRVF provides a robust, edge-friendly, task-oriented world model that supports queryable semantic reasoning and grasp planning, while explicitly handling depth-failure scenarios and enabling repair via map-prior depth rendering.

Abstract: Mobile manipulators need world models that are current, queryable, semantically meaningful, and usable under edge-compute constraints. This technical report presents KRVF, a source-aware semantic voxel world representation for edge mobile manipulation. Unlike reconstruction-centric mapping pipelines that primarily optimize global geometric fidelity, KRVF represents local world state as task-oriented voxels that encode occupancy, color, semantic evidence, temporal freshness, and evidence source. The representation separates measured occupancy from semantic-prior hypotheses, enabling depth-failure-aware object reasoning without silently corrupting persistent geometry. KRVF also closes a feedback loop between mapping and sensing by rendering map-prior depth for repair, and exposes task-level query operators for semantic objects and grasp candidates. The report formalizes the KRVF representation and documents a ROS 2 implementation that turns online RGB-D observations into a task-facing robot memory.

</details>


### [182] [Scaling Nonlinear Optimization: Many Problems One GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26341)
*John Viljoen,Johanna Haffner,Masayoshi Tomizuka,Negar Mehr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: First GPU-batched NLP solver based on IPOPT implemented in JAX, enabling concurrent solving of multiple NLPs for robotics; achieves substantial throughput gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between CPU-bound, single-instance NLP solvers and GPU-batched learning pipelines in robotics, to enable scalable, constrained optimization in GPU workflows.

Method: Redesign IPOPT to remove control flow via heterogeneous iteration fusion, introduce iteration-level batching, implement in JAX as jaxipm, and evaluate on quadrotor MPC benchmarks with obstacle avoidance, multi-agent, and cluttered environments.

Result: Up to 32.85x increase in throughput over IPOPT on quadrotor MPC benchmarks; demonstrates effective GPU batching for constrained optimization in robotics; open-source release.

Conclusion: GPU-batched NLP solvers are feasible and can dramatically accelerate constrained optimization in learning-driven robotics, enabling rapid generation of locally optimal solutions for large-scale, GPU-based pipelines.

Abstract: Many robotics problems, including trajectory optimization, inverse kinematics, and contact-rich motion planning, reduce to nonlinear programs (NLPs). Mature NLP solvers such as IPOPT can solve these problems, offering hard constraint satisfaction, optimality guarantees, and favorable scaling with problem dimension. These solvers underpin gradient-based methods in robotics, yet remain CPU-bound and solve only one problem at a time, preventing their integration into GPU-batched learning pipelines. On the other hand, sampling-based approaches such as reinforcement learning, model predictive path integral, and imitation learning have become the core of modern robotics research due to their ability to leverage GPU-batched simulators. These simulators can generate orders of magnitude more dynamics rollouts per second than was previously possible. If a GPU-batched NLP solver existed, it would unlock similar speedups in the number of constrained, locally optimal solutions generated per second. This regime of solving many problems concurrently versus solving a single problem at a time is a key requirement for integrating NLP solvers in modern GPU-batched robotics frameworks. To this end, we introduce \texttt{jaxipm}, the first GPU-batched NLP solver, based on IPOPT, and implemented in JAX. We accomplish this by redesigning IPOPT's algorithm to eliminate control flow with \textit{heterogeneous iteration fusion}, and by minimizing GPU idle time with \textit{iteration level batching}. We evaluate \texttt{jaxipm} on a variety of quadrotor nonlinear model predictive control benchmarks, including reference tracking in the presence of obstacles, multi-quadrotor navigation without collision, and navigation in a cluttered environment. We demonstrate up to a $32.85\times$ increase in throughput over IPOPT. Our complete open-source codebase is available at https://github.com/johnviljoen/jaxipm.

</details>


### [183] [MPC-Injection: Biasing Off-Policy Locomotion RL Toward Controller-Induced Behavior Basins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26392)
*Roy Xing,Seyoung Ree,Brian Plancher*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MPC-Injection inserts MPC-generated transitions into the RL replay buffer to bias learning toward a designer-preferred gait, avoiding reward shaping and discriminators while achieving deployable locomotion with minimal reward terms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL for locomotion often converges to undeployable or suboptimal gaits; a lightweight method is needed to steer learning toward a designer-preferred gait without rewriting rewards or introducing adversarial components.

Method: During training, transitions from a model predictive controller solving the same MDP are inserted into the RL replay buffer. This biases actor-critic updates toward states visited by the controller. The approach uses a simple (1–2 term) task reward and introduces no discriminators, kinematic retargeting, or auxiliary objectives.

Result: In a 2D walker and in sim-to-real experiments on a Go2 quadruped, MPC-Injection steers the policy into the controller’s gait basin, producing qualitatively comparable gaits to reward shaping with many terms and to adversarial motion priors, but with lower overhead. It improves learning by biasing updates toward controller-visited states, enabling behaviors that plain RL with simple rewards may miss.

Conclusion: MPC-Injection provides a low-overhead, effective way to steer RL-based locomotion toward designer-preferred gaits by transferring the controller’s behavior distribution into the replay buffer, reducing reliance on complex reward engineering or adversarial components.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for locomotion frequently converges to locally optimal but undeployable behaviors, such as vibrating limbs or scooting on the torso, that maximize return without producing a usable gait. We present MPC-Injection, a low-overhead method that steers RL toward a designer-preferred gait by inserting transitions into the replay buffer from a model predictive controller solving the same Markov decision process. Unlike reward shaping, MPC-Injection does not require redesigning the task reward, and unlike adversarial imitation learning, it adds no discriminator, no kinematic retargeting, and no auxiliary objective. Instead, the controller's preferred behavior is transferred to the policy purely through the replay state distribution. On a 2D walker in simulation and with sim-to-real evaluation on a Go2 quadruped, we show that MPC-Injection drives the policy into the controller's behavior basin using a one to two-term task reward, producing gaits qualitatively comparable to those of reward shaping with twenty-one tuned terms and of adversarial motion priors without their discriminator and retargeting overhead. We further analyze how the injected transitions bias actor-critic updates toward controller-visited states, allowing the policy to learn behaviors that pure RL may fail to reach under simple reward functions.

</details>


### [184] [Exploring the Intrinsic Geometry of Diffusion Models with Constrained Inverse Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26408)
*Miguel Angel Rogel Garcia,Phone Thiha Kyaw,Jonathan Kelly*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Diffusion models recover the geometry of constraint manifolds in inverse kinematics; intrinsic dimension estimated from the model's score matches the analytical degrees of freedom (DOF) of the constraint manifolds for two manipulators, and latent-space interpolation preserves proximity to the manifolds, indicating learned geometric structure beyond dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether diffusion models learn and encode the intrinsic geometry of data manifolds, not just coarse sample distributions, by using analytically tractable geometry from constrained inverse kinematics (IK) as ground truth ground-truth manifolds.

Method: Train a single conditional diffusion model across seven constraint families for each robot (6-DoF UR5 and 7-DoF Franka). The constraint families span from discrete IK branches to self-motion manifolds. Evaluate the intrinsic dimension by analyzing the model’s score function and assess geometry preservation via linear interpolation in latent space, checking proximity to the corresponding constraint manifolds across all tasks.

Result: The intrinsic dimension recovered from the model’s score function matches the analytical DOF of each constraint manifold for both robots. Linear interpolation in the latent space yields generated solutions that stay close to the appropriate constraint manifolds, indicating the learned representation captures the geometric structure of the constraint family beyond just the intrinsic dimension.

Conclusion: Constrained inverse kinematics offers a controlled setting to study the intrinsic geometry learned by diffusion models and provides evidence that diffusion models encode manifold geometry aligned with known analytical degrees of freedom.

Abstract: Recent studies suggest that diffusion models can recover geometric structure in the data manifolds they are trained on, yet the supporting evidence has so far come mostly from natural-image data, where the underlying geometry itself is unknown. We study this question in a setting where the geometry is analytically tractable: constrained inverse kinematics (IK). Each task-space constraint defines a configuration-space manifold with known intrinsic dimension, giving direct ground truth for evaluating the geometry learned by the model. For each of the 6-DoF UR5 and 7-DoF Franka, we train a single conditional diffusion model across seven constraint families, spanning solution manifolds from discrete IK branches to self-motion manifolds. Our empirical results reveal that the intrinsic dimension recovered from the model's score function matches the analytical degrees of freedom of the corresponding constraint manifold across both robots. Moreover, linear interpolation in the latent space leads to generated solutions that remain close to the appropriate constraint manifold, indicating that the learned representation further captures geometric structure of the constraint family beyond intrinsic dimension alone. Constrained IK therefore offers a controlled setting for studying the intrinsic geometry learned by diffusion models.

</details>


### [185] [CoStream: Composing Simple Behaviors for Generalizable Complex Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26423)
*Haonan Chen,Yuxiang Ma,Stephen Tian,Xiaoshen Han,Wenlong Huang,Feiyang Wu,Yunzhu Li,Jiajun Wu,Edward H. Adelson,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modular, compositional framework for long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation that combines semantic, predictive, and reactive core behaviors into SE(3) pose commands, enabling high precision and out-of-the-box generalization without rigid pipelines or monolithic policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the trade-off between brittle, task-specific pipelines and opaque end-to-end policies by enabling recomposable, independent behaviors that preserve precision and generalize to new tasks.

Method: Introduce ostem? the abstract calls it \ourshort, a framework that orchestrates foundation-model-based semantic reasoning, predictive trajectory forecasting via imagined-keypoint videos, and reactive tactile/force corrections. Outputs are composed on a shared SE(3) interface by right-multiplication to yield a single pose command per control step, executed by a compliant controller; demonstrated on 8 real-world tasks with multiple sensing modalities.

Result: Demonstrates strongest gains in contact-rich assembly and object transfer, with robust recovery from manual perturbations during execution across 8 tasks.

Conclusion: Compositional, behavior-based orchestration can naturally yield complex manipulation capabilities that match high precision and broad generalization, suggesting a practical path beyond rigid pipelines and monolithic policies for long-horizon manipulation.

Abstract: Long-horizon, contact-rich complex manipulation tasks, such as seating a GPU into a PCIe slot, demand both millimeter high precision and out-of-the-box generalization to new tasks. Existing paradigms struggle to satisfy both: classical pipelines use brittle, task-specific interfaces to achieve high-precision control but require costly pipeline redesigns to adapt to new tasks, whereas monolithic end-to-end policies provide better generalization but lack high precision on complex, out-of-distribution tasks unless retrained with new data. Both paradigms share an implicit assumption: once a manipulation capability is acquired, it must be deployed as a rigid pipeline or monolithic whole, rather than being freely decomposed and recomposed. In this paper, we show that complex manipulation capabilities can emerge naturally from the composition of simple, independent behaviors. Rather than deploying a monolithic policy or a rigid pipeline, we propose \ourshort, a framework orchestrating foundation models and diverse sensing modalities into multiple composable core behaviors: a semantic behavior extracting spatial constraints via foundation models; a predictive behavior forecasting trajectories by tracking keypoints in imagined videos; and a reactive behavior providing high-frequency tactile and force corrections. On a shared $SE(3)$ interface, these outputs compose by right-multiplication into a single pose command at each control step, executed by a compliant controller. We demonstrate \ourshort on 8 real-world tasks spanning everyday manipulation and precision assembly, with the strongest gains in contact-rich assembly and object transfer, and show robust recovery from manual perturbations during execution. {Website:} https://costream-simple.github.io

</details>


### [186] [A System for Fast, Resilient, and Adaptable Loco-Manipulation Behaviors on Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26425)
*Duncan William Calvert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A runtime-editable, robot-local behavior authoring framework for humanoid locomotion and manipulation, integrating affordance templates, a Behavior-Tree-inspired structure, and runtime perception to enable rapid, observable, and adaptable loco-manipulation across multiple robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a highly observable, predictable, and directable control architecture for humanoid robots operating in human-centric, hazardous, or repetitive tasks, with operator supervision and runtime authoring/repair.

Method: Integrate object-centric Affordance Templates with a Behavior-Tree-inspired architecture; runtime-editable perception via a behavior scene and primitive scene actions; a whole-body controller enabling arm motion during walking; a concurrent action layering algorithm; an operator interface synchronized for runtime authoring, monitoring, and repair; a large library of real-robot tasks and multi-robot deployment.

Result: Demonstrates a library of >20 real-robot tasks (e.g., push/pull doors with various mechanisms, multi-step exploration, obstacle clearing, table-to-table manipulation); demonstrates adaptation, extension, and combination of existing behaviors to create novel loco-manipulation behaviors in minutes to hours; evaluated across capability, speed, reliability, and ease of behavior creation; deployment across multiple humanoid platforms (Atlas, Valkyrie, Nadia, H1-2, Alex).

Conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, observable, and directly controllable pathway for rapid authoring and deployment of loco-manipulation behaviors on diverse humanoid robots, with demonstrated reusability and adaptability across platforms.

Abstract: Humanoid robots could take on physically demanding, hazardous, and repetitive work in spaces built for humans. However, a useful robot for these spaces must coordinate locomotion, whole body motion, perception, contact, and operator supervision. This thesis presents a robot-local, runtime-editable behavior authoring and runtime system.
  Our system strives to be maximally observable, predictable, and directable following Coactive Design principles developed during the DARPA Robotics Challenge. Our operator interface remains continuously synchronized to the robot for runtime authoring, monitoring, and repair. Our behavior architecture uniquely combines object-centric Affordance Templates, organization and logic inspired by Behavior Trees, and runtime-editable perception through a behavior scene and primitive scene actions. Action primitives build on a whole-body controller that supports moving the arms while walking, and use a concurrent action layering algorithm for speed.
  The behavior library developed during this work covers more than twenty real-robot task variants, including push and pull doors with knob, push-bar, and lever-handle mechanisms, multi-step exploration sequences, obstacle clearing, and reactive table-to-table manipulation tasks. This behavior system has been deployed on many humanoid robots, such as Boston Dynamics' DRC Atlas, NASA's Valkyrie, IHMC and Boardwalk Robotics' Nadia, Unitree's H1-2, and IHMC's Alex.
  We evaluate our system across capability, speed, reliability, and speed of behavior creation, adaptation, extension, and combination. Our experiments demonstrate that we can adapt, extend, and combine existing behaviors to create novel loco-manipulation behaviors in minutes or hours. Videos: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLJK5CTyotYqsfgfnXb-09YNFeBose6uEY.

</details>


### [187] [Play2Perfect: What Matters in Dexterous Play Pretraining for Precise Assembly?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26428)
*Tyler Ga Wei Lum,Kushal Kedia,C. Karen Liu,Jeannette Bohg*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Play2Perfect: a task-agnostic RL pretraining framework where a multi-fingered robot learns through diverse play to acquire reusable manipulation priors, then fine-tunes for precise assembly; achieves 33x sample efficiency, zero-shot sim-to-real with 60% success on tight insertions (0.5 mm clearance) and >50% on longer assembly tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning for contact-rich manipulation is data-inefficient due to sparse rewards and difficult data collection. Prior work relies on specialized hardware and fixtures. A generalizable pretraining strategy could provide reusable priors to accelerate precise assembly.

Method: Pretrain via Play2Perfect: task-agnostic RL through diverse play across objects/goals to acquire priors (grasping, in-hand reorientation, pose reaching). Finetune on precise assembly, focusing exploration on final contact-rich interactions. Systematically study design choices: object diversity, training objective, trajectory diversity, goal precision. Demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.

Result: Prior is 33x more sample-efficient than RL from scratch, even with dense, multi-stage rewards. Achieves zero-shot sim-to-real with 60% success on tight insertions (0.5 mm clearance) and >50% on long-horizon multi-part assembly and screwing.

Conclusion: Task-agnostic play pretraining yields reusable manipulation priors that substantially reduce data needs and enable effective sim-to-real transfer, advancing the capability to perform precise multi-fingered assembly after modest fine-tuning.

Abstract: Multi-fingered robots promise the speed and dexterity of human hands, yet challenging problems such as precise assembly have remained out of reach. These tasks are contact-rich, making data collection for imitation learning difficult, and sparse-reward, making direct exploration with reinforcement learning (RL) intractable. Consequently, prior work has made progress by structuring the problem with specialized grippers, tool attachments, and environment fixtures. In this work, we argue that before a robot can perfect precise assembly, it must first learn to play. We further ask the question: what factors in the process of learning to play matter for precise assembly? We propose Play2Perfect, an RL framework for task-agnostic pretraining through play on diverse objects and goals, which is then perfected on precise assembly. The goal of play is to acquire reusable manipulation priors, such as grasping, in-hand reorientation and pose reaching. Finetuning then adapts this general prior to assembly, focusing exploration on the final contact-rich, high-precision interactions needed for success. We systematically study key design choices in play pretraining, including object diversity, training objective, trajectory diversity, and goal precision. We show that our prior is 33x more sample-efficient than RL training from scratch, even when provided with dense, multi-stage rewards. We demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, achieving 60% success on tight insertions with only 0.5 mm contact clearance, and over 50% success on long-horizon multi-part assembly and screwing.

</details>


### [188] [WatchAct: A Benchmark for Behavior-Grounded Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26443)
*Baiqi Li,Ce Zhang,Yu Fang,Yue Yang,Shangzhe Li,Mingyu Ding,Gedas Bertasius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WatchAct is a long-horizon, video-grounded robot manipulation benchmark with 3,000 instances across 14 tasks, enabling evaluation of video-to-plan reasoning, policy execution, and full task completion. Current systems lag humans by a wide margin, highlighting the need for richer cognitive-reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate robotic manipulation in contexts where humans perform actions over time and where video provides information (spatial layouts, object histories, gestures) that language alone cannot, addressing the gap in current benchmarks that pair a single current image with an instruction.

Method: Assemble WatchAct by pairing real-world human-action videos with language instructions, align each instance with a simulator scene and an executable LIBERO task, covering four cognitive domains—Event Grounding, Procedural Reasoning, Implicit Intent Inference, and Episodic Reasoning—and propose a disentangled evaluation protocol that assesses video-to-plan reasoning, policy execution under oracle plans, and full task completion with planner–policy pipelines. Evaluate in both simulation and on a Franka Research 3 robot.

Result: Best pipeline (Gemini-3.1-Pro with π0.5) attains 16.3% Success Rate in simulation and 14.0% on the real robot; Plan SR = 36.8% (humans ≈97.1%); Task SR under oracle plans = 21.5% and 10.6% on out-of-domain scenarios.

Conclusion: WatchAct establishes a scalable, reproducible benchmark that exposes implementation gaps in video-to-action reasoning for long-horizon robotic manipulation; dataset and code are publicly available, providing a resource for evaluating and accelerating progress toward integrated perception–planning–execution systems.

Abstract: A robot working alongside people must reason about what they have done, in what order, and with what intent. Video carries the spatial layouts, object histories, and gestures that language leaves underspecified, yet today's manipulation benchmarks pair an instruction with a single current image, offering no way to evaluate reasoning over observed human behavior. We introduce WatchAct, a benchmark for robot manipulation grounded in observed human behavior. Each instance pairs a real-world human-action video and a language instruction with an aligned simulator scene and an executable LIBERO task, enabling scalable and reproducible evaluation. WatchAct comprises 3,000 long-horizon instances across 14 tasks in four capability domains drawn from the cognitive demands of watching another agent: parsing events (Event Grounding), recovering procedural structure (Procedural Reasoning), inferring unstated intent (Implicit Intent Inference), and tracking how the scene was changed (Episodic Reasoning). We further propose a disentangled evaluation protocol that separately measures (i)~video-to-plan reasoning by vision-language models, (ii)~policy execution under oracle plans, and (iii)~full task completion by integrated planner--policy pipelines. In both simulation and on a Franka Research 3 robot, current systems remain far from solving WatchAct. The best pipeline, Gemini-3.1-Pro with $π_{0.5}$, reaches only 16.3% Success Rate (SR) in simulation and 14.0% on the real robot. Gemini-3.1-Pro attains just 36.8% Plan SR (vs. 97.1% for humans), while $π_{0.5}$ reaches only 21.5% Task SR under oracle plans and drops to 10.6% on out-of-domain scenarios. Dataset and code are available at https://baiqi-li.github.io/watchact_page/.

</details>


### [189] [OSC2Runner: OpenSCENARIO 2.x Compliant High-Fidelity AV Simulation in CARLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26533)
*Thoshitha Gamage,Lasanthi Gamage*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OSC2Runner enables deterministic execution of OpenSCENARIO v2.x scenarios in CARLA by compiling the v2.x DSL into behavior trees and mapping to CARLA APIs, replacing static playback with AST-driven execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To eliminate spatiotemporal drift, asynchronous latencies, and kinematic snapping that arise when running v2.x logic on legacy interpreters, enabling reproducible co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and LLM-driven scenario generation.

Method: A multi-pass transpiler architecture that translates v2.x DSL into type-safe Abstract Syntax Trees, which are then compiled into dynamic, deterministic behavior trees (py_trees) mapped to CARLA's atomic APIs. The pipeline avoids static trajectory playback, enforces continuous environmental boundaries via kinematic analysis, and uses a cross-actor blackboard with 100 ms synchronization to ensure deterministic, tick-level execution.

Result: Empirical validation in highly concurrent adversarial case studies shows tick-by-tick determinism, exact spatial trigger evaluation, and 100 ms cross-actor blackboard synchronization. The approach achieves strict adherence to environmental boundaries.

Conclusion: The architecture provides a mathematically rigorous, deterministic backend for scenario-based testing, enabling co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and automated LLM-driven generation pipelines.

Abstract: Scenario-Based Testing predominantly relies on the legacy ASAM OpenSCENARIO 1.x XML standard because existing continuous simulation frameworks lack native execution support for the recently matured v2.x Domain-Specific Language (DSL). Adapting legacy interpreters to evaluate v2.x logic introduces spatiotemporal drift, asynchronous event latencies, and artificial kinematic snapping. Addressing this execution gap, OSC2Runner introduces the first orchestration framework capable of natively mapping the OpenSCENARIO v2.x DSL to CARLA. The framework achieves this by formalizing scenario translation as a compilation pipeline through a multi-pass transpiler architecture. Bypassing static trajectory playback, the architecture synthesizes type-safe Abstract Syntax Trees directly into dynamic deterministic behavior trees (py_trees) natively mapped to CARLA's atomic APIs. Empirical validation in highly concurrent adversarial case studies demonstrates tick-by-tick determinism, exact spatial trigger evaluation, and 100.0 ms cross-actor blackboard synchronization. Kinematic analysis proves the strict adherence to continuous environmental boundaries. This architecture transitions Scenario-Based Testing from approximate behavioral interpretation to mathematically rigorous execution, establishing the deterministic backend required for co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and automated LLM-driven generation pipelines.

</details>


### [190] [IDEA: Insensitive to Dynamics Mismatch via Effect Alignment for Sim-to-Real Transfer in Multi-Agent Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26575)
*Chenlong Liu,Zhuohui Zhang,Xinyan Chen,Zhipeng Wang,Bin Cheng,Bin He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a sim-to-real method for multi-agent control that uses effect alignment with semantic action abstractions and closed-loop control, plus an action synchronization mechanism; claims improved training efficiency and higher real-world success under dynamics mismatch across four navigation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the sim-to-real gap in learning-based multi-agent control, where reliance on accurate dynamics models and low-level control makes policies brittle to dynamics mismatch.

Method: Introduce effect alignment by randomizing environmental structure and using discrete semantic actions within a closed-loop control framework, elevating policy learning to a semantic abstraction level; implement an action synchronization mechanism to reduce inter-agent timing mismatches and improve temporal consistency.

Result: Experiments on four multi-agent navigation tasks show substantial training efficiency gains over mainstream transfer methods and higher success rates in real-world tests, indicating improved robustness and deployment stability under dynamics mismatch.

Conclusion: The approach reduces sensitivity to dynamics mismatch and improves temporal coordination in multi-agent systems, enabling more robust and practical sim-to-real deployment of learning-based controllers.

Abstract: Complex multi-agent control tasks remain challenging for traditional rule-based and model-based approaches, motivating the adoption of learning-based methods. However, learning-based methods often struggle with sim-to-real transfer because they rely on accurate dynamics modeling or system identification and learn policies in low-level control spaces that are highly sensitive to dynamics mismatch, making them costly and fragile in complex environments. To address this issue, we propose a sim-to-real method for multi-agent control, which is insensitive to dynamics mismatch via effect alignment. Our method combines random environmental structure with discrete semantic actions through closed-loop control, elevating policy learning to a semantic abstraction level. Additionally, we develop an action synchronization mechanism that mitigates inter-agent action timing mismatches, thereby enhancing the temporal consistency of the system. Experiments on four multi-agent navigation tasks demonstrate that our method substantially improves training efficiency over mainstream transfer methods and achieves higher success rates in real-world scenarios, thereby improving the robustness and deployment stability of multi-agent systems under dynamics mismatch.

</details>


### [191] [Inference-Time Robot Behavior Steering through Physically-Aware Reconfiguration of Task-Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26588)
*Yiyuan Pan,Hanjiang Hu,Shangtao Li,Xusheng Luo,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReStruct enables inference-time steering of learned robot policies by rewiring task structure via a neural automaton skeleton while keeping the low-level controller frozen, achieving physically-aware control and outperforming VLA baselines by up to 25%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable flexible, non-retraining steering of learned policies at test time, addressing the limitations of end-to-end fine-tuning and rigid predefined-symbol approaches.

Method: Decompose the visuomotor policy into a high-level skeleton (state-machine) and a low-level residual controller; use a neural automaton to encode user preferences; integrate via a synchronous product to reconfigure task structure; freeze the low-level controller and update the skeleton's action priors to realize physically-aware control under the modified structure.

Result: Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate steering across object-centric to temporal-logic preferences; ReStruct surpasses existing methods, with up to 25% improvement in task success and preference-following over VLA models.

Conclusion: ReStruct provides a general, physically-aware inference-time steering framework that flexibly encodes diverse user preferences while preserving low-level control integrity, yielding empirical gains over baselines.

Abstract: A central challenge in deploying learned robot policies is inference-time behavior steering: redirecting a policy at test time to satisfy user preferences not anticipated during training, without retraining. Existing methods fail in two modes: end-to-end methods require fine-tuning or expert-level guidance, while neuro-symbolic methods rely on predefined symbols whose edits can result in logically reasonable but physically infeasible plans. To address this challenge, we propose ReStruct, which builds upon a neural automaton policy that decomposes a visuomotor policy into a high-level state-machine skeleton capturing task structure and a low-level continuous controller represented as a residual policy. Specifically, ReStruct adopts the automaton to represent the preference and incorporates it into the skeleton through a synchronous product, thereby reconfiguring the task structure. With the controller kept frozen, the action priors provided by the skeleton are updated accordingly to enable physically-aware control under a modified task structure. Extensive experiments from simulation and real-world show that ReStruct steers a wide range of preferences, from object-centric specifications to temporal-logic constraints, and after steering surpasses existing methods, exceeding VLA models in both task success and preference-following by up to 25%.

</details>


### [192] [Bridging Handheld and Teleoperated Supervision for Contact-Rich Manipulation via State-Gated Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26603)
*Vidullan Surendran,Neehar Peri,David Watkins*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BRIDGE: a bi-modal routing approach that blends handheld data with targeted teleoperation via a gated mixture of diffusion-policy experts, enabling phase-aware use of desired actions in contact-rich manipulation and yielding substantial improvements over handheld-only training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between observed actions from handheld data and desired actions from teleoperation across task phases, especially in contact-sensitive phases, to improve data efficiency and safety in manipulation policy learning.

Method: Introduce BRIDGE, a mixture of diffusion policy experts with phase-specific heads (specialists) gated by current robot state. Routes between experts to apply desired actions during critical contact phases while leveraging handheld supervision for tolerant phases, using a subset of teleop demonstrations rather than full-task teleoperation.

Result: Outperforms handheld-only baselines by up to 36.7% success across three contact-rich manipulation tasks; naive mixing of data underperforms handheld data alone, demonstrating the need for phase-aware routing.

Conclusion: Phase-aware, gate-controlled routing among task-phase experts enables efficient integration of desired actions during risky phases, offering improved data efficiency and performance in contact-rich manipulation.

Abstract: Handheld data collection systems, such as the Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI), enable scalable data collection across diverse environments but only capture observed actions rather than the desired actions executed by a robot controller. In contrast, teleoperation captures desired actions directly, but is prohibitively time-consuming to collect. We revisit this trade-off through the lens of action validity across task phases. We observe that handheld trajectories provide valid supervision in tolerant, free-space phases, but lack dynamic feasibility in contact-sensitive phases, where tracking observed trajectories at high stiffness produces large, unsafe contact forces. We study the interaction between these two supervision types for contact-rich manipulation and find that training policies that combine handheld data with a small number of targeted teleoperated demonstrations provide an efficient hybrid strategy. Specifically, rather than teleoperating the entire task, we only collect partial teleoperated demonstrations for task segments where base handheld policies fail. However, naively mixing handheld and teleoperated phase-specific data yields worse performance than training on handheld data alone. To address this mismatch between observed and desired supervision, we propose Bi-modal Routing for Imitation Data via Gated Experts (BRIDGE), a mixture of diffusion policy experts that routes between specialist task phase heads conditioned on the current robot state. Notably, our approach enables task-phase specific use of desired actions during contact sensitive segments and improves success rates over handheld-only baselines by up to 36.7% across three contact-rich manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [193] [A Closed-Form 4-DoF Inter-Robot Pose Estimator using Bearing-only Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26616)
*Qixin De,Ao Zhuang,Yechen Zhang,Zhuozhou Qian,Danping Zou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A 4-DoF inter-robot pose estimator for bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization; analyzes observability, and introduces an autonomous observability test; demonstrates higher accuracy and lower computation with broader maneuver coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome observability degeneracy and slow convergence in 6-DoF methods under real-world constraints; need reliable inter-robot pose estimation with minimal infrastructure.

Method: Closed-form 4-DoF estimator by relaxing rotational constraints and using error projection for translation; theoretical observability analysis identifying degeneracy under collinear and shape-preserving motions; autonomous observability test module to choose estimation instants; validation via simulations and real-world experiments.

Result: Higher estimation accuracy, significantly reduced computational cost; observability test module enhances reliability while minimizing data-collection interval.

Conclusion: 4-DoF approach reduces excitation requirements, broadens acceptable cooperative maneuvers; observability-aware estimation improves robustness and efficiency of bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization.

Abstract: Bearing-odometry-based cooperative localization has attracted increasing research interest due to its minimal infrastructure requirements, low communication bandwidth and broad applicability in complex environments. However, existing 6-DoF approaches still face challenges in rapidly obtaining accurate and reliable inter-robot pose estimation, as the system is prone to observability degeneracy under specific motion patterns. To address these issues, we first propose a closed-form 4-DoF inter-robot pose estimator, which relaxes nonlinear constraints for rotations estimation and employs error projection for translations estimation. We then conduct a theoretical analysis of the system's observability, identifying degeneracy under two typical motion patterns: collinear and shape-preserving formations. The analysis further shows that the proposed 4-DoF system requires less stringent motion excitation for observability, enabling reliable estimation under a broader range of cooperative maneuvers. Furthermore, an observability test module is introduced to autonomously determine the optimal estimation instant, eliminating reliance on a predefined fixed-length sliding window. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves higher estimation accuracy with significantly low computational cost, and the observability test module ensures estimation reliability while minimizing the data collection interval.

</details>


### [194] [Hardware Design for Table Tennis Robot Capable of Beating Professional Players](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26643)
*Nobuhiko Mukai,Pavel Adodin,Stefan Heusser,Alexander Sigrist,Divij Grover,Guillem Torrente,Farshad Khadivar,Takekazu Kakinuma,Peter Dürr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An engineering-oriented study detailing the design of a high-performance 8-DoF table tennis robot, Ace, aimed at defeating pro players through topology-optimized hardware, inverse-dynamics-driven actuation, and delay-compensated RL control, achieving fast cycle times and high racket velocity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To meet the demanding requirements of professional table tennis play by defining explicit performance targets (workspace, payload, force resistance, speed, serve capability, end-effector accuracy) and delivering a robot capable of beating pros.

Method: Iterative hardware design: define target specs, build 8-DoF robot Ace, apply topology optimization to reduce mass with preserved stiffness, select motors/gears via inverse-dynamics torque modeling, develop low-order per-joint dynamics with delay compensation, integrate into simulations, and train/use an RL control policy.

Result: Empirical demonstrations of repeated full-stroke swings (cycle time 0.8 s) with peak racket-center velocity 22 m/s; robot defeated multiple professional players.

Conclusion: The integrated hardware–control pipeline achieves professional-level performance in table tennis and showcases the effectiveness of topology-optimized hardware, inverse-dynamics informed actuation, and delay-compensated RL control for high-speed racket sports.

Abstract: This paper focuses on the hardware specifications required for a table tennis robot to beat professional players. After analyzing the motions of elite players, we defined target specifications for the workspace, payload, external-force resistance, physical performance, serve capability, and end-effector accuracy. Based on these specifications, we developed "Ace", a custom 8-DoF robot. The mechanical structure was improved through topology optimization to minimize mass while preserving stiffness. Motor and gearbox selection was optimized using an inverse-dynamics torque model. Low-order per-joint dynamics models with delay compensation were identified and integrated into simulation to enable the use of an RL control policy. Experiments demonstrated repeated full-stroke swings with a cycle time of 0.8 s and a peak racket-center velocity of 22 m/s. The robot successfully defeated multiple professional players.

</details>


### [195] [LAMP: Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives for Feasible Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26661)
*Sangjin Han,Hoseong Jung,Jeongtae Her,Changhyun Choi,H. Jin Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LAMP introduces lane-aligned motion primitives via VQ-VAE with a feasibility-aware selector to produce topology-consistent, diverse predictions without sacrificing accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard multivariate motion forecasts optimize displacement errors but ignore lane topology for low-probability modes, yielding unsafe or infeasible trajectories.

Method: Train a VQ-VAE to learn discrete motion primitives as intention queries; introduce a feasibility-aware intention selector with a lane-topology prior to filter/unlock predictions; decoder prioritizes topology-consistent intentions while preserving diversity.

Result: On Argoverse 2, achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art baselines and improves feasibility and diversity metrics.

Conclusion: LAMP successfully anchors multimodal forecasts to lane topology, improving safety-related feasibility and diversity without compromising overall accuracy.

Abstract: Motion forecasting is essential for autonomous driving systems to enable safe decision-making and planning in complex driving scenarios. While existing predictors excel at minimizing standard displacement errors, they often overlook the adherence to lane topology of multimodal predictions, particularly for lower-probability modes. Consequently, predicted trajectories may violate physical and logical constraints, making the prediction set unreliable for safety-critical planning. In this paper, we propose LAMP (Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives), a topology-aware forecasting framework that anchors multimodal prediction to structured motion primitives aligned with lane topology. Specifically, we use a VQ-VAE to learn shape-aware motion primitives as discrete intention queries, capturing spatiotemporal patterns beyond endpoint-based intentions. We further introduce a feasibility-aware intention selector trained with a lane-topology prior for filtering unreachable intention queries, guiding the decoder to prioritize topology-consistent intentions while preserving behavioral diversity. Extensive experiments on the Argoverse 2 dataset demonstrate that LAMP achieves prediction accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art baselines while outperforming them in feasibility and diversity metrics.

</details>


### [196] [Tactile-WAM: Touch-Aware World Action Model with Tactile Asymmetric Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26663)
*Siyu Wu,Linjing You,Junjie Zhu,Yaozu Liu,Changhao Zhang,Jian Liu,Weiqiang Wang,Qi Li,Jituo Li,Hengshuang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A tactilely aware World Action Model (Tactile-WAM) with VideoClean masking and a touch-aware attention bias reduces tactile pollution, improving performance in contact-rich manipulation tasks (38.9% average, 86% on contact-rich).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual world models often produce visually plausible futures that are physically incomplete in contact-rich manipulation; unconstrained tactile signals can degrade predictions (tactile pollution). There is a need to integrate tactile information without corrupting visual dynamics.

Method: Introduce Tactile-WAM with a Tactile Asymmetric Attention Mechanism (TAAM). TAAM uses a VideoClean mask to block tactile tokens from video-query access while preserving action-query access, and a touch-aware bias derived from predicted touch changes to modulate action attention to tactile tokens during denoising.

Result: On ManiFeel, Tactile-WAM improves mean success rate by 38.9% overall and by 86% on contact-rich tasks, demonstrating robust fusion of tactile cues with visual dynamics.

Conclusion: Tactile-WAM successfully mitigates tactile pollution in world action models by separating visual and tactile information streams and biasing action generation toward tactile signals, yielding substantial gains in contact-rich manipulation.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) generate actions together with predicted futures, offering a powerful interface for robot decision making. In contact-rich manipulation, however, visually plausible futures can be physically incomplete: insertion, assembly, search, and reorientation often depend on slip, jamming, contact normals, or small alignment errors that are weakly visible or hidden in RGB. A natural solution is to predict future tactile states, however, we identify tactile pollution, a failure mode where unconstrained tactile-token injection degrades video and action prediction by forcing a visual dynamics model to absorb sparse, local, event-driven contact signals. To address this, we propose Tactile-WAM, a touch-aware WAM with a Tactile Asymmetric Attention Mechanism (TAAM). TAAM combines a VideoClean mask, which blocks video-query access to tactile key/value tokens while preserving action-query access, with a touch-aware bias for action attention. The VideoClean mask protects visual prediction while keeping contact information available for action generation; the touch-aware bias is derived from predicted touch changes and modulates action attention to tactile tokens during denoising. On ManiFeel, Tactile-WAM improves the mean success rate by 38.9% overall and by 86% on contact-rich tasks.

</details>


### [197] [Learning Motion Feasibility from Point Clouds in Cluttered Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26700)
*Sajid Ansari,Arthi,Girish Varma,Antony Thomas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Large-scale benchmark for learning motion feasibility from RGB-D for a 7-DOF manipulator in clutter; compares MLP, volumetric-CNN, and point-cloud Transformer; GRASPFC-PTX (point-cloud Transformer) achieves AUROC 0.996 on novel objects and runs faster than sampling-based motion planners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational cost of infeasible planning attempts in cluttered environments by moving feasibility decision-making from costly SBMP iterations to data-driven predictions learned from raw sensor data.

Method: Assemble a large-scale dataset with 2.7M grasp feasibility labels across 88 scanned objects and 190 cluttered tabletop scenes. Benchmark three classifier families (MLP-based, volumetric-CNN, point-cloud Transformer) under matched training conditions. Identify GRASPFC-PTX, a point-cloud Transformer, as the best performer.

Result: GRASPFC-PTX achieves AUROC 0.996 on Novel objects and provides predictions significantly faster than SBMPs; the approach demonstrates strong feasibility discrimination directly from RGB-D observations.

Conclusion: Learning-based motion feasibility predictors from RGB-D are viable for cluttered manipulation tasks and can substantially accelerate planning by replacing or accelerating feasibility checks. The introduced benchmark enables systematic evaluation and comparison across architectures; further work should address generalization, real-world deployment, and integration with planning pipelines.

Abstract: Motion feasibility prediction plays a central role in robotics, particularly in task and motion planning and manipulation. A major bottleneck for this problem in cluttered environments is that infeasible planning attempts by Sampling-based motion planners (SBMPs) can incur substantial computational cost. Also existing approaches for infeasibility certification are limited to low-dimensional configuration spaces and often assume simplified geometric environments represented by primitive objects with known parameters. We study the complementary problem of learning motion feasibility prediction directly from raw RGB-D observations for a 7-DOF manipulator operating in realistic cluttered scenes. We introduce the first large-scale benchmark for this setting, comprising 2.7M grasp feasibility labels over 88 scanned objects and 190 cluttered tabletop scenes. We benchmark three representative classifier families spanning MLP- based, volumetric-CNN, and point-cloud-based Transformer architectures under matched training conditions. Our best model, GRASPFC-PTX (a point-cloud transformer), achieves an AUROC of 0.996 on Novel objects while providing predictions significantly faster than SBMPs.

</details>


### [198] [PressMimic: Pressure-Guided Motion Capture and Control for Humanoid Robot Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26741)
*Yi Lu,Shenghao Ren,Tianyu Xiong,Zhaoxiang Li,Jiaqi Li,He Zhang,Tao Yu,Qiu Shen,Xun Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PressMimic unifies perception and control for humanoid motion imitation by integrating pressure sensing as a physical grounding signal. It introduces FRAPPE++ (RGB+pressure) for pose estimation, PSP (pressure-supervised policy) for RL control, and MotionPRO—a synchronized RGB/pressure/motion capture dataset. Empirical results show improvements in estimation accuracy, trajectory consistency, and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current humanoid imitation pipelines rely heavily on vision-based motion capture and kinematic imitation, neglecting contact dynamics. This leads to artifacts like foot sliding, floor penetration, and unstable behaviors. A physical grounding signal (pressure) can resolve contact ambiguities and improve both perception and control.

Method: 1) Perception: FRAPPE++ fuses RGB and pressure to jointly estimate 3D pose and global motion, using pressure to constrain contact and support relationships. 2) Control: PSP (pressure-supervised policy) injects pressure-derived signals into reinforcement learning to foster physically consistent contact patterns during execution. 3) Data: MotionPRO dataset with synchronized RGB, pressure, and motion capture data.

Result: Experiments indicate that incorporating pressure improves motion estimation accuracy, trajectory consistency, and execution stability compared to vision-only baselines.

Conclusion: Pressure serves as an effective physical grounding signal that bridges perception and control, enabling more physically consistent humanoid motion imitation and potentially reducing contact artifacts.

Abstract: Humanoid motion imitation requires not only accurate perception of human kinematics but also faithful reproduction of physical interactions with the environment. However, existing pipelines rely primarily on vision-based motion capture and kinematic imitation, largely ignoring contact dynamics, leading to artifacts such as foot sliding, floor penetration, and unstable behaviors. In this work, we revisit humanoid motion imitation from the perspective of physical grounding and leverage pressure as a unified modality across perception and control. We present PressMimic, a framework that integrates pressure into the full pipeline from motion capture to humanoid control. In the perception stage, we introduce FRAPPE++, a multimodal model that fuses RGB and pressure to jointly estimate 3D pose and global motion, where pressure provides explicit contact and support constraints to resolve ambiguity in vision-based estimation. In the control stage, we propose a pressure-supervised policy (PSP) that incorporates pressure-derived signals into reinforcement learning, enabling physically consistent contact patterns during execution. We further construct MotionPRO, a large-scale dataset with synchronized RGB, pressure, and motion capture data. Experiments show that pressure improves motion estimation accuracy, trajectory consistency, and execution stability. These results demonstrate that pressure serves as an effective physical grounding signal, bridging perception and control for physically consistent humanoid motion imitation.

</details>


### [199] [SSI-Policy: Learning Structured Scene Interfaces for Vision-Language Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26800)
*Kaijun Wang,Zikai Ouyang,Xuping Wu,Jinyi Hong,Wei Pan,Haibo Lu,Jia Pan,Wei Zhang,Linfang Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SSI-Policy introduces a modular RGB-only Structured Scene Interface (SSI) that fuses monocular depth features, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D motion trajectories to decouple perception from control, enabling data-efficient learning from action-free video. It achieves ~15% gains on the LIBERO benchmark with 10 demonstrations per task and generalizes to 13 real-world tasks, with ablations showing geometry and motion cues are complementary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world robotic manipulation requires spatial grounding, task-aware reasoning, and precise control under limited data. RGB-only or depth-dependent approaches either drift over long horizons or require depth sensing, making scalable, task-level reasoning difficult. A robot-agnostic, learnable RGB-only intermediate representation could unify perception and enable efficient policy learning from videos.

Method: Define SSI as a unified RGB-only intermediate representation that encodes monocular depth features, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D motion trajectories. Build SSI-Policy around this interface, trainable from action-free video and a small number of demonstrations. Evaluate on LIBERO with 10 demos per task and perform ablations to assess the contributions of geometric and motion cues. Validate on 13 real-world tasks spanning spatial reasoning, cross-embodiment transfer, and contact-rich manipulation.

Result: On LIBERO, SSI-Policy yields ~15% improvement over the strongest prior method under the 10-demo setting and remains competitive with methods using 50 demonstrations with large-scale pretraining. Ablations indicate geometric and motion cues provide complementary benefits within the SSI. Real-world evaluation across 13 tasks demonstrates broad applicability and transferability.

Conclusion: SSI-Policy demonstrates that a robot-agnostic RGB-only Structured Scene Interface can enable data-efficient, perception-to-control learning for manipulation, achieving strong gains and broad real-world applicability with limited demonstrations.

Abstract: Real-world robotic manipulation demands spatial grounding, task-aware reasoning, and precise control. Learning such capabilities becomes particularly challenging in the low-data regime. Prior methods often trade off scalable task-level reasoning and explicit physical structure: video-based approaches can drift geometrically over long horizons, 3D approaches often require depth sensing, and many flow/trajectory interfaces emphasize motion without an explicit RGB-only geometric representation. We introduce SSI-Policy, a modular framework built around a Structured Scene Interface (SSI) -- a unified, RGB-only intermediate representation that jointly encodes monocular depth features, language-grounded object layouts, and instruction-conditioned 2D motion trajectories. Critically, SSI is robot-agnostic and trainable from action-free video, decoupling perception from control so that the downstream policy can learn from few demonstrations. On the LIBERO benchmark with only 10 demonstrations per task, SSI-Policy improves over the strongest prior method by nearly 15\% and remains competitive with 50-demo methods that leverage large-scale external pretraining. Ablations show that geometric and motion cues provide complementary benefits within the shared interface. We further validate on 13 real-world tasks spanning spatial reasoning, cross-embodiment transfer, and contact-rich manipulation.

</details>


### [200] [Improving Vision-Language-Action Model Fine-Tuning with Structured Stage and Keyframe Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26801)
*Yuan Xu,Yixiang Chen,Kai Wang,Jiabing Yang,Peiyan Li,Qisen Ma,Yan Huang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: StaKe adds two plug-in auxiliary heads (stage classifier and keyframe predictor) to Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to provide structured, demonstration-derived supervision during fine-tuning, improving long-horizon manipulation performance without changing the base inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA fine-tuning applies uniform action supervision across all timesteps, neglecting the manipulation stages and target transitions. This leads to failures at challenging gripper-event transitions, especially in long-horizon tasks.

Method: StaKe introduces two lightweight auxiliary heads derived from demonstration gripper states: a stage classifier identifying the current manipulation stage, and a keyframe predictor estimating the target joint action at the next gripper transition. These heads augment training-time representations while preserving the base VLA architecture and inference loop.

Result: Empirical evaluations on bimanual simulation and single-arm Franka real-robot tasks show StaKe yields consistent success-rate gains: 14% relative on bimanual simulation and 56% on the Franka setup, with larger improvements on longer-horizon tasks involving more transitions. Ablations validate design choices; qualitative analysis shows learned representations track manipulation stages.

Conclusion: Structured supervision via StaKe is an effective and general strategy for enhancing VLA fine-tuning in long-horizon robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for generalizable robotic manipulation. During fine-tuning, however, action supervision applies equally across all timesteps, without structured supervision on which manipulation stage the robot is in or what the next gripper-event target should be. This causes failures to concentrate around challenging gripper-event transitions. To address this, we propose StaKe, a plug-in auxiliary supervision framework that automatically derives two complementary signals from demonstration gripper states without manual annotation: a stage classifier that identifies the current manipulation stage, and a keyframe predictor that estimates the target joint action at the next gripper transition. Both are modeled as lightweight auxiliary heads that enrich the learned representations during training, while leaving the base VLA policy architecture and inference loop unchanged.
