Experiments on bimanual simulation and single-arm Franka real-robot tasks show that StaKe consistently improves success rates (relative gains of 14% and 56%, respectively), with larger improvements on longer-horizon tasks that involve more gripper-event transitions. Ablation studies validate each design choice, and qualitative analysis confirms that the learned representations faithfully track manipulation stages. These results indicate that structured supervision is an effective and general strategy for enhancing VLA fine-tuning in long-horizon manipulation. Project website: https://hi-yuanxu.github.io/StaKe-Web/

</details>


### [201] [Ordinal Neural Collapse as a Representation Prior for Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26839)
*E-In Son,Jung-Taak Kim,Seung-Woo Seo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ORION imposes ordinal structuring on the encoder to align representations with the navigation action order, improving robustness in vision-based navigation and outperforming baselines in simulation and real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end imitation learning yields action-agnostic, high-variance representations due to indirect supervision and diverse environments; robust navigation requires disentangling visual factors relevant to action decisions.

Method: Introduce ORION: ordinal neural collapse that organizes encoder features along an ordinal axis corresponding to goal-directed actions; encourages neighboring actions to share visual contexts and suppresses off-axis variance; integrate with a diffusion-based navigation framework and fine-tune end-to-end.

Result: ORION outperforms end-to-end and neural-collapse baselines in navigation success rate and goal progress in both simulation and real-world tests, with notable gains in challenging scenarios like complex multi-way intersections.

Conclusion: Explicitly structuring the representation space along navigation action ordinality improves robustness to distractors and scene variation, enabling more reliable, goal-directed visual navigation when combined with a diffusion-based planner.

Abstract: Learning robust navigation policies directly from visual observations remains a fundamental challenge in vision-based robotic navigation. In end-to-end imitation learning approaches, the visual encoder and action decoder are jointly optimized using a single action loss, which provides only an indirect supervisory signal to the encoder. This indirect supervision frequently results in the encoder learning ambiguous, action-agnostic representations. The problem is further complicated by substantial variations in scene structure and appearance across diverse environments, as well as the prevalence of visual distractors inherent to real-world navigation settings. Such action-agnostic features cause the navigation policy to produce inconsistent actions at ambiguous decision points, leading to navigation failure. To overcome these limitations, we propose ORION (Ordinal Neural Collapse for Visual Navigation), a method that explicitly organizes the encoder's representation space according to the ordinal structure of navigation actions. In the context of goal-directed navigation, ego-centric control categories from Far Left to Far Right exhibit a natural ordinal relationship in which neighboring classes share similar visual contexts, while semantically opposing classes differ substantially in appearance. We encourage class representations to be arranged sequentially along a single discriminative axis, while suppressing off-axis variance within each class. The pretrained encoder is then integrated into a diffusion-based navigation framework, and the full pipeline is fine-tuned end-to-end. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings show that ORION consistently outperforms end-to-end and neural collapse baselines in navigation success rate and goal progress, with notable gains in visually challenging scenarios such as complex multi-way intersections.

</details>


### [202] [Humanoid-DART: Humanoid Loco-Manipulation using Diffusion-guided Augmentation through Relabeling and Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26855)
*Pranav Debbad,Kanish Thiagarajan,Victor Dhédin,Shafeef Omar,Majid Khadiv*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A self-supervised framework for humanoid loco-manipulation that bootstraps from sparse demonstrations to learn a goal-conditioned policy, using diffusion-based trajectory generation combined with RL to autonomously explore and expand its repertoire with minimal supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demonstration-driven learning is powerful but expensive and difficult to scale; reducing human annotation and enabling continual expansion of skills is essential for versatile humanoid control.

Method: Integrates a diffusion model to generate goal-directed trajectories for loco-manipulation and uses reinforcement learning to track these diffusion-generated trajectories. The approach bootstraps from sparse demonstrations in a self-supervised loop, progressively expanding the agent's behavioral repertoire and learning a goal-conditioned policy across a range of loco-manipulation skills.

Result: Ablation studies and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the framework's effectiveness across multiple humanoid loco-manipulation skills, indicating improvements in skill diversity and robustness with reduced reliance on expert supervision.

Conclusion: Diffusion-guided trajectory generation combined with RL enables scalable, goal-conditioned learning for humanoid loco-manipulation, achieving automatic exploration of the goal space and progressive expansion of the skill repertoire with minimal human supervision.

Abstract: Imitating human demonstrations has emerged as a dominant paradigm for learning humanoid loco-manipulation policies. However, scaling these approaches remains challenging due to the high cost of collecting diverse demonstrations and the need for continual human intervention to correct policy failures. In this paper, we present a self-supervised framework that bootstraps from sparse demonstrations and progressively expands its behavioral repertoire, enabling the learning of a goal-conditioned policy that automatically explores the goal space with minimal expert supervision. Our approach combines diffusion-based trajectory generation with reinforcement learning, where the latter is used to track goal-conditioned trajectories produced by the diffusion model for a range of loco-manipulation skills. Through extensive ablation studies and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework on multiple humanoid loco-manipulation skills.

</details>


### [203] [PlanRL: A Trajectory Planning Architecture for Reinforcement Learning-based Driving Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26858)
*Joonhee Lim,Yongjae Lee,Jangho Shin,Dongsuk Kum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning (RL) driven driving expert is integrated with a polynomial trajectory planner in a Frenet frame, augmented by a kinematic feasibility check, achieving notable gains on CARLA benchmarks compared to state-of-the-art control-based RL methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address interpretability gaps and the high spatial complexity of learning road geometries in direct-control RL driving policies, and improve compatibility with end-to-end planning architectures by introducing a planning-based, interpretable framework.

Method: Use a Frenet-frame coordinate system to simplify road geometry into a curvilinear representation; combine an RL policy with a polynomial-based trajectory planner; integrate a kinematic feasibility check during planning to respect vehicle dynamics; evaluate on CARLA Offline Leaderboard v1 and NoCrash benchmarks.

Result: On CARLA Offline Leaderboard v1 and NoCrash benchmarks, driving score improves by 5% and 11%, respectively, while success rate increases by 8% and 19%.

Conclusion: The trajectory-planning–augmented RL framework yields improved performance, interpretability, and compatibility with modern planning architectures, with feasibility checks mitigating planning errors and enhancing reliability.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a prominent framework for developing driving experts in autonomous vehicles. However, most existing RL-based experts are designed to output direct control commands (e.g., throttle, steering), which suffer from a lack of interpretability, high spatial complexity in learning road geometries, and poor compatibility with modern end-to-end planning architectures. To address these limitations, we propose a novel trajectory planning architecture for RL driving experts that integrates an RL policy with a polynomial-based trajectory planner. By employing a Frenet-frame coordinate system, our method simplifies complex road geometries into a curvilinear framework, offering a structured coordinate prior that facilitates policy learning. Furthermore, we incorporate a kinematic feasibility check into the planning stage to ensure that generated trajectories remain within the vehicle's physical limits, effectively mitigating cumulative tracking errors typically found in planning-based systems. We evaluate our approach on key CARLA benchmarks, where it significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art control-based RL experts. On the CARLA Offline Leaderboard v1 and NoCrash benchmarks, our method improves the driving score by 5% and 11%, respectively, and increases the success rate by 8% and 19%.

</details>


### [204] [Risk-Aware Selective Multimodal Driver Monitoring with Driver-State World Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26922)
*Daosheng Qiu,Haozhuang Chi,Hao Su,Shu Long,Xinyue Miao,Yongle Dong,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a cost-aware selective inference framework for deployable multimodal driver monitoring by fusing RGB vision with physiological signals via a lightweight RGB-physiological student and a gating mechanism to abstain for safety; demonstrates low-latency inference and reduced unsafe misses, but faces calibration drift and partial synchronization limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe, low-latency in-cabin monitoring in automated vehicles by leveraging multimodal priors while avoiding the latency and reliability issues of large vision-language models.

Method: A lightweight RGB-physiological student integrates in-cabin visual observations with window-level HR/EDA signals. A learned gate decides whether to emit a fast prediction or abstain for safety intervention. Additional controls show that the learned scores carry sample-level information beyond scenario priors. A driver-state world modeling module rolls out latent driver-state features to estimate future fast-model errors and counterfactual action costs. Evaluation focuses on scenario-induced driver-demand recognition with the above architecture.

Result: On scenario-induced driver-demand recognition, the RGB-physiological student achieves 0.7440 Macro-F1 and 0.9099 balanced accuracy with 11.39M parameters and 3.08 ms inference latency, outperforming RGB-only and physiology-only baselines. Cost-aware selective inference reduces unsafe false negatives from 17.37% (always-fast) to ~5% across seeds while maintaining deployment-level latency. The learned scores reflect sample-level information beyond priors, though exact physiological synchronization remains a limitation.

Conclusion: Reliable edge driver monitoring benefits from advances in perception, risk-aware selective control, and group-robust calibration; driver-state modeling provides predictive value but calibration drift under worst-case groups highlights the need for robust calibration and group-aware strategies.

Abstract: Continuous driver monitoring in automated vehicles requires low-latency inference while avoiding unsafe decisions under uncertain driver states. Large vision-language models provide broad multimodal priors, but their latency and limited reliability in this setting make them unsuitable as always-on in-cabin monitors. We propose a cost-aware selective inference framework for deployable multimodal driver monitoring. The core system is a lightweight RGB-physiological student that combines in-cabin visual observations with window-level HR/EDA signals, and a learned gate that decides when to accept the fast prediction or abstain for safety intervention. Additional controls show that the learned scores contain sample-level information beyond scenario priors, while exact physiological synchronization remains a limitation. To incorporate predictive evidence, we further study a compact driver-state world modeling module that rolls out latent driver-state features and estimates future fast-model errors and counterfactual system-level action costs. On scenario-induced driver-demand recognition, the RGB-physiological student improves over RGB-only and physiology-only baselines, reaching 0.7440 Macro-F1 and 0.9099 balanced accuracy with 11.39M parameters and 3.08ms inference latency. Cost-aware selective inference reduces unsafe false negatives from 17.37% under always-fast inference to approximately 5% across seeds, while maintaining deployment-level latency. While driver-state world modeling offers valuable predictive signals, worst-group evaluations highlight persistent operating-point calibration drift. Ultimately, reliable edge driver monitoring requires advancing not only perception backbones, but also risk-aware selective control and group-robust calibration.

</details>


### [205] [UAV-MapFusion: RTK-Aligned Uncertainty-Aware Coarse-to-Fine Multi-Session UAV Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26928)
*Feng Pan,Chunran Zheng,Bing Xue,Yukang Cui,Jiayu Wen,Zhiyu Chen,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware, multi-session point cloud map merging for UAVs integrating RTK via DTW and MOGP within a scene-graph and factor-graph framework to improve drift suppression and local geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale UAV mapping is constrained by flight time and onboard storage. Existing multi-session methods struggle to simultaneously suppress long-range drift and preserve local geometric accuracy across sessions.

Method: 1) initial multi-session map merging using a scene graph; 2) RTK spatiotemporal alignment module with temporal offsets estimated by Dynamic Time Warping (DTW); 3) continuous RTK constraints recovered using Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGP) under incomplete sampling and frame dropouts; 4) construct a unified uncertainty-aware factor graph; 5) refine local geometry via iterative plane-factor refinement.

Result: Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the method’s effectiveness and robustness; authors plan to release code and dataset to facilitate further research.

Conclusion: The framework effectively suppresses long-range drift while preserving local geometric accuracy in large-scale UAV mapping, leveraging DTW and MOGP within an uncertainty-aware graph-based optimization pipeline.

Abstract: Large-scale point cloud maps are essential for robotics and spatial intelligence tasks. UAVs provide an efficient means for large-scale map acquisition; however, due to limited flight endurance and onboard storage, mapping a large-scale scene within a single flight remains difficult. Existing multi-session map merging methods can extend the mapping range, yet in UAV scenarios they still struggle to simultaneously suppress long-range drift and preserve local geometric accuracy. To address this issue, an uncertainty-aware multi-session point cloud map merging and coarse-to-fine optimization system is proposed. The proposed method first performs initial multi-session map merging based on a scene graph, and then incorporates RTK observations through an RTK spatiotemporal alignment module, where temporal offsets are estimated using Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), and continuous RTK constraints are recovered using Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGP) under incomplete sampling and frame dropouts. On this basis, a unified uncertainty-aware factor graph is constructed, and local geometric accuracy is further improved through iterative plane-factor refinement. Experiments on real-world datasets validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method. To facilitate further research and development in the community, our code and dataset will be publicly released.

</details>


### [206] [RobOralScan: Learning Active Intraoral Scanning for Robotic Dental Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26955)
*Jinhyung Lee,Haeun Yun,Siwon Kim,Gihyun Baek,Sungho Moon,Sehyun Hwang,Sunghoon Im*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An RL-based robotic intraoral scanner with geometric memory and tooth-wise coverage learning improves full-arch scanning by accumulating partial observations and optimizing coverage; demonstrates strong quantitative metrics and sim-to-real feasibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce labor-intensive, low-automation full-arch intraoral scanning in the confined oral cavity where narrow FOV and operator workload hinder consistent reconstructions.

Method: RobOralScan employs a geometric memory-based observation space that aggregates partial scans into a tri-state representation, enabling history-aware reasoning; introduces tooth-wise coverage learning with coverage-based rewards and progressive training; uses the policy to select relative scanner motions from memory and proprioception for closed-loop control.

Result: Chamfer Distance 0.00838; average coverage 92.58%; lower-tail per-tooth coverage 88.45%; normalized AUC 0.6674; completes the scan criterion in 8 of 10 episodes; zero-shot sim-to-real demonstration on a physical robot-scanner setup.

Conclusion: First RL-based pipeline for robotic automatic intraoral scanning; demonstrates feasibility of automated, coverage-balanced full-arch scanning and sim-to-real transfer; hints at potential clinical deployment with further work on generalization, safety, and real-time constraints.

Abstract: Intraoral scanning is widely used for digital optical impressions in prosthodontic, implant, and orthodontic treatment, but full-arch and long-span scanning remain labor-intensive tasks with limited automation. In the confined oral cavity, operators must continuously adjust scanner motion while accumulating narrow field-of-view observations, making reconstruction quality sensitive to missing tooth surfaces and operator workload.
  We propose RobOralScan, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first reinforcement learning (RL)-based pipeline for robotic automatic intraoral scanning. RobOralScan introduces a geometric memory-based observation space that accumulates partial scan observations into a tri-state geometric representation, allowing the policy to reason over scan history and insufficiently observed regions. It further introduces tooth-wise coverage learning, combining coverage-aware reward signals and a progressive training scheme to improve global reconstruction coverage while reducing uneven coverage across individual teeth. The learned policy selects relative scanner motions from accumulated geometric memory and robot proprioception for closed-loop scan control within the oral workspace.
  RobOralScan achieves a Chamfer Distance of 0.00838, an average coverage of 92.58%, a lower-tail per-tooth coverage of 88.45%, and a normalized AUC of 0.6674, completing the scan criterion in 8 of 10 evaluation episodes. Furthermore, zero-shot sim-to-real experiments demonstrate its practical feasibility on a physical robot-scanner setup.

</details>


### [207] [In-Context Model Predictive Generation: Open-Vocabulary Motion Synthesis from Language Models to Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26981)
*Xiaomeng Fu,Junfan Lin,Yang Liu,Yaowei Wang,Guanbin Li,Liang Lin,Ziliang Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ICMPG combines an LLM-based planner with an inference-time physics evaluator in a closed-loop Model Predictive Generation framework, yielding motion sequences that are both semantically faithful to text and physically plausible without task-specific retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-motion synthesis struggles to balance semantic fidelity and physical realism. LLMs offer flexible open-vocabulary interpretation but can violate physics; physics-based controllers ensure realism but lag on fine-grained semantics and novel concepts. A unified, flexible approach is needed to leverage language planning while enforcing physics in real time.

Method: Two-module architecture: Context-Aware Motion Generation (CAMG) uses an LLM as a planner to decompose commands and generate candidate motion tokens; Model Predictive Generation (MPG) assesses candidate sequences via physical simulation and semantic alignment, computes a composite reward, and selects the best sequence to guide subsequent steps. This MPC-like loop enables closed-loop refinement without task-specific policy retraining and is compatible with different LLM backbones.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows ICMPG generalizes well across standard and zero-shot open-vocabulary settings, producing motions that are more physically plausible and semantically faithful than representative baselines on the evaluated benchmarks.

Conclusion: ICMPG effectively bridges semantic interpretation and physical simulation in a flexible, retraining-free framework, enhancing controllability and robustness for text-driven motion synthesis and accommodating various LLM backbones.

Abstract: Synthesizing human motion from textual descriptions is essential for immersive digital applications, yet existing methods face a persistent trade-off between semantic fidelity and physical realism. Large language model (LLM)-based approaches can interpret diverse open-vocabulary instructions and compose high-level action plans, but they often generate motions that violate physical constraints. Physics-aware models improve realism through simulation or control, but they struggle with semantic complexity, fine-grained instructions, and novel concepts. To address this gap, we propose In-Context Model Predictive Generation (ICMPG), a framework that integrates language-model planning with inference-time physical feedback. ICMPG reformulates motion synthesis as a Model Predictive Control (MPC)-like process with two modules. The Context-Aware Motion Generation (CAMG) module uses an LLM as a planner to decompose textual commands and generate candidate motion sequences from motion tokens. The Model Predictive Generation (MPG) module evaluates these candidates through physical simulation and semantic alignment, estimates a composite reward, and selects the best sequence to guide subsequent generation steps. Unlike open-loop generation, this closed-loop refinement enables ICMPG to adapt motions to both the input semantics and the simulated physical environment without task-specific policy retraining. Extensive experiments across standard and zero-shot open-vocabulary settings show that ICMPG generalizes robustly to diverse commands and produces motions that are more physically plausible and semantically faithful than representative baselines on the evaluated benchmarks. The framework bridges semantic interpretation and physical simulation while remaining flexible enough to incorporate different LLM backbones, enabling more versatile and controllable text-driven motion synthesis.

</details>


### [208] [RelAfford6D: Relational 6D Affordance Graphs for Constraint-Driven Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27036)
*Guodong Zhang,Qichen He,Wenyuan Xie,Shaokai Wu,Yanbiao Ji,Qiuchang Li,Bayram Bayramli,Yue Ding,Hongtao Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Training-free RelAfford6D leverages a Relational 6D Affordance Graph to convert semantic instructions into SE(3) constraints for robust, zero-shot robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging abstract semantics and precise control for open-world manipulation; current data-driven policies rely on isolated contacts or latent affordances and lack explicit kinematic constraints, limiting generalization and robustness.

Method: Construct Relational 6D Affordance Graph from free-form instructions to infer a semantic topology linking the primary interacting part to its anchor. Elevate topology nodes to SE(3) poses via vision foundation models, then formulate execution as a kinematic constraint satisfaction problem. Synthesize trajectories by tracking defined physical manifolds (e.g., revolute/prismatic axes) with closed-loop replanning for disturbances.

Result: Achieves superior zero-shot success rates, cross-category generalization, and robustness in both simulation and real-world environments, outperforming existing data-driven baselines.

Conclusion: Physically grounded framework that fuses semantic topology with rigid-body kinematics enables robust, generalizable manipulation in open-world settings through analytic constraint-based execution.

Abstract: Bridging abstract semantics and precise physical control remains a fundamental challenge in open-world robotic manipulation. While recent data-driven policies show promise, their reliance on isolated contact points or latent affordance embeddings lacks the rigorous kinematic constraints necessary for complex articulated objects.To overcome the limitation, we introduce RelAfford6D, a novel training-free framework centered on a Relational 6D Affordance Graph. Given a free-form instruction, our system deduces a semantic topology linking a primary interacting part to its physical anchor. By elevating these topological nodes into precise metric $SE(3)$ poses via vision foundation models, we analytically formulate downstream execution as a kinematic constraint satisfaction problem. The robot synthesizes continuous trajectories by tracking strictly defined physical manifolds (e.g., revolute or prismatic orbits). Coupled with a closed-loop tracking mechanism for dynamic replanning against disturbances, our physically grounded approach achieves superior zero-shot success rates, cross-category generalization and execution robustness in both simulation and the real world environments, outperforming existing data-driven baselines.

</details>


### [209] [ForesightSafety-VLA: A Unified Diagnostic Safety Benchmark for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27079)
*Mingyang Lyu,Yinqian Sun,Yiyang Jia,Sicheng Shen,Moquan Sha,Huangrui Li,Feifei Zhao,Yi Zeng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces ForesightSafety-VLA, a diagnostic benchmark for embodied safety in vision-language-action (VLA) models, with a 13-category safety taxonomy (Safe-Core, Safe-Lang, Safe-Vis) and three controlled variation axes to diagnose failure sources beyond aggregate task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA safety evaluation lacks a structured, process-focused framework. There is a need to understand how perception, grounding, and control contribute to safety under realistic embodied tasks.

Method: Define a 13-category safety taxonomy; design ForesightSafety-VLA with scene structure, language command, and visual observation variations; introduce metrics like cumulative safety cost (CC) and risk exposure time (RET); provide a four-quadrant safe/unsafe decomposition; instantiate 66 safety-augmented base scenarios in RoboTwin across 5 embodiments; evaluate representative VLA baselines.

Result: Even the strongest VLA policy incurs non-trivial safety cost and unsafe nominal success; structure and visual variation cause more safety degradation than language variation; safety is tightly coupled to perception, grounding, and control and cannot be achieved by post-hoc safety filtering alone.

Conclusion: Embodied safety is foundational to system competence; the benchmark enables diagnosing specific failure sources and guiding improvements in perception, grounding, and control components of VLA systems.

Abstract: In embodied intelligence, safety is a prerequisite for reliable robot deployment in the physical world. Current vision-language-action (VLA) models continue to advance toward general-purpose task capability, yet their embodied safety limits remain poorly understood. To address this gap, we introduce ForesightSafety-VLA, a diagnostic benchmark that makes safety the primary evaluation target for VLA systems. We define a 13-category safety taxonomy covering physical interaction safety (Safe-Core), instruction-side safety (Safe-Lang), and perception-side safety (Safe-Vis), and evaluate policies under three controlled dimensions of variation -- scene structure, language command, and visual observation -- so that failure sources can be diagnosed rather than hidden in a single aggregate score. Beyond binary task success, ForesightSafety-VLA measures process-level risk through cumulative safety cost (CC) and risk exposure time (RET), together with a four-quadrant decomposition of safe/unsafe success and failure. We instantiate 66 safety-augmented base scenarios in RoboTwin across 5 embodiments and report results on representative VLA baselines. Across the evaluated baselines, even the strongest policy incurs non-trivial safety cost and unsafe nominal success, while structure and visual variation induce substantially stronger safety degradation than ordinary language variation. These results suggest that embodied safety is tightly coupled to perception, grounding, and control competence rather than being reducible to post-hoc safety filtering alone.

</details>


### [210] [Proposal-Conditioned Latent Diffusion for Closed-Loop Traffic Scenario Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27123)
*Shubham Vaijanath Phoolari,Aleyna Kara,Christoph Lauer,Steven Peters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Efficient diffusion-based closed-loop traffic scenario generator with instance-centric context, multimodal priors, and test-time guidance; validated on Waymo Open Motion Dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for realistic, interactive multi-agent behaviors in closed-loop traffic simulation while reducing computation to fit time-constrained replanning for autonomous vehicles.

Method: Diffusion-based framework conditioned on instance-centric scene context and multimodal proposal priors; optional test-time guidance; compact action-latent representation; proposal-based initialization to accelerate sampling without retraining.

Result: On Waymo Open Motion Dataset, achieves favorable trade-offs among realism, safety, and controllability across diverse interactive scenarios; test-time guidance enables adjustable trade-offs among competing objectives.

Conclusion: Provides efficient, controllable, safe scenario generation for closed-loop traffic simulation with tunable behavior via test-time guidance; suitable for real-time AV planning and simulation.

Abstract: Closed-loop traffic simulation remains challenging because it must generate interactive multi-agent behaviors that are scene-consistent and controllable throughout rollout. Prior diffusion-based approaches achieve strong realism, but their computational cost can hinder deployment in time-constrained replanning loops for autonomous vehicle planning and simulation. We present a diffusion-based scenario generation framework conditioned on instance-centric scene context and multimodal proposal priors, with optional test-time guidance for shaping safety-critical behaviors. A compact action-latent representation and proposal-based initialization improve sampling efficiency and reduce per-step runtime without retraining. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset demonstrate a favorable balance among realism, safety, and controllability across diverse interactive scenarios, while showing that test-time guidance enables systematic trade-offs among competing objectives.

</details>


### [211] [PAMAE: Phase-Aware-MoE Action Experts Towards Reliable Flow-Matching Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27144)
*Jiayu Yang,Tao Yang,Xiang Chang,Fei Chao,Changjing Shang,Qiang Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAMAE introduces a phase-aware mixture-of-experts action module for VLA policies, replacing a single action expert with a sparse expert mixture guided by phase routing, using a phase predictor and two-stage training, achieving up to 9.2% task-success gains in multi-stage manipulation and demonstrating the importance of phase supervision and staged optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single shared action expert in flow-matching VLA policies limit phase-specific control patterns across stages, hindering reliability in multi-stage manipulation.

Method: Replace the action expert with a sparse mixture of experts; add a phase-aware router that uses execution-phase cues to route action generation; include a lightweight phase-prediction head and a routing alignment objective; adopt a two-stage training: warm-up under standard flow-matching loss, then optimize phase-consistent routing with auxiliary supervision.

Result: Improved task success by up to 9.2% on multi-stage manipulation simulations versus strong VLA baselines; ablations show both phase-supervised routing and staged optimization are essential for gains.

Conclusion: Phase-consistent expert allocation improves reliability and action quality of flow-matching VLA policies; the method is plug-and-play with the pretrained VLA backbone and yields gains through phase-aware specialization.

Abstract: Reliable action generation for multi-stage robotic manipulation remains challenging for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. While existing flow-matching VLA policies offer strong multimodal grounding and generalization, they typically employ a single shared action expert, limiting their ability to capture phase-specific control patterns across distinct execution stages. We propose a plug-and-play Phase-Aware Mixture-of-Experts Action Module (PAMAE), as a step towards more reliable phase-consistent action generation. PAMAE replaces the original flow-matching action expert with a sparse expert mixture while preserving the pretrained VLA backbone. PAMAE introduces a phase-aware router that leverages execution-phase cues to allocate action generation across experts, supported by a lightweight phase prediction head and a routing alignment objective. To stabilize specialization, we adopt a two-stage training scheme that first warms up the expert module under the standard flow-matching loss and then optimizes phase-consistent routing under auxiliary supervision. On multi-stage manipulation simulation tasks, PAMAE improves task success by up to \textbf{9.2\%} over strong VLA baselines. Further ablations show that both phase-supervised routing and staged optimization are essential for the observed gains. Our results highlight phase-consistent expert allocation as an effective mechanism for improving the reliability and action quality of flow-matching VLA policies.

</details>


### [212] [PhysReflect-VLA: Physical Feasibility and Self-Reflective Regulation for Reliable Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27146)
*Jiayu Yang,Tao Yang,Weijun Li,Xiang Chang,Fei Chao,Changjing Shang,Qiang Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces PhysReflect-VLA, a plug-and-play execution-time reliability framework that augments Vision-Language-Action policies with online physical feasibility checks and self-reflection, improving stability and task success in long-horizon, contact-rich robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon manipulation is highly sensitive to physically infeasible transitions and execution disturbances; existing VLA policies lack online feasibility checks and self-diagnosis, risking failure and inefficiency.

Method: Incorporates a Feasibility Operator to assess dynamically feasible state transitions, an Action Explanation Operator to verify transition coherence, and an LLM-based Reflection Module to analyze discrepancies and generate corrective guidance. A two-stage training procedure stabilizes feasibility modeling and integrates reflection into the control loop; evaluated in real-world, multi-stage manipulation tasks.

Result: Across real-world tasks, the approach yields consistent improvements in stage-wise stability and overall task success, with an average gain of 5.4% over representative VLA baselines. Ablation studies show both feasibility checking and reflection contribute to robustness.

Conclusion: Embedding physical consistency checks and online self-reflection is beneficial for reliable long-horizon robotic manipulation, enabling more robust policy execution in the presence of disturbances and contact-rich dynamics.

Abstract: Long-horizon robotic manipulation is highly sensitive to physically infeasible transitions, contact-induced disturbances, and the lack of effective self-correction during execution. Although Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models provide strong task grounding through multimodal learning, they typically generate actions in a feed-forward manner without explicitly checking physical feasibility or diagnosing execution errors online. We present PhysReflect-VLA, a plug-and-play execution-time reliability framework that augments VLA policies with physical feasibility evaluation and structured self-reflection in a closed-loop control pipeline. A Feasibility Operator evaluates whether candidate actions induce dynamically consistent state transitions; an Action Explanation Operator verifies transition coherence; and an LLM-based Reflection Module analyzes state discrepancies to generate corrective guidance for subsequent actions. A two-stage training procedure stabilizes feasibility modeling and integrates reflection into the control loop. Experiments on multi-stage, contact-rich real-world manipulation tasks show consistent improvements in stage-wise stability and overall task success compared with representative VLA baselines with an average gain of 5.4\%. Ablation results further indicate that feasibility checking and reflection-based correction both contribute to improved execution robustness. These results highlight the importance of embedding physical consistency checks and online self-reflection for reliable long-horizon robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [213] [Learning to Fold: prizewinning solution at LeHome Challenge 2026 (1st place online, 2nd offline)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27163)
*Ilia Larchenko*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An RL-enhanced vision-language-action policy for bimanual garment folding, self-predictive value estimates, achieves top placements in ICRA 2026 challenge; blends AWR+RECAP for flow-matching VLA with a scalable RL pipeline and a sim-to-real recipe.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust, high-performance bimanual garment folding by unifying action prediction with self-assessed task progress signals, improving learning efficiency and sim-to-real transfer.

Method: A single network predicts actions and also predicts success, progress, and several future quantities; these predictions drive advantage estimation, live failure detection, and candidate selection. Combines AWR and RECAP for flow-matching VLA; asynchronous distributed training and rollout via HuggingFace Hub; inference-time hyperparameter optimization via Thompson sampling; sim-to-real pipeline including camera-alignment tooling, heavy augmentation, and DAgger-like HIL data collection.

Result: Online simulation round: 1st out of 62 teams. Real-world final: 2nd place.

Conclusion: The work reuses established RL ideas in a practical, modular recipe that can be used as a whole or selectively; demonstrates strong competitive performance and a viable path for sim-to-real deployment in garment-folding tasks.

Abstract: I describe my solution to the LeHome Challenge 2026, an ICRA 2026 competition on bimanual garment folding. The system placed 1st of 62 teams in the online (simulation) round and 2nd in the real-world final. It improves a vision-language-action (VLA) policy with a reinforcement-learning loop. The policy is its own value function: the same network that predicts actions also predicts success, progress, and a few task-relevant future quantities, and those predictions drive advantage estimation, live failure detection, and candidate selection. The work mostly recombines existing RL ideas with engineering and optimization contributions that can be used together as one recipe or individually: AWR + RECAP combined for flow-matching VLA; an asynchronous distributed training / rollout pipeline through HuggingFace Hub; inference-time hyperparameters optimization via Thompson sampling; a sim-to-real recipe with camera-alignment tooling, heavy augmentation and DAgger-like HIL data collection.

</details>


### [214] [HumanoidUMI: Bridging Robot-Free Demonstrations and Humanoid Whole-Body Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27239)
*Hongwu Wang,Chenhao Yu,Youhao Hu,Jiachen Zhang,Yuanyuan Li,Shaqi Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces HumanoidUMI, a portable, robot-free data-collection framework for humanoid whole-body demonstrations using lightweight VR devices and UMI-inspired grippers to capture sparse human keypoint trajectories, wrist views, and gripper actions, enabling training of high-level policies that are retargeted to robot-native references and executed by a whole-body controller.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality demonstrations are essential for humanoid whole-body skill learning, but traditional data collection via teleoperation is limited by hardware access, operator expertise, and efficiency. A scalable, accessible data-collection approach is needed to produce transferable demonstrations without heavy teleoperation.

Method: HumanoidUMI employs lightweight VR devices and UMI-inspired grippers to collect sparse human keypoint trajectories, wrist-view observations, and gripper actions. A high-level policy is trained to predict future keypoints, which are retargeted to robot-native whole-body references and controlled by a whole-body controller for execution. The framework is demonstrated in five real-world scenarios.

Result: Experiments across five real-world scenarios validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework and show the demonstrated data is suitable for transferable humanoid whole-body skill learning.

Conclusion: The proposed HumanoidUMI framework provides a practical and scalable solution for collecting high-quality humanoid whole-body demonstrations without relying on teleoperation hardware, enabling transferable skill learning to humanoid robots.

Abstract: High-quality demonstration data are essential for humanoid robot skill learning, especially for whole-body behaviors that require coordinated perception, locomotion, and manipulation. Existing data-collection methods largely rely on robot teleoperation, which is constrained by hardware accessibility, operator expertise, and limited efficiency. Inspired by the Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI), we propose HumanoidUMI, a portable and robot-free framework for humanoid whole-body data collection. HumanoidUMI uses lightweight VR devices and UMI-inspired grippers to collect sparse human keypoint trajectories, wrist-view observations, and gripper actions. These demonstrations train a high-level policy to predict future keypoints, which are retargeted to robot-native whole-body references and executed by a whole-body controller. Experiments in five real-world scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework and validate the collected demonstrations for transferable humanoid whole-body skill learning.

</details>


### [215] [Advancing Omnimodal Embodied Agents from Isolated Skills to Everyday Physical Autonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27251)
*Junhao Shi,Zezheng Huai,Siyin Wang,Jia Chen,Yubang Wang,Zhaoye Fei,Hechang Chen,Jingjing Gong,Xipeng Qiu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OmniAct: a hierarchical asynchronous framework that unifies cyber-physical action spaces, memory, and verification to enable persistent autonomy for long-horizon tasks in unstructured environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persistent autonomy over extended operation requires a single, cohesive architecture that combines planning across cyber (APIs, IoT) and physical (manipulation, navigation) domains, scalable memory to prevent unbounded context growth, and robust failure detection/recovery; existing systems treat planning, memory, and execution as separate problems.

Method: A three-part architecture: (i) a multimodal semantic planner that routes skills across a unified cyber-physical action space; (ii) an adaptive hierarchical memory with event-boundary-driven compression to achieve sub-linear context growth; (iii) an asynchronous visual preemption engine that closes the semantic loop during physical execution; evaluated on 40 real-world long-horizon tasks across two robotic platforms coordinating four IoT devices.

Result: Consistent improvements in end-to-end success across all complexity levels, near-flat token consumption despite accumulating interaction tokens (under 100k+ tokens), and enabling mid-scale open-weight models to proprietary-level performance.

Conclusion: A hierarchical asynchronous architecture with explicit separation of planning, memory, and verification enables persistent autonomy; OmniAct demonstrates practical viability and improved resilience for long-horizon cyber-physical systems.

Abstract: Building persistent embodied agents in unstructured environments demands unified orchestration of heterogeneous tools spanning both cyber (APIs, IoT) and physical (manipulation, navigation) domains, coupled with autonomous recovery from physical failures that inevitably arise over extended operation. Existing systems treat these as separate problems: VLM-based planners lack a unified cyber-physical action space, agent frameworks accumulate unbounded context that degrades temporal coherence, and VLA policies execute open-loop without detecting their own failures. We argue that persistent autonomy requires not a monolithic model but a hierarchical asynchronous architecture with explicit separation of planning, memory, and verification. To this end, we present OmniAct, a framework integrating a multimodal semantic planner for skill routing across unified action spaces, an adaptive hierarchical memory with event-boundary-driven compression for sub-linear context growth, and an asynchronous visual preemption engine that closes the semantic loop during physical execution. Across 40 real-world long-horizon tasks on two robotic platforms coordinating four IoT devices, OmniAct achieves consistent improvements in end-to-end success across all complexity levels, maintains near-flat token consumption over under 100k+ accumulated interaction tokens, and elevates mid-scale open-weight models to proprietary-level performance.

</details>


### [216] [E-TTS: A New Embodied Test-Time Scaling Framework for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27268)
*Wen Ye,Peiyan Li,Tingyu Yuan,Yuan Xu,Xiangnan Wu,Chaoyang Zhao,Jing Liu,Nianfeng Liu,Yan Huang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: E-TTS is a modular, history-aware test-time scaling framework that unifies reasoning and action scaling for embodied robotic manipulation via closed-loop, feedback-driven iterative refinement with vision-language verifiers, achieving significant performance gains without extra data or retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two gaps in embodied test-time scaling: (1) the underexplored scaling mechanism for reasoning in policies, and (2) the critical role of historical, long-horizon information which current open-loop TTS methods neglect.

Method: E-TTS introduces a history buffer and modular, plug-and-play components that perform joint reasoning-action sampling and scoring in a pairwise manner. It uses vision-language verifiers to assess candidates, and a feedback-driven closed-loop refinement process to generate new samples, enabling iterative improvement. Each component is independently configurable to suit task requirements.

Result: Evaluated across 4 benchmarks, 6 environments, 3 embodiments, and 4 base vision-language-action models, E-TTS consistently improves performance without extra data collection or retraining, achieving up to 33.14% gains in simulation and 26.62% in real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: E-TTS provides a flexible, history-aware framework for embodied test-time scaling that jointly considers reasoning and action, leverages historical context to enhance decision quality, and enables efficient, adaptable improvements across diverse tasks with modular components.

Abstract: Recently, a few works have made early attempts to study test-time scaling for embodied tasks. However, two major challenges remain unsolved: (1) reasoning can effectively improve the performance of the policy, but its scaling mechanism has seldom been studied; (2) historical information is essential, as embodied tasks are inherently long-horizon and sequential, making sole reliance on current observations for action scaling inadequate due to the lack of historical context utilization. To address these challenges, we introduce E-TTS, a modular and plug-and-play Embodied Test-Time Scaling framework that unifies reasoning and action scaling for robotic manipulation via history-aware iterative refinement with vision-language verifiers. To support joint reasoning-action scaling, E-TTS performs reasoning-action joint sampling and scoring in a pairwise manner. To better utilize historical information, E-TTS uses a history buffer to store historical context, which is then used by reasoning and action verifiers to evaluate the sampled candidates. Unlike conventional open-loop TTS methods, E-TTS introduces feedback generation into the sampling process to form a closed-loop iterative refinement mechanism, enhancing both inference efficiency and environmental adaptability. Each component functions as an independent and composable module, allowing flexible and adaptive configuration depending on task requirements. To evaluate the advantages of our framework, we conduct experiments across 4 different benchmarks, 6 environments, 3 embodiments, and 4 base vision-language-action models. The experimental results demonstrate that, without requiring additional expert data collection or retraining, E-TTS consistently improves performance, achieving up to a 33.14% increase in simulation and 26.62% in real-world scenarios.

</details>


### [217] [BOWConnect: Parallel Bayesian Optimization over Windows with Learned Local Cost Maps for Sample-Efficient Kinodynamic Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27292)
*Sourav Raxit,Abdullah Al Redwan Newaz,Jose Fuentes,Leonardo Bobadilla*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A bidirectional, parallel kinodynamic motion planner, BOWConnect, uses Bayesian Optimization over Windows as a learning-based steering function within a parallel tree exploration framework to overcome sample inefficiency, dynamic-cost heuristics issues, and narrow-passage difficulties; it achieves 100% success in benchmarks and real-time hardware tests, with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome core limitations of sampling-based planners: (i) sample inefficiency in high-dimensional state spaces, (ii) unreliable cost heuristics under dynamic constraints, and (iii) poor performance in narrow passages, which are often exacerbated by random control sampling and purely geometric heuristics.

Method: Introduce Bayesian Optimization over Windows (BOW) as a learning-based steering function embedded in a parallel, bidirectional tree-based exploration. Each worker learns local cost maps and dynamics constraints to guide sampling toward feasible, collision-free controls. The planner grows forward and backward trees in parallel, uses spatial hashing for fast connection queries, and employs a boundary value problem (BVP) solver to generate kinodynamically consistent bridge trajectories.

Result: Evaluations over ten benchmark environments show 100% success, with the fastest or near-fastest planning times in complex scenarios, including narrow passages and non-convex spaces where other planners fail. Real-world deployment on a ground vehicle and a quadrotor demonstrates real-time planning with no collisions. Open-source code and videos are available at the project site.

Conclusion: BOWConnect effectively addresses the key limitations of classic sampling-based planners by combining learning-based steering with bidirectional, kinodynamic planning, achieving robust, real-time planning across simulation and real hardware, and validating practicality for real-world robotic systems.

Abstract: This paper presents BOWConnect, a bidirectional parallel kinodynamic motion planner that addresses three fundamental limitations of existing sampling-based methods: sample inefficiency in high-dimensional state spaces, unreliable cost heuristics under dynamic constraints, and poor performance in narrow passage environments. Unlike classical planners that rely on random control sampling and geometric distance heuristics, BOWConnect integrates Bayesian Optimization over Windows (BOW) as a learning-based steering function within a parallel tree-based exploration framework, enabling each worker to learn local cost maps and constraints to guide sampling toward dynamically feasible and collision-free controls. A bidirectional architecture simultaneously grows forward and backward trees from the start and goal regions in parallel threads, with a spatial hashing mechanism enabling fast connection queries and a boundary value problem solver generating kinodynamically consistent bridge trajectories. Extensive evaluations across ten benchmark environments demonstrate that BOWConnect achieves 100\% success while delivering the fastest or near-fastest planning time in complex scenarios, including narrow passages and non-convex spaces where state-of-the-art planners fail or degrade substantially. Real-world deployment on a ground vehicle and a quadrotor confirms real-time planning with no collisions. Videos of real-world and simulated experiments, high-resolution versions of the figures, and the open-source code are available at https://bow-connect.github.io/.

</details>


### [218] [LA4VLA: Learning to Act without Seeing via Language-Action Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27295)
*Tao Lin,Yuxin Du,Yiran Mao,Zewei Ye,Yilei Zhong,Bing Cheng,Yiming Wang,Jiting Liu,Yang Tian,Junchi Yan,Feiran Wu,Zenan Meng,Hu Wei,Yuqian Fu,Gen Li,Bo Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LA4VLA introduces language-action pretraining for vision-language-action models to learn language-conditioned action priors without visual inputs, using a large LA dataset and a small VLA model; mixed LA-VLA pretraining yields substantial gains over VLA alone in both simulation and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models often rely on dense visual cues due to sparse language-action supervision, causing sensitivity to visual variation and limited language-conditioned action execution. A language-conditioned action prior that operates without visual observations can improve generalization and robustness.

Method: Decompose expert demonstrations into atomic action segments and pair each segment with a corresponding low-level action description to create LA4-33K, a dataset of 33K Language-Action episodes derived from existing demonstrations. Train LA4VLA-1B, a lightweight 1B-parameter VLA model, under three paradigms: LA-only pretraining, sequential LA-to-VLA pretraining, and mixed LA-VLA pretraining to fuse language-action and vision-language-action signals.

Result: LA-pretrained policies outperform matched VLA-pretrained counterparts. Combining LA and VLA supervision yields further gains. Mixed LA-VLA pretraining improves the average success rate of LA4VLA-1B over the no-pretraining baseline by up to 17.8 percentage points in simulation and 45.0 percentage points in real-world tasks.

Conclusion: LA4VLA provides an effective and complementary pretraining strategy for robust VLA policies, enabling language-conditioned action priors that generalize across tasks and scenes while reducing reliance on scene-specific visual cues.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are commonly pretrained on robot demonstrations by jointly mapping visual observations and language instructions to actions. However, dense visual-action supervision can dominate the comparatively sparse language-action signal. As a result, policies may rely on visual shortcuts rather than learn how language conditions action execution, making them sensitive to visual variations. To address this limitation, we propose LA4VLA, a language-action pretraining framework that enables policies to acquire language-conditioned action priors without visual observations. These priors capture reusable manipulation skills shared across tasks and scenes, reducing reliance on scene-specific visual cues. Specifically, LA4VLA decomposes expert demonstration trajectories into atomic action segments and pairs each segment with a corresponding low-level action description. This yields LA4-33K, a dataset of 33K Language-Action (LA) episodes derived entirely from existing demonstrations without additional robot data collection. We further develop LA4VLA-1B, a lightweight 1B-parameter VLA model, and investigate three paradigms for incorporating language-action supervision into VLA learning: LA-only pretraining, sequential LA-to-VLA pretraining, and mixed LA-VLA pretraining. Across simulation and real-world tasks, LA-pretrained policies consistently outperform matched VLA-pretrained counterparts, while combining LA and VLA supervision leads to further gains. In particular, mixed LA-VLA pretraining improves the average success rate of LA4VLA-1B over the no-pretraining baseline by up to 17.8 and 45.0 percentage points in simulation and real-world tasks, respectively. These results establish LA4VLA as an effective and complementary pretraining strategy for building stronger and more robust VLA policies.

</details>


### [219] [VibeAct: Vibration to Actions for Contact-Rich Reactive Robot Dexterity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27344)
*Yuemin Mao,Uksang Yoo,Jean Oh,Jonathan Francis,Jeffrey Ichnowski*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VibeAct decouples vibro-acoustic sensing from raw audio to enable sim-to-real RL for dexterous manipulation by learning a tactile representation from real microphone data and using it in simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dexterous dexterous manipulation requires fast, local contact information; high-bandwidth vibro-acoustic sensing is hard to simulate faithfully, hindering end-to-end sim-to-real RL.

Method: Embed piezo microphones in a dexterous hand to collect vibro-acoustic data via teleoperation; replay recordings in a calibrated digital clone to label per-finger contact/slip; train a tactile estimator to map waveforms to contact/slip; train manipulation policies in simulation using a representation derived from simulated contacts; then transfer policies to a real hand-arm platform.

Result: Outperforms a proprioception-and-point-cloud baseline across five contact-rich tasks; largest gains on tasks requiring sustained reactive control; continuous slip-magnitude channel provides informative observations; policies transfer to a physical hand-arm platform with improved success rates.

Conclusion: A shared physical representation of contact and slip bridges real vibro-acoustic sensing and simulation-based RL, enabling rapid tactile feedback for dexterous manipulation and promising transfer to real robots.

Abstract: Dexterous manipulation depends on contact events that are fast, local, and often visually occluded. Piezoelectric microphones offer a compact and high-bandwidth way to sense these interactions, but the resulting vibro-acoustic signals are difficult to simulate faithfully enough for end-to-end sim-to-real policy learning on dexterous robot hands. We propose VibeAct, a framework that bridges real vibrotactile sensing and simulation-based reinforcement learning through a shared physical representation of contact and slip. In the real world, we embed piezoelectric microphones into a dexterous robot hand and collect vibro-acoustic data through teleoperation, then replay the recordings in a calibrated digital clone to automatically label per-finger contact and slip. A tactile estimator learns to predict contact and slip from real microphone waveforms, while manipulation policies are trained in simulation on the same representation computed directly from simulated contacts. This decoupling lets policies exploit rapid tactile feedback without simulating raw audio. Across five contact-rich tasks spanning regrasping, in-hand reorientation, and insertion, VibeAct consistently outperforms a proprioception-and-point-cloud baseline in simulation, with the largest gains on tasks requiring sustained reactive control, where the continuous slip-magnitude channel proves the most informative observation. The learned policies transfer to a physical dexterous hand-arm platform, improving success rates on deployed tasks. Project videos and additional details are at https://vibeact.github.io/.

</details>


### [220] [Bridging Performance and Generalization in Reinforcement Learning for Agile Flight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27348)
*Jonathan Green,Jiaxu Xing,Nico Messikommer,Angel Romero,Davide Scaramuzza*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a zero-shot generalization framework for RL-based drone racing using task-aware switching guided by learning progress and a physically informed procedural track generator; achieves 7.4x generalization gains without test-time adaptation, validated in simulation and real-world including a vision-based end-to-end control setting where prior methods fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot generalization in high-speed aerial robotics is intrinsically difficult due to high task variability and the tight coupling of safety and performance; existing RL generalization methods uncouple cost-to-train vs speed, often sacrificing speed to generalize.

Method: Combine task-aware switching based on learning progress with a physically informed procedural track generator to train a fast, robust generalist policy; no test-time adaptation; aims for broad zero-shot transfer to unseen racetracks; includes vision-based end-to-end control.

Result: 7.4x improvement in generalization over state-of-the-art approaches; strong zero-shot performance across unseen racetracks in real world; maintains competitive racing speeds; validated in both simulation and real-world settings, including a challenging vision-based end-to-end control without explicit state estimation where prior approaches fail to generalize.

Conclusion: The framework enables fast, robust zero-shot generalization for RL-based drone racing without test-time adaptation, broadens generalization to unseen racetracks while preserving speed, and demonstrates practical viability in real-world and vision-based control scenarios.

Abstract: Autonomous drone racing is a fundamentally challenging regime for autonomous aerial robots, requiring time-optimal control while operating under persistent actuation saturation. While reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved human-level performance in this domain, current methods fail to generalize; policies trained on specific environments often crash immediately in unseen configurations. This failure reflects the intrinsic difficulty of zero-shot generalization in agile flight, arising from high-dimensional task variation and the tight coupling between safety and performance at high speeds. Existing approaches that improve generalization impose a substantial cost on flight speed: control policies must significantly degrade performance to achieve even modest levels of generalization. In this work, we propose a framework for zero-shot generalization in agile flight for RL-based drone racing. By combining task-aware switching based on learning progress with a physically informed procedural track generator, the framework produces a fast and robust generalist policy without test-time adaptation. Our method achieves strong zero-shot performance across a wide range of unseen racetracks in the real world, demonstrating a 7.4x improvement in generalization over the state-of-the-art approaches, while maintaining competitive racing speeds. We validate our method's results in both simulation and real-world settings, including a challenging vision-based, end-to-end control setting that operates without explicit state estimation, where all prior approaches fail to generalize.

</details>


### [221] [Continual Robot Policy Learning via Variational Neural Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27353)
*Jiaxu Xing,Zhiyuan Zhu,Yunfan Ren,Ismail Geles,Yifan Zhai,Rudolf Reiter,Davide Scaramuzza*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A continual learning framework that models unobserved, recurring dynamics with a physics-informed residual and latent condition encoder, enabling policy adaptation via differentiable simulation and online inference, yielding faster recovery and improved robustness on real quadrotor experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots experience changing dynamics in the real world; fixed learning models fail to exploit deployment data for improvement; need continual, condition-aware adaptation to unseen disturbances.

Method: Learn a condition-aware dynamics model by combining analytical physics priors with a neural residual for unmodeled effects. Use a recurrent encoder to infer hidden conditions from recent interactions; condition both residual model and policy; train policies in differentiable simulation with diverse dynamics sampled from latent model; at deployment, infer conditions online and substitute into the policy/dynamics to adapt without re-fitting residuals.

Result: Extensive simulations and real-world tests show improved policy performance under diverse disturbances. Real quadrotor wind changes: recovery in ~1s (about 5x faster than online residual re-fitting). Large-disturbance hover and tracking errors reduced by 65.7% and 53.3% over state-of-the-art online adaptation.

Conclusion: The approach enables rapid recovery and robustness to recurring, hidden dynamics by recognizing and leveraging deployment experience through a latent-conditioned, physics-informed model and differentiable simulation; practical for real robots.

Abstract: Robots deployed in the real world rarely operate under a single fixed dynamics model: wind changes, payloads vary, batteries drain, contacts shift, and hardware wears. Yet most learning-based controllers are trained once and deployed as if learning were complete. This prevents the robot from using deployment experience to further improve task performance. In this work, we propose a continual learning framework that uses real-world experience to improve robot policies under hidden and recurring dynamics. Our method learns a condition-aware dynamics model from real state-action trajectories by combining an analytical physics prior with a neural residual for unmodeled effects. A recurrent encoder infers the current hidden condition from recent interaction, and this estimate conditions both the residual model and the policy. Policy learning is performed via differentiable simulation using diverse learned dynamics sampled from the latent model. At deployment, these sampled conditions are replaced by conditions inferred online from recent real interaction, allowing the policy to recover recurring dynamics by recognition rather than residual re-fitting. Through extensive simulation studies and real-world experiments, we demonstrate that the framework improves policy performance under diverse unobserved disturbances. On real quadrotor trajectory tracking under changing wind, the policy recovers from recurring disturbances in roughly 1s, about 5x faster than online residual re-fitting. It also reduces large-disturbance hover and tracking errors by 65.7% and 53.3% over the state-of-the-art online adaptation approaches

</details>


### [222] [RouterVLA: Turning Smoke Tests into Supervision for Heterogeneous VLA Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27355)
*Xingyu Ren,Chugang Yi,Ge Ma,Youran Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Outcome-disjoint cross-fitting with pre-deployment probes enables policy selection; a simple probe-based rule matches learned scorers under scalar profiles; reusing scored trials inflates gains; credible routing requires outcome separation and scales with model improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether pre-deployment evaluation rollouts can supervise policy selection and quantify risks of evaluation leakage; evaluate the benefit and limitations of outcome-separated routing and the role of model scaling.

Method: Apply outcome-disjoint cross-fitting: build a probe-based profile for each frozen expert from probes; score a separate trial for the selected expert without using its profile; analyze 34,752 LIBERO-Plus rollout records; compare probe-based rules with learned scorers; examine effects of reusing scored trials and scaling.

Result: Probe-success rule raises held-out success from 0.4686 to 0.6149 (increase of 0.1463). Under scalar-only profiles, learned scorers are statistically indistinguishable from the rule. Reusing the scored trial inflates measured gain by about 1.87x. Model scaling improves individual policies; commissioning-aware routing improves the overall system.

Conclusion:  Credible ledger (outcome-separated) routing is essential to avoid leakage and to realize routing gains; the value in routing largely derives from commissioning; scaling helps individual policies; integrating commissioning-aware routing yields system improvements.

Abstract: We study whether pre-deployment evaluation rollouts can be reused to supervise policy selection. Robot teams routinely smoke test candidate vision-language-action (VLA) policies, then compress those trials into a global winner. RouterVLA evaluates this idea with outcome-disjoint cross-fitting: recorded probes build a profile for each frozen expert, and a separate trial scores the selected expert without entering its profile. Across 34,752 LIBERO-Plus rollout records, a transparent probe-success rule raises held-out success from 0.4686 to 0.6149, a +14.64pp gain. Under the scalar-only profiles studied here, learned scorers are statistically indistinguishable from this rule, showing that commissioning carries the routing value while extra scalar scorer capacity does not create it. Reusing the scored trial inflates the measured gain by $1.87\times$, so credible ledger routing needs outcome separation; model scaling improves individual policies, while commissioning-aware routing improves the system built from them.

</details>


### [223] [World Action Models Enable Continual Imitation Learning with Recurrent Generative Replays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27374)
*Manish Kumar Govind,Dominick Reilly,Smit Patel,Hieu Le,Srijan Das*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: REGEN leverages World Action Models to generate pseudo-replay trajectories for continual imitation learning, reducing forgetting without storing demonstrations and approaching privileged experience replay, with identified bottlenecks in long-horizon visuals and action-observation consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable continual robot learning from past tasks without storing human demonstrations by exploiting generative world models to replay past experiences.

Method: REGEN recursively queries a World Action Model to synthesize pseudo-replay trajectories conditioned on prior task instructions and current-task observations, enabling rehearsal of previously learned tasks during continual adaptation.

Result: SIGNIFICANT reduction of catastrophic forgetting (up to 50% relative to sequential fine-tuning) and performance close to privileged replay methods that use real replay data; validated in both simulation and real-world manipulation; analysis identifies long-horizon visual degradation and action-observation inconsistency as main bottlenecks.

Conclusion: World Action Models offer a promising foundation for continual robot learning without stored demonstrations, though challenges in generated replay quality—especially over long horizons and with tight action-observation alignment—remain.

Abstract: Going beyond predicting robot actions, World Action Models (WAMs) can also generate future visual observations. We build on this generative capability to propose Recurrent Generative Replay (REGEN), a continual imitation learning framework that synthesizes pseudo-replay trajectories, enabling a robot policy to rehearse previously learned tasks without storing their original human demonstrations. During continual adaptation, REGEN recursively queries the WAM to synthesize pseudo-replay trajectories conditioned only on prior task instructions and current-task observations. Experiments in both simulation and real-world manipulation settings show that REGEN reduces catastrophic forgetting by up to $50\%$ relative to sequential fine-tuning, while approaching the performance of privileged experience replay methods that require access to real replay data. Finally, we analyze the factors limiting generated replay, identifying long-horizon visual degradation and action-observation inconsistency as the primary bottlenecks. Our results establish WAMs as a promising foundation for continual robot learning without stored demonstrations.

</details>


### [224] [Scalable Behavior Cloning with Open Data, Training, and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27375)
*Arthur Allshire,Himanshu Gaurav Singh,Ritvik Singh,Adam Rashid,Hongsuk Choi,David McAllister,Justin Yu,Yiyuan Chen,Huang Huang,Pieter Abbeel,Xi Chen,Rocky Duan,Phillip Isola,Jitendra Malik,Fred Shentu,Guanya Shi,Philipp Wu,Angjoo Kanazawa*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces ABC, an open-source manipulation stack centered on ABC-130K: the largest open-source teleoperation dataset (3,500 hours, 130K episodes, 195 tasks), with open hardware, training infra, and simulation pipeline; includes 400 hours of sim-teleop data, a sim-to-real co-training proxy, and evaluations of Diffusion Transformer and Vision-Language-Action models on dexterous tasks, yielding a reproducible toolkit for behavior cloning research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Democratize manipulation learning and enable robust, reusable baselines by providing an open, end-to-end stack (data, hardware, training, simulation) and a practical proxy for ablations, reducing real-world evaluation cost and facilitating community-driven evaluation of model designs and training recipes.

Method: Assemble and release ABC-130K teleoperation dataset (3,500 hours, 130K episodes, 195 tasks); publish 400 hours of sim-teleop data; provide accessible hardware, training infrastructure, and simulation pipeline; propose a co-training recipe linking simulated and real-world evaluation; experiment with various training recipes and compare Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model architectures; validate policies on real-world dexterous tasks (e.g., box folding, card extraction).

Result: Demonstrates that the policies can perform complex dexterous tasks in the real world; establishes a widely accessible, reproducible toolkit and dataset that can serve as a common baseline for behavior-cloning research; identifies practical training recipes and architecture choices grounded in real-world evaluation.

Conclusion: Provides a foundation for community-driven advancement in behavior cloning by lowering entry barriers, enabling systematic ablations, and promoting shared standards through an open, end-to-end ABC stack.

Abstract: We introduce ABC, a fully open-source stack for manipulation with behavior cloning. At its core is ABC-130K: the largest open-source teleoperation dataset to date, featuring 3,500 hours of data spanning over 130K episodes across 195 diverse tasks. Furthermore, we open-source our accessible hardware setup, training infrastructure, and simulation pipeline. We also release 400 hours of sim-teleop data and provide a co-training recipe that produces correlated simulation and real-world evaluation, offering a reliable proxy for ablating model-design and training decisions before costly real-world evaluation. We explore various training recipes and compare common architectural choices for Diffusion Transformers (DiT) and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, grounding our findings in real-world evaluations. The resulting policies successfully execute dexterous tasks such as box folding and extracting credit cards from wallets. By providing a reproducible toolkit, we aim to place researchers on an equal footing, establishing the necessary foundation to learn the ABCs of Behavior Cloning together as a community.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [225] [Physics-guided Convolutional Neural Network for Domain Growth Prediction in Systems with Conserved Kinetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26128)
*Vijay Yadav,Madhu Priya,Manish Dev Shrimali,Prabhat K. Jaiswal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An attention-based, physics-guided CNN surrogate accurately learns long-time phase-field evolution under Cahn–Hilliard dynamics, preserving composition and domain-growth behavior, consistent with Lifshitz–Slyozov law.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide efficient, stable surrogates for computationally expensive nonlinear PDE solvers describing spatiotemporal evolution, ensuring conservation and correct domain-growth scaling.

Method: An attention-enabled, physics-guided convolutional neural network trained to predict full time-evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn–Hilliard equation, incorporating physics constraints (conservation, domain-growth consistency).

Result: The surrogate yields stable, accurate long-time rollouts for both critical and off-critical mixtures, preserves overall mixture composition, and captures domain growth in agreement with Lifshitz–Slyozov scaling.

Conclusion: The framework effectively models conserved-kinetics dynamical systems and is extensible to other complex spatiotemporal dynamics.

Abstract: The spatiotemporal evolution of many physical, chemical, and biological systems is described by nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs). Recently, deep neural network-based surrogate models have gained increasing interest as efficient alternatives to computationally expensive traditional numerical solvers. In this work, we propose an attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network as a surrogate model to learn the microstructural evolution of such systems. We train the model to accurately predict the full time-evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation.
