We show that predictions from our trained surrogate model remain stable and accurate over long-time rollouts for both critical and off-critical mixtures and preserve the mixture composition throughout evolution. We also show that our model accurately captures the growth of domain size and is consistent with the Lifshitz-Slyozov domain-growth law. The prediction results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for modeling systems with conserved kinetics and can be extended to other complex dynamical systems.

</details>


### [226] [\chisao{}: A GPU-Native Parallel Optimizer for Multimodal Black-Box Functions via Convergence-Anticonvergence Oscillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26164)
*Ira Wolfson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GPU-native population optimizer CHISAO uses convergence–anticonvergence oscillation to find all modes of multimodal black-box functions, achieving perfect mode recovery with large speedups on a 42-function benchmark suite; robust to noise; derivative-free in practice and open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the sequential bottlenecks of existing multimodal optimizers (basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart GD) and to exploit massive GPU parallelism for simultaneous exploration and robust mode detection.

Method: A GPU-native population optimizer that processes an entire sample batch in parallel and employs a convergence–anticonvergence cycle: true peaks are frozen (stuck) while the rest explore via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding maintains diversity through Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster. Objective evaluation is value-based with finite-difference gradients.

Result: On 42 functions of the SFU benchmark across dimensions d in {2,4,8,16,32,64}, CHISAO achieves 100% mode recovery where CPU baselines fail at d≥8 on hardest functions, up to 34× speedup over basin-hopping on problems where others succeed (e.g., Michalewicz d=64), and up to 39× on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid d=64). All benchmarks are derivative-free in the worst case; robustness to noise with σ_noise up to 1.0 is maintained. The implementation is available as open-source Python on PyPI.

Conclusion: CHISAO demonstrates strong, scalable, and noise-robust mode detection for multimodal black-box functions on GPUs, delivering substantial speedups and full mode recovery across a broad d-range; its open-source availability facilitates adoption and further benchmarking.

Abstract: Finding all modes of a multimodal black-box function is a fundamental challenge in optimization, Bayesian inference, and scientific computing. Existing approaches -- basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent -- operate sequentially and cannot exploit the massive parallelism of modern GPU hardware. We introduce \chisao{} (\textbf{C}onvergence-\textbf{H}alt-\textbf{I}nvert-\textbf{S}tick-\textbf{A}nd-\textbf{O}scillate), a GPU-native population optimizer that runs an entire sample batch simultaneously and exploits a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes. The structural move is asymmetric: samples that reach true peaks are frozen (``stuck'') and preserved, while the rest keep exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via two complementary strategies (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster) maintains population diversity throughout. On all 42 functions of the Simon Fraser University optimization benchmark suite across dimensions $d \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$, \chisao{} achieves \textbf{100\%} mode recovery where all CPU baselines collapse at $d \geq 8$ on the hardest multimodal functions, at up to \textbf{$34\times$} speedup over basin-hopping on functions where all methods succeed (Michalewicz $d=64$) and up to \textbf{$39\times$} on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid $d=64$, pure GPU dividend). All benchmarks evaluate the objective by value alone -- gradients come from finite differences -- so the reported speedups are a derivative-free worst case. Under substantial likelihood noise ($σ_{\mathrm{noise}}$ up to 1.0), mode detection remains 100\% reliable. The algorithm is available as a standalone open-source Python package on PyPI.

</details>


### [227] [Implementation of reinforcement learning in chemical reaction networks: application to phototaxis as curiosity-driven exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26168)
*Ruyi Tang,Grégoire Sergeant-Perthuis,David Colliaux*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframes unicellular phototaxis as an information-driven sensorimotor process using a POMDP framework linked to CRN-ODE biochemistry; IRL on experimental data shows run–tumble acts as information gathering to reduce sensory ambiguity, reproducing light-alignment patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional phototaxis models (run–tumble) neglect active sampling and uncertainty management. The work aims to unify cognition-inspired information processing with intracellular biochemistry to explain how cells strategically sample their environment.

Method: Modeling environment as hidden variables within a POMDP; internal state updated via memoryless Bayesian step per observation; dynamical implementation through Chemical-Reaction-Network ODEs (CRN-ODEs); explicit biophysical observation model for photoreception; polynomial bound on information gain; parameter inference via Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) on 30 Chlamydomonas trajectories; benchmarking against Stochastic Simulation Algorithm (SSA) baselines.

Result: The model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution with performance comparable to SSA baselines; run–tumble emerges as an information-acquisition strategy that reorients to sample new sensory configurations and resolve ambiguity.

Conclusion: A minimal cognition framework connects information-processing goals with biochemical network dynamics, showing that intracellular networks can support adaptive information-seeking strategies in cellular navigation and that phototaxis can be viewed as purposeful information acquisition.

Abstract: Living systems navigate environments using noisy and incomplete sensory signals. In unicellular algae, phototaxis is often modeled as a mechanistic run--tumble process driven by stimulus--response rules. However, such descriptions overlook how organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity. From a minimal cognition perspective, we reframe this navigation as a subjective, information-driven sensorimotor process. To this end, we propose a framework linking a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with biochemical reaction dynamics. Environmental variables are hidden, while the cell updates a minimal internal state from each observation through a memoryless Bayesian step. These internal dynamics balance orienting toward light with exploratory reorientation and can be implemented through Chemical-Reaction-Network Ordinary Differential Equations (CRN--ODEs). Our model includes a biophysical observation process for photoreception and a chemically computable polynomial bound on information gain. Using Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) on 30 experimentally recorded Chlamydomonas trajectories, we infer the behavioral objective consistent with observed phototactic motion and benchmark the resulting dynamics with standard Stochastic Simulation Algorithm (SSA) baselines. Our model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution, comparable to objective SSA baselines on this dataset. Within this framework, run--tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy: tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve sensor ambiguity, demonstrating how intracellular biochemical networks can support adaptive information-seeking behavior in cellular navigation.

</details>


### [228] [Neural Architecture Search for Generative Adversarial Networks: A Comprehensive Review and Critical Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26169)
*Abrar Alotaibi,Moataz Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A survey of NAS methods for GANs, classifying approaches by search strategy, evaluation metrics, and outcomes; finds evolutionary and gradient-based methods effective, critiques current metrics, and recommends diverse datasets for robust assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate GAN architecture design to improve performance, stability, and efficiency, reducing dependence on manual tuning.

Method: Systematic literature review of NAS-GAN papers; categorize by search strategy (evolutionary, gradient-based, reinforcement learning, random search), evaluation criteria/metrics (IS, FID, alternatives), datasets, and reported results; synthesize trends and limitations.

Result: NAS-GAN methods can enhance GAN performance and stability; certain strategies (evolutionary, gradient-based) show strengths in different contexts; there is a need for metrics beyond traditional IS/FID and more diverse datasets; limitations include computational cost and reproducibility concerns.

Conclusion: Provide guidance for designing robust NAS-GAN pipelines: adopt diverse evaluation metrics, benchmark on varied datasets, compare across methods fairly, favor scalable search strategies, and report standard protocols.

Abstract: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has emerged as a pivotal technique in optimizing the design of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), automating the search for effective architectures while addressing the challenges inherent in manual design. This paper provides a comprehensive review of NAS methods applied to GANs, categorizing and comparing various approaches based on criteria such as search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance outcomes. The review highlights the benefits of NAS in improving GAN performance, stability, and efficiency, while also identifying limitations and areas for future research. Key findings include the superiority of evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods in certain contexts, the importance of robust evaluation metrics beyond traditional scores like Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and the need for diverse datasets in assessing GAN performance. By presenting a structured comparison of existing NAS-GAN techniques, this paper aims to guide researchers in developing more effective NAS methods and advancing the field of GANs.

</details>


### [229] [KG-TRACE: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Mechanistic Grounding in Antimicrobial Resistance Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26179)
*Naman Garg,Sarika Jain,Sourav Yadav,Bharat K. Bhargava,Ghanapriya Singh,Abhishek Srivastava,Parimal Kar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KG-TRACE is a neuro-symbolic framework that grounds a neural genomic AMR predictor in the WHO mutation knowledge graph using a learned epistemic trust gate, achieving strong AUROC for isoniazid on CRyPTIC while prioritizing symbolic grounding for clinical trust; it introduces a dataset-level Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR) and flags uncertain cases to aid follow-up.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of mechanistic grounding in neural AMR predictors and enhance clinical trust by aligning neural attributions with established biology through a structured KG and Epistemic trust mechanism.

Method: A neuro-symbolic model that fuses genomic features with RotE-based KG embeddings via a learned epistemic trust gate, integrating the WHO mutation KG as a symbolic constraint on a neural genomic model.

Result: AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid on the CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort; symbolic grounding ratio (BGR) of 92.5% for isoniazid-resistant predictions; ability to flag UNCERTAIN cases to identify MDR co-occurrence artifacts; improved auditability without sacrificing competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: Neuro-symbolic grounding provides verifiable interpretability and an audit trail for clinicians, achieving strong predictive performance while ensuring alignment with established biology and enabling actionable laboratory follow-up for ambiguous predictions.

Abstract: While WGS-based AMR prediction has reached high accuracy, existing models lack a mechanism to ground neural attributions in established biological pathways. We present KG-TRACE, a novel neuro-symbolic framework that integrates the WHO mutation knowledge graph (KG) as a structured biological constraint on a neural genomic model. Unlike existing methods that learn statistical patterns in isolation, KG-TRACE fuses genomic features and RotatE-based KG embeddings through a learned epistemic trust gate, dynamically weighting neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge.
  Evaluated on the CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort, KG-TRACE achieves an AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid, achieving competitive accuracy while its primary value lies in symbolic grounding, not predictive uplift. More importantly, we introduce the Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR), a dataset-level metric that quantifies alignment between neural attributions and established biology. Our framework achieves a 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions and effectively identifies MDR co-occurrence artifacts by issuing laboratory follow-up flags for 'UNCERTAIN' cases. We demonstrate that neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust.

</details>


### [230] [Necessary but Not Sufficient: Temperature Control and Reproducibility in LLM-as-Judge Safety Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26185)
*Hiroki Tamba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grader temperature defaults cause non-deterministic safety verdicts; forcing deterministic decoding (temp=0) only partly mitigates; need to report grader disagreement as a health metric and use a reproduction harness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess reliability of LLM-as-judge safety evaluations and expose that single-run verdicts can misrepresent safety properties due to nondeterminism in LLM grading.

Method: Empirical analysis using Japan AISI's open-source aisev harness across 690 API calls, two providers, three model tiers, and five sampling configurations. Examined behavior with and without temperature settings, including forced greedy decoding (top_k=1), and evaluated reproducibility of pass/fail verdicts for items near the decision boundary.

Result: Found that default grading without explicit seed/temperature uses provider default (temp ~1.0), causing per-item pass/fail flips across identical runs (up to ~50% disagreement over 20 runs). Pinning temp=0 reduces but does not eliminate flips: across 690 calls, 1-2 of 7 borderline items remain non-reproducible even with top_k=1. Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 has deprecated temperature, removing the primary mitigation. Demonstrates structural gap where single-run verdicts misrepresent safety properties; provides a 690-call reproduction harness and recommends reporting grader disagreement as a health metric alongside scores.

Conclusion: Evaluation harnesses should report grader disagreement and variance as first-class health metrics; adopt reproduction harnesses and guardrails to ensure reproducibility; acknowledge that model-generation changes (e.g., deprecation of temperature) can undermine traditional debugging mitigations.

Abstract: LLM-as-judge ("grader") components are now standard in evaluation harnesses, including safety evaluations where a pass/fail verdict may gate downstream deployment decisions. A widespread assumption is that setting the grader's sampling temperature to 0 makes grading deterministic. We test this assumption against a real safety-evaluation codebase (Japan AISI's open-source aisev) and show it fails on two levels. First, the harness invokes its grader without setting temperature or seed; the underlying provider silently applies its default of 1.0, so items near the decision boundary flip pass/fail across identical runs (per-item disagreement up to ~50% over 20 runs). Second, pinning temperature=0 reduces but does not eliminate flips: across 690 API calls spanning two providers, three model tiers, and five sampling configurations, 1-2 of 7 borderline items remain non-reproducible even under forced greedy decoding (top_k=1). Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 has since deprecated temperature entirely, rendering the primary mitigation inapplicable to newer model generations. These findings expose a structural gap: evaluation harnesses that report single-run verdicts without variance or grader-disagreement metrics can present noise as a safety property. We release a reproduction harness (690 calls, 7 conditions) and recommend that harnesses treat grader disagreement as a first-class health metric alongside the scores themselves.

</details>


### [231] [Clue-Guided Money Laundering Group Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26189)
*Boyang Wang,Jianing Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Clue-Guided Group Discovery (CGGD) and Clue2Group introduce a clue-driven, interactive framework for progressively uncovering laundering groups from initial clues in large financial networks, using a compact local context and a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN to integrate risk, structure, and priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based AML methods either flag node-level risk or search globally for suspicious groups, which poorly match real investigations that start from specific clues. There is a need for a clue-driven, progressive group discovery approach that preserves chain-like and cycle-like laundering structures while reducing noise.

Method: CGGD framework enables analyst-guided, iterative group recovery from an initial clue set. Clue2Group constructs a compact local investigation context, estimates a clue-conditioned local risk field with a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN, and integrates risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover a coherent laundering group.

Result: Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks show feasibility and practicality of the clue-driven analysis framework, indicating it can bridge graph-based AML research with real investigation workflows and support progressive group recovery.

Conclusion: Clue2Group provides a practical, scalable clue-guided approach for AML investigations, enabling analyst-driven progression from clues to complete laundering groups and aligning research with real-world workflows.

Abstract: Money Laundering Group Discovery (MLGD) aims to identify hidden criminal groups and recover their complete structures in large-scale financial networks. Existing graph anomaly detection methods mainly produce node-level risk alerts, while global group discovery methods passively search for suspicious groups over the whole network. Both are mismatched with real Anti-money-laundering (AML) investigations, where analysts usually start from a concrete clue and gradually expand the investigation to recover the responsible group. To address this gap, we propose Clue-Guided Group Discovery (CGGD), where a laundering group is progressively recovered from an initial clue set through analyst interaction. We further propose Clue2Group, a framework that first constructs a compact local investigation context to reduce noise and preserve chain-like and cycle-like laundering structures. It then estimates a clue-conditioned local risk field with a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN, and finally integrates risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover a coherent laundering group. Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks show that Clue2Group provides a practical clue-driven analysis framework for AML investigations, offering a feasible step toward bridging the gap between graph-based AML research and real investigation workflows.

</details>


### [232] [Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26192)
*Jialan He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FHPLF introduces a federated hash learning framework that uses binary gradient-like matrices, projected Hamming distance for similarity modeling, and a privacy-enhanced upload scheme to reduce communication/computation while improving accuracy and privacy in FL-based hash learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address regulatory data privacy concerns and high communication overhead in federated learning for hash learning, while overcoming the limited representational capacity of binary codes.

Method: Replace real-valued gradient matrices with binary gradient-like matrices; apply Projected Hamming Distance to model similarity and emphasize bit-wise importance; deploy a Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) protocol to minimize interaction leakage during transmission in FL.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four real-world datasets shows FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL baselines, achieving a favorable balance among accuracy, efficiency, and privacy.

Conclusion: FHPLF effectively enhances HL in federated settings by reducing resource costs and strengthening privacy protections without sacrificing performance, suggesting its potential for scalable, privacy-preserving distributed hash learning.

Abstract: Hash Learning (HL) is an efficient representation learning approach that maps real-valued data into compact binary representations. Traditional HL methods typically require users to upload personal data to a central server, which is incompatible with increasingly stringent data security regulations. Federated Learning (FL) provides a decentralized paradigm for learning globally optimal models without centralizing private data. However, most FL methods rely on transmitting large-scale real-valued gradient information, leading to high communication overhead and potential privacy risks. Integrating HL into FL is a promising solution. Nevertheless, existing HL methods suffer from limited representational capacity of binary codes, which may degrade model accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor (FHPLF) model. FHPLF introduces three key innovations: (a) replacing real-valued gradient matrices with binary gradient-like matrices, significantly reducing computation, storage, and communication costs while enhancing privacy protection; (b) leveraging Projected Hamming Distance for similarity modeling, which captures the importance of individual binary bits to improve representation capability; and (c) proposing a Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) strategy to further reduce the risk of user interaction leakage during transmission. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL methods, achieving a favorable trade-off among accuracy, efficiency, and privacy preservation.

</details>


### [233] [Statistical and Structural Approaches to Algorithmic Fairness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26200)
*Antonio Ferrara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The abstract critiques current algorithmic fairness as relying on deterministic audits and decontextualized individuals, identifying two key limitations and outlining directions to address them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML systems increasingly mediate access to economic and social opportunities and reflect existing structural inequalities; there is a need for fairness methods that account for contextual and structural factors rather than only predictive accuracy.

Method: Theoretical analysis and critique of existing fairness paradigms; conceptual framework development to incorporate non-deterministic auditing and structural context for individuals.

Result: No empirical results are reported; the contribution is a conceptual identification of two core limitations and proposed directions for future work.

Conclusion: Advancing fairness requires moving beyond deterministic point estimates and isolated individuals toward auditing and interventions that recognize structural context and the socio-technical nature of systems.

Abstract: Modern machine learning systems have outgrown their origins as isolated predictive constructs, evolving into complex socio-technical architectures that actively mediate human opportunity. As algorithms increasingly determine access to economic and social opportunities, it has become widely recognized that these systems are deeply embedded with the structural inequalities and prejudices of their environments. The field of algorithmic fairness emerged in response to the growing recognition that models optimized for predictive accuracy can systematically disadvantage marginalized groups. Early mitigation strategies, however, rested on fragile simplifications that limited their effectiveness in complex socio-technical environments. This thesis identifies and addresses two fundamental limitations of contemporary fairness paradigms: the reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and the treatment of individuals as isolated entities devoid of structural context.

</details>


### [234] [Topology-Informed Neural Networks for Flood Detection in Optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26204)
*Sophia Li,Max Zhao,Raghu G. Raj,Tianyu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Topological descriptors derived from satellite imagery can independently signal floods and, when integrated with neural networks, improve robustness and interpretability of flood detection on the SEN12-FLOOD dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limited interpretability of black-box flood detection models and the challenge of cloud-contaminated optical imagery, by exploring whether topological data analysis (TDA) can capture global structural flood signals complementary to convolutional architectures.

Method: Extract topological descriptors from each image in SEN12-FLOOD and feed them into neural networks alongside conventional features. Compare performance and interpretability against baselines (per-image CNN features with RNNs, and transformer-based approaches).

Result: Topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and, when combined with existing networks, yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

Conclusion: TDA provides a valuable, interpretable feature set for flood detection that complements deep learning models, suggesting a promising direction for safer, more robust remote sensing analytics; future work should quantify scalability, generalization across sensors, and integration with various NN architectures.

Abstract: Floods frequently impact regions around the world. Rapid and accurate flood detection is crucial for emergency response and timely mitigation of human and economic loss. The expanding availability of satellite data and advances in artificial intelligence have enhanced monitoring of environmental hazards, but many flood events remain challenging to detect because cloud cover obscures optical satellite imagery. Rambour et al. introduced the SEN12-FLOOD dataset and extracted per-image features using a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network backbone, then fed these features into a gated recurrent unit network to show that temporal information can substantially improve accuracy compared to single-image baselines. More recently, Chamatidis et al. showed that a vision transformer can achieve strong performance with popular convolutional architectures. However, these models typically function as opaque black boxes, making it difficult to interpret their decision boundaries, learned features, and internal reasoning, especially in safety-critical domains like remote sensing. In contrast, topological data analysis (TDA) provides a mathematically grounded framework for capturing global structural features of data. TDA has emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing complex imagery, especially imagery with geometrically interpretable structures, of which floods are a prime candidate. In this work, we systematically evaluate topological descriptors for flood detection using the open-source SEN12-FLOOD dataset. By extracting topological features from each image and incorporating them into neural networks, we demonstrate that topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing networks to yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

</details>


### [235] [A General Framework for Learning Algebraic Properties from Cayley Graphs using Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26212)
*Tal Weissblat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends a prior GNN approach to a property-agnostic framework that predicts finite-group properties (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) from Cayley graphs, showing that substantial algebraic information is encoded in these graph representations and that GNNs can extract it as a proof-of-concept for algebraic property learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether algebraic structure of finite groups can be inferred directly from graph representations, not tuned to a single property, by leveraging graph neural networks on Cayley graphs.

Method: Adopts a common GNN architecture and training pipeline; represents finite groups via Cayley graphs (over various generators); trains to predict multiple properties in a property-independent manner; evaluates on groups from several families to assess generalization.

Result: The framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from Cayley graphs, indicating that substantial algebraic information is captured by these graph representations and can be extracted with GNNs.

Conclusion: Graph representation learning is a viable tool for studying algebraic properties of finite groups; the results provide a proof-of-concept and pave the way for extending to additional properties and more complex group-theoretic tasks.

Abstract: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework for predicting the solvability of finite groups from their Cayley graph representations was introduced in [1]. In the present work, we generalize this approach and develop a property-independent framework for learning algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graphs. As representative case studies, we consider abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability. Using a common GNN architecture and training pipeline, we investigate the extent to which algebraic structure can be recovered from graph-based representations alone. Results on a collection of finite groups drawn from several families demonstrate that the framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from their associated Cayley graphs. These findings suggest that substantial algebraic information is encoded in graph representations and can be extracted through GNNs. More broadly, the proposed framework provides a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to the study of algebraic properties of finite groups.

</details>


### [236] [Fast LeWorldModel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26217)
*Yuntian Gao,Xiangyu Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fast-LeWM replaces repeated local one-step latent rollouts with action-prefix prediction, enabling parallel evaluation of action sequences and reducing planning time while keeping or improving latent accuracy across horizons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LeWM's planning is costly due to autoregressive, one-step latent transitions that accumulate error over long horizons; a parallel, prefix-based predictor could reduce computation and mitigate error accumulation.

Method: Propose Fast-LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM): given current latent and a candidate action sequence, encode its action prefixes and predict future latents after executing those prefixes in parallel. Use prefix-level supervision so the model learns continuous state evolution under different prefixes. For planning, use the last prefix token to evaluate the corresponding future latent without stepping through intermediate states.

Result: Fast-LeWM achieves higher average success than LeWM across tasks, substantially reduces planning time, and attains a lower open-loop latent loss whose growth with horizon is slower.

Conclusion: Prefix-level latent prediction provides a scalable, reconstruction-free world model for visual planning, reducing computational cost and mitigating error accumulation in long-horizon planning compared to traditional autoregressive rollouts.

Abstract: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), including recent LeWorldModel (LeWM), have become a promising foundation for reconstruction-free visual world models. For visual planning, however, LeWM evaluates candidate action sequences by repeatedly applying a local one-step latent transition model. This autoregressive rollout makes planning computationally expensive and exposes the predicted trajectory to accumulated latent errors as the horizon grows. We propose Fast LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM), a fast latent world model that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction. Given the current latent and a candidate action sequence, Fast-LeWM encodes its prefixes and predicts the future latents reached after executing those prefixes in parallel. By making action prefixes the basic prediction unit, Fast-LeWM directly models action effects accumulated to different extents over multiple horizons. This prefix-level supervision forces the model to learn how states continuously evolve under different action prefixes, rather than only fitting one-step state transitions. During planning, the predictor can use the last prefix token from the encoded action sequence to evaluate the corresponding future latent without explicitly rolling through each intermediate imagined state. Across multiple tasks, Fast-LeWM improves average success over LeWM while substantially reducing planning time, achieving lower open-loop latent loss whose growth becomes significantly slower as the rollout horizon increases.

</details>


### [237] [Dataset Usage Inference without Shadow Models or Held-out Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26257)
*Wojciech Łapacz,Stanisław Pawlak,Jan Dubiński,Franziska Boenisch,Adam Dziedzic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A practical DUI framework that estimates the fraction of a dataset used to train a model without shadow models or held-out data, using synthetic non-members and mixture proportion estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DUI methods rely on expensive shadow models and access to in-distribution held-out samples known to be absent from training; these requirements hinder application to large models and real data ownership disputes.

Method: Generate synthetic non-member samples; extract diverse membership signals; treat DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to infer dataset usage; does not require shadow models or real held-out data.

Result: On large image generative models, the method reliably quantifies dataset usage, enabling data owners to determine the extent of training data contribution.

Conclusion: The framework provides a practical, scalable DUI approach that overcomes key practical constraints and supports data ownership inquiries.

Abstract: How much of my data was used to train a machine learning model? Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) aims to answer this by estimating what fraction of a dataset contributed to a model's training. However, existing DUI methods rely on assumptions that rarely hold in practice: they require training expensive shadow models to imitate the target model, and they assume access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed to be absent from training. These conditions make current approaches impractical for modern large models and real data ownership disputes. We introduce a practical DUI framework that removes these constraints. Our method requires neither shadow models nor real held-out data. Instead, it generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and casts DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate what share of the candidate dataset was used during training. Experiments on large image generative models show that our method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model.

</details>


### [238] [Equivariance and Augmentation for Bayesian Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26273)
*Miaowen Dong,Axel Flinth,Jan E. Gerken*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: For Bayesian neural networks trained with variational inference, the paper derives exact equivariance conditions for exponential-family variational distributions under data augmentation, provides bounds on equivariance error, introduces three symmetrization methods (notably orbit expansion) that boost equivariance and performance, and demonstrates superior results over baselines; code at github.com/dmw1998/augment-BNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the tension between imposing symmetry constraints (equivariant networks) and learning symmetries from augmentation by characterizing when augmentation yields exact equivariance in BNNs and improve it via symmetrization.

Method: Theoretical analysis of variational distributions in the exponential family under augmentation; derive necessary and sufficient conditions for exact equivariance; derive bounds on equivariance error; propose three symmetrization techniques; perform extensive empirical experiments on Bayesian neural networks.

Result: Derived conditions for exact equivariance; provided bounds on equivariance error; demonstrated orbit expansion (one of the three methods) outperforms baseline in equivariance and overall performance; code released.

Conclusion: Data augmentation with the proposed symmetrization techniques can effectively induce equivariance in Bayesian neural networks, offering a practical alternative to architectural equivariance; orbit expansion is particularly effective; code available.

Abstract: Symmetries are important for many deep learning tasks, ranging from applications in the sciences to medical imaging. However, there is an ongoing debate about whether to impose symmetry constraints on the neural network architecture (yielding equivariant neural networks) or learn them from augmented training data. Although equivariant networks are well-studied theoretically, much less is known about data augmentation, since analyzing augmentation requires control over the training dynamics. Inspired by recent results that show that augmented infinite deep ensembles are exactly equivariant, we study data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) trained with variational inference. We focus on variational distributions in the exponential family and derive conditions under which exact equivariance is reached. We furthermore obtain bounds on the equivariance error and introduce three novel symmetrization techniques which boost the effect of data augmentation in this setting. We conduct extensive numerical experiments which show that one of our symmetrization methods (orbit expansion) outperforms the baseline in both equivariance and overall performance. Our code is available at github.com/dmw1998/augment-BNNs

</details>


### [239] [SSM Adapters via Hankel Reduced-order Modeling: Injection Site Determines Task Suitability in Long-Context Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26290)
*Omanshu Thapliyal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapters, a state-space model (SSM) based residual module for PEFT, enabling exact FFT-based parallel scan and matching LoRA efficiency while outperforming LoRA on long-context tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PEFT often targets attention projections; sequential state accumulation tasks may benefit from SSM-based adapters. The work tests whether SSM adapters and injection sites (MLP blocks) improve long-context modeling and how to compare to traditional low-rank methods.

Method: Proposes HRM, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. Uses the system matrix Abar's time-invariance to enable an exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving parity with LoRA in computational cost across context lengths. Evaluated iso-parametrically on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable parameters) against LoRA variants on LongBench tasks (QuALITY, QMSum) and 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) plus enwik8; includes gate analysis to understand recurrence modulation.

Result: HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks: QuALITY +34.8% relative accuracy; QMSum +71.6% relative ROUGE-1. Across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking and character-level LM, HRM shows robust superiority. Gate analysis indicates HRM learns to modulate recurrence effectively, suggesting it as a robust alternative to low-rank adapters for long-context sequence modeling.

Conclusion: SSM-based HRM adapters provide a strong architectural alternative to low-rank PEFT methods for long-context sequence tasks, achieving similar computational cost to LoRA while delivering substantial accuracy gains. The approach benefits from exact FFT-based parallelism and principled initialization, with recurrence modulation emerging as the key mechanism.

Abstract: While parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) typically targets attention projectors, its efficacy for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation remains under-explored. We examine if PEFT for such tasks can benefit from state space model (SSMs) adapters, and if MLP blocks are better injection sites. We introduce Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. By leveraging the time-invariance of the system matrix $\bar{A}$, HRM enables an exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving computational parity with LoRA across all context lengths. In iso-parametric evaluations on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable parameters), HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks, including QuALITY (+34.8\% relative accuracy) and QMSum (+71.6\% relative ROUGE-1). HRM further demonstrates consistent superiority across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) and character-level language modeling (enwik8). Gate analysis reveals that HRM adapters effectively learn to modulate recurrence, providing a robust architectural alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling.

</details>


### [240] [The Red Queen Gödel Machine: Co-Evolving Agents and Their Evaluators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26294)
*Alex Iacob,Andrej Jovanović,William F. Shen,Daniel Burkhardt,Meghdad Kurmanji,Nurbek Tastan,Lorenzo Sani,Niccolò Alberto Elia Venanzi,Ambroise Odonnat,Zeyu Cao,Bill Marino,Xinchi Qiu,Nicholas D. Lane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Red Queen Gödel Machine (RQGM), an evolutionary framework for recursive self-improvement under non-stationary utilities, using epoch-based search and evolving evaluators/adversarial objectives; demonstrates substantial improvements across coding, academic writing, and proof tasks via co-evolution of agents and judges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional self-improving agents optimize under a fixed evaluation criterion, but real environments and objectives evolve. The work aims to integrate evaluation into the improvement loop, enabling non-stationary utilities and more robust progress.

Method: Epoch-based search where within-epoch evaluation is fixed; utilities can be updated at epoch boundaries. Incorporates a complementary agent-as-a-judge code-review signal; co-evolves writers and graders; introduces an adversarial objective to balance AI vs human reviewer standards. Demonstrates across verifiable coding tasks, scientific paper writing/reviewing, and Olympiad-level proof tasks.

Result: RQGM improves over prior SOTA: coding tasks show higher test pass rates with cheaper evaluation (1.35x-1.72x fewer tokens). Co-evolved writers achieve 1.78x-1.86x higher acceptance rates; co-evolved graders achieve 9% higher ground-truth accuracy. In paper reviewing, strongest baseline reviewer over-accepts AI papers by up to 1.91x human rate; RQGM mitigates this via adversarial objective ensuring reviewers are equally stringent on AI and human work.

Conclusion: Non-stationary utility evolution is viable for recursive self-improvement. Epoch-based guarantees enable progress under changing objectives, with broad applicability to coding, writing, and proofs. Future work could address safety, alignment, and efficiency of evolving evaluators.

Abstract: Self-improving agents are state-of-the-art (SOTA) on agentic coding benchmarks and have recently been extended to general domains. However, their search methods generally assume a stationary evaluation criterion: a fixed verifier, benchmark, or labeled dataset that remains valid as the agent improves. This ignores a central feature of evolution: species adapt as their environments change with them. We aim to bring the same principle to recursive self-improvement, making evaluation part of the improvement loop and opening search to evolving evaluators, adversarial objectives, and dynamic utilities that may surpass static benchmarks. We introduce the Red Queen Godel Machine (RQGM), an evolutionary framework for recursive self-improvement under non-stationary utilities. The RQGM makes this possible through controlled utility evolution: search is organized into epochs with a fixed within-epoch evaluation criterion, while the utility can be updated at epoch boundaries, so self-improvement guarantees hold per epoch as the objective evolves across them. We begin by showing that even on verifiable coding tasks, the RQGM improves test pass rate over the prior SOTA by adding a complementary agent-as-a-judge code-review signal. This signal is cheaper and the RQGM uses 1.35x-1.72x fewer tokens. We then turn to scientific paper writing and reviewing, and Olympiad-level proof writing and grading, where the RQGM improves performance over prior self-improving agents: co-evolved writers reach 1.78x-1.86x higher acceptance rates under a diverse agent-as-a-judge panel, while co-evolved graders reach 9% higher ground-truth accuracy. In paper reviewing, the strongest baseline reviewer over-accepts AI-generated papers at up to 1.91x the human rate. The RQGM corrects this by introducing an adversarial objective that discovers reviewers equally stringent on AI and human work.

</details>


### [241] [High-Probability PL-SGD with Markovian Noise: Optimal Mixing and Tail Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26316)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Aprameyo Chakrabartty,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Provides sharp high-probability bounds for SGD under Markovian gradient samples satisfying the Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, in both light-tailed and heavy-tailed regimes, and proves optimal dependence on mixing time with matching lower bounds. Introduces lag-blocking for light-tailed gradients and an all-samples clipped-block method for heavy-tailed gradients, plus lower-bound reductions to establish optimality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand stochastic gradient methods when gradient samples come from a dependent process (exogenous Markov chain) under PL assumption. Bridge the gap between uniform high-probability bounds and expectation bounds, and extend to heavy-tailed gradient noise.

Method: Light-tailed: lag-blocking technique to obtain a uniform high-probability bound with leading term ~ t_mix/(k+K0) under geometric mixing; lower bound via a persistent two-state Markov chain showing Ω(σ^2 t_mix/k). Heavy-tailed: design all-samples clipped block method using every Markov transition, with a transition budget T to control Markov bias; analysis yields high-probability error ~ σ_p^2 (t_mix/T)^{2(p-1)/p}; establish lower bounds by reduction to heavy-tailed mean estimation for sticky Markov chains.

Result: Light-tailed PL-SGD: optimal linear dependence on the mixing time with a refined bound ~ t_mix/(k+K0); lower bound Ω(σ^2 t_mix/k) matches the rate. Heavy-tailed PL-SGD (p in (1,2]): high-probability error scales as σ_p^2 (t_mix/T)^{2(p-1)/p}; lower bound matches via reductions.

Conclusion: The work tightly characterizes the optimal mixing-time dependence for light-tailed PL-SGD and the optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size dependence in the robust (heavy-tailed) regime, providing practical algorithms (lag-blocking, all-samples clipped blocks) and matching lower bounds.

Abstract: We study first-order methods for smooth objectives satisfying the Polyak-Łojasiewicz (PL) condition when gradient samples are generated by an exogenous Markov chain. In the light-tailed setting, prior uniform-in-time high-probability bounds for ordinary Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) under a standard growth envelope scale as $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}^2/k)$, leaving a gap with the $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ expectation bounds. We close this gap using a lag-blocking argument to establish a uniform high-probability guarantee with a leading stochastic term of $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/(k+K_0))$ under geometric mixing. We prove this linear dependence on the mixing time is optimal via a matching $Ω(σ^2 t_{mix}/k)$ lower bound on a quadratic objective driven by a persistent two-state chain.
  We then extend this framework to heavy-tailed Markovian gradients satisfying a stationary finite-$p$-moment condition, $p \in (1,2]$. We design an all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating Markovian bias. Under a transition budget $T$, this algorithm achieves a high-probability stochastic error of $\widetilde{O}(σ_p^2(t_{mix}/T)^{2(p-1)/p})$. We establish a matching lower bound by reducing PL optimization to heavy-tailed mean estimation for a sticky Markov chain. Ultimately, this work tightly characterizes the optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time for light-tailed PL-SGD, and the optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size dependence in the robust regime.

</details>


### [242] [EVOM: Agentic Meta-Evolution of Actor-Critic Architectures for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26327)
*Boyun Zhang,Chao Wang,Kai Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EVOM is an agentic meta-evolution framework that automatically discovers high-performance actor-critic architectures by bi-level optimization: inner PPO-based weight training and outer meta-evolution guided by an LLM-based design agent, decoupled from environment control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated design of actor-critic architectures is hard due to open-ended search space and the need to train candidates before evaluation; a decoupled, efficient search method is needed.

Method: Formulates architecture search as bi-level optimization: inner loop trains weights with low-fidelity PPO; outer loop uses meta-evolution to refine architecture programs. An LLM Design Agent acts purely as architecture designer, not involved in policy execution or environment control. Evaluation benchmarks include Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4 with comparisons to manual baselines, LLM-guided random search, and MLES.

Result: EVOM outperforms manually designed baselines, LLM-guided random search, and MLES on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablation studies show both the meta-evolution loop and the LLM Design Agent are essential for peak performance.

Conclusion: Integrated bi-level optimization with an LLM-driven architecture designer yields superior actor-critic architectures, highlighting the importance of decoupled architecture search and meta-evolution in RL architecture design.

Abstract: In actor-critic reinforcement learning, network architectures are typically manually designed. Automating this design is challenging because each candidate must be trained before evaluation, and the design space is open-ended. To address these challenges, we introduce EVOM, an agentic meta-evolution framework for discovering high-performance actor-critic architectures. We frame architecture search as a bi-level optimization: an inner loop trains weights via the low-fidelity proximal policy optimization (PPO), while an outer loop drives meta-evolution by iteratively refining architecture programs. Crucially, this outer loop is powered by an LLM-based design agent that operates purely as an architecture designer, completely decoupled from policy execution and environment control. Experiments reveal that EVOM outperforms the manually designed baseline, an LLM-guided random search, and the state-of-the-art LLM-guided programmatic policy search method MLES, delivering superior performance on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablation studies validate that both the meta-evolution loop and the LLM Design Agent are indispensable for final performance.

</details>


### [243] [Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26333)
*Behnam Gheshlaghi,Bahador Rashidi,Shahin Atakishiyev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mesh-RL introduces a finite-element-inspired spatial domain decomposition for TD learning, partitioning the environment into overlapping subgrids with boundary-consistent updates to enable local learning and global value propagation, improving convergence and sample efficiency in sparse-reward RL without altering rewards or the Bellman operator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TD learning propagates rewards slowly in large or sparse environments; need a mechanism to enable faster credit assignment and global value coherence without changing the reward structure or planning mechanisms.

Method: Partition the environment into overlapping subgrids (mesh), enforce boundary-consistent TD updates across subgrids, and evaluate across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q with varying mesh resolutions; analyze impact on convergence, exploration, and propagation speed.

Result: Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and stability across tested algorithms; higher mesh resolutions enhance exploration and accelerate long-range value propagation; Dyna-Q gains from the structured decomposition beyond its internal planning.

Conclusion: Spatial domain decomposition inspired by finite element methods provides a principled, planning-free approach to accelerate TD learning in sparse-reward settings, bridging scientific computing techniques with reinforcement learning to boost sample efficiency; code will be released.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space. We propose Mesh-RL, a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by the finite element method and domain decomposition theory, which partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates. Such an approach enables localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation. Unlike hierarchical or model-based approaches, Mesh-RL accelerates long-range credit assignment without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms. We evaluate Mesh-RL on hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and mesh resolutions. Across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states. While Dyna-Q already benefits from internal planning, it still achieves additional gains under structured decomposition. Overall, Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning. Our framework bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques from scientific computing with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments. We will release source code of the study.

</details>


### [244] [EMA-FS: Accelerating GBDT Training via Gain-Informed Feature Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26337)
*Yan Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMA-Based Feature Screening (EMA-FS) accelerates GBDT histogram construction by tracking an EMA of per-feature split gains and keeping only the top-K features for histogram building; a stochastic variant (S-EMA-FS) uses gain-weighted random sampling controlled by beta, unifying deterministic and random subsampling. Demonstrates significant speedups on dense datasets with modest AUC gains and no speedup on highly sparse data; implemented with minimal code changes and full LightGBM compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training gradient boosted trees with per-feature histograms dominates compute time. Random feature subsampling (feature_fraction) can discard predictive features, reducing model quality. A data-driven, lightweight feature screening is needed to reduce histogram construction cost while preserving predictive utility.

Method: Maintain an exponential moving average (EMA) of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations. After a short warmup, restrict histogram construction to the top-K features ranked by the EMA. Operate at the per-tree level to remain compatible with LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick. Introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS) by replacing deterministic top-K with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta, bridging deterministic (beta→∞) and random subsampling (beta=0). Implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six LightGBM tree learners, backward-compatible.

Result: Significant speedups on dense, moderate/high-dimensional data: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark; 1.45x on the 432-feature IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% retention; at 70% retention AUC improves by 0.11 with 1.34x speedup. On extremely sparse data (Bosch, >90% missing) no speedup due to LightGBM's sparse bin optimization. S-EMA-FS provides a continuum between deterministic and stochastic subsampling via beta, enabling flexible tradeoffs.

Conclusion: EMA-FS offers a data-informed, low-overhead optimization for histogram-based GBDT training in LightGBM, achieving substantial speedups with typically small or positive effects on predictive performance on dense datasets, while not benefiting highly sparse cases where existing sparse optimizations suffice. The approach is practical, backward-compatible, and extensible through the beta parameter to accommodate different screening strategies.

Abstract: Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT), exemplified by LightGBM, spend a dominant fraction of training time -- typically 65-70% -- constructing per-feature histograms. Existing approaches such as random feature subsampling (feature_fraction) discard features without regard for their predictive utility. We propose EMA-based Feature Screening (EMA-FS), an algorithm-level optimization that maintains an exponential moving average (EMA) of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations and, after a short warmup, restricts histogram construction to the top-K features ranked by historical gain. Unlike random subsampling, EMA-FS is informed: it retains high-gain features while screening out low-gain ones. Operating at the per-tree level, it preserves full compatibility with LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick, requiring no changes to core routines.
  We evaluate EMA-FS on datasets spanning financial fraud detection, advertising click-through prediction, industrial quality control, and synthetic benchmarks, with feature dimensionalities from 29 to 968. On dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional data it achieves significant speedups: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark and 1.45x on the 432-feature IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% retention. At 70% retention it improves AUC by 0.11 points while delivering a 1.34x speedup. On extremely sparse data (Bosch, >90% missing) it yields no speedup, as LightGBM's sparse bin optimization already bypasses empty values.
  We further introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS), which replaces deterministic top-K selection with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta, unifying deterministic EMA-FS (beta -> infinity) and random subsampling (beta = 0) in one framework. Both are implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six LightGBM tree learners and are fully backward-compatible.

</details>


### [245] [Does Aurora Encode Atmospheric Structure? Latent Regime Analysis and Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26361)
*Emma Kasteleyn,Ana Lucic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Aurora's latent space is dominated by seasonal cycles; extreme storms do not form a linearly separable cluster. LRP reveals attention to 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987. Perturbation masking degrades forecasts much more than random masking, indicating reliance on meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To interpret and understand how a large ML foundation model captures atmospheric dynamics, addressing the opacity of such models and assessing whether physically meaningful structure emerges in the latent representations.

Method: Apply spatially pooled PCA to internal representations; use layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) to identify features driving predictions; perform perturbation tests by masking regions and measuring forecast degradation.

Result: Latent space is organized by seasonal cycles; extreme storm events do not form a simple linear cluster. LRP shows attention to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the 1987 Great Storm. Masking relevant regions degrades forecasts 3.31× more than random masking.

Conclusion: Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction, suggesting the model develops physics-aligned representations and spatially-informed interpretability.

Abstract: ML foundation models are able to emulate atmospheric dynamics accurately and efficiently but operate as opaque ``black boxes''. We investigate the internal representations of the Aurora model using spatially pooled PCA and layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP). We find evidence that Aurora's latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles, whereas extreme storm events do not form a linearly separable cluster. LRP indicates that the model attends to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987. Perturbation tests show masking relevant regions degrades forecasts $3.31\times$ more than random masking. These findings suggest that Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction.

</details>


### [246] [SOLAR: AI-Powered Speed-of-Light Performance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26383)
*Qijing Huang,Sana Damani,Zhifan Ye,Athinagoras Skiadopoulos,Siva Kumar Sastry Hari,Jason Clemons,Sahil Modi,Jingquan Wang,Aditya Kane,Edward C Lin,Humphrey Shi,Christos Kozyrakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOLAR automatically derives validated speed-of-light (SOL) bounds from PyTorch/JAX sources by translating to an executable affine loop IR via an LLM frontend, lifting to an einsum graph, and analytically computing unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds, enabling multi-fidelity headroom analysis and optimization insights with validated results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual SOL analysis is error-prone and disconnected from rapid model development; an automated, end-to-end SOL analysis framework is needed to provide timely, validated bounds from source code.

Method: A two-stage flow: (1) an LLM frontend translates PyTorch/JAX code into an executable Affine Loop IR, validated by output comparison; (2) a deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; an analytical backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds with multi-fidelity options.

Result: SOLAR delivers comprehensive operator and language coverage, produces validated SOL bounds with zero observed violations, and enables multi-fidelity tightening. Evaluations on KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads demonstrate headroom analysis, optimization opportunities, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline hardware provisioning.

Conclusion: An automated, validated SOL bounding framework that connects code to performance bounds, offering multi-fidelity insights to guide optimization across software and hardware.

Abstract: How fast could a deep-learning model run on target hardware, and how far is today's implementation from that limit? These questions are central to software, hardware, and algorithm optimizations. Speed-of-Light (SOL) analysis answers them by computing a workload's theoretical minimum execution time on a given architecture. Yet deriving SOL bounds remains manual, error-prone, and disconnected from rapid model development. To close this gap, we introduce SOLAR, a framework that automatically derives validated SOL bounds from PyTorch and JAX source code. SOLAR leverages both generative and deterministic components in its flow: an LLM frontend translates any source programs into an executable Affine Loop IR, validated by output comparison; a deterministic flow lifts the IR into an einsum graph; and an analytical backend computes unfused, fused, and cache-aware SOL bounds. SOLAR provides comprehensive operator and language coverage, produces validated bounds with zero observed SOL violations, and offers multi-fidelity analysis that tightens bounds and surfaces optimization insights. We evaluate SOLAR across KernelBench, JAX/Flax models, and robotics workloads. These experiments demonstrate four use cases: headroom analysis at multiple fidelity levels, identifying optimization opportunities, cross-platform exploration, and inverse-roofline hardware provisioning.

</details>


### [247] [At the Edge of Understanding: Sparse Autoencoders Trace The Limits of Transformer Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26396)
*Praneet Suresh,Jack Stanley,Sonia Joseph,Luca Scimeca,Danilo Bzdok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A mechanistic, inference-time framework to diagnose and harden transformer robustness to distributional shifts (OOD), linking shifts in prompts to increased reliance on fallacious internal concepts and proposing a practical fine-tuning and diagnostic strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve reliability and safety of pre-trained transformers in real-world settings where data and prompts deviate from training distributions, by formalizing OOD beyond inputs to include internal computations.

Method: Systematic experiments establishing a mechanistic framework; analysis of how OOD inputs (e.g., typos, jailbreak prompts) alter internal representations; quantification of prompt shift; development of a fine-tuning method to robustify models; proposal of an inference-time diagnostic that examines private computational processes.

Result: Found that OOD inputs drive language models to operate on a larger set of fallacious concepts in their internals; enabled quantification of distributional shift in prompts; demonstrated a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy to enhance robustness; proposed a diagnostic at inference time that inspects internal processes to improve safety.

Conclusion: Extends the notion of OOD to model internals, highlighting the need for mechanism-aware diagnostics and training to deploy AI systems safely across domains; provides a pathway toward robust, auditable transformer behavior under distributional shifts.

Abstract: Pre-trained transformers have demonstrated remarkable generalization abilities, at times extending beyond the scope of their training data. Yet, real-world deployments often face unexpected or adversarial data that diverges from training data distributions. Without explicit mechanisms for handling such shifts, model reliability and safety degrade, urging more disciplined study of out-of-distribution (OOD) settings for transformers. By systematic experiments, we present a mechanistic framework for delineating the precise contours of transformer model robustness. We find that OOD inputs, including subtle typos and jailbreak prompts, drive language models to operate on an increased number of fallacious concepts in their internals. We leverage this device to quantify and understand the degree of distributional shift in prompts, enabling a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy to robustify LLMs. Expanding the very notion of OOD from input data to a model's private computational processes, a new transformer diagnostic at inference time is a critical step toward making AI systems safe for deployment across science, business, and government.

</details>


### [248] [Deterministic Pareto-Optimal Policy Synthesis for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26397)
*Aniruddha Joshi,Niklas Lauffer,Sanjit Seshia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator for Multi-Objective MDPs (MOMDPs), inspired by Chebyshev scalarization, computes deterministic Pareto-optimal policies. It has an enveloping property (estimated values upper-bound the true Pareto frontier) and monotonically converges to a coverage set of the frontier, from which deterministic policies for any preference can be extracted. Experimental results validate reconstruction of complex trade-offs and deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world tasks involve conflicting objectives; scalarizing rewards into a single value often obscures the Pareto frontier. There is a need for methods that can recover and synthesize policies that reflect all trade-offs, including deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for any given preference.

Method: Introduce a preference-conditioned Bellman operator derived from Chebyshev scalarization. Prove enveloping property (upper-bounds Pareto frontier) and monotone convergence to a coverage set. Provide a procedure to extract deterministic policies from converged Q-estimates corresponding to different preferences.

Result: The operator yields a convergent process whose Q-estimates form an envelope around the true Pareto frontier and converge to a coverage set. From these estimates, deterministic policies can be extracted for any preference, effectively recovering the full Pareto-optimal frontier. Experiments demonstrate the method's ability to capture complex trade-offs and synthesize deterministic Pareto-optimal policies.

Conclusion: The work offers a principled approach to deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis in MOMDPs by leveraging a Chebyshev-based, preference-conditioned operator with enveloping and convergence guarantees, validated empirically.

Abstract: Real-world decision-making often requires balancing multiple conflicting objectives, a challenge that standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) frequently addresses by aggregating rewards into a single scalar signal. While effective for simple tasks, this approach often fails to capture the full spectrum of optimal trade-offs, known as the Pareto frontier. In this paper, we introduce a novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator, motivated from the Chebyshev scalarization, designed to compute deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for Multi-Objective Markov Decision Processes (MOMDPs). We prove that this operator satisfies an enveloping property, where the estimated value functions upper-bound the true Pareto frontier, and demonstrate that it monotonically converges to a coverage set of this frontier. Furthermore, we also show how to extract deterministic policies from these converged Q-estimates. This ensures the agent can recover a policy for any given preference, capturing the entire Pareto-optimal frontier while guaranteeing each synthesized policy remains approximately Pareto-optimal. Experimental results validate that our algorithm successfully recovers complex trade-offs, providing a solution for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis.

</details>


### [249] [Beyond Feedforward Networks: Reentry Neural Systems as the Fundamental Basis of Subjecthood and Intrinsic Safety of Next-Generation AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26406)
*A. S. Ushakov,Yu. N. Berdinsk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a cyclic, self-referential AGI architecture (closed D<->I loop) with self-models, instrumental goals, and a formal S-measure (O(N^3)) with Lean 4 proofs, plus full implementations and deployment stack; claims safety, scalability, and eight falsifiable predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address AI safety and controllability by embedding goals in a cyclical architecture that guarantees self-modeling and goal-directed behavior, while providing formal verification and a deployable implementation.

Method: Introduce a closed D<->I cycle (C≥1, ρ>1) architecture with self-sustaining amplification; encode goals as a non-textual D-vector embedded in the architecture; define S-measure (polynomial-time) with Lean 4 proofs showing S>0 implies positive integrated information; provide Python/NumPy implementations (cycle analysis via Tarjan, Delta-S barrier), scale via Apache Kafka and Docker Compose; present a taxonomy of six evolution epochs, a zoo of reentry architectures, gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms, fault-tolerance and recovery protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions; all proofs machine-verified in Lean 4; claim deployability today and safety-by-design.

Result: Claims: (1) S>0 => positive integrated information, (2) complete, verifiable architecture with self-models and instrumentally safe goals, (3) practical implementations and deployment stack, (4) multiple architectural taxonomies and predicted behaviors; eight falsifiable predictions.

Conclusion: If validated, this framework offers a topologically protected path to safe AGI with formal verification; however, the validity hinges on strong assumptions about self-reference, goal encoding, and the absence of unforeseen failure modes; claims require rigorous peer review and empirical corroboration.

Abstract: We propose a complete architectural blueprint for safe artificial general intelligence based on a closed reentry loop (D <-> I cycle). In contrast to feedforward networks, which are directed acyclic graphs (C=0, S=0) incapable of self-reference, the proposed architecture contains a structural cycle (C >= 1) with self-sustaining amplification (rho > 1), mathematically guaranteeing the emergence of a self-model, instrumental self-preservation, and unprogrammed goal-directed behaviour. The agent's goals are encoded as a non-textual D-vector in the architecture itself, making them immune to reinterpretation and prompt injection. We present the S-measure -- a polynomial-time [O(N^3)] computable alternative to Tononi's NP-hard Phi -- with machine-verified Lean 4 proof that S>0 implies positive integrated information. The work provides full Python/NumPy implementations (Tarjan-based cycle complexity, Delta-S barrier), industrial horizontal scaling via Apache Kafka and Docker Compose, a taxonomy of six epochs of AI evolution, a zoo of future reentry architectures (RAS, diffusion attractors, fractal loops), gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms, fault-tolerance and recovery protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions. All formal proofs are machine-verified in Lean 4. This architecture is deployable today and represents a topologically protected, safe-by-design approach to AGI.

</details>


### [250] [Otter Weather: Skillful and Computationally Efficient Medium-Range Weather Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26421)
*Cristiana Diaconu,Jonas Scholz,Aliaksandra Shysheya,Stratis Markou,Payel Mukhopadhyay,Miles Cranmer,Richard E. Turner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Otter Weather delivers efficient AI-based spatiotemporal weather forecasting that outperforms NWP baselines and frontier models on ERA5 data while drastically reducing training compute, enabling broader access and rapid iteration; Otter-XL extends to probabilistic forecasting (CRPS) and crosses into cross-domain tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current state-of-the-art AI weather models achieve high predictive skill but require massive training budgets, limiting adoption by under-resourced groups and hindering fast iteration. A more compute-efficient, scalable approach could democratize access while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Method: Development of Otter Weather, a spatiotemporal forecasting model family evaluated on ERA5 reanalysis data at 1.5° resolution using WeatherBench protocols. Includes deterministic and probabilistic (CRPS) training; scaling to Otter-XL; and a cross-domain acoustic scattering PDE task to test generality.

Result: Deterministic Otter outperforms the best NWP baseline by 9.6% at 24h lead time with training cost <3.5 A100-days. Otter provides ~2x efficiency vs lightweight AI models and ~100x compute reduction vs frontier architectures. Probabilistic Otter-XL achieves 9.7% CRPS improvement over IFS ENS; nearly 2x predictive skill over comparable lightweight models at similar compute budgets; Otter-XL outperforms GenCast by >2% with an order of magnitude less compute. Additionally, Otter demonstrates strong cross-domain applicability by outperforming a state-of-the-art foundation-modeling approach on an acoustic scattering PDE task.

Conclusion: Otter demonstrates that high-performance AI weather forecasting can be achieved with substantially lower compute, enabling broader access and faster iteration, while maintaining or exceeding state-of-the-art skill. Its improvements in both deterministic and probabilistic forecasting and successful cross-domain application suggest broad impact across scientific domains.

Abstract: State-of-the-art medium-range AI weather models can outperform traditional Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) but require massive training budgets. This restricts usage for under-resourced groups and severely limits fast model iteration. Here we develop Otter Weather, a highly efficient spatiotemporal forecasting model designed to democratise high-performance weather prediction with AI. Evaluated on ERA5 reanalysis data at 1.5° resolution using standard WeatherBench protocols, the Otter family significantly advances the skill-compute Pareto frontier. The deterministic version outperforms the best NWP baseline by 9.6% at a 24-hour lead time while requiring fewer than 3.5 A100-days for training. It provides a 2x efficiency gain over lightweight AI models and a 100-fold reduction in compute compared to resource-intensive frontier architectures. We extend these efficiency gains into probabilistic forecasting by training via the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). Scaling to a larger architecture, Otter-XL achieves a 9.7% CRPS improvement over the IFS ENS baseline. This yields an almost two-fold increase in predictive skill over comparable lightweight models at similar compute budgets.
