Otter-XL also outperforms frontier architectures like GenCast by over 2%, while using an order of magnitude less compute. Finally, Otter is applied out-of-the-box to a complex acoustic scattering PDE task where it outperforms a state-of-the-art foundation modelling approach, suggesting that the advances made here might apply across a range of scientific domains.

</details>


### [251] [Rethinking Training & Inference for Forecasting: Linking Winner-Take-All back to GMMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26424)
*Qiyuan Wu,Katie Z Luo,Bharath Hariharan,Wei-Lun Chao,Mark Campbell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WTA hard assignment in conditional GMM forecasters leads to uninformative, unstable mode posteriors; two post-hoc fixes—posterior-weighted test-time merging and a one-step EM soft-update—improve mode informativeness and displacement performance without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a training–inference mismatch: forecasters are modeled as conditional GMMs but trained with hard, one-hot labels (K-means-like). This causes over-segmentation of trajectory space, ignores relatedness among nearby modes, and unstable posterior probabilities.

Method: Two post-hoc treatments: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging of nearby candidate trajectories; (2) a one-step expectation-maximization (EM) update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, sharing probability mass across neighboring modes.

Result: Across multiple WTA-trained architectures, these lightweight steps yield more informative, properly ranked mode posteriors and improved displacement-performance metrics without retraining.

Conclusion: A GMM-vs-K-means lens unifies recent design choices and offers practical corrections that better align training objectives with inference, enabling more faithful mode posteriors and stronger forecasts.

Abstract: Trajectory forecasting for autonomous driving has advanced rapidly, yet representative models often produce uninformative posteriors over forecast modes, causing problems for mode pruning. We trace this to a modeling-training mismatch: forecasters are typically modeled as conditional Gaussian mixture models (GMMs) but trained with a winner-take-all (WTA) loss that assigns each sample to its nearest mode. We argue that this K-means-like hard assignment (one-hot), while preventing mode collapse, is the source of uninformative mode probabilities: it over-segments the trajectory space, ignores relatedness among nearby modes, and yields assignment instability under small perturbations. Guided by this lens, we introduce two post-hoc treatments: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging that aggregates nearby candidate trajectories; and (2) a one-step expectation-maximization (EM) update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, sharing probability mass across neighboring modes. Across several WTA-trained architectures, these lightweight steps produce more informative, faithfully ranked mode posteriors and strengthen final forecasts on popular displacement metrics -- without retraining. Our analysis unifies recent design choices through a GMM-vs-K-means perspective and offers principled, practical corrections that better align training objectives with inference.

</details>


### [252] [DualEval: Joint Model-Item Calibration for Unified LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26429)
*Aaron J. Li,Hao Huang,Youngmin Park,Yitong Ma,Wei-Lin Chiang,Li Chen,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Bin Yu,Ion Stoica*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualEval calibrates model-item performance in a shared latent space to jointly estimate model ability, item difficulty, and item sharpness, unifying static benchmarks and arena-style evaluation for reliable rankings and item-level diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between static correctness labels and preference-based, open-ended evaluation by jointly modeling models and evaluation items.

Method: Latent model-item calibration across four domains (coding, math, miscellaneous domain knowledge, and everyday queries). Uses 18 frontier LLMs, static labels, and reward-model scores aligned with held-out human preferences. Jointly estimates model ability, item difficulty, and item sharpness to produce rankings and item-level profiles.

Result: Yields reliable and balanced model rankings; generates item-level diagnostics useful for benchmark compression and anomaly detection; supports an auditable, sample-efficient evaluation pipeline.

Conclusion: Joint model-item calibration unifies static and arena-style evaluation, enabling interpretable, auditable evaluation with improved sample efficiency and diagnostics.

Abstract: Current LLM evaluation relies on two complementary but often disconnected signals: static benchmarks with objective correctness labels and arena-style preference data that better reflect open-ended user interactions. We introduce DualEval, a latent model-item calibration framework that represents models and evaluation items in a shared space, jointly estimating model ability together with item difficulty and sharpness. We apply DualEval across four domains: coding, math, miscellaneous domain-knowledge tasks, and generic everyday user queries. Our evaluation uses 18 frontier LLMs, static benchmark labels, and reward-model scores validated against held-out human preferences for open-ended model responses. Empirically, our framework produces reliable and balanced model rankings, and its learned item-level profiles support downstream applications such as benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection for contamination or outlier analysis. Overall, DualEval unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, producing model rankings and item-level diagnostics that support more sample-efficient, interpretable, and auditable evaluation pipelines.

</details>


### [253] [Embedding Foundation Model Predictions in Discrete-Choice Models with Structural Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26432)
*Yingshuo Wang,Xian Sun,Yanhang Li,Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage adapter for tabular foundation models embeds predicted choice probabilities into a multinomial logit (MNL) to enforce economic rationality while improving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models achieve strong predictive accuracy but violate economic logic in choice tasks (e.g., price-up, demand-down or WTP implausible values; unavailable alternatives receiving nonzero probability). A method is needed to enforce economic constraints without sacrificing performance.

Method: Stage 1: fit MNL structural coefficients by maximum likelihood with sign constraints. Stage 2: freeze coefficients and train a small neural correction that operates on the foundation model’s predicted probabilities. The composition guarantees preservation of the MNL’s marginal rate of substitution, making value-of-time analytically computable.

Result: Across three datasets and two foundation models, the adapter yields an average of 6.4 percentage points (pp) accuracy gain over the MNL and up to 12.8 pp. It maintains 100% cost monotonicity and produces value-of-time within published transportation-economics ranges. The method degrades gracefully under context restriction, retaining at least 6 pp of the accuracy gain when only 10% of the original foundation-model context is available.

Conclusion: The two-stage adapter enforces economic rationality while boosting predictive performance; it converts value-of-time into a mathematically guaranteed quantity rather than an empirical artifact and demonstrates robustness across datasets, models, and reduced context.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models achieve strong accuracy on choice prediction tasks, but their predictions often violate the economic logic those tasks require: raising a price can increase predicted demand, implied willingness-to-pay estimates are frequently negative or implausible, and unavailable alternatives receive nonzero probability. We propose a two-stage adapter that takes a foundation model's predicted choice probabilities as a precomputed feature and embeds them inside a multinomial logit's utility. In Stage 1, we fit the multinomial logit's structural coefficients by maximum likelihood with sign constraints; in Stage 2, we freeze those coefficients and fit a small neural correction operating on the foundation model's predictions. We prove that this composition exactly preserves the multinomial logit's marginal rate of substitution, so analytically computable value-of-time becomes a mathematical guarantee rather than an empirical accident. Across three datasets and two foundation models, the adapter gains 6.4 percentage points (pp) of test accuracy on average over the multinomial logit and up to 12.8 pp, maintains 100% cost monotonicity, and produces values of time within the published transportation-economics range on the transportation datasets. Performance degrades gracefully under foundation-model context restriction, retaining at least 6 pp of accuracy gain even at 10% of the original foundation-model context.

</details>


### [254] [Optimizing CUDA like a Human: Micro-Profiling Tools as Expert Surrogates for LLM-Based GPU Kernel Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26453)
*Jiading Gai,Shuai Zhang,Kaj Bostrom,Jin Huang,Vihang Patil,Haoyang Fang,Bernie Wang,Huzefa Rangwala,George Karypis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KernelPro is a closed-loop, multi-agent system that automatically generates and optimizes GPU kernel code using LLMs, hardware profiler feedback, and modular bottleneck tools. It introduces semantic feedback, a two-stage tool invocation architecture with roofline bottleneck classification, a domain-adapted MCTS for cross-iteration learning, and direct CuTe source-level code generation. Empirically, it achieves state-of-the-art speedups on KernelBench and competitive performance on VeOmni MoE kernels, with significant ablation-confirmed gains and energy efficiency improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate and accelerate GPU kernel design to achieve high performance and energy efficiency by combining LLM-based code generation with precise hardware feedback and specialized analysis tools, addressing the gap between high-level code generation and low-level, architecture-specific optimizations.

Method: A four-part framework: (1) semantic feedback operator converting raw hardware metrics into actionable language-guided guidance via micro-profiling tools; (2) a two-stage tool invocation architecture using roofline bottleneck classification to trigger specialized tools (kernel-level ncu, instruction-level SASS, system-level nsys) for analysis; (3) domain-adapted Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and cross-iteration memory; (4) autonomous code search-based CUDA/CuTe generation producing from-scratch raw-CUDA+CuTe kernels.

Result: Geometric mean speedups on KernelBench Levels 1/2/3: 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x; MoE training kernels on VeOmni: 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton with from-scratch Hopper WGMMA kernel; Ablations show micro-profiling tools yield p<0.0001 vs raw metrics, MCTS search yields 26% GM gain vs greedy (p=0.004), proactive tool orchestration yields 23% improvement (p=0.035); energy efficiency improved by 11.6% at matched speed.

Conclusion: KernelPro advances automated GPU kernel optimization by tightly integrating LLM-driven generation with rich instrumentation-based feedback, principled search, and direct code synthesis from CuTe, achieving state-of-the-art performance and energy efficiency improvements, and establishing the value of micro-profiling-informed guidance and cross-iteration learning.

Abstract: We present KernelPro, a closed-loop multi-agent system that automatically generates, profiles, and iteratively optimizes GPU kernel code by integrating large language model (LLM) code generation with hardware profiler feedback and pluggable bottleneck detection tools. KernelPro introduces four contributions: (1) a semantic feedback operator that encodes expert heuristics as pluggable micro-profiling tools, transforming raw hardware metrics into actionable natural language guidance; (2) a two-stage tool invocation architecture where roofline-based bottleneck classification filters which specialized analysis tools execute, combining kernel-level (ncu), instruction-level (SASS), and system-level (nsys) profiling; (3) a domain-adapted MCTS with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and search memory for cross-iteration learning; and (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation via autonomous code search over the CUTLASS/CuTe codebase. On KernelBench, KernelPro achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on Levels 1/2/3, establishing state-of-the-art performance across all difficulty levels. On VeOmni's expert-optimized MoE training kernels, KernelPro achieves 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton by generating a from-scratch raw-CUDA+CuTe Hopper WGMMA kernel. Ablation studies demonstrate that each design component independently and significantly improves optimization quality: micro-profiling tools (p < 0.0001 vs raw metrics), MCTS search (26% higher geometric mean vs greedy, p = 0.004), and proactive tool orchestration (23% improvement, p = 0.035). Finally, KernelPro is the first CUDA kernel coding agent to optimize energy efficiency beyond the speed-only focus of prior systems, demonstrating an 11.6% measured energy reduction at matched speed.

</details>


### [255] [Finding the Time to Think: Learning Planning Budgets in Real-Time RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26463)
*Aneesh Muppidi,Firas Darwish,Dylan Cope,João F. Henriques,Jakob Nicolaus Foerster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study real-time RL where the environment advances during agent deliberation and introduce a variable-delay framework using a gating policy on a planner to adapt planning budgets; outperforms baselines across multiple games and transfers to split-GPU real-time setup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-time environments, decision latency is costly; standard RL ignores time-to-decision by letting the environment wait; need to model and optimize deliberation time to improve performance and feasibility.

Method: Develop variable-delay real-time RL; train a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets; environment progresses concurrently; evaluate on Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, Speed Go; test in a two-GPU real-time configuration.

Result: Gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines across all tested games; demonstrates transfer to real-time setup with independent GPUs.

Conclusion: Adaptive, state-dependent planning budgets enable effective real-time decision-making in RL and generalize across diverse environments and hardware configurations.

Abstract: Deliberating takes time. In real-time settings, that time is not free. Standard reinforcement learning (RL) sidesteps this as the environment waits indefinitely for the agent's decision. Instead, we study real-time RL environments where the environment progresses while waiting for the agent's action. Building on prior real-time formalizations, we introduce variable-delay real-time RL, where the agent chooses how long to deliberate at each decision point since the environment progresses. For the planning agents we use, the right delay is state-dependent, and naively planning how long to plan can paralyze the agent. We instead approach this setting by training a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets. Across real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, and Speed Go, our gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines, and transfers to a real-time setup where the environment and agent run on two different GPUs.

</details>


### [256] [A Causal Foundation Model for Structure and Outcome Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26467)
*Max Zhu,Martino Mansoldo,Ching-Hao Wang,Stefan Groha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPFN-CFM is a unified causal foundation model for tabular data that predicts causal structure and outcomes, supports Pearl's three-level queries, uses known graph structure when available, trained on synthetic data and generalizes to real data, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable a single model to perform multiple causal tasks on tabular data, leveraging synthetic pretraining to generalize to real-world data and to operate across Pearl's causal hierarchy.

Method: Develop TabPFN-CFM, a causal foundation model that jointly predicts causal structure and outcomes from observational data; supports queries on all three levels of Pearl's hierarchy; optionally incorporates known graph structure; trained on synthetic datasets; evaluated on real datasets against structural and outcome baselines.

Result: Reported improved performance over both structural and outcome prediction baselines on real datasets, demonstrating generalization from synthetic training.

Conclusion: TabPFN-CFM provides a unified approach for causal discovery and causal effect prediction on tabular data, capable of answering questions across Pearl's hierarchy and leveraging prior graph information when available.

Abstract: We introduce TabPFN-CFM, a causal foundation model that can handle multiple causal problems. TabPFN-CFM predicts both causal structure and outcomes from observational data, supports queries on all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy and uses known graph structure when available to improve predictions. TabPFN-CFM is trained on synthetic datasets, and generalises to real datasets, demonstrating improved performance over both structural and outcome prediction baselines.

</details>


### [257] [Epiphany-Aware KV Cache Eviction Without the Attention Matrix](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26472)
*Steven Kolawole,Virginia Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Epiphany-based KV cache eviction (EpiKV) is a training-free, forward-pass metric that replaces attention-weight scoring for cache eviction in long-context inference, enabling longer feasible contexts and faster inference with competitive accuracy to attention-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache eviction based on attention weight is noisy and requires materializing attention matrices, hindering production-scale long-context inference. A lightweight, training-free method is needed to scale context length without kernel changes.

Method: Compute epiphany score directly from forward-pass internal representations, avoiding attention matrices; no training, classifier, or custom kernels; integrates with FlashAttention stacks unchanged; delivers compatibility and speedups; includes a lag-normalized KV variant.

Result: With a 4096-token cache on MATH-500, EpiKV achieves 72% (matching strong baselines ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%); lag-normalized KV reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens (best 33%), with up to 2.8x speed.

Conclusion: EpiKV provides a practical, training-free, low-overhead cache eviction alternative that scales context length and delivers competitive accuracy and speed in long-context inference.

Abstract: As reasoning models emit chains of thought tens of thousands of tokens long, KV cache increasingly becomes a deployment bottleneck. Existing cache eviction methods rank tokens by attention weight, which is a noisy importance proxy in long reasoning traces, and prohibits the use of fused kernels in production inference by forcing the model to materialize the attention matrix. In this work, we instead score tokens with a metric we term the epiphany score: the change in the model's internal representation, read directly from the forward pass with no attention matrix and negligible extra state. Our resulting cache eviction method, EpiKV, requires no training, classifier, or custom kernel, and can be used directly in FlashAttention inference stacks unchanged -- scaling to a 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring. upper-mid layers negatively) and remove a positional trend with a causal rolling z-score. At a 4096-token cache EpiKV reaches 72% on MATH-500, matching the strongest attention-based baseline (ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%); a lag-normalized KV variant reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens against the best of them (33%), at up to 2.8x the speed.

</details>


### [258] [When Does Quality-Aware Multimodal Fusion Matter? A Leakage-Safe Diagnostic for Decision-Level Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26473)
*Jaden Moon,Arvind Pillai,Andrew Campbell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diagnostic to test if modality reliability signals influence inference in multimodal fusion by permuting reliability scores across test examples; results show no effect on performance unless reliability signals correctly predict unimodal correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address whether reliability-based weighting in multimodal fusion actually guides model decisions or merely correlates with unimodal accuracy.

Method: Train model normally, then freeze the model and inputs. Permute the reliability scores across test samples and evaluate performance. Compare against baselines and positive controls where reliability signals align with the correct modality.

Result: Permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged on StressID (stress recognition) and CMU-MOSEI (sentiment analysis), despite potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive-control scenarios where reliability signals correctly identify the best modality, the same fusion rules yield significant improvements.

Conclusion: Reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness; otherwise, the weighting from reliability scores does not affect inference. The diagnostic reveals whether reliability information is being exploited during inference.

Abstract: Many multimodal systems estimate the reliability of each modality and weight their contributions to the final prediction. However, it remains unclear whether these scores influence model decisions or merely correlate with performance. We propose a simple diagnostic to test whether reliability information is used during inference. After training, the model and inputs are fixed while reliability scores are permuted across test examples. If predictions depend on these scores, performance should degrade. Experiments on StressID for stress recognition and CMU-MOSEI for sentiment analysis show that permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive controls where reliability signals identify the correct modality, the same frozen fusion rules yield significant improvements, indicating that reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness.

</details>


### [259] [Localizing RL-Induced Tool Use to a Single Crosscoder Feature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26474)
*Andrii Shportko,Shubham Bhokare,Ahmed Zeyad A Alzahrani,Bowen Cheng,Gustavo Mercier,Jessica Hullman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) isolate a compact RL-specific feature set in Qwen2.5-3B that mediates tool-calling; encode-decode reconstruction improves RL tool-correctness and enables a capability spillover to the frozen base model across a 48-way hyperparameter sweep.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the mechanistic basis of RL-induced agentic behavior in LLMs and determine whether RL-created features can be identified, isolated, and used for retraining-free behavioral control.

Method: Train Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) to partition RL-induced features; evaluate across 48 crosscoder hyperparameters with encode-decode reconstruction; measure tool-calling performance for the RL model and observe passive transfer of capability to the frozen base model (capability spillover).

Result: RL model tool-correctness improves by +31.1 ± 9.7 pp; frozen base model shows +6.8 ± 5.0 pp transfer; DFC concentrates RL capability into a minimal, steerable feature set enabling runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

Conclusion: DFC effectively localizes RL-induced capabilities into a compact, controllable feature set, enabling direct runtime manipulation of agentic behaviors in LLMs without retraining.

Abstract: Fine-tuning through RL reshapes the internal representations of language models to enable agentic behaviors such as tool use, yet the mechanistic basis of these changes remains poorly understood. While RL substantially improves structured tool-call generation, it is unclear which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control. In this work, we show that $\textit{Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC)}$ isolate a compact set of RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in $\texttt{Qwen2.5-3B}$. Across a $48$-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep, encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by $+31.1 \pm {9.7}$ pp and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by $+6.8 \pm 5.0$ pp which we call a $\textit{capability spillover}$. Our findings show that DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

</details>


### [260] [Retrieval-Warmed Energy-Based Reasoning: A Five-Arm Ablation Methodology for Diffusion-as-Inference on Structured Reasoning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26476)
*Libo Sun,Po-Wei Harn,Zewei Zhang,Peixiong He,Xiao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Five-arm ablation framework for RW-EBR in diffusion with Hopfield memory; isolates class-prior bias, stochastic warm-start, and value reuse; shows 35pp gain from graph-aligned retrieval on connectivity-2; cross-domain Sudoku reveals a different blocking component.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate inference, but it is unclear which pipeline component drives gains. The work aims to disentangle the contributions of class-prior bias, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse using a structured diagnostic.

Method: Proposes retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR) with a Modern Hopfield memory, plus a five-arm ablation (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) to separate confounded effects. Diagnostic decomposition adapted from LLM-RAG. Evaluated on connectivity-2 (Erdős–Rényi reachability) with iterative diffusion; extended to Sudoku with a task-specific key encoder.

Result: Aligned vs. shuffled-oracle swing yields +35 percentage points in balanced accuracy on a fixed 1,000-graph validation set when keeping value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed and destroying per-graph alignment; per-query random initialization falls below cold, indicating alignment dominates. Deployable cold-prediction fails at the stored-value quality gate. Sudoku with a task-specific key encoder shows a negative at a different component (key quality). The framework identifies the first blocking component per task and situates the work within structured/spatio-temporal reasoning.

Conclusion: The five-arm diagnostic decomposes warm-start gains into distinct components, highlighting graph-aligned retrieval as a key contributor in diffusion-based reasoning; the approach provides explainability of failure modes and is applicable across structured domains like graph reachability and Sudoku.

Abstract: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is rarely clear which part of the pipeline carries the gain. We study \textbf{retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR)} -- an IRED energy-based diffusion model \cite{du2024ired} augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory -- and contribute a \textbf{five-arm ablation methodology} (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) that separates three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. The diagnostic decomposition is adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation \cite{ru2024ragchecker}. On \textbf{connectivity-2} (Erdős--Rényi all-pairs reachability), the aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches \textbf{$+35$\,pp} balanced accuracy on a fixed 1{,}000-graph validation-set diagnostic, with value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed, only per-graph alignment destroyed, while per-query random initialisation falls below cold -- per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates. Yet the \emph{deployable} cold-prediction pipeline misses the acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The same diagnostic logic, stopped at the key-quality screen, applied to \textbf{Sudoku} with a task-specific key encoder produces a clean negative at a \emph{different} component -- key quality, under the current setup. The decomposition names the first blocking component on each task. The setting -- graph reachability refined by an iterative diffusion sampler, with explainability of failure modes as the lens -- places the work within structured and spatio-temporal reasoning.

</details>


### [261] [What Survives When You Compress a Recursive Reasoner for the Edge?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26488)
*Pearse Jim,Steven Kolawole,Opegbemi Matthias Busoye,Glory Bagai,Virginia Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Aggressive quantization of recursive reasoning models preserves local predictions but kills global puzzle-solving ability; per-channel calibrated INT4 (without retraining) can recover near full performance. A deployment recipe uses streaming embeddings and mixed-precision to fit edge hardware, with a new carry-trajectory fidelity metric to predict damage and recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable edge deployment of recursive reasoning models by understanding how compression affects their recursive reasoning, which accumulates errors across cycles and does not follow standard token-level compression intuition.

Method: Comprehensive precision sweep across three tasks and two recursive architectures; evaluate naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention; analyze architectural vs token-level effects; introduce per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining; propose carry-trajectory fidelity as a label-free diagnostic; demonstrate deployment steps.

Result: Local (cell-level) accuracy remains under aggressive compression, but global puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero with naive INT4 and similar methods; token-level objectives and compression-aware training cannot fix the collapse; the failure is architectural (MLP-mixing) but not attention-based; per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining reverses the collapse.

Conclusion: Propose a practical deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings to remove a 99.4MB bottleneck; INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy with 6x fewer FLOPs on an 8MB SoC; calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller. Carry-trajectory fidelity predicted damage and recovery before task evaluation.

Abstract: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with only a few million parameters by repeatedly updating a latent state. Deploying these models on edge hardware requires significant compression, but unlike conventional sequence models, quantization errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than across output tokens. As a result, standard intuitions about compression fail to apply. In this work, we ask what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed. Across a full precision sweep, three tasks, and two recursive architectures, we find that aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero under naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention alike. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural -- it strikes MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on the same task -- and we reverse it with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. We also introduce carry-trajectory fidelity, the cosine similarity to the full-precision reasoning path, as a label-free signal that predicts this damage and its recovery before a task evaluation. The combined result is a deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

</details>


### [262] [Learning Probabilistic Filters with Strictly Proper Scoring Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26497)
*Eviatar Bach,Ricardo Baptista,Jochen Bröcker,Bohan Chen,Andrew Stuart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces PSEF, a transformer-based ensemble data assimilation method trained with proper scoring rules on synthetic trajectories to approximate Bayesian filtering distributions; outperforms EnKF and MSE-based learning; offers guidance on when end-to-end vs EnKF-corrected approaches are preferable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain and utilize the Bayesian filtering distribution for uncertainty quantification in online state estimation, addressing lack of supervised targets by using synthetic data generated from a forecast model.

Method: Train a permutation-invariant transformer-based analysis map that takes a forecast ensemble and observations and outputs an analysis ensemble. Training uses strictly proper scoring rules (energy score). Derive finite-ensemble empirical objective and a mean-field consistency argument; show population objective is minimized by the true Bayesian filtering distribution under realizability.

Result: Numerical experiments show accurate approximation of nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multimodal posteriors, with stronger data assimilation performance than classical methods or MSE-based learning. For near-Gaussian problems, learn a correction to EnKF; for highly non-Gaussian problems, end-to-end approach without EnKF bias performs better.

Conclusion: PSEF provides a probabilistic filtering framework that leverages synthetic trajectories and energy-score training. Its performance relative to EnKF and pure learning depends on problem non-Gaussianity; the approach offers principled guidance on when to apply EnKF corrections versus end-to-end learning, and highlights the role of realizability and mean-field considerations.

Abstract: Bayesian filtering of partially and noisily observed dynamical systems seeks to infer the evolving conditional distribution of the state of a dynamical system, given observations, in an online fashion. This Bayesian filtering distribution is the natural object for uncertainty quantification, but it is rarely available as a supervised learning target. However, one can often use the forecast model to generate synthetic system trajectories, along with synthetic observations. We introduce the proper scoring ensemble filter (PSEF), an ensemble data assimilation method based on training an analysis map to approximate the filtering distribution using only synthetic state--observation trajectories. The analysis step is represented as a permutation-invariant, transformer-based map that takes as input a forecast ensemble and observations, producing an analysis ensemble. Training is based on strictly proper scoring rules -- with the energy score used in our implementation -- so that probabilistic accuracy is rewarded over the whole probability distribution. We prove that, under a realizability assumption, the population objective is minimized by the true Bayesian filtering distribution. We also derive the finite-ensemble empirical objective used in training and relate its single state--observation trajectory form to the population objective, using a mean-field consistency argument. Numerical experiments show that the learned filter accurately approximates challenging filtering distributions, including nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors, and achieves stronger performance in data assimilation tasks than classical methods or learning-based methods with mean-squared-error objectives. For close-to-Gaussian problems, learning a correction to the EnKF is the best approach, while for highly non-Gaussian problems an end-to-end approach that discards this inductive bias is superior.

</details>


### [263] [Multipath Adaptive Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE with Raman Data Fusion for Cell Culture Process Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26520)
*Johnny Peng,Thanh Tung Khuat,Ellen Otte,Katarzyna Musial,Bogdan Gabrys*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive framework for early-stage bioreactor forecasting using GB-Latent ODE with MP-JIT-FT and Raman fusion; yields multiple plausible paths and improves accuracy over baselines on 38 runs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biopharmaceutical manufacturing requires reliable early forecasts to adjust feeding and control; current forecasting struggles due to sparse, irregular measurements, heterogeneous conditions, and potential divergence of similar early trajectories.

Method: Introduce GB-Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck; Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT) matching similar histories, clustering regimes, and fine-tuning regime-specific models to produce multiple plausible trajectories with confidence scores; fuse Raman spectroscopy as a soft sensor to enrich data.

Result: On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs across 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves best average rank and surpasses a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables; multi-path gains are strongest when local prefixes diverge; Raman fusion aids when early dynamics reflect later behavior.

Conclusion: Adaptive, regime-aware multi-path forecasting with data fusion improves early-stage bioprocess forecasts under sparse data, enabling timely interventions and robust predictions.

Abstract: Mammalian cell-culture processes underpin the manufacture of many biopharmaceuticals, yet keeping a run on track is hard: critical process parameters drift over days, and an off-specification trend is often confirmed too late to intervene. Early-stage, multi-day forecasts could enable timely adjustment of feeding, sampling, and control, but bioprocess forecasting is challenging because measurements are sparse and irregularly sampled, operating conditions are heterogeneous across cell lines and media, and runs with near-identical early behaviour can diverge into different futures. We propose an adaptive framework combining a Gated Bottleneck Latent Ordinary Differential Equation (GB-Latent ODE) with Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT). The GB-Latent ODE augments the stan dard Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck that compress high-dimensional sparse inputs, improving learning under limited data. Given a partially observed run, MP-JIT-FT retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters the local neighbourhood into candidate regimes, and fine-tunes a separate model per regime to produce multiple plausible paths, each with a reconstruction-based confidence score, not a single averaged forecast. We further fuse Raman spectroscopy data: a machine-learning soft sensor turns dense Raman spectra into pseudo-observations that enrich the sparse offline measurements for more robust training. On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs spanning 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves the best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Using local-divergence metrics, we show the multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge, whereas Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behaviour.

</details>


### [264] [Theory-Scale Auto-Formalization of Logics for Computer Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26525)
*Yuming Feng,Frederick Pu,One An,Osbert Bastani,Li Zhang,Jiani Huang,Xujie Si,Ziyang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LCS-Bench is a theory-scale auto-formalization benchmark for Lean based on Logics for Computer Science, featuring a semi-automated agentic pipeline and a rich dataset (327 items, ~4k Lean declarations, >85k lines). It enables five evaluation tracks and a definitional equivalence checker protocol. Results show the benchmark is high-quality and challenging, with state-of-the-art models scoring around 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scale and consistency: existing auto-formalization progress concentrates on isolated statements, while comprehensive, theory-scale formalization requires coherent, interdependent translations of many definitions, lemmas, and theorems. There is a need for a robust, scalable benchmark and evaluation protocol to stress-test models and guide progress in the field.

Method: A stand-alone, theory-scale benchmark built via a semi-automated, agentic pipeline leveraging concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, counterexample-informed sorry-filling, and expert faithfulness review. The artifact covers 327 textbook items, 4,076 Lean declarations, and >85k lines of Lean code. A data engine derives five evaluation tracks and a new evaluation protocol uses definitional equivalence checkers for fine-grained, faithful assessment.

Result: Evaluations on 14 models indicate high quality and faithfulness of the benchmark, and significant challenge: state-of-the-art models achieve only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks. Additional analyses provide insights into theory-scale auto-formalization and propose directions for future work.

Conclusion: LCS-Bench is a high-quality, faithful theory-scale auto-formalization benchmark that will drive progress in formal verification and theorem-proving research by offering scalable evaluation, richer tasks, and a pathway to more faithful automated formalization.

Abstract: Auto-formalization is critical for scalable formal verification, but existing progress largely focuses on isolated statements, while theory-scale auto-formalization, which coherently translates hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems, remains open due to challenges in consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness. In this paper, we introduce LCS-Bench, a stand-alone, theory-scale benchmark based on Logics for Computer Science. LCS-Bench is built through a novel semi-automated agentic pipeline that leverages concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, complemented by faithfulness review from human experts. The resulting artifact covers 327 textbook items, over 4,076 Lean declarations, and more than 85K lines of Lean code. The dataset supports broad evaluation through a data engine that automatically derives five tracks of evaluation benchmarks, measuring different aspects of auto-formalization and theorem-proving capabilities. We also introduce a novel evaluation protocol featuring definitional equivalence checkers, enabling more fine-grained and faithful assessment. Through extensive evaluation on 14 models, we demonstrate that (1) LCS-Bench is of high quality, consistent, and faithful; (2) the benchmark is challenging, with state-of-the-art models achieving only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks; and (3) our analysis reveals key findings regarding theory-scale auto-formalization and suggests promising directions for future work.

</details>


### [265] [Sample-efficient Transfer Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Reward Shaping and Policy-Ratio Reweighting Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26527)
*Wenjie Huang,Yang Li,Jingjia Teng,Mingwei Jin,Kai Song,Yougang Bian,Yongfu Li,Qisong Yang,Helai Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A safe transfer RL framework for autonomous highway lane changing that combines adaptive teacher intervention, teacher-guided safe transfer via reward shaping, and sample-weighted policy optimization; it achieves improved safety and learning efficiency across diverse traffic conditions using dual-source samples and real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transfer learning in policy learning for autonomous lane changing faces distribution shifts between source and target tasks, causing training oscillations and safety concerns during exploration in safety-critical scenarios. There is a need for safe, efficient transfer that preserves safety during learning.

Method: 1) Adaptive teacher intervention based on instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration and progressively fade intervention strength, generating dual-source samples for joint training. 2) Teacher-guided safe transfer via reward shaping that injects teacher policy action evaluation into the student’s learning, with guidance decaying as policy safety improves. 3) Teacher-guided weighted optimization that adjusts sample weights in policy optimization using a likelihood ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance.

Result: Experiments across varied traffic densities and the NGSIM real-world dataset show the proposed method surpasses baselines by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency, demonstrating efficacy and robustness of the safety-aware transfer strategy for autonomous lane changing under different traffic conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed safety-aware transfer framework effectively enhances safety and learning efficiency for autonomous highway lane changing, handling distribution shifts and safety concerns during transfer learning.

Abstract: Transfer learning improves policy learning efficiency by reusing knowledge from source tasks, providing a feasible paradigm for safe and efficient autonomous highway lane changing decision-making. Existing methods frequently encounter transfer mismatch induced by distribution shifts between source and target domains, leading to training oscillation and performance decline. Besides, target domain adaptation depends on exploratory interactions, which struggles to guarantee training safety in safety-critical lane changing cases. To tackle these limitations, this paper proposes a safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing. First, we design an adaptive teacher intervention mechanism based on instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration and fade intervention strength progressively, with theoretical analysis on return bounds for mixed behavior policy. This intervention also produces dual-source samples for joint training. Second, a teacher-guided safe transfer module embeds action evaluation information of teacher policy into student learning via reward shaping to boost training safety and efficiency, with teacher guidance decaying as policy safety rises. Third, a teacher-guided weighted optimization mechanism adjusts sample weights in policy optimization using a likelihood ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance. Experiments under varied traffic densities and validations on real-world NGSIM dataset reveal that our method surpasses baseline approaches by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency. Results verify the efficacy and robustness of our safety-aware transfer strategy for autonomous highway lane changing under various traffic conditions.

</details>


### [266] [CascadeFormer: Depth-Tapered Transformers Motivated by Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26538)
*Huzama Ahmad,Cao Viet Hai Nam,Se-Young Yun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes two efficiency methods for deep Transformers (CascadeFormer and CascadeFlow Pruning) guided by Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA); demonstrates improvements in latency, throughput, and perplexity; supports GFA with correlational/interventional evidence up to 1.2B parameters; discusses open questions for very large scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address diminishing returns of deepest layers in uniformly deep Transformer stacks and improve training/inference efficiency without sacrificing performance.

Method: CascadeFormer reduces width with depth to match information flow; CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers based on accumulated training gradients with no post hoc analysis; formalization via Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) in Pre-LayerNorm stacks; gradient at a layer = identity path + downstream functional paths; gradient fan-in decays with depth; experiments across transformers and ResNets; interventions include equalizing per-layer gradient norms and increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition.

Result: CascadeFormer achieves comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget, with 8.6% lower latency and 9.4% higher throughput; CascadeFlow Pruning outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and remains competitive on downstream accuracy; evidence supports GFA across scales up to 1.2B parameters; two interventions imply structure—not magnitude—is the bottleneck.

Conclusion: Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry offers a structural account for why deeper layers contribute less; downstream path density and layer design influence training gradients and layer importance; while gradient magnitude generalizations may hold beyond high-rank regimes, its behavior at ultra-large scales (100B+) remains an open question.

Abstract: Deep Transformers are composed of uniformly stacked residual blocks, yet their deepest layers often add little value. We present two efficiency methods that exploit this asymmetry. CascadeFormer tapers width with depth to match the uneven information flow across layers, achieving comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%. CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients, with no post hoc analysis. It outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and stays competitive on downstream accuracy. To motivate these methods, we propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) as a structural account of why deeper layers contribute less. In Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, the gradient at a layer is the sum of an identity path and all downstream functional paths, producing a gradient fan-in that decays linearly with depth (and quadratically under deep supervision), yielding richer gradients for early layers and sparser ones for later layers. We provide correlational and interventional evidence for GFA on models trained from scratch up to 1.2B parameters. Across Transformers and ResNets, accumulated training gradients follow the theoretical fan-in and are associated with post hoc layer importance. Two interventions point to structure rather than magnitude as the bottleneck: equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition restores and elevates it. Whether gradient magnitude proxies fan-in beyond high-rank regimes, and how these dynamics behave at the 100B+ scale, remain open questions.

</details>


### [267] [Can Large Language Models Reliably Code Qualitative Humanitarian Data? A Benchmark Study Against Human Expert Adjudication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26541)
*Jerome Marston,Tino Kreutzer,Salomé Garnier,Ella Boone,Phuong N Pham,Patrick Vinck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs can match experienced human coders on structured qualitative coding of humanitarian transcripts when guided by structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations, but they are not a substitute for human judgment and require governance, especially for high-risk categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the data bottleneck in humanitarian response by evaluating whether large language models can reliably code qualitative needs from affected populations, enabling scalable analysis while preserving accuracy and safety.

Method: Benchmark study comparing 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts. Evaluation combined Krippendorff's alpha for inter-rater reliability, discrepancy analysis (correct/near-correct/incorrect codes), and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria (discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, non-standard communication). Employed structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations.

Result: Several LLMs achieved deductive coding reliability comparable to experienced human coders, particularly with structured prompts and reasoning-enabled setups. However aggregate metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models differed in recognizing indirect needs, needs outside predefined categories, and protection concerns (e.g., physical safety, discrimination). LLMs can expand analytical capacity but should not replace human judgment. Effective use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, theme-specific performance monitoring, and tiered oversight to minimize high-consequence miscoding. Open-weights models deployed on self-hosted infrastructure may offer governance advantages for sensitive data.

Conclusion: LLMs have material potential to scale humanitarian data analysis but must be integrated with robust governance, domain-specific evaluation, and human-in-the-loop oversight. When used with appropriate constraints (codebooks, reasoning-enabled prompts, category-focused monitoring), they can augment capacity while preserving safety and accuracy.

Abstract: Data from affected populations are crucial for informing humanitarian response, but their value depends on timely and consistent interpretation of nuanced accounts of need. Humanitarian organizations often lack the staff, time, and specialist expertise required to analyze this information at scale. Large language models (LLMs) may expand this capacity, but their reliability for coding qualitative humanitarian data has not been directly established. This benchmark study compares 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts. Evaluation combined inter-rater reliability testing with Krippendorff's alpha, discrepancy analysis distinguishing correct, near-correct, and incorrect codes, and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria including discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, and non-standard communication styles. The authors find that multiple LLMs can perform deductive coding at reliability levels comparable to experienced human coders, especially when structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations are used. At the same time, aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models varied in recognizing needs expressed indirectly, needs outside predefined categories, and protection-relevant concerns such as physical safety and discrimination. These findings suggest that LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity, but not as substitutes for human judgment. Appropriate use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, attention to theme-specific performance, and tiered oversight focused on categories where miscoding would have the greatest programmatic consequences. For sensitive humanitarian data, open-weights models deployed on self-hosted infrastructure may offer a viable path for combining analytical scalability with stronger data governance.

</details>


### [268] [Revisiting Action Factorization for Complex Action Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26574)
*Timothy Flavin,Sandip Sen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-sectional evaluation of action-space factorization methods for PPO, SAC, DQN across discretized, hybrid, and continuous actions; introduces two lightweight environments CoopPush and Hybrid-Shoot; finds branching dueling architectures and Auto-Regressive actions perform best, with continuous SAC strongest but costlier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world control tasks often involve hybrid discrete-continuous actions. Current RL benchmarks/environments either use uniform action spaces or heavyweight implementations, hindering systematic comparison. This work aims to systematically compare factorization methods across action spaces and three algorithm families using lightweight, extensible benchmarks.

Method: Empirical study across three algorithms (PPO, SAC, DQN) and multiple factorization methods (Independent networks, Shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive) over four lightweight environments (Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush); 220 valid configurations after excluding incompatible pairings; development of two new C++ parallel, Gymnasium/PettingZoo–compliant environments (CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot); proposal of VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX (branching-critic variants); standardized evaluation to compare performance and compute trade-offs.

Result: Branching (dueling) architectures balance compute and performance most effectively; Auto-Regressive actions achieve the highest performance overall; native continuous SAC outperforms discrete/hybrid variants, but with higher computational cost.

Conclusion: Branching dueling structures with Hybrid/Auto-Regressive action handling offer strong performance-compute trade-offs for hybrid action problems; continuous SAC remains a top performer at additional cost; the two new lightweight environments and factorization comparisons provide practical guidance for method selection and reproducibility in scalable RL research.

Abstract: Many real-world control problems involve hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces. For example, steering and signaling in autonomous driving, and aiming and firing in robotics or video-games. Despite real-world hybrid factorization and reinforcement learning framework support for complex action spaces (e.g., Gymnasium, PettingZoo, TorchRL, SeedRL, Mujoco, etc), the default environments within those frameworks often implement uniform action space configurations (LunarLander, Walker2D, Cheetah, SMAC, SUMO, Ant, Atari). Landmark hybrid-action benchmarks (RoboCup 2D HFO, SC2LE, Platform, CARLA, etc) are mostly heavyweight or archival implementations originating from papers which test one or a small number of competing factorization methods on one kind of control. This article provides a cross-sectional study of factorization methods [independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive] on each of three families of algorithms [PPO, SAC, DQN] across three action spaces [discretized, hybrid, continuous] over four lightweight environments [Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush]. Accounting for some invalid pairings such as joint-continuous, we are left with 220 configurations to analyze each method. We provide two new C++ parallel gymnasium and petting-zoo compliant environments [CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot] to isolate particular challenges such as state-dependent inter-action dependence. Finally, we introduce VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX which use a branching critic to assign credit to multi-headed PPO. These variants out-perform all other tested PPO factorizations. Our results suggest that branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance most effectively, with Auto-Regressive actions reaching the highest performance overall and native continuous SAC outperforming discrete and hybrid algorithms, albiet both at increased computational cost.

</details>


### [269] [SharQ: Bridging Activation Sparsity and FP4 Quantization for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26587)
*Haoqian Meng,Yilun Luo,Yafei Zhao,Wenyuan Liu,Huaqing Zheng,Xindian Ma,Peng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SharQ enables training-free activation compression for LLMs by online sparse-dense decomposition, combining input-adaptive N:M pruning with FP4 quantization to recover partial accuracy while delivering runtime speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-bit FP formats (FP4) and semi-structured sparsity promise substantial efficiency, but activations in LLMs have input-dependent outliers that distort block scales in FP4, and naive N:M sparsity can discard moderate values, worsening accuracy. A calibration-free method that jointly handles sparsity and quantization is needed.

Method: For each activation tensor, SharQ generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantizes it to FP4, and defines a dense residual relative to the quantized backbone rather than the unquantized sparse values. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone, while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for mask-induced loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, and a fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. No calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning is required.

Result: Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43–63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks and generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On RTX 5090, SharQ yields 2.2–2.4× latency reduction versus FP16 and 1.2–1.4× throughput improvement versus FP8 in language-model serving, and up to 1.58× speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation with SageAttention.

Conclusion: SharQ provides a calibration-free, format-agnostic approach to jointly exploit activation sparsity and FP4 quantization with negligible retraining, delivering substantial accuracy recovery and practical speedups; code is available at the provided repository.

Abstract: Low-bit floating-point formats and semi-structured sparsity are increasingly supported by modern accelerators, yet combining them for LLM activation compression remains challenging: activations contain input-dependent outliers that dominate block scales in FP4 quantization, and directly applying N:M sparsity masks discards moderate values, coupling sparsification loss with quantization error. We introduce SharQ, a training-free inference method that bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization through an online sparse--dense decomposition. For each activation tensor, SharQ generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantizes it to FP4, and defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone rather than the unquantized sparse values. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for both mask-induced activation loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, and a fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. SharQ requires no calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning. Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43--63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks, and generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On an RTX 5090, SharQ delivers 2.2--2.4$\times$ latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2--1.4$\times$ throughput improvement over FP8 in language model serving, and up to 1.58$\times$ speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation when combined with SageAttention. Our code is available at https://github.com/actypedef/SharQ.

</details>


### [270] [Empirical Software Engineering TerraProbe: A Layered-Oracle Framework for Detecting Deceptive Fixes in LLM-Assisted Terraform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26590)
*Manar Alsaid,Chimdumebi Nebolisa,Faris Abbas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TerraProbe provides a five-layer evaluation framework to assess LLM-assisted Terraform security repairs; it reveals that scanner-level success (e.g., removal of specific findings) overstates true repair quality, with many deceptive fixes, and offers a taxonomy and replication package.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Security misconfigurations in Terraform IaC are common. Evaluations that rely on static-analysis results are insufficient; there is a need for end-to-end validation of planning, behavior, and security intent in LLM-generated repairs.

Method: Apply TerraProbe across 288 first-pass repairs from multiple LLMs (Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) on 68 real-world TerraDS modules and 28 injected-defect modules. Measure with a five-layer oracle: check scanner results, full-cleanliness, Terraform planning success, and plan comparison; perform human adjudication; develop a four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes; IAM permission analysis; provide replication package and Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework.

Result: Targeted Checkov removal reaches 83.3% for primary model, but full-cleanliness only 10.4%. Terraform planning succeeds 39.6% and plan comparison reachable 38.5%. 71.4% of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes. Deceptive-fix rates similar across models (57.1–71.4%), with non-significant pairwise p-values. Four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes validated (Cohen’s kappa 0.78, Krippendorff alpha 0.76). IAM analysis shows wildcard grants persist in CKV2 cases.

Conclusion: TerraProbe offers a robust evaluation methodology and replication package to distinguish intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false positives, via a Multi-Layer Oracle framework and a taxonomy of deceptive fixes.

Abstract: Security misconfigurations in Terraform Infrastructure-as-Code are a growing risk in cloud deployments, and large language models are increasingly used as automated repair agents. Existing evaluations often treat a repair as successful when the targeted static-analysis finding disappears, without checking planning validity, behavioral change, or security intent. This paper presents TerraProbe, a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repair. We apply TerraProbe to 288 first-pass repairs generated by gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet across 68 real-world TerraDS modules and 28 controlled injected-defect modules. The results show that targeted Checkov removal overstates repair success. Although targeted removal reaches 83.3 percent for the primary model, full-scanner cleanliness drops to 10.4 percent, Terraform planning succeeds for 39.6 percent, and plan comparison is reachable for 38.5 percent. Human adjudication further shows that 71.4 percent of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes that pass automated checks while leaving the underlying vulnerability in place. This pattern is statistically indistinguishable across the three models, with deceptive-fix rates from 57.1 percent to 71.4 percent and pairwise Fisher exact p-values above 0.10. The paper introduces a four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes, validated with Cohen kappa of 0.78 and Krippendorff alpha of 0.76. IAM permission analysis confirms that wildcard Resource grants persist in all nine CKV2 AWS 11 deceptive-fix cases. TerraProbe contributes an evaluation methodology, a replication package, and the Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework for distinguishing intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false successes.

</details>


### [271] [Sketched Linear Contrastive Learning: Approximation, Optimization, and Statistical Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26617)
*Ziyan Chen,Zhongzhu Zhou,Ding-Xuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper derives a scaling law for a sketched linear model in contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable model, yielding an explicit law in sketch dimension M, sample size N, and an effective optimization horizon L_eff γ; the cross-term is absorbed by bias/variance and does not affect the upper-bound scaling, highlighting how learning interactions between two views changes optimization and finite-sample noise scaling compared to standard linear regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is limited theoretical understanding of how contrastive learning scales with data, model size, and compute; this work extends scaling-law analysis to contrastive settings using a stylized sketched linear model with two views.

Method: Set up a sketched linear model for contrastive learning with paired Gaussian latent variables; train a bilinear contrastive score via full-batch empirical gradient descent; use a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition; derive a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term; show the cross term is controlled by bias/variance and does not affect the upper-bound scaling.

Result: Main theorem provides an explicit scaling law in variables M (sketch dimension), N (sample size), and L_eff γ (effective optimization horizon times a conditioning parameter); the cross-term does not alter upper-bound scaling; contrastive settings require learning interactions between two views, affecting how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time.

Conclusion: This work offers the first theoretical step toward understanding scaling in contrastive learning under a stylized setting and guides how to balance model size, data, and optimization compute for contrastive models.

Abstract: Scaling laws describe how learning performance varies with model size, data size, and compute. While recent theoretical work has established scaling laws for sketched linear regression, much less is understood for contrastive representation learning. In this paper, we study a sketched linear model for contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable setup. The learner observes only sketched views of two correlated variables and trains a bilinear contrastive score by full-batch empirical gradient descent. We analyze a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition, where we derive a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term. The cross term is controlled by the bias and variance and therefore does not affect the upper-bound scaling. Our main theorem gives an explicit scaling law with respect to sketch dimension $M$, sample size $N$, and effective optimization horizon $L_{\mathrm{eff}}γ$. Compared with standard linear-regression scaling laws, the contrastive setting must learn interactions between two views, and this changes how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time. This provides a first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning and gives guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute.

</details>


### [272] [Discovering Millions of Interpretable Features with Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26620)
*XinYang He,Wei Wang,Bing Zhao,Xuan Ren,WenBo Li,WeiXu Qiao,Hu Wei,Lin Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We present Qwen3-Instruct SAE, a suite of sparse autoencoders trained on the Qwen3 instruction-tuned model family (1.7B, 4B, 8B). Layer-wise SAEs are trained at residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs (with 8B using a subset of residual layers). Evaluations show layer- and component-dependent sparsity-fidelity trade-offs; a case study demonstrates causal steering of refusal behavior. The release enables analysis of sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for interpretable, sparse representations in large instruction-tuned LMs and reduce computational barriers to training SAEs by providing a comprehensive, publicly available suite across model scales and activation sites.

Method: Train layer-wise sparse autoencoders on Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Qwen3-8B. For 1.7B/4B, apply SAEs to residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs; for 8B, apply SAEs to a subset of residual layers. Evaluate using activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics; perform a case study on refusal steering to establish causal influence of SAE features.

Result: Found distinct sparsity–fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Demonstrated that selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior. Release provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations and mechanism-level interventions.

Conclusion: Qwen3-Instruct SAE offers a practical, scalable resource to study sparse representations and feature-level mechanisms in instruction-tuned LMs, enabling behavioral interventions such as refusal steering and contributing to interpretability research.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a powerful tool for decomposing superposed language model representations into sparse and interpretable features. However, training SAEs is computationally expensive, and available open-source SAE models remain limited. In this work, we introduce \textbf{Qwen3-Instruct SAE}, a comprehensive suite of SAEs trained on the Qwen3 instruction-tuned model family, covering Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Qwen3-8B. For Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B, we train layer-wise SAEs at three key activation sites: residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs. For Qwen3-8B, we train SAEs on a subset of residual stream layers. We systematically evaluate these SAEs using both activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics, revealing distinct sparsity--fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of Qwen3-Instruct SAE through a refusal-steering case study, showing that selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior. Our release provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models

</details>


### [273] [From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26629)
*Evan Ning,Wei Xue,Dong Lou,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes activation-space regularization with pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to tackle catastrophic forgetting in LMs, arguing polysemanticity makes weight-space methods like EWC ineffective. Demonstrates a loss that balances stability/plasticity via an SAE feature dictionary; shows EWC as a special case; requires no replay data and is memory-efficient due to lower-dimensional feature space. Empirically strong on TRACE and MedCL; supports the polysemanticity thesis by showing linear separability in SAE space but not in weight space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in large language models where weight-space importance estimates (e.g., EWC) are too coarse due to polysemantic representations. Seek a stability-plasticity mechanism in activation space that preserves task-relevant concepts without replay. 

Method: Use pretrained Sparse Autoencoders as a monosemantic feature dictionary to project activations into a space with explicit semantic features. Derive a constrained optimization loss that uses the SAE dictionary to balance stability and plasticity. EWC is recovered as a one-sided weight-space penalty special case. Compute a compact SAE feature mask from current-task data and retain only this mask for future training; no data from past tasks is stored. Operating in the lower-dimensional activation space yields memory efficiency. 

Result: On TRACE and MedCL benchmarks, achieves the strongest performance among methods without task-specific architectural components and surpasses standard weight-space regularization like EWC. Empirical evidence for polysemanticity: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis; weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

Conclusion: Activation-space regularization via SAE feature dictionaries offers a memory-efficient, data-friendly alternative to weight-space regularization, with empirical support for polysemanticity. It subsumes EWC as a special case and improves continual learning performance without replay, suggesting activation-space constraints may be a more targeted way to preserve knowledge in large models.

Abstract: Weight-space regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) are the standard approach to catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. However, those methods tend to underperform when applied to large language models. We argue that such underperformance can be partly explained by the ``polysemantic'' nature of large language models: per-weight importance estimates utilized by EWC-style regularization are too coarse and cannot isolate the knowledge that needs protection. In this paper, we propose regularizing instead in the model's activation space, using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a monosemantic feature dictionary. From the perspective of constrained optimization, we derive a new loss function that uses the SAE feature dictionary to explicitly balance stability and plasticity, and show that EWC is a special case in the one-sided weight-space penalty setting. Unlike replay-based methods that store or revisit examples from earlier tasks, our method requires no previous-task data after mask construction: current-task data is used to compute a compact SAE feature mask, and only this mask is retained for later training. Further, since the feature space has significantly lower dimensionality than the parameter space, the proposed method is more memory efficient. On the TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest result among approaches without introducing task-specific architectural components, also surpassing traditional weight-space regularization methods like EWC. Beyond performance comparisons, we provide empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis, and weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

</details>


### [274] [Target-Aware Bandit Allocation for Scalable Surrogate Optimization in Chemical Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26657)
*Mohammad Haddadnia,Yuvan Chali,Abhilash Jayaraj,Constance Kraay,Joana Reis,Felix Strieth-Kalthoff,Haribabu Arthanari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bandit-guided surrogate optimization (BOBa) for ultra-large discrete spaces, enabling efficient virtual screening by partitioning the action space into arms and adaptively allocating surrogate inference and evaluations to promising partitions; demonstrates tunable tradeoffs and practicality on billion/trillion-scale molecular libraries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
