Motivation: Massive discrete search spaces (billions/trillions of compounds) and expensive evaluations; full-library surrogate inference is computationally prohibitive; need scalable, sample-efficient optimization for drug discovery.

Method: Model partitions of the action space as arms in a multi-armed bandit, apply optimism-under-uncertainty bandits to focus inference/evaluations on promising partitions; adaptively allocate computation across partitions rather than building a full surrogate over the entire library; use meaningful action space partitioning.

Result: Empirical results on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries show improved screening efficiency; demonstrate a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost; establish viability of ultra-large library virtual screening.

Conclusion: BOBa provides a scalable framework for high-utility candidate identification in massive discrete spaces, balancing inference cost and screening performance; enables practical optimization over current libraries and paves the way for ultra-large library virtual screening.

Abstract: Identifying high-utility candidates from massive discrete spaces under expensive evaluations is a recurring challenge across the sciences, with structure-based drug discovery as a prominent example. While surrogate-based optimization can increase sample efficiency by reducing the number of expensive evaluations, modern molecular libraries have reached billions to trillions of compounds, making full-library surrogate inference itself a major computational bottleneck. We introduce BOBa, a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that eliminates full-library inference by adaptively allocating computation across partitions of the action space. By treating partitions as arms in a multi-armed bandit, BOBa concentrates inference and evaluations on empirically promising partitions while maintaining principled exploration. Experiments on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries demonstrate that optimism-under-uncertainty bandits, combined with meaningful action space partitioning, are essential for effective allocation of inference and evaluations. Our findings reveal a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost, which supports practical optimization over current libraries, and establishes a viable route to ultra-large library virtual screening.

</details>


### [275] [Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26662)
*Mingsong Yan,Zhida Wang,Sui Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits of GNDEs, proving well-posedness and rigorous convergence on sparse graphs; derives DTO/OTD discretization rates; and demonstrates zero-shot transfer to larger graphs via experiments on graphons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability and transferability of continuous-time graph dynamics modeled by GNDEs. Establish a rigorous link to graphon theory, quantify trajectory/convergence rates on sparse graphs, and analyze discretization training schemes.

Method: Introduce Graphon-NDEs and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits of GNDEs. Prove well-posedness. Establish trajectory-wise convergence with rate O((α_n n)^(-1/2)) up to log factors, and uniform-in-time gradient bounds for adjoint systems. Analyze discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) under explicit Euler with M steps, showing O(1/M) and O(1/M^2) discrepancies (up to sparsity/log factors). Validate with experiments on HSBM and tent graphons; perform zero-shot transfer across graphon classes.

Result: Theoretical guarantees for size-transfer on sparse graphs and discretization consistency; empirical rates supported by experiments; demonstration of zero-shot transfer to larger graphs.

Conclusion: Grounds a principled framework for GNDE size-transfer via graphon limits, offering both rigorous insights and practical training/deployment guidance; confirms transferability and discretization behavior with experimental evidence.

Abstract: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

</details>


### [276] [PersistentKV: Page-Aware Decode Scheduling for Long-Context LLM Serving on Commodity GPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26666)
*Muhammad Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive, page-aware KV decode scheduling (PersistentKV) with a calibrated policy improves autoregressive LLM serving throughput; achieves up to ~1.4x gains over a FlashInfer baseline across varied long-context workloads by switching between PersistentKV modes and FlashInfer, and by using a non-empty-task workqueue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV-cache movement dominates serving latency in autoregressive LLMs; the best single-kernel is not always optimal across varying batch sizes and context lengths; adaptive, page-aware scheduling can better utilize GPU resources.

Method: Introduce PersistentKV: a native block-table decode attention engine for grouped-query attention with KV-head grouping, tile reuse of K,V, and native page tables; a compact workqueue that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. Develop a calibrated adaptive policy to select among FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for B1 long-context steps, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for B8 long-context steps. Evaluate on an RTX 3060 FP16 with page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128; compare against FlashInfer; fix thresholds on calibration traces.

Result: Throughput gains observed: 1.063–1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads; 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace. On the B4 bimodal boundary, the policy avoids regression by selecting FlashInfer. Demonstrates a concrete niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling and shows that work assignment is a decisive variable in serving systems, not just attention math.

Conclusion: Adaptive page-aware scheduling combined with KV-head grouping and non-empty-task workqueues constitutes a practical, effective augmentation to autoregressive LLM serving, capturing performance gains beyond traditional attention kernel optimizations.

Abstract: Autoregressive large language model (LLM) serving is increasingly limited by key-value (KV) cache movement rather than dense matrix multiplication. Modern paged-attention systems reduce KV-cache fragmentation and mature kernels such as FlashInfer provide highly optimized native-paged decode attention. However, the best single-kernel implementation is not always the best serving schedule: low-active long-context decode can under-utilize commodity GPUs, while mixed sequence lengths introduce a tension between many exact-length launches and coarse padded batches. We present PersistentKV, a native block-table decode attention engine and page-aware scheduling study for grouped-query attention (GQA). PersistentKV maps work by KV-head group, is designed to reuse K,V tiles across grouped query heads, supports native page tables, and adds a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. On an RTX 3060 with FP16, page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128, and identical correctness tolerance against FlashInfer, a calibrated adaptive policy selects FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for B1 long-context steps, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for B8 long-context steps. With thresholds and split counts fixed on calibration traces, one held-out trace seed improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads and by 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace. On the B4 bimodal boundary case, the policy avoids the PersistentKV regression by selecting FlashInfer. These results identify a concrete systems niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling and show that work assignment, not only attention math, is a decisive serving-system variable.

</details>


### [277] [Algorithmic Foundations of Deep Learning: Complexity-Theoretic Rates and a Characterization of Universal Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26705)
*Anastasis Kratsios,Simone Brugiapaglia,Bum Jun Kim,Gregory Cousins,Haitz Sáez de Ocáriz Borde*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NN expressivity arises from algorithmic complexity; universality requires non-affine nonlinearity; NN circuits can emulate real-valued circuits; results include universal approximation, minimax optimality on Besov classes, logarithmic-error for holomorphic functions, and emulation of numerical algorithms; a tropical DP circuit yields highly efficient NN parameter counts for shortest-path problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of regularity-based views of NN expressivity by introducing a computation- and circuit-theoretic perspective; establish when NNs are universal approximators and connect to algorithmic efficiency and complexity.

Method: Define definable NN models under a parallelization condition, allowing multivariate nonlinearities; prove a necessary-and-sufficient condition for universality (presence of a non-affine nonlinearity); derive universal approximation for continuous functions, minimax-optimal results for Besov classes, and logarithmic-error for holomorphic functions; show how standard algorithms (Newton-Raphson, power iteration) can be emulated by NNs; construct a tropical dynamic-programming circuit for shortest-path that compiles to NNs with sharply reduced parameter count.

Result: Universal approximation holds for continuous functions; minimax-optimal approximations for Besov classes; logarithmic-inverse error complexity for holomorphic functions; ability to emulate numerical algorithms within NN architectures; shortest-path example yields NN with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially better than generic Lipschitz bounds (≈ ε^{-c k^2}).

Conclusion: NN complexity is governed by algorithmic complexity in addition to regularity; a broad class of NN models becomes universal with a non-affine nonlinearity; the framework unifies approximation theory with computation, enabling efficient NN constructions that implement algorithms and perform tasks like optimization and dynamic programming with tight parameter budgets.

Abstract: Feedforward neural network (NN) expressivity is typically studied by emulating optimal basis-expansion schemes. While powerful, this perspective is incomplete: it primarily captures complexity through regularity, and therefore does not distinguish intuitively simple and complicated objects with comparable regularity, such as the square-root function and a typical Brownian path.
  The guiding message is that neural networks should be viewed not only as flexible basis functions, but also as models of computation. If a function is computable by a real-valued circuit over a prescribed elementary gate language, then it can be computed to comparable accuracy by an NN with explicit depth, width, and non-zero-parameter bounds controlled by the depth, width, gate count, and gate structure. Thus, neural-network complexity is not governed by regularity alone, but also by algorithmic complexity. We then show that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition, allowing possibly multivariate non-linearities such as attention or layer normalization, is a universal approximator if and only if it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
  The scope of our theory is illustrated by deducing universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal approximation guarantees for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and by showing that NNs can emulate numerical algorithms such as Newton-Raphson root finding and power iteration without architecture-specific arguments. Its precision is illustrated by shortest-path computation on $k$-vertex graphs: compiling the tropical dynamic-programming circuit yields NNs with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving in 1/ε over the generic $O(ε^{-c k^2})$ Lipschitz-approximation scale, for a constant c>0.

</details>


### [278] [HyperDFlash: MHC-Aligned Block Speculative Decoding with Gated Residual Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26744)
*Luxi Lin,Shuang Peng,Rui Ma,Junhao Hua,Shuwei Fan,Zhengda Qin,Qiang Wang,Hongjian Sun,Fangmin Chen,Songwei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HyperDFlash is a block-parallel speculative decoding framework for DeepSeek-V4's multi-hyper-connection (MHC) architecture. It achieves better draft quality and speed by aligning with the MHC residual streams via (1) pre-collapse residual conditioning and (2) a lightweight gated residual reducer, plus targeted KL distillation on the LM-head. It outperforms native MTP and vanilla DFlash on math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address degradation in draft accuracy due to error accumulation in the MTP module when switching to a multi-path MHC architecture. Existing one-pass DFlash cannot align with MHC residual streams, leading to misalignment and poorer drafting performance.

Method: Two model-aligned optimizations for MHC residual streams: (1) use pre-collapse residual states as the exclusive conditioning signal to preserve multi-path information and align with the target model's native prediction pathway; (2) replace the heavy linear compressor with a lightweight gated residual reducer, inheriting parameters from the built-in hyper-connection head for input-aware path aggregation with ~1000x fewer parameters. Additionally, apply targeted KL distillation loss to the LM-head to regularize predictions toward the full target distribution during training.

Result: HyperDFlash consistently improves over the native MTP baseline and vanilla DFlash adaptation across math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks. It achieves substantial gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speedup, validating MHC alignment, gated reduction, and targeted distillation as effective design choices for high-performance speculative decoding.

Conclusion: MHC-aligned, lightweight architectural adjustments combined with distillation yield robust, faster speculative decoding that generalizes across domains, with dramatically reduced parameter footprint compared to generic compressors.

Abstract: We present HyperDFlash, a block-parallel speculative decoding framework tailored to the novel multi-hyper-connection (MHC) architecture proposed by DeepSeek-V4. Despite the strong initial-token drafting performance of the native Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) module in DeepSeek-V4, its draft accuracy degrades sharply at later positions, as error accumulation from unverified intermediate tokens harms acceptance rates. Although the original DFlash method supports efficient one-pass block drafting, it cannot be seamlessly adapted to the MHC paradigm, since the multi-path residual stream of DeepSeek-V4 induces feature misalignment with conventional drafting designs. To resolve this mismatch, we propose two model-aligned optimizations for MHC residual streams. First, we adopt pre-collapse residual states as the exclusive conditioning signal, preserving multi-path structural information and aligning the drafter with the native prediction pathway of the target model. Second, we replace the heavy generic linear compressor with a lightweight gated residual reducer, whose parameters are inherited from the built-in hyper-connection head. This design yields input-aware path aggregation with three orders of magnitude fewer parameters while maintaining architectural alignment. We further enhance training via a targeted KL distillation loss applied to the LM-head, which regularizes predictions against the full target probability distribution and improves draft quality at early training stages. Experiments across math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks show that HyperDFlash consistently outperforms both the native MTP baseline and vanilla DFlash adaptation. It achieves substantial gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speedup, validating the effectiveness of MHC alignment, gated reduction, and targeted distillation for high-performance speculative decoding.

</details>


### [279] [Structure Before Collapse: Transient semantic geometry in next-token prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26749)
*Yize Zhao,Isabel Papadimitriou,Christos Thrampoulidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transient semantic clustering under one-hot training: early emergence of latent structure in language-model representations, followed by a transition to a symmetric, equally separated configuration predicted by Neural Collapse; Gram-matrix analysis and a modified unconstrained features model are used.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain how gradient descent discovers latent semantic structure under one-hot supervision in next-token prediction and reconcile with Neural Collapse.

Method: Construct three synthetic settings where inputs carry latent semantic factors but are mapped to distinct one-hot labels; analyze representation geometry during training; track Gram matrix evolution; propose a preliminary modification to the unconstrained features model to capture emergent geometry.

Result: Semantic attributes cluster in early training without explicit supervision; the structure is transient and gives way to the symmetric NC state with sufficient capacity/time; Gram-matrix dynamics reveal the phase transition.

Conclusion: The emergent semantic geometry is a transient phenomenon under one-hot training; understanding it requires modeling training dynamics beyond static end-state descriptions; the proposed modification to the unconstrained features model provides a first step to capture early geometry and inform future theory and architectures.

Abstract: Neural Collapse predicts that balanced one-hot classification pushes model representations to be equally far from each other; a symmetric configuration that depends only on the output label and ignores any semantic similarity in the inputs. This creates a puzzle: next-token prediction language models are trained predominantly (as context length increases) with one-hot labels: the same context is very unlikely to appear twice in training with different labels. However, they clearly learn latent structural features. That is, despite the one-hot training regime, a language model's contextual embeddings represent the fact that the next word in ''Mary broke the ___'' is likely to be filled by tokens in the latent classes of a) medium-sized, b) rigid, c) inanimate nouns. How does gradient descent find such categorical semantic structure when co-occurrence statistics collapse to one-hot sparsity, eliminating any shared next-tokens among different contexts? To investigate this tension we identify three synthetic controlled settings where inputs have latent semantic factors but are mapped to distinct one-hot labels. We find that semantic geometry emerges early in training, and that representations cluster by shared attributes despite receiving no explicit supervision to do so. This structure is transient: with sufficient capacity and time, the model eventually reaches the predicted symmetric state where all representations are equally separated. We study this phase transition through Gram matrix analysis and propose a preliminary modification to the commonly used unconstrained features model to capture the emergent semantic geometry.

</details>


### [280] [Automating Potential-based Reward Shaping with Vision Language Model Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27180)
*Henrik Müller,Daniel Kudenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VLM-PBRS learns a PBRS potential function from vision-language model preferences to accelerate RL with sparse rewards, using small VLMs to reduce cost; preserves optimal policies and improves sample efficiency; validated in Meta-World and Franka Kitchen; shows trade-off between VLM label accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse rewards hinder exploration and make credit assignment difficult; naive reward shaping risks reward hacking; PBRS preserves the optimal policy but relies on a heuristic potential function; this work automates potential learning via VLM feedback to reduce hand-tuning.

Method: Query a lightweight vision-language model for pairwise image preferences and train a model of the potential function from these preferences; integrate the learned potential into PBRS; use small, efficient VLMs to keep cost down; evaluate in Meta-World and Franka Kitchen and analyze relation between label accuracy and learning efficiency.

Result: Demonstrates accelerated learning (improved sample efficiency) and robustness to reward hacking; potential function guided by VLM preferences preserves optimal policies; smaller VLMs yield cheaper but noisier preferences yet still beneficial.

Conclusion: First application of VLM preference-based learning to synthesize a PBRS potential function; offers a principled, low-cost approach using small VLMs; empirically shows improved sample efficiency and robustness, with a clear link between VLM label accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Sparse rewards are inherently challenging for reinforcement learning agents as they lack intermediate feedback to guide exploration and to correctly attribute the sparse success rewards to relevant parts of the trajectory. Naive reward shaping can induce reward hacking, yielding policies that exploit auxiliary signals instead of solving the intended task. Potential-based reward shaping (PBRS) guarantees preservation of the optimal policy set, but requires the definition of a heuristic potential function over the state space. In this work, we introduce the VLM-guided PBRS framework VLM-PBRS that learns the potential function directly from vision language model (VLM) feedback. We query a lightweight VLM to obtain preferences over image pairs and train a model of the potential function using these preferences. As this approach is based on potential-based reward shaping, it preserves the original optimal policies, and removes the need for expert-designed reward shaping terms. Because large VLMs are prohibitively expensive to invoke repeatedly during policy learning, we employ smaller, more computationally efficient VLMs. Although the resulting preference labels are less accurate, empirical evidence shows that the preference labels can still be used to accelerate learning. We validate our method empirically in the Meta-World and Franka Kitchen environments and highlight the connection between VLM preference label accuracy and sample efficiency improvements. Our contributions are threefold: (1) the first application of VLM preference-based learning to synthesize a potential function for PBRS, (2) a principled, low-cost solution that leverages small VLMs, and (3) extensive empirical demonstration of improved sample efficiency and robustness to reward hacking.

</details>


### [281] [Batch-Invariant Spectral Intelligence for Robust and Explainable Insect Authentication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26757)
*Majharulislam Babor,Giacomo Rossi,Annalisa Altavilla,Oliver Schlüter,Marina M. -C. Höhne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Batch-Invariant Spectral Network (BISN) for robust insect species authentication from near-infrared spectra across production batches; achieves 0.93 mean leave-one-batch-out accuracy, with interpretable lipid/protein features and public code/dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NIR-based insect species authentication suffers from batch-to-batch spectral variation, hindering reliability and regulatory compliance in industrial settings; a cross-batch robust and interpretable method is needed.

Method: End-to-end BISN with a learnable preprocessing module initialised using Savitzky-Golay filtering; employs an entropy-regularised adversarial objective to suppress batch-specific variation before learning species features; contrasts with Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks (DANN) by suppressing batch effects prior to feature extraction; evaluated on 2,700 spectra from three species (Acheta domesticus, Hermetia illucens, Tenebrio molitor) collected across three production batches with leave-one-batch-out cross-validation.

Result: Mean leave-one-batch-out accuracy: 0.93 (SD 0.04); strongest baseline is outperformed by ~4 percentage points; explainable AI analysis indicates model decisions rely on lipid and protein absorption regions across folds, linking performance to known insect biochemistry; dataset and code are publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.

Conclusion: BISN improves cross-batch robustness and biochemical interpretability for automated insect species authentication under realistic industrial conditions; the approach is potentially extensible to other spectral batch-variation problems and supports reproducibility via open-source data and code.

Abstract: Edible insects offer an efficient source of alternative protein, requiring less land, water and emitting less greenhouse gas than conventional livestock. However, their successful integration into the food supply chain demands reliable species authentication to control allergen exposure, prevent adulteration, and meet regulatory standards. Near-infrared spectroscopy provides a rapid analytical tool, but its performance drops when applied to production batches unseen during training due to batch-to-batch variation in spectral measurements. We introduce the Batch-Invariant Spectral Network (BISN), an end-to-end framework that combines a learnable preprocessing module, initialised with Savitzky-Golay filtering, with an entropy-regularised adversarial objective to suppress batch-specific spectral variation. In contrast to Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks, which enforce domain adaptation only after feature extraction, BISN suppress batch-effects before species-specific features are learned. Using 2,700 spectra from three species (Acheta domesticus, Hermetia illucens, and Tenebrio molitor) collected across three independent production batches, BISN achieves a mean leave-one-batch-out accuracy of 0.93 (standard deviation 0.04), outperforming the strongest baseline by four percent. Further insights gained by using explainable AI confirm that model decisions consistently rely on the lipid and protein absorption regions across all folds, connecting predictive performance to known insect biochemistry. BISN addresses both cross-batch robustness and biochemical interpretability for automated insect species authentication under realistic industrial conditions. The source code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/majharB/bisn.

</details>


### [282] [Escaping Iterative Parameter-Space Noise: Differentially Private Learning with a Hypernetwork](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26772)
*Naoki Nishikawa,Shokichi Takakura,Satoshi Hasegawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A DP learning framework that replaces iterative gradient-based updates with a hypernetwork that maps privatized low-dimensional dataset embeddings to the target model's parameters, reducing the noise impact of DP-SGD. The approach yields theoretical utility gains and improved FID in diffusion-model LoRA fine-tuning compared to DP-SGD and other public-data-guided methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP-SGD's repeated injection of high-dimensional noise into gradient updates degrades utility. There is a need for a DP training paradigm that confines privacy noise to a low-dimensional representation and leverages public data to guide model parameter generation.

Method: Convert private data into low-dimensional embeddings, aggregate and add DP noise to produce a privatized dataset embedding, and train a hypernetwork on public data to map this embedding to the target model’s parameters. Privacy noise is injected only once into the low-dimensional representation. The method is demonstrated by applying LoRA-style fine-tuning of diffusion models.

Result: Theoretically, under a fixed privacy budget, models produced by this approach attain higher utility than DP-SGD in synthetic settings. Empirically, the method achieves lower Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) than LoRA models trained with DP-SGD and other public-data-guided approaches on diffusion models.

Conclusion: Injecting privacy noise in a low-dimensional embedding via a hypernetwork-guided parameter generation can substantially reduce the adverse effects of DP noise, offering improved DP utility and compatibility with public-data-informed strategies.

Abstract: Differentially private (DP) training of neural networks is often hindered by the large amount of noise required by gradient-based methods such as DP-SGD, which repeatedly inject high-dimensional noise in parameter space throughout training. In this paper, we propose a new framework for DP learning that avoids iterative optimization in parameter space. Instead of updating the target model using privatized gradients, we employ a hypernetwork trained on public datasets to map a private dataset to the parameters of the target model. Specifically, each example is embedded into a low-dimensional representation, the embeddings are aggregated and perturbed to obtain a DP dataset embedding, and the hypernetwork generates the target model parameters from this noisy embedding. Because privacy noise is injected only once into a low-dimensional dataset representation, our approach can significantly reduce the adverse effect of noise. We theoretically show in a synthetic setting that, under a fixed privacy budget, models produced by our approach achieve higher utility than those trained with DP-SGD. Moreover, we apply our approach to LoRA fine-tuning of diffusion models and show that it achieves lower FID than LoRA models trained with DP-SGD and other public-data-guided methods.

</details>


### [283] [Hallucination in World Models is Predictable and Preventable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27326)
*Nicklas Hansen,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MMBench2 reveals that hallucinations in visual world models are driven by data-coverage gaps and can be mitigated via a data-centric pipeline: coverage-aware sampling and curiosity-guided data collection, enabling data-efficient fine-tuning of a 350M model (≈50 real trajectories).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand and mitigate hallucination in action-controllable visual world models by diagnosing its data-coverage roots and developing actionable data-centric methods for detection and mitigation.

Method: Create the MMBench2 dataset (427 hours, 210 tasks) and train a 350M-parameter world model; characterize three hallucination modes (perceptual, action-marginalized, scene-diverging) tied to pipeline stages; develop three predictive signals; implement coverage-aware sampling to close training gaps; use hallucination predictors as curiosity rewards for targeted data collection; finetune the pretrained model on unseen environments with as few as 50 real trajectories.

Result: Identified three distinct hallucination modes linked to different pipeline stages; developed predictors that accurately forecast failures; coverage-aware sampling improves coverage; curiosity-driven data collection enables data-efficient adaptation to new environments with ~50 trajectories.

Conclusion: Hallucination in world models is fundamentally a data-coverage issue; the same signals used to detect hallucination can be repurposed to mitigate it, enabling efficient adaptation to unseen environments.

Abstract: Modern generative world models render increasingly realistic action-controllable futures, yet they frequently hallucinate: rollouts remain visually fluent while drifting from the ground-truth dynamics. We hypothesize that hallucination concentrates in low-coverage regions of the state-action space, where lightweight data-centric signals can both detect it and guide mitigation. To test this, we introduce MMBench2, a 427-hour, 210-task dataset for visual world modeling with ground-truth actions, rewards, and live simulators, and train a 350M-parameter world model on it. We identify three distinct hallucination modes: perceptual, action-marginalized, and scene-diverging -- each anchored to a different stage of the pipeline, and develop three signals that accurately predict where the model will fail. To close coverage gaps at training time, we develop a coverage-aware sampling technique; to close them online, our hallucination predictors serve as curiosity rewards for targeted data collection, yielding a data-efficient finetuning recipe that adapts the pretrained world model to entirely unseen environments with as few as 50 real environment trajectories. Overall, our findings reveal that hallucination in world models is inherently a data coverage issue, and that the same signals used to detect it can also be used for mitigation.
  An interactive web version of our paper is available at https://www.nicklashansen.com/mmbench2

</details>


### [284] [Reproducibility Study of "AlphaEdit: Null-Space Constrained Knowledge Editing for Language Models"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26783)
*Ananth K S,Arya Hariharan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reproduces AlphaEdit results in the original setup and tests generalization to new architectures, longer editing horizons, and more benchmarks. Finds AlphaEdit's guarantees are architecture- and scale-sensitive; performance degrades with many edits and affects safety/competence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To perform a reproducibility study of AlphaEdit, a null-space constrained projection method for locate-then-edit knowledge editing, and to evaluate its generalization, robustness, and limits beyond the original experimental scope.

Method: Reproduce reported results on LLaMA3, GPT2-XL, and GPT-J; extend evaluation to newer model families; increase sequential edit horizon beyond original; evaluate on BoolQ, HellaSwag, and XSTest; assess metrics including fluency, consistency, task performance, and safety refusals; analyze architectural assumptions inherent in locate-then-edit.

Result: Original-model metrics are reproducible; a discrepancy in fluency/consistency metric is observed. AlphaEdit's advantage does not generalize uniformly to newer models due to architectural differences. When edits are extended beyond original horizons, performance degrades, indicating the null-space projection protection is bounded. Additional benchmarks show degraded general task competence and safety refusals under large-scale sequential editing.

Conclusion: AlphaEdit operates as reported within its original scope, but its core guarantees are not universal. Generalization is constrained by model architecture and editing scale, with practical implications for deployment and the need to reconsider safety and robustness under extended editing.

Abstract: Fang et al. (2025) introduced a null-space constrained projection, named AlphaEdit, for locate-then-edit knowledge editing methods, theoretically guaranteeing that edits do not disrupt previously preserved knowledge, and reports substantial gains over existing editing methods on LLaMA3, GPT2-XL, and GPT-J. In this work, we present a reproducibility study of AlphaEdit, reproducing its reported results under the original experimental setup and extending the evaluation along three axes: new model architectures, additional downstream benchmarks, and substantially longer sequential editing horizons. We successfully reproduce AlphaEdit's reported metrics across the original models, though we identify a discrepancy in the reported fluency and consistency metric. Extending AlphaEdit to newer model families, we find that its advantage does not generalize uniformly, which we trace to architectural assumptions in the locate-then-edit paradigm that are violated by these newer models. We further stress-test AlphaEdit's central sequential-editing claim by extending the number of edits well beyond those evaluated in the original paper, and find that performance, which is stable at the originally reported scale, degrades as edits reach a much higher count, indicating that the null-space projection's protection against catastrophic forgetting is bounded rather than unconditional. Finally, we extend evaluation of edited models on three extra benchmarks, namely, BoolQ, HellaSwag, and XSTest, and we find that large-scale sequential editing degrades both general downstream task competence and safety-relevant refusal behavior. Our results confirm that AlphaEdit performs as reported within its original scope, while showing that its core theoretical guarantees are sensitive to model architecture and editing scale in ways that have practical implications for its deployment.

</details>


### [285] [AIGP: An LLM-Based Framework for Long-Term Value Alignment in E-Commerce Pricing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26787)
*Chennan Ma,Yanning Zhang,Siqi Hong,Xiuchong Wang,Fei Xiao,Keping Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AIGP is a knowledge-aware pricing framework that uses an LLM guided by domain knowledge and structured/text data, with an offline RL-based Long-Term Value Estimator to align pricing with long-term business objectives, achieving notable improvements in GMV, ROI, and milestone metrics while providing interpretable rationales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional dynamic pricing suffers from limited interpretability, underutilization of unstructured information, and misalignment with long-term goals like GMV, ROI, and milestone achievement. AIGP aims to address these gaps by integrating domain-informed LLMs and a long-horizon value estimator.

Method: Leverage a Large Language Model prompted with domain knowledge, structured data, and textual context to generate interpretable pricing decisions. Apply supervised fine-tuning for knowledge distillation to enable efficient deployment. Introduce a Long-Term Value Estimator (LTVE) trained via offline reinforcement learning on historical data to score action candidates as a reward model. Use Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with preference pairs from LTVE to align the pricing policy with long-term objectives.

Result: Offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B tests on Tao Factory show significant improvements: +13.21% GMV, +7.59% ROI, and +8.20% milestone achievement rate over 14 days compared to the production baseline, with interpretable pricing rationales.

Conclusion: AIGP demonstrates that a knowledge-enabled, interpretable pricing framework guided by an offline RL-based LTVE can improve long-term business metrics while maintaining transparency, and it can be deployed efficiently through knowledge distillation.

Abstract: Traditional dynamic pricing models in large-scale e-commerce suffer from limited interpretability, poor utilization of unstructured information, and misalignment with long-term business objectives such as cumulative Gross Merchandise Value (GMV), Return on Investment (ROI) and milestone achievement. We propose AIGP, a novel framework that leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) prompted with domain knowledge, structured data and textual context to make interpretable, knowledge-aware pricing decisions. For efficient deployment while maintaining high-quality outputs, we employ supervised fine-tuning for knowledge distillation. Central to AIGP is the Long-Term Value Estimator (LTVE), trained via offline reinforcement learning on historical data, which serves as a reward model to score candidate pricing actions and select preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), thereby aligning the pricing policy with long-term business objectives. Extensive offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B tests on Tao Factory demonstrate that AIGP achieves significant improvements: +13.21% in GMV, +7.59% in ROI, and +8.20% in milestone achievement rate over 14 days compared to the production baseline, while simultaneously providing interpretable and transparent pricing rationales.

</details>


### [286] [Reasoning Quality Emerges Early: Data Curation for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26797)
*Hongyi Henry Jin,Wenhan Yang,Meysam Ghaffari,Carlos Morato,Baharan Mirzasoleiman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-curation method for SFT using early-token losses on perturbed checkpoints; identifies hard/diverse reasoning examples with token efficiency, improving SFT data quality; outperforms baselines on Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3.1-8B across M23K and OpenThoughts-Math, with up to 1.7% gains and 91% token savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality SFT data curation is costly and relies on strong reasoning models; a cheaper, scalable selection criterion is needed that preserves data quality.

Method: Evaluate loss on the first 100 reasoning tokens at randomly perturbed pretrained checkpoints; difficult problems show high loss; analyze loss patterns over the first 1k tokens across a small set of perturbed checkpoints along the fine-tuning trajectory; show these patterns correlate with similar gradients, enabling efficient data curation.

Result: Empirical improvement: up to 1.7% gain over baselines; 91% token efficiency; validated on Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3.1-8B across M23K medical reasoning and OpenThoughts-Math datasets.

Conclusion: Early-token loss-based, checkpoint-perturbation screening is an effective, cost-efficient data curation method for SFT that yields more diverse and challenging reasoning examples and improved downstream performance.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a small, high-quality set of long reasoning traces is an effective approach for eliciting strong reasoning capabilities in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing methods for curating high-quality SFT data rely heavily on strong reasoning models to filter examples based on diversity and difficulty, making the curation process costly while often yielding suboptimal data quality. In this work, we show that diverse and challenging reasoning examples can be identified using only the initial reasoning tokens. Specifically, we demonstrate that difficult problems can be reliably detected based on the loss of the first 100 reasoning tokens evaluated at a randomly perturbed checkpoint of the pretrained model. We further show that examples exhibiting similar loss patterns over their first 1k reasoning tokens across a small number of perturbed checkpoints extrapolating along the fine-tuning trajectory provably induce similar gradients. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on fine-tuning Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3.1-8B models on the M23K medical reasoning and OpenThoughts-Math datasets. Our method outperforms existing baselines by up to 1.7% while being 91% more token efficient.

</details>


### [287] [Quantization in Federated Learning: Methods, Challenges and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26822)
*Farwa Ikram,Dipanwita Thakur,Antonella Guzzo,Giancarlo Fortino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A first FL-centric systematic review of quantization, proposing a taxonomy across FL-specific dimensions and offering design guidelines for scalable, robust, privacy-preserving quantized FL on edge devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FL scalability is limited by communication, device heterogeneity, and non-IID data. Quantization can reduce communication, computation, and energy costs, but needs FL-specific treatment rather than generic compression.

Method: Literature survey that introduces a FL-centered taxonomy with axes like client heterogeneity, aggregation consistency, communication-scheduling adaptation, non-IID robustness, privacy/security integration, and hardware/energy co-optimization; analyzes interactions with FL phenomena and provides gaps and guidelines.

Result: A comprehensive taxonomy and analysis linking quantization to core FL behaviors (drift, partial participation, convergence, secure aggregation, DP); cross-method insights; identified gaps; practical guidelines for deployment on mobile/IoT/edge platforms.

Conclusion: Quantization should be viewed as a fundamental system component in FL that shapes performance, robustness, and practicality, rather than mere compression.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has become a foundational paradigm for privacy-preserving distributed intelligence, yet its scalability remains fundamentally constrained by communication bottlenecks, device heterogeneity, and the challenges of training under statistically non-IID data. Quantization is one of the most effective mechanisms for mitigating these limitations, reducing both uplink/downlink payloads and on-device computation. This paper provides the first FL-centric systematic review of quantization, introducing a novel taxonomy organized around FL-specific dimensions, including client heterogeneity, aggregation consistency, communication-scheduling adaptation, non-IID robustness, privacy/security integration, and hardware/energy co-optimization. Beyond cataloging existing methods, we analyze how quantization interacts with core FL behaviors such as client drift, partial participation, convergence stability, secure aggregation, and differential privacy. We further identify cross-method insights, open research gaps, and design guidelines for practitioners deploying quantized FL on mobile, IoT, and edge platforms. This survey thus establishes quantization not merely as a compression technique, but as a fundamental systems component shaping the performance, robustness, and practicality of modern FL.

</details>


### [288] [Asymptotically Optimal Learning for Parametric Prophet Inequalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26893)
*Jung-hun Kim,Anna Grebennikova,Vianney Perchet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study learning in prophet inequalities with i.i.d. rewards drawn from an exponential-type parametric family with unknown θ; derive optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio; show unbounded-support limit and bounded-support limit; propose online confidence-based DP policy that achieves the same asymptotic ratio using online data; obtain distribution-specific convergence rates and numeric evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address online decision-making under unknown reward distribution within a parametric exponential-type family; close the gap between offline-sample optimality and online learning.

Method: (i) Characterize the full-information asymptotic competitive ratio for the parametric family, distinguishing unbounded vs bounded support. (ii) Propose a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy that leverages the explicit parametric structure to learn θ online and attain the same optimal asymptotic ratio without offline samples. (iii) Derive distribution-specific convergence rates for canonical examples. (iv) Validate via numerical experiments on synthetic instances.

Result: Unbounded-support asymptotic ratio: (θ/(θ−c_+))^{c_+/θ} / Γ(1−c_+/θ). Bounded-support asymptotic ratio: 1. The online confidence-based DP policy matches the optimal asymptotic ratio using only online observations. Distribution-specific convergence rates established for canonical examples. Numerical experiments corroborate the performance.

Conclusion: Online learning within this parametric prophet-inequality framework achieves the optimal asymptotic competitive ratio without offline samples, with explicit dependence on θ and c_+. The approach yields provable convergence rates and practical DP-based methods, supported by experiments.

Abstract: We study learning in prophet inequalities with i.i.d. rewards drawn from an exponential-type parametric family with an unknown parameter $θ$, a class that includes exponential, Pareto, and bounded-support power-family distributions. We first characterize the optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio for this family. In the unbounded-support case, the limit is $ {\left(θ/({θ-c_+})\right)^{c_+/θ}}/ {Γ(1-c_+/θ)},$ while in the bounded-support case, the limit is $1$. We then propose a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy for online learning. By exploiting the explicit parametric structure, the policy achieves the same optimal asymptotic competitive ratio using only online observations, without external offline samples. We further derive distribution-specific convergence rates for canonical examples. Finally, numerical experiments on synthetic instances illustrate the performance of our algorithm.

</details>


### [289] [GEOALIGN: Geometric Rollout Curation for Robust LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26917)
*Ting Zhou,Zhenqing Ling,Yiyang Zhao,Ying Shen,Daoyuan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geoalign is a lightweight rollout-curation plug-in for online LLM RL that mitigates directional inconsistency by aligning reward-displacement directions through an online projector and batch-consensus-based pruning, improving stability and final performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online RL for LLM alignment with reward signals often destabilizes under noisy or misspecified rewards. A failure mode—directional inconsistency—occurs when a few high-reward rollouts steer representation directions away from the batch consensus, causing high-variance updates.

Method: Geoalign operates as a forward-pass plug-in: (i) forms within-prompt preference pairs; (ii) trains an online projector on per-rollout hidden states to concentrate reward-ordered displacement directions; (iii) detects directionally inconsistent rollouts via angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype and replaces them with within-prompt stable alternatives. It has negligible overhead.

Result: Empirical evaluation on dialogue alignment with a learned reward model and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards shows improved final performance and reduced training oscillation, outperforming PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO.

Conclusion: Latent directional consensus is an effective reliability signal for online LLM RL; Geoalign demonstrates that aligning reward-displacement directions within a batch can stabilize training and improve performance.

Abstract: Online reinforcement learning is widely used to align large language models (LLMs) with reward signals, yet training can be unstable under noisy or misspecified rewards. We identify a failure mode we call directional inconsistency: within a batch, a small set of high-reward rollouts induces representation-space preference directions that sharply disagree with the batch majority, resulting in high-variance and destabilizing updates. We propose geoalign, a lightweight plug-in for rollout curation in iterative policy optimization. Geoalign (i) forms within-prompt preference pairs, (ii) learns an online projector on per-rollout hidden states to concentrate reward-ordered displacement directions, and (iii) detects directionally inconsistent rollouts via their angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype and rectifies them with within-prompt stable alternatives. Geoalign is forward-pass only and adds negligible overhead. Across dialogue alignment with a learned reward model and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards, Geoalign improves final performance and reduces training oscillation, outperforming PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO. These results suggest latent directional consensus as an effective reliability signal for online LLM RL.

</details>


### [290] [Decision-Aligned Evaluation of Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26990)
*Annika Schneider,Tommy Rochussen,Joshua Stiller,Vincent Fortuin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decision-alignment exposes that common uncertainty metrics often misalign with downstream decision utility; it introduces prior-weighted utility metrics, a class of proper scoring rules, that better reflect real decision value across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To close the gap between generic UQ metrics (e.g., NLL, ECE) and the actual value those metrics provide for downstream decisions; many metrics may encode incorrect priors or be misaligned with decision tasks.

Method: Formalize a decision-alignment framework; diagnose misalignment in standard UQ metrics; introduce prior-weighted utility metrics as a subset of proper scoring rules; validate through benchmark experiments and real-world case studies.

Result: Prior-weighted utility metrics consistently align with realized decision utility, while conventional metrics fail to do so; the study reveals flaws in current UQ evaluation protocols.

Conclusion: Advocate adopting decision-aligned, priors-aware evaluation for uncertainty quantification; provide a principled extension of UQ metrics toward decision-relevant evaluation and guidance for practitioners.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimates in machine learning are typically evaluated using generic metrics such as the negative log-likelihood and expected calibration error, yet good performance on such metrics does not necessarily imply high utility in downstream decisions. We introduce decision-alignment, a criterion that reveals which evaluation metrics meaningfully align with downstream utilities. Applying this framework, we show that many widely used uncertainty metrics are either misaligned with common decision problems or encode pathological prior beliefs about the downstream task. We then propose prior-weighted utility metrics, a special class of proper scoring rules that provides decision-aligned uncertainty evaluation. Across benchmark experiments and real-world case studies, our metrics consistently align with realized decision utility, while conventional metrics do not. Our results surface flaws in the current UQ evaluation protocol and offer a principled extension of existing metrics toward decision-relevant UQ evaluation.

</details>


### [291] [Uncertainty quantification via conformal prediction in data assimilation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27001)
*Catherine George,Alireza Javanmardi,Tijana Janjić,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal prediction (CP) is evaluated for uncertainty quantification in a 1D modified shallow water model; three CP variants (Standard CP, Normalized CP, Conformalized Quantile Regression) are compared against ensemble methods; CP is integrated into the data assimilation cycle; results show CP has strengths and limitations and can complement ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify uncertainty in probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation for numerical weather prediction. CP offers distribution-free, state-conditional uncertainty sets; this study tests its applicability in a controlled, idealized setting.

Method: Use a one-dimensional modified shallow water model to mimic convection. Apply CP to generate prediction sets at a chosen confidence level. Evaluate three CP variants: Standard CP, Normalized CP, and Conformalized Quantile Regression. Compare CP-derived uncertainty with traditional ensemble measures (standard deviation intervals, ensemble spread). Assess CP perturbations within the data assimilation cycle.

Result: CP variants show strengths and limitations in providing uncertainty guarantees and interval efficiency. CP can complement ensemble-based uncertainty quantification. The integration into the data assimilation cycle is explored using CP perturbations in a simplified setup.

Conclusion: Conformal prediction is a promising tool to enhance uncertainty quantification alongside ensembles in simplified atmospheric models. The study delineates when each CP variant performs best and highlights considerations for integrating CP into data assimilation; further validation in more realistic models is suggested.

Abstract: Quantifying the evolution of uncertainty is critical to both probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation in numerical weather prediction. In this study, we investigate the applicability of conformal prediction (CP), a recent machine learning (ML) method, to quantify uncertainty in a controlled, idealized setting. We use the one dimensional modified shallow water model, designed to mimic the convective process. CP provides a set of possible outcomes with a chosen confidence level. Here, we compare and evaluate the average empirical coverage, the average interval length, miss low, miss high and average interval score loss (AISL) for three variants of CP, namely a) Standard CP, b) Normalized CP and c) Conformalized Quantile Regression. We further compare these CP-based uncertainty estimates with traditional ensemble-based measures such as standard deviation intervals and ensemble spread. In addition, we investigate the integration of CP-derived uncertainty within the data assimilation cycle through CP perturbations. Our results highlight the strengths and limitations of each approach, providing insight into the effectiveness of CP to complement common ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models.

</details>


### [292] [A Generalization Theory for JEPA-Based World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27014)
*Jingyi Cui,Qi Zhang,Hongwei Wen,Yisen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First generalization theory for JEPA-based world models; shows JEPA pretraining corresponds to low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence; derives finite-sample generalization bounds and planning regret trade-offs with latent dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the theoretical gap in understanding JEPA-based world models and their generalization, linking pretraining to downstream planning performance.

Method: Formulate JEPA pretraining as conditional spectral graph learning; prove equivalence to low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix; derive a finite-sample generalization bound; connect pretraining error to planning regret; analyze approximation vs. sample errors as latent dimension varies.

Result: Establishes theoretical connections between pretraining error and planning regret; provides finite-sample generalization bounds; reveals a trade-off between approximation and sample errors with latent dimension, clarifying strengths/limitations of latent predictive models vs input-level predictors.

Conclusion: Delivers the first generalization theory for JEPA-based world models, enabling principled analysis of latent predictive approaches and guiding future design and empirical validation.

Abstract: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for world modeling by learning predictive dynamics in a latent space rather than generating future observations at the input level. Despite their empirical success, the theoretical understanding of JEPA-based world models remains limited. In this paper, we develop the first generalization theory for JEPA-based world models. We formulate JEPA pretraining as a conditional spectral graph learning problem and show that the JEPA objective is equivalent to a low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix. Building on this characterization, we establish a connection between JEPA pretraining error and downstream planning regret, leading to a finite-sample generalization bound for JEPA-based world models. Our analysis reveals an inherent trade-off between approximation and sample errors with respect to the latent dimension, providing theoretical insights into the advantages and limitations of latent predictive models compared with input-level predictive approaches.

</details>


### [293] [Just how sure are you? Improving Verbalized Uncertainty Calibration in Medical VQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27023)
*Eren Senoglu,Federico Toschi,Nicolo Brunello,Andrea Sassella,Mark James Carman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-based calibration framework for multimodal LLMs in Medical VQA that finetunes models with a composite loss to improve calibration and discrimination while preserving accuracy, using perturbation-driven image-text alignment and KL stabilization; evaluated on three benchmarks across two architectures with strong improvements and ablation-confirmed component necessity; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical VQA models often overconfidently misjudge uncertainty, especially in multimodal tasks where visual input and language priors interplay. Existing calibration methods for text-only LLMs fail to account for multimodal inputs and require a calibration approach that integrates visual information and language priors during training.

Method: Finetune Multimodal LLMs with a composite loss: (1) Brier-style calibration term to align predicted probabilities with actual outcomes; (2) anchor regularizer to prevent confidence collapse to extreme values; (3) contrastive image-text alignment term; (4) KL-based model stabilization term. Alignment signal derived from a 2x2 factorial perturbation crossing image presence and text integrity to probe reliance on visual versus language priors. A top-K KL divergence regularizer protects answering ability during finetuning.

Result: Across three Medical VQA benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), calibration error reduced by 60%+ and discrimination improved by 26%+, while preserving accuracy. On average, outperforms prompting-based, sampling-based, and training-based baselines. Ablation confirms each loss component is necessary. Code for experiments publicly available.

Conclusion: The proposed training-based calibration framework effectively improves model reliability and robustness in medical multimodal VQA, leveraging perturbation-driven alignment and regularization to maintain performance, with strong empirical gains and a public code release.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) applied to Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA) tend to produce overconfident outputs regardless of actual correctness, and existing verbalized confidence calibration methods, developed primarily for text only LLMs, do not account for the multimodal nature of medical image understanding.
  This work proposes a training based framework that finetunes MLLMs to improve their calibration using a composite loss function combining a Brier style calibration term, an anchor regularizer that prevents confidence collapse toward extreme values, a contrastive image text alignment term, and a KL based model stabilization term. The alignment signal is derived from a $2 \times 2$ factorial perturbation design that crosses image presence with text integrity, probing the reliance of the model on visual modality input versus language priors. Finally, a top K KL divergence regularizer is used to protect the answering ability of the model during finetuning.
  Across three Medical VQA benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), our method reduces calibration error by 60% or more, and improves discrimination by 26% or more, while preserving predictive accuracy. On average across benchmarks, the technique outperforms prompting based, sampling based, and training based approaches, and ablation experiments confirm that each component of the loss function is indeed necessary for improving the calibration. All code for the experiments is publicly available.

</details>


### [294] [Symplectic Neural Networks for learning Generalized Hamiltonians](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27029)
*Harsh Choudhary,Vyacheslav Kungurtsev,Chandan Gupta,Melvin Leok,Georgios Korpas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Efficient training of Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNNs) using implicit symplectic integration with adjoint sensitivity equivalence; predictor–corrector ODE solving and fixed-point iteration reduce cost, enabling robust system identification and energy preservation under noisy data; backward error analysis yields a more accurate modified Hamiltonian without finer discretization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify a system's Hamiltonian from noisy trajectory data while ensuring long-term fidelity (energy conservation) via symplectic integration; implicit symplectic methods preserve structure but are computationally costly, requiring efficient backpropagation strategies.

Method: Use symplectic discretizations of the adjoint system that yield the same sensitivities as standard backpropagation. Train the HNN with an implicit symplectic integrator, employing a predictor–corrector ODE solver and fixed-point iteration to mitigate computational cost. Post-process the learned Hamiltonian with backward error analysis to obtain a modified Hamiltonian.

Result: Demonstrates numerical advantages in system identification and energy preservation for non-separable chaotic systems. Achieves efficient gradient updates and favorable memory/computation complexity. Backward error analysis yields a more accurate Hamiltonian without needing more accurate flow-map discretizations.

Conclusion: The proposed implicit-symplectic HNN training approach enables efficient, energy-preserving system identification from noisy data and yields an improved Hamiltonian via backward error analysis, even in chaotic, non-separable settings.

Abstract: Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNNs) integrate physical priors into neural models by learning a system's Hamiltonian, improving generalization and sample efficiency. Identifying the system Hamiltonian from noisy observations of state variables is a challenging task. For simulations to faithfully reflect the long-term behavior of Hamiltonian systems, especially energy conservation, it is essential to use symplectic integrators, which preserve the system's geometric structure. This fidelity comes at a cost: implicit symplectic integrators are more computationally intensive and make backpropagation through the ODE solver non-trivial. However, by leveraging the fact that symplectic discretizations of the adjoint system yield the same sensitivities associated by backpropagation, we obtain an efficient method of training the Neural Network parameters. In our work, we explore this alternate method of HNN training under noisy observation of trajectories with our HNN model based on an implicit symplectic integrator. Computationally, a predictor-corrector based ODE solver and fixed point iteration help to mitigate the computational cost of the implicit timestepping, resulting in more efficient generation of gradient updates. We showcase the numerical advantage, in experiments, in system identification and energy preservation on a range of non-separable, chaotic systems and the efficient computation and memory complexity of our method. We also observe that the post-processing of the learned Hamiltonian using backward error analysis yields a modified Hamiltonian that is a more accurate approximation of the true Hamiltonian without the need to use more accurate discretizations of the flow map.

</details>


### [295] [State Representation Matters in Deep Reinforcement Learning: Application to Energy Trading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27032)
*Jesper Klicks,Sander Vržina,Vincent François-Lavet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Combining feature families for state representation greatly improves RL-based energy storage trading transferability and performance, exceeding single-feature representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State representation governs policy performance and transfer in energy trading RL; prior work lacks systematic comparison of feature families across markets.

Method: Fixed Double DQN in HydroDam; vary market features (absolute, relative, forecast, and combinations); train on 2007–2011 Belgian day-ahead prices; evaluate on 2012–2025 test set and 39 ENTSO-E zones.

Result: Single-feature representations underperform; absolute-only 28.8% test; absolute+relative 49.9% test and 39.8% cross-zone; absolute+relative+forecast 55.6% test and 47.5% cross-zone; relative-only and forecast-only underperform a crossing heuristic.

Conclusion: State representation is a central design choice in storage-trading RL; robust transfer requires integrating price scale, recent relative context, and forecast information rather than relying on any single feature family.

Abstract: Energy trading decisions depend not only on current market prices, but also on expected future market conditions, and operational constraints. This makes the state representation given to a reinforcement learning agent an important design choice. We study this in HydroDam, a pumped-storage arbitrage environment, using a fixed Double DQN agent. The environment, action space, reward function, network, and training protocol are kept fixed; only the market features are changed. We compare absolute price/calendar features, relative features that compare current prices with recent market history, forecast features, and all combinations of these three feature families. Policies are trained and selected using 2007--2011 Belgian day-ahead prices and evaluated on two test settings: a later same-market test set from 2012--2025 and 39 other ENTSO-E market zones. Absolute features only reaches 28.8% on the test set and a median 5.7% across zones. Relative-only and forecast-only states also stay below a rolling price-score heuristic in the cross-zone median. Combining feature families is much stronger: absolute + relative reaches 49.9% on the test set and a 39.8% cross-zone median, while absolute + relative + forecast reaches 55.6% and 47.5%. These results suggest that state representation is not a minor preprocessing choice in storage-trading RL, but a central part of the policy design: robust transfer requires combining price scale, recent relative price context, and short-horizon forecast information, rather than relying on any single feature family.

</details>


### [296] [Finding Stationary Points by Comparisons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27082)
*Helin Wang,Chenyi Zhang,Xiwen Tao,Yexin Zhang,Tongyang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a comparison-oracle-based method to find ε-stationary points for non-convex twice-differentiable functions with Lipschitz gradient/Hessian, achieving in classical query complexity ~n^2/ε^{1.5} and with a Hessian-estimation subroutine ~n^2 log(1/δ); extends to a quantum comparison-oracle model attaining ~n/ε^{1.5} queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing non-convex optimization when only relative (comparison) access to the objective is available, which is common in settings where function values are expensive, noisy, or subjective; extending to quantum query models to leverage superposition for faster optimization.

Method: 1) Develop a classical algorithm that iteratively reaches an ε-stationary point using comparison-based access and a Hessian-normalization subroutine. 2) Introduce a subroutine to estimate the normalized Hessian to accuracy δ with tilde O(n^2 log(1/δ)) queries. 3) Adapt the approach to a quantum comparison oracle, enabling superposition queries to obtain an ε-stationary point with tilde O(n/ε^{1.5}) queries.

Result: Classical setting achieves an ε-stationary point with tilde O(n^2/ε^{1.5}) comparison queries; Hessian-normalization subroutine costs tilde O(n^2 log(1/δ)) queries; Quantum setting achieves ε-stationary point with tilde O(n/ε^{1.5}) queries using a quantum comparison oracle.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that comparison-oracle access suffices for non-convex stationary-point finding with near-optimal scaling in n and ε under the given smoothness assumptions; quantum access yields a substantial speedup in the problem’s query complexity, highlighting potential quantum advantages in non-convex optimization under oracle models.

Abstract: We study the problem of finding stationary points of non-convex functions when access to the objective is provided only through a comparison oracle that, given two points, outputs which has the larger function value. For a twice differentiable $f\colon\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R$ with Lipschitz gradient and Hessian, we develop an algorithm that visits an $ε$-stationary point using $\widetilde O(n^2/ε^{1.5})$ queries. Our approach uses a subroutine that estimates the normalized Hessian to accuracy $δ$ using $\widetilde O(n^2\log(1/δ))$ queries. We further study this problem with a quantum comparison oracle model where queries can be made in superpositions, and develop the first quantum algorithm that finds an $ε$-stationary point, which takes $\widetilde O(n/ε^{1.5})$ queries.

</details>


### [297] [Data-Free Reservoir Features for Efficient Long-Horizon Cold-Start Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27095)
*Augustinas Jučas,Yangchen Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Cold-start exemplar-free class-incremental learning requires learning a growing set of classes without replay, external pretraining, or a large initial task. Existing cold-start methods typically either train the backbone throughout the stream and compensate for semantic drift, or freeze a backbone after the first task, producing features biased toward the initial classes. These choices also create a computational tension: drift-compensation methods require repeated backbone training and increasingly expensive updates as the task horizon grows, while frozen-backbone methods are cheap but weak under cold start. We study a third option: a feature extractor that is never fit to image data at all. We propose CIRCLE, a class-incremental classifier built from fixed bidirectional two-dimensional reservoir features, adapted from BiRC2D for image classification, and streaming linear discriminant analysis heads. CIRCLE groups multiple random reservoir instantiations into feature ensembles and averages the softmax outputs of independent SLDA heads, yielding a tunable bias-variance tradeoff between richer random features and prediction-level ensembling. Because the feature extractor is fixed and the head admits streaming closed-form updates, CIRCLE performs sample-wise training without replay, task-boundary information, or backbone backpropagation. On CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, ImageNet-Subset, and ImageNet-1k, CIRCLE is competitive at 10-20 task splits and substantially outperforms strong CS-EFCIL baselines at 50, 100, and 500 task splits, while training much faster than trained-backbone drift-compensation methods. Ablations show that the BiRC2D-style extractor, SLDA head, and balanced feature/prediction ensembling each contribute to the final performance.

</details>


### [298] [Transformer-Based Classification of Bacterial Raman Spectra with LOOCV](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27096)
*Jamile Mohammad Jafari,Thomas Bocklitz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer-based Raman spectral classification beats traditional pipelines under replicate-aware validation, with strong performance on raw spectra.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve Raman spectral classification robustness and accuracy, addressing limitations of conventional pipelines and the need for replicate-aware validation.

Method: Systematic evaluation using nested leave-one-replicate-out cross-validation; comparison of a transformer against PCA/ICA+LDA, SVM, and Random Forest; dataset of 5,417 single-cell spectra from six bacterial species across nine replicates; assessment with and without preprocessing; latent-space analysis.

Result: Transformer achieves the highest classification performance across independent test replicates, outperforming all conventional methods; latent space shows improved class separation; it remains superior when applied to raw spectra without preprocessing, indicating robustness across replicates.

Conclusion: Transformer-based models are promising for robust Raman spectral classification; replicate-aware validation is crucial for realistic evaluation; potential for practical deployment in Raman spectroscopy workflows.

Abstract: Transformer-based models have recently attracted increasing attention for Raman spectral classification. In this study, a transformer-based approach was systematically evaluated using a nested leave-one-replicate-out cross-validation framework and compared with conventional machine-learning pipelines combining PCA or ICA with LDA, SVM, and Random Forest classifiers. A bacterial Raman dataset comprising 5,417 single-cell spectra from six bacterial species and nine independent measurement replicates was used. The transformer consistently achieved the highest classification performance across independent test replicates and significantly outperformed all conventional approaches. Analysis of the learned latent feature space revealed improved class separation compared with PCA- and ICA-based representations. Furthermore, the transformer maintained superior performance when applied directly to raw Raman spectra without preprocessing, demonstrating robust behavior across measurement replicates. These findings highlight the potential of transformer-based models for robust Raman spectral classification and emphasize the importance of replicate-aware validation for realistic model evaluation.

</details>


### [299] [Heavy-Ball Q-Learning with Residual Weighting Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27112)
*Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Corrected heavy-ball Q-learning with convergence guarantees; shows conditions for faster convergence than standard Q-learning; extends to linear function approximation with analogous results; analyzed via switched linear systems and joint spectral radius.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
