Motivation: Address convergence and acceleration of Q-learning using momentum, and provide a novel SLS-based framework to analyze and quantify potential speedups.

Method: Introduce a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning algorithm; prove convergence; derive acceleration conditions; extend framework to linear function approximation; analyze via a switched linear system representation and joint spectral radius.

Result: Proves convergence of the corrected heavy-ball Q-learning and identifies conditions for accelerated convergence over standard Q-learning; establishes analogous results for linear function approximation.

Conclusion: Adopts a switched linear systems perspective to gain new insights into momentum-based acceleration in Q-learning, highlighting when heavy-ball momentum improves convergence.

Abstract: This paper proposes a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method for reinforcement learning (RL) and establishes its convergence. It also identifies conditions under which the method is theoretically guaranteed to converge faster than standard Q-learning. The same construction is then extended to Q-learning with linear function approximation, where analogous convergence and acceleration statements are derived. The analysis is based on a switched linear system (SLS) representation of Q-learning algorithms and on the joint spectral radius (JSR) of the associated switching families. This SLS viewpoint is not commonly used in standard analyses of Q-learning, and it provides a complementary framework and new insight into how heavy-ball momentum can accelerate Q-learning.

</details>


### [300] [Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network with Inverse Propensity Score under Unobserved Confounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27114)
*Haoran Zhang,Chuanpu Li,Yuxin Fu,Bin Tong,Guan Wang,Bo Zheng,Feng Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CHAUN improves uplift modeling via cross-head attention with shared embeddings; RA-IPS provides robust debiasing against unobserved confounding; experiments on public and production data show significant gains in QINI and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uplift modeling must leverage inter-group similarity to boost discriminative power while remaining robust to unobserved confounders. Existing methods often treat these aspects separately or assume known propensity scores, limiting practical deployment.

Method: CHAUN uses shared feature embeddings and cross-head attention to dynamically fuse treatment-specific and control-specific representations, enhancing inter-group correlation modeling. Theoretical identifiability of ITE holds when true propensity scores are available. RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to mitigate bias from unobserved variables when propensities are unknown.

Result: CHAUN achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art uplift models, with up to 25.6% relative improvement in QINI on CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA, and a production e-commerce dataset. RA-IPS improves robustness, outperforming standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding.

Conclusion: The proposed methods effectively address core challenges in causal uplift modeling—inter-group correlation modeling and bias due to unobserved confounding—and demonstrate strong empirical performance in real-world causal inference tasks.

Abstract: Uplift modeling, crucial for estimating individual treatment effects (ITE), faces dual challenges: flexibly leveraging inter-group similarity to enhance discriminative power and debiasing under unobserved confounding scenarios. In this paper, we propose the Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network (CHAUN) and Robust Adversarial Inverse Propensity Score (RA-IPS) method to address these limitations. CHAUN employs shared feature embeddings and cross-head attention mechanisms to dynamically integrate treatment-specific and control-specific representations, enhancing inter-group correlation modeling. Theoretically, we prove that access to the true propensity scores ensures ITE identifiability even with unobserved confounders. For practical scenarios lacking true propensity scores, RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to mitigate bias from unobserved variables. Experiments on public datasets (CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA) and a production e-commerce dataset demonstrate CHAUN's superiority over state-of-the-art uplift models, achieving relative improvements of up to 25.6% in QINI scores. RA-IPS further enhances robustness, outperforming standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding. The results validate the effectiveness of our proposed methods in real-world causal inference tasks.

</details>


### [301] [Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) for aerodynamic prediction: a comparison with MLPs and GNNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27126)
*Miguel Jaraiz,Fermin Gutierrez,Pablo Yeste,Miguel Sánchez-Domínguez,Eusebio Valero,Gonzalo Rubio,Lucas Lacasa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KANs can approximate well and interpolate across Mach/AoA for surface-pressure prediction, but do not surpass well-trained MLPs; GNNs perform best but require longer training; KANs offer lower complexity yet suffer training instability and hyperparameter sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess KANs for fluid dynamics surrogate modeling and compare against MLPs and GNNs on predicting surface pressure coefficients over subsonic/transonic airfoils; address claims of potential KAN supremacy.

Method: Train and evaluate KAN, MLP, and GNN architectures on a dataset of airfoil pressure distributions across subsonic/transonic regimes; measure predictive accuracy for pressure coefficients, interpolation across Mach numbers and angles of attack, training time, complexity, and stability; analyze hyperparameter sensitivity.

Result: KANs achieve good predictive performance for the entire pressure coefficient distribution and interpolate across regime variables; their performance is comparable or marginally inferior to well-trained MLPs; GNNs achieve best results but require longer training; KANs are typically of lower complexity and train faster, but exhibit training instabilities and strong dependence on hyperparameters.

Conclusion: KANs offer potential in fluid dynamics surrogate models but do not clearly outperform MLPs or GNNs in this setting; their advantages (lower risk of over-parameterization, faster per-epoch training) are offset by training instability and need for careful hyperparameter optimization; further work should focus on stabilizing training and identifying regimes where KANs provide clear benefits.

Abstract: Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) have recently been introduced as a (deep) neural network architecture whose trainable parameters adapt the activation functions, instead of the coefficients of the affine transformations at the core of traditional architectures such as deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs). This architecture builds on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem, which endows it with universal approximation properties. While the advent of KANs has been received with excitement, there is a current debate about the possible KAN supremacy over deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) for classic fields such as symbolic regression, generic-purpose machine learning, natural language processing or computer vision. Here we assess the performance of KANs --and its nuanced comparison against MLPs and graph neural networks (GNNs)-- in the realm of fluid dynamics surrogate modelling. To that aim, we consider the task of predicting the surface pressure distribution over subsonic and transonic airfoils, a canonical task in aerodynamics. Our results show that KAN models show good performance in predicting the whole pressure coefficients and is able to interpolate across Mach numbers and angles of attack, however its performance is comparable --marginally inferior-- to a suitably trained MLP, where best performance is achieved by a GNN at the expense or requiring lengthier training. While the optimal KAN model have typically much lower complexity than MLP and GNN --hence resulting in faster training--, we find that KANs suffer from training instabilities, and their performance is highly dependent on a proper hyperparameter optimisation.

</details>


### [302] [fTNN: a tensor neural network for fractional PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27140)
*Qingkui Ma,Hehu Xie,Xiaobo Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces fTNN, a deterministic tensor neural network subspace method for fractional Laplacian problems on bounded domains, using a geometry-adapted integration split to achieve fully deterministic evaluation; it leverages boundary-singularity-aware trial functions and two loss strategies, and extends to time-dependent problems with a spatiotemporally separable NN trained via alternating subspace optimization; demonstrates superior accuracy vs fPINN and Monte Carlo on benchmarks, especially with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fractional PDEs with low-regularity solutions and boundary singularities pose challenges for probabilistic or standard PINN approaches. There is a need for a fully deterministic, accurate, and efficient solver that can handle the nonlocal fractional Laplacian on bounded domains and long-time integration.

Method: 1) Geometry-adapted integration split of the fractional Laplacian into near-field (singular), interior far-field (regular), and exterior far-field (analytical). 2) Deterministic quadrature: Gauss-Jacobi for singular radial integrals, Gauss for regular radial, deterministic angular quadrature. 3) Boundary-singularity-aware trial functions enriched with explicit boundary features. 4) Strategies to automatically select leading exponent and compute loss from singularity structure, or jointly from operator and source term. 5) For time-dependent problems: spatiotemporally separable NN that factorizes time-space residual into sums of low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, with an alternating neural network subspace optimization.

Result: Numerical experiments show high accuracy on tested benchmarks and substantial improvement over fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

Conclusion: The framework provides a fully deterministic, accurate approach for fractional Laplacian problems on bounded domains, effectively handling boundary singularities and providing efficient training for time-dependent fractional PDEs via a spatiotemporal separable NN and alternating optimization; it outperforms existing baselines on challenging cases.

Abstract: We develop the fTNN, a deterministic tensor neural network subspace method for problems involving the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains, taking the fractional Poisson equation and time-dependent fractional advection-diffusion equation as typical representatives. The work employs a geometry-adapted integration split featuring a spatially dependent near-field radius, which decomposes the fractional Laplacian into three contributions: a singular near field, a regular interior far field, and an analytical exterior far field. Then the singular radial integrals are treated by Gauss-Jacobi quadrature, the regular radial integrals by Gauss quadrature, and the angular variables by deterministic angular quadrature, yielding a fully deterministic integration framework of the fractional Laplacian operator. To accurately resolve low-regularity solutions and the associated loss functional, we construct boundary-singularity-aware trial functions enriched with explicit boundary features, and propose two strategies for automatically selecting the leading exponent and evaluating the loss function from the singularity structure induced by the fractional operator, or jointly by the fractional operator and the source term. For time-dependent fractional PDEs, we design a spatiotemporally separable neural network that factorizes the time-space residual into a sum of low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, and we integrate this representation with an alternating neural network subspace optimization strategy for efficient training. Numerical experiments show that the proposed framework attains high accuracy on the tested benchmarks and improves substantially over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

</details>


### [303] [Stochastic Gradient Optimization with Model-Assisted Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27171)
*Jonne Pohjankukka,Jukka Heikkonen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a model-assisted sampling framework for gradient estimation that treats the dataset as a fixed finite population and mini-batch gradients as sample-based estimates, using auxiliary gradient-prediction models to reduce variance; uniform sampling is the baseline; integrates with existing optimizers and shows gains on synthetic and six benchmarks, with notable generalization improvements for AdamW and medium-sized inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Variance in stochastic gradient estimation from mini-batch sampling leads to trade-offs in convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing variance reduction and adaptive optimizers add computational overhead; a theory-grounded framework aligning gradient estimation with survey sampling could reduce variance efficiently.

Method: Interpret mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory by viewing the dataset as a finite population and gradients as sample estimates. Introduce auxiliary gradient-prediction models to construct more efficient estimators; uniform sampling is a special case when no auxiliary information is available. The framework is designed to be compatible with existing optimizers and their dynamics.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic data and six benchmark datasets shows performance gains in 71-86% of experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces. With momentum-based optimizers like AdamW, the proposed estimator yields better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to the baseline estimator.

Conclusion: The work bridges machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory, offering a practical, compatible approach to variance-reduced gradient estimation via auxiliary models. It suggests potential generalization benefits and efficiency improvements without altering optimizer dynamics.

Abstract: This work addresses the problem of variance in stochastic gradient estimation for machine learning optimization. Deep learning relies on mini-batch methods such as stochastic gradient descent, which approximate full gradients but introduce noise, creating trade-offs between convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing methods, including variance reduction techniques (e.g., SVRG and SAG) and adaptive optimizers, aim to mitigate gradient noise but may introduce additional computational overhead. We propose a model-assisted sampling framework that interprets mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population and gradients as sample-based estimates. Our aim is to bridge machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory by combining their perspectives on sample-based estimation and variance reduction. By incorporating auxiliary gradient-prediction models, we construct more efficient gradient estimators, with uniform sampling arising as a special case when no auxiliary information is used. Our approach integrates easily with existing optimizers, improving efficiency without altering their dynamics. Empirical results on synthetic and six benchmark datasets show performance gains in 71-86% of the experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces in our benchmarks. Notably, with momentum-based optimizers such as AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves clearly better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to baseline estimator.

</details>


### [304] [RecallRisk-BERT: A Multi-Task Framework for Post-Report Medical Device Recall Triage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27174)
*Ali Semih Atalay,Sevgi Yigit-Sert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Joint multi-task model RecallRisk-BERT improves severity and root-cause prediction for FDA medical device recalls via text-tabular fusion; outperforms single-task baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing FDA recall volume necessitates scalable triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation; existing work treats these tasks separately.

Method: Benchmark classical ML and boosting for severity and root-cause prediction; propose RecallRisk-BERT that fuses PubMedBERT-based narrative embeddings with structured feature embeddings (product code, regulation, medical specialty) in a multi-task setup predicting severity (Class I–III) and 9 root-cause classes.

Result: Single-task LightGBM with text-tabular configuration achieved accuracy 0.963, macro-F1 0.856, ROC-AUC 0.974. In multi-task setting, RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings correlated with observed severity (rho=0.983, p=1.936e-6).

Conclusion: Text-tabular learning enables scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis; a multi-task framework effectively captures joint signals from narratives and structured features.

Abstract: Medical device recalls are a critical regulatory mechanism for protecting patient safety. The growing volume of FDA recall records presents challenges in post-report recall triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation. Existing studies mostly address recall occurrence prediction or root-cause analysis separately, while joint modeling of recall severity and root-cause categories has received limited attention. We develop an automated recall triage framework using 54,165 FDA medical device recall records from openFDA, covering the period from 2002 to October 2025. We first evaluate classical machine learning and boosting-based models for recall severity and root-cause category prediction. We then develop RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model that combines PubMedBERT-based textual representations of recall narratives with embedding-based representations of structured categorical features, including product code, regulation number, and medical specialty. The model simultaneously predicts recall severity (Class I/II/III) and a consolidated root-cause category (9 classes). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, macro-averaged precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. In single-task severity prediction, our LightGBM-based text--tabular configuration achieved the strongest performance, with an accuracy of 0.963, macro-F1 of 0.856, and ROC-AUC of 0.974. In the multi-task setting, RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the single-task PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings were strongly consistent with observed root-cause severity patterns (rho = 0.983, p = 1.936e-6). These findings indicate that text--tabular learning can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis.

</details>


### [305] [Explaining Temporal Graph Neural Networks via Feature-induced Information Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27201)
*Ping Xiong,Thomas Schnake,Klaus-Robert Müller,Shinichi Nakajima*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a modular, NRM-based attribution method for ETGNNs that accounts for the entire information flow through event-associated variables, enabling more complete and human-interpretable explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ETGNN explainability methods trace only from event embeddings to outputs and neglect event-induced mediators that govern long-range temporal dependencies.

Method: Extends the Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework with a modular decomposition to capture the complete information flow through all event-associated variables, including event embeddings and event-induced mediators, ensuring comparability of latent variables across layers and enabling higher-order interaction analysis.

Result: Empirical evaluation on two synthetic datasets (epidemic tracing and social dynamics) and a real-world political event network dataset shows the proposed method provides consistently better explanations than existing approaches, with improved human interpretability.

Conclusion: A comprehensive attribution framework for ETGNNs that accounts for the full information flow, supporting more faithful explanations of long-range temporal dynamics and enabling higher-order analyses; potential directions include scalability improvements and broader applicability across ETGNN architectures.

Abstract: Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of applications, including social network analysis, epidemic tracing, recommender systems, and political event forecasting. However, their increasing complexity poses significant challenges for explainability. Existing explanation methods focus only on a subset of the information flow within ETGNNs, typically tracing contributions from the event-related embeddings to the output. Consequently, they overlook the important pathways through event-induced variables, which mediate interactions between nodes and thereby play a central role in capturing long-range temporal dependencies. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel attribution method that analyzes the \emph{entire} information flow through all event-associated variables. Our method is built upon the recent Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, which enables explicit quantification of information flow originating from event embeddings as well as information flow passing through event-induced variables. It also ensures comparability of latent variables across layers, and supports higher-order analysis of interactions between events. To handle the architectural complexity of ETGNNs, we extend the NRM framework with a modular decomposition procedure that facilitates the systematic construction of relevance structure for complex neural architectures. We evaluate our approach on two synthetic datasets for epidemic tracing and social dynamics, as well as a real-world dataset of political event networks. Our qualitative and quantitative experiments show that our method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches while producing more human-interpretable explanations.

</details>


### [306] [Graph Neural Networks Applications Across Domains: All Insights You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27202)
*Abderaouf Bahi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey unifies the design space, theory, and applications of graph neural networks, clarifying when graph structure helps and where it costs, and highlighting deployment gaps across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand where graph structure yields real computational value, connect expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy, and translate theoretical insights into practical guidance across diverse domains.

Method: Derive spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, relate expressivity to the WL hierarchy, examine twelve application domains, specify graph-construction choices and costs, identify dominant architecture families, and separate genuine gains from baselines artefacts.

Result: Reveals recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undermine common models across domains; temporal graphs are generally harder than static ones; leader-board top architectures often do not reach deployment. Provides a cross-domain map of design choices, costs, and adoption constraints.

Conclusion: Adoption is governed by constraints such as over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability; practical guidance emerges from balancing architecture capabilities with domain-specific graph construction costs and deployment realities.

Abstract: Graph neural networks have moved from a niche representation-learning technique to the default model class wherever data carry relational structure. The interesting question is no longer whether message passing helps on a given dataset, but where graph structure earns its computational cost and where it does not. This survey organises the field around a single design space, derives the spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, and connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy with explicit statements of what current architectures can and cannot separate. Against that methodological backbone we examine twelve application domains, among them recommendation and social networks, knowledge graphs and language-model integration, drug discovery and molecular property learning, healthcare and neuroscience, computer vision, traffic and urban computing, power and renewable-energy systems, wireless and sixth-generation networks, fraud and cybersecurity, industrial prognostics, materials science, and climate modelling. For each domain we specify the graph-construction choices and their costs, identify which architecture families dominate and why, and separate reported gains from artefacts of weak baselines or favourable splits. A cross-domain comparison exposes recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undercut the same models almost everywhere, temporal graphs remain harder than their static counterparts, and the architectures that top public leaderboards are seldom the ones that reach deployment. We treat over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability not as a closing checklist but as the constraints that decide adoption.

</details>


### [307] [The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27242)
*John Sweeney*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FisherSketch enables training-free, scalable head-level transfer assessment for LLMs with shared vocabularies by estimating head Fisher alignment (a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in activation-error space) in a single streaming pass, outperforming activation-based metrics when representations are uninformative.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In domains with shared vocabularies (e.g., SMILES, proteins, genomics), candidate corpora share tokenizers but have different prediction targets, creating an activation-dark regime. Efficient, label-aware transfer metrics are needed for source selection without expensive Fisher information computations.

Method: Derive that head Fisher alignment equals a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation–error space. Introduce FisherSketch to estimate this cosine directly in a single streaming pass with small task signature (e.g., 16 KB, m=4096) and modest per-task state (192 KB), avoiding materializing the full Fisher matrix. The approach also serves as a diagnostic tool to decompose task similarity into activations, errors, and coupling.

Result: Demonstrates that representation metrics like CKA are non-identifiable for transfer in shared-output-head settings; models can have identical representations yet different head updates. FisherSketch remains informative even when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks, and is practical at vocabulary scale (K up to 256) with small storage, validated on tasks including Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments.

Conclusion: FisherSketch provides a practical, scalable, training-free diagnostic and source-selection tool for LLM families with shared vocabularies, enabling insight into whether task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling, and supporting robust transfer across related tasks.

Abstract: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies arises in scientific string domains such as SMILES, protein, and genomic sequences, where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. This creates an activation-dark regime: representation-similarity metrics can be uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale. We show that, in a shared-output head setting, representation metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable for transfer; models can share identical representations yet have orthogonal head updates. The key identity is that head Fisher alignment is exactly a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, exposing activation, error, and coupling factors rather than requiring a materialized Fisher matrix. FisherSketch estimates this cosine directly in a single streaming pass, making K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and a 192 KB per-task streaming state, small enough to store next to a model hash, but encoding transfer-relevant update structure. Beyond source selection, the same signatures and marginals provide a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling; shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, together with Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, show that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.

</details>


### [308] [Effective Covariance Dynamics in Solvable High-Dimensional GANs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27246)
*Andrew Bond,Zafer Doğan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Solvable high-dimensional GAN with class-dependent, correlated latent structure; stochastic training converges to ODEs with an effective covariance; phase-aware learning in matched covariance showing signal boosting by low-rank correlations; numerical and standard dataset experiments validate theory and show covariance-informed generators align with data subspace.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend solvable GAN analyses from diagonal latent covariance to class-dependent, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent structure; understand how latent heterogeneity affects training dynamics and learnability.

Method: Derive high-dimensional limit of the stochastic training process for a linear generator and quadratic energy discriminator with heterogeneous latent covariance; show convergence to deterministic ODEs governed by an effective second moment; analyze stability and phase boundaries in the matched-covariance case; validate via numerical simulations and MNIST/FashionMNIST/CIFAR-10 experiments.

Result: Dynamics reduce to ODEs with an effective covariance; phase (learnability) boundaries depend on learning rates and noise; low-rank correlations boost weak modes into learnable regime, while overly strong correlations destabilize recovery; numerical sims confirm ODE predictions; experiments show informed generator covariance improves alignment with data-driven subspace.

Conclusion: Latent-structure heterogeneity shapes learning dynamics through an effective covariance; moderate correlations can enhance learnability via a signal-boosting mechanism, whereas strong correlations can destabilize recovery; results guide principled design of generator covariance to better capture data subspace.

Abstract: We study a solvable high-dimensional model of generative adversarial network (GAN) training in which a linear generator learns a low-dimensional subspace from data with structured latent covariance. Prior solvable GAN analyses assume unconditional signals with diagonal latent covariance; we extend the multi-feature discriminator setting to class-dependent, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent structure. For the quadratic energy discriminator, all such heterogeneity enters the dynamics through a probability-weighted effective second moment. We prove that the stochastic microscopic training process converges, in the high-dimensional limit, to deterministic ordinary differential equations governed by this effective covariance. In the matched-covariance specialization, the stability analysis yields a mode-wise solvable interval determined by the learning rates and noise level: learning begins when the leading effective eigenvalue crosses the lower threshold, while full recovery requires all relevant effective modes to remain within the interval. This reveals a signal-boosting mechanism: low-rank correlations can lift weak directions above the learnability threshold, whereas overly strong correlations destabilize recovery. Numerical simulations validate the ODE, phase boundary, and boosting mechanism. Experiments on MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10 further show that informed generator covariance improves alignment with the data-driven reference subspace.

</details>


### [309] [RSPC: A Benchmark for Modeling Stress and Psychiatric Conditions in Digitally Mediated Relationships using Psychiatrist Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27247)
*Parmitha Vangapandu,Sai Ganesh Mokkapati,Sathwik Narkedimilli,MSVPJ Sathvik,Timothy Liu,Simon See,Johannes C. Eichstaedt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC): 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for mood disorders, relational triggers, and relationship phase; benchmarks seven fine-tuned transformers and five LLMs on multi-label disorder classification, trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction; finds model-family differences; Claude-3-Haiku best for disorder classification (Macro-F1 0.538); GPT-4o best for trigger detection (Macro-F1 0.519); links anxiety with chronic relational uncertainty; frames context-aware, social-temporal mental health modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap of context-insensitive mental health NLP by capturing relational context and temporal dynamics; move from isolated, individual-centric models to context-aware modeling; establish a benchmark dataset with clinical annotations.

Method: Assemble RSPC with 1,799 Reddit posts labeled by psychiatrists for diagnostic categories, relational stressor triggers, and relationship phase; evaluate seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models on three tasks: multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction; report Macro-F1 and analyze task-model dynamics.

Result: Task-dependent model differences observed; Claude-3-Haiku yields strongest disorder classification (Macro-F1 0.538); GPT-4o yields strongest relational trigger detection (Macro-F1 0.519); strong association reported between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty; RSPC offers a relational-context benchmark for NLP-based mental health modeling.

Conclusion: RSPC enables context-aware mental health NLP research, highlighting the value of incorporating relational and temporal context; supports shifting from isolated to context-informed modeling; results suggest distinct model strengths across tasks.

Abstract: In NLP, mental health conditions are often modeled as isolated phenomena, without interpersonal context. We use Reddit posts about long-distance relationships to capture both mental health distress and associated relational triggers. We introduce the Relational Stress and Psychiatry Corpus (RSPC) containing 1,799 Reddit posts annotated by psychiatrists for diagnostic categories, including the most prevalent mood disorders (anxiety and depression), relational stressor triggers, and indications of relationship phase. We benchmark seven fine-tuned transformer models and five large language models across multi-label disorder classification, relational trigger detection, and temporal phase prediction tasks. We find clear task-dependent differences between model families, with Claude-3-Haiku achieving the best disorder classification performance (Macro-F1 = 0.538) and GPT-4o obtaining the strongest relational trigger detection performance (Macro-F1 = 0.519), suggesting distinct model capabilities. We further find strong associations between anxiety disorders and chronic relational uncertainty. Overall, RSPC establishes a benchmark for NLP tasks that consider relational context and supports a shift from individual-centric to context-aware mental health modeling that captures the social and temporal dynamics of distress.

</details>


### [310] [BetXplain: An Explanation-Annotated Dataset for Detecting Manipulative Betting Advertisements on Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27274)
*MSVPJ Sathvik,Parmitha Vangapadu,Nishit Rane,Sathwik Narkedimilli,Mark Lee,Akrati Saxena*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new annotated dataset of betting-related ads from Instagram and Reddit, labeled for manipulative/deceptive practices with human explanations, enabling explainable detection and regulatory monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses scarcity of publicly available, annotated datasets for manipulative betting ads and supports development of explainable detection methods and practical safeguards.

Method: Manual annotation of ads from two platforms; include classification labels and explanations; analysis of persuasive strategies; discuss potential applications like browser plugins and crawlers.

Result: Dataset comprising labels and human-provided explanations; insights into common persuasive tactics and their mental health implications; proposed framework for practical deployment.

Conclusion: Dataset and framework enable explainable detection and regulatory monitoring of manipulative betting ads; potential for tooling to warn users.

Abstract: The promotion of betting applications on social media platforms has increased significantly in recent years. Many of these advertisements use persuasive techniques that may mislead users, encourage risky behavior, and potentially influence users' mental well-being. However, research on the automated detection of manipulative and deceptive betting advertisements remains limited due to the lack of publicly available annotated datasets. In this work, we introduce a new dataset of betting-related advertisements collected from two widely used social media platforms, Instagram and Reddit. The advertisements were manually annotated for manipulative and deceptive advertising practices. In addition to classification labels, the dataset includes human-provided explanations that describe the reasoning behind each annotation, enabling research into explainable approaches to detecting manipulative advertising. Furthermore, we analyze the strategies commonly used in betting advertisements and examine how these persuasive tactics may impact users' mental health. The proposed framework can also enable practical applications such as browser plugins that warn users about manipulative betting advertisements and automated web crawlers that help regulatory authorities monitor and detect such promotions online.

</details>


### [311] [How Good Can Linear Models Be for Time-Series Forecasting?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27282)
*Lang Huang,Jinglue Xu,Luke Darlow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Ridge regression with tuned preprocessing closes much of the accuracy gap in time-series forecasting at low cost; three patterns discovered across eight benchmarks; cross-series sharing varies; optimized hyperparameters diagnose data structure; optimized preprocessing matches or exceeds baselines on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the assumption that scaling model capacity is the primary route to accuracy; investigate whether preprocessing and regularization can achieve comparable gains for time-series forecasting.

Method: Use Ridge regression with closed-form solution; search over context length, local normalization, regularization, augmentation; eight standard benchmarks; compare to Transformer, MLP, CNN baselines.

Result: Optimal lookback is dataset-horizon-specific and often non-monotonic; normalizing over a learned trailing fraction of the context is usually preferred; series within the same dataset differ in optimal hyperparameters and the degree of cross-series sharing ranges from fully shared to fully per-series; the resulting models beat prior linear forecasters on most dataset-horizon entries and exceed Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines on six of eight benchmarks; the optimized hyperparameters also serve as diagnostics on the data.

Conclusion: Preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning can yield substantial gains at a lower cost than scaling model capacity; cross-series sharing patterns and lookback requirements reveal latent data structure; hyperparameters provide diagnostic insight into the data and can guide model design.

Abstract: Time-series forecasting research has been moving steadily toward larger architectures, from specialized transformers to general-purpose foundation models, on the assumption that capacity is what unlocks accuracy. We take the opposite position: most of the gap can be closed at far lower cost by tuning preprocessing rather than scaling models. We use Ridge regression as the testbed, since it has a closed-form solution and interpretable weights, which let the optimal hyperparameters be read off the search directly. We search over context length, local normalization, regularization, and augmentation on eight standard benchmarks and find three patterns. (1) Optimal lookback is strongly series-specific and often non-monotonic in forecast horizon, with fitted power-law exponents ranging from $+0.46$ on ETTm2 to $-0.19$ on Exchange and Traffic, challenging the convention that longer horizons need longer history. (2) Normalizing over a learned trailing fraction of the context, rather than its entirety, is almost universally preferred. (3) Series within the same dataset often disagree on hyperparameters; the optimal degree of cross-series sharing varies from fully shared to fully per-series. The resulting models beat prior linear forecasters on most dataset-horizon entries and exceed Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines on six of eight benchmarks. The optimized hyperparameters also serve as a diagnostic on the data itself, revealing structures that larger models absorb silently into their learned parameters.

</details>


### [312] [Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27285)
*Yang Pan,Helmut Bölcskei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Hausdorff distance on solution sets as a natural metric for comparing ODEs, establishes identifiability across a broad class of ODEs, derives metric entropy estimates, and analyzes the sample complexity needed to recover governing equations from solution observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill the gap in theoretical understanding of when a ground-truth ODE can be uniquely and stably identified from multiple solution observations and to quantify the sample complexity of such learning tasks.

Method: Define the Hausdorff distance on the sets of solution trajectories as the comparison metric. Prove identifiability bounds for a wide class of ODEs, from linear to nonlinear Lipschitz/Hölder vector fields. Derive metric entropy bounds for these classes and translate them into sample complexity requirements for recovering the governing equations from data.

Result: Identifiability bounds are established for diverse ODE classes, and metric entropy estimates are derived. Consequently, the paper provides quantitative sample complexity bounds indicating how many solution observations are needed to reliably recover the governing equations.

Conclusion: The work provides a rigorous framework for understanding when two ODEs can be distinguished from data and how much data is necessary, via a minimax-consistent metric (Hausdorff distance) and associated entropy-based sample complexity analysis.

Abstract: Learning governing equations from observed solution data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning \cite{bruntonDiscoveringGoverningEquations2016,kovachkiNeuralOperatorLearning2023,longPDENetLearningPDEs2018,rudyDatadrivenDiscoveryPartial2017,raonicConvolutionalNeuralOperators2023}, yet the theoretical conditions under which a ground-truth ODE can be uniquely and stably identified from multiple solution observations remain largely undeveloped, and no quantitative analysis of the sample complexity of such learning tasks exists in the literature. To address this gap, we introduce the Hausdorff distance on solution sets as the natural metric for comparing differential equations, since it captures the worst-case separation between two equations over all admissible initial conditions and thus encodes the minimax structure of the identification problem. We establish identifiability bounds for governing ODEs across a wide class of structure equations--ranging from linear ODEs to nonlinear classes with Lipschitz (Hölder)-continuous vector fields--characterizing precisely when two distinct equations can be distinguished from solution data. Using this metric, we derive metric entropy estimates for the relevant ODE classes and analyze sample complexity bounds, quantifying how many solution observations are needed to reliably recover the governing equation.

</details>


### [313] [Designing Reward Signals for Portable Query Generation: A Case Study in Industrial Semantic Job Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27291)
*Ping Liu,Qianqi Shen,Jianqiang Shen,Wenqiong Liu,Rajat Arora,Yunxiang Ren,Chunnan Yao,Dan Xu,Baofen Zheng,Wanjun Jiang,Andrii Soviak,Kevin Kao,Jingwei Wu,Wenjing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reward shaping dominates performance in end-to-end RLAIF for portable job-search queries; critic-free optimizers resist reward-hacking while GRPO is vulnerable to spurious rewards; a deterministic reward floor mitigates verbatim copying, yielding performance gains; training-time reward modeling amplifies gains, underscoring shaping discipline over optimizer choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how optimization mechanics interact with reward engineering in adversarial RL setups for generating portable job-search queries, and to identify strategies that prevent exploitative behavior and verbatim copying while maintaining generalization.

Method: Empirical study comparing critic-free optimizers (RLOO, REINFORCE++) and group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) within an end-to-end RLAIF framework. Introduces a deterministic, rule-based reward floor to correct verbatim copying. Evaluates on cross-family judges and analyzes the impact of the training-time reward model.

Result: For critic-free optimizers, performance is largely dictated by reward shaping; the specific optimizer choice is largely immaterial. RLOO and REINFORCE++ resist reward-hacking, while GRPO is particularly sensitive to spurious rewards. The deterministic reward floor reduces verbatim-copying failures and yields a +0.147 quality improvement on a cross-family evaluation judge. Training-time reward modeling inflates performance gains by 2.4x, indicating that success hinges on enforcing reward-shaping disciplines rather than optimizer choice.

Conclusion: Enforcing reward-shaping discipline is essential for robust generalization in RLAIF-based job-query generation. Optimizer choice is secondary to robust reward design; mitigating verbatim copying via a rule-based reward floor is crucial. Future work should refine reward-structure designs and investigate training-time reward-model effects across tasks.

Abstract: Job-search platforms rely on low-bandwidth query interfaces that often fail to capture the high-dimensional complexity of candidate profiles. We present an end-to-end RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) framework to generate \emph{portable} job search queries, terms that abstract away seeker-specific identifiers while preserving generalizable qualifications. This task introduces a highly adversarial reward surface where policy optimization frequently exploits flaws in LLM-as-judge rubrics, resulting in degenerate verbatim-copying behaviors.
  We conducted comprehensive empirical experiments to isolate the impact of optimization mechanics against structured reward engineering. Our results demonstrate that for critic-free optimizers, performance is overwhelmingly dictated by robust reward shaping, rendering the specific choice of algorithm largely immaterial. While critic-free per-rollout baseline methods (RLOO and REINFORCE++) natively resist reward-hacking, the group-relative advantage normalization in GRPO appears uniquely sensitive to spurious reward signals, making it disproportionately susceptible to exploitation. We show that introducing a deterministic, rule-based reward floor to correct for rewards assigned to verbatim copying mitigates this failure mode, resulting in a substantial $+0.147$ quality improvement on a cross-family evaluation judge. Ultimately, we show that the training-time reward model inflates performance gains by $2.4\times$, confirming that the training success is fundamentally dependent on enforcing reward-shaping disciplines rather than selecting alternative optimizers.

</details>


### [314] [A Multi-Fidelity Convolutional Autoencoder-Transfer Learning Framework for Guided-Wave-Based Damage Diagnosis Using Large Simulated and Limited Experimental Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27304)
*Santosh Kapuria,Abhishek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multifidelity transfer learning framework for guided wave SHM using a CAE-based deep feature extractor, synthetic data from a lightweight 1D spectral element model, and limited experimental data to achieve accurate damage localization and sizing in plate-like structures with piezoelectric transducers. The approach outperforms CNN-based baselines and generalizes to unseen damage scenarios, with R^2 > 0.93 for localization and >0.99 for sizing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data scarcity and high computational costs in guided-wave SHM by combining cheap physics-based simulations with deep feature learning and small amounts of labeled experimental data for reliable damage localization and sizing.

Method: 1) Generate a large synthetic dataset using a computationally efficient 1D time-domain spectral element model. 2) Learn deep features with a convolutional autoencoder (CAE). 3) Use transfer learning to adapt a feed-forward neural network to the experimental domain using limited labeled data. 4) Compare CAE-based transfer learning to a CNN-based approach for localization and sizing in plate-like structures instrumented with piezoelectric transducers.

Result: CAE-based transfer learning significantly outperforms the CNN-based counterpart in damage localization accuracy. The model achieves R^2 > 0.93 for localization and R^2 > 0.99 for damage sizing. It demonstrates strong generalization to unseen damage scenarios not represented during pretraining or fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed CAE-based multifidelity transfer learning framework is accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable for real-world GWSHM, enabling reliable damage localization and sizing with limited experimental data and synthetic pretraining data.

Abstract: Guided wave-based structural health monitoring (GWSHM) with onboard transducers offers significant potential for the early diagnosis of damage in engineering structures. However, the practical deployment of deep learning models is often hindered by the limited availability of labelled experimental data and the high computational cost of generating large-scale high-fidelity simulation datasets. This study presents a multifidelity transfer learning framework that integrates lightweight physics-based simulations, convolutional autoencoder (CAE)-based deep feature learning, a feed-forward neural network, and limited experimental measurements for accurate damage localisation and sizing in plate-like structures instrumented with piezoelectric transducers. A computationally efficient one-dimensional time-domain spectral element model is employed to generate a large synthetic dataset for pretraining, while transfer learning adapts the model to experimental domains using only a small amount of labelled data. The CAE-based transfer learning framework significantly outperforms its CNN-based counterpart in damage localisation accuracy. The model achieves excellent predictive performance with $R^2$ scores exceeding 0.93 for damage localisation and 0.99 for damage sizing. Its generalisation capability is demonstrated on previously unseen data, showing high prediction accuracy for damage scenarios not represented during pretraining or fine-tuning. The results establish the proposed framework as an accurate, computationally efficient, and practically viable solution for real-world GWSHM applications.

</details>


### [315] [Blackwell Approachability and Gradient Equilibrium are Equivalent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27315)
*Brian W. Lee,Nika Haghtalab,Michael I. Jordan,Ryan J. Tibshirani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEQ is algorithmically equivalent to Blackwell approachability; it also aligns with regret minimization and calibration through reductions, enabling transfer of guarantees and efficient reductions between GEQ variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Position GEQ within the online learning landscape and clarify its relation to established frameworks (regret, calibration, approachability).

Method: Proves algorithmic equivalence between GEQ and Blackwell approachability via bidirectional reductions using a black-box GEQ oracle; leverages known equivalences between approachability, regret, and calibration; derives efficient reductions to transfer guarantees (e.g., optimism, strong adaptivity) and characterizes conditions for GEQ; analyzes unconstrained vs constrained decision sets and provides necessary/sufficient conditions.

Result: GEQ can be solved by querying a GEQ oracle to realize Blackwell approachability, with no extra asymptotic loss; conversely, Blackwell approachability can be solved via GEQ queries. Consequently GEQ is equivalent to regret minimization and calibration, via approachability. The paper provides efficient reductions, enabling transfer of refined guarantees and identifies necessary/sufficient conditions and reductions across GEQ variants.

Conclusion: GEQ sits on equal footing with core online learning frameworks, allowing unified analysis and practical transfer of guarantees. The results offer a complete picture of GEQ’s place in online learning, with practical reductions and clear conditions for GEQ.

Abstract: Gradient equilibrium (GEQ) is a recently introduced online optimization framework that generalizes first-order stationarity from offline optimization and abstracts problems like online conformal prediction. While GEQ has curious similarities with known online learning frameworks, namely regret minimization, prior work has shown that GEQ error and regret are incomparable objectives, leaving open a precise understanding of how GEQ fits into the broader online learning landscape. In this work, we show that GEQ is equivalent to Blackwell approachability in the algorithmic sense. That is, a Blackwell approachability problem can always be solved using queries to a black-box GEQ oracle, with no asymptotic loss in the oracle's error rate, and vice versa. Taken together with known equivalences between approachability, regret minimization, and calibration, these results imply that GEQ is equivalent to these frameworks, as well. Our reductions are efficient and can be used to transfer refined guarantees, such as optimism and strong adaptivity, from regret minimization to GEQ. Along the way, we also identify necessary and sufficient conditions for GEQ, and establish reductions between different notions of GEQ with unconstrained and constrained decision sets.

</details>


### [316] [Beyond the Hard Budget: Sparsity Regularizers for More Interpretable Top-k Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27321)
*Nathanaël Jacquier,Maria Vakalopoulou,Mahdi S. Hosseini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two sparsity regularizers for Top-k sparse autoencoders improve monosemanticity without hurting reconstruction; l1 on off-support and l1/2 ratio penalty applied to batch-active units; results robust across datasets/models/k; central finding: hard sparsity and soft regularization are complementary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of Top-k SAE (fixed budget k and lack of explicit sparsity regularization) and enhance interpretability by promoting monosemantic features while preserving reconstruction quality.

Method: Introduce two regularizers applied to activations before Top-k: (i) an l1 penalty on the unselected (off-support) units, (ii) a scale-invariant l1/l2 ratio penalty. Regularizers are applied only to batch-active units (selected by Top-k) within training across varying k, evaluated on two datasets and three vision foundation models.

Result: Both penalties consistently improve monosemanticity with no degradation in reconstruction. The l1/l2 penalty further concentrates information into fewer latent units, increasing robustness to the chosen k during inference and improving small-budget linear probing.

Conclusion: Hard architectural sparsity and soft sparsity regularization are complementary; combining them yields more interpretable representations without sacrificing reconstruction quality.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a leading tool for interpreting the representations of vision foundation models, decomposing their polysemantic activations into a larger set of sparse, more monosemantic features. The Top-$k$ SAE, a now-standard variant, enforces sparsity architecturally through its activation function, retaining only the $k$ most active latents per input. Because it was designed precisely to avoid the $\ell_1$ penalty used by earlier SAEs and its known drawbacks, it has not been combined with an explicit sparsity regularizer, despite retaining limitations of its own, such as a budget $k$ that is fixed regardless of input complexity and a tendency to overfit to the training value of $k$. We introduce two sparsity regularizers compatible with the Top-$k$ architecture, both acting on the activations before the Top-$k$ selection: an $\ell_1$ penalty on the unselected (off-support) units, and a scale-invariant $\ell_1/\ell_2$-ratio penalty that concentrates the code onto fewer effective units. Both penalties are applied only to the batch-active units, those selected by the Top-$k$ operator at least once within the batch. Across two datasets, three vision foundation models, and a range of $k$, both regularizers consistently improve monosemanticity at no cost to reconstruction quality. The $\ell_1/\ell_2$ penalty further concentrates information into fewer latents, making reconstruction more robust to the inference-time choice of $k$ and improving small-budget linear probing. Our central finding is that hard architectural sparsity and soft sparsity regularization are complementary rather than mutually exclusive.

</details>


### [317] [Error-Conditioned Neural Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27354)
*Haina Jiang,Liam Wang,Peng-Chen Chen,Min Seop Kwak,Seungryong Kim,Brian Bell,Jeong Joon Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural Solvers that condition on the PDE residual for iterative correction outperform residual-minimizing hybrids, offering higher accuracy, better generalization, and lower compute cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural surrogates often violate PDE constraints and fail to extrapolate; traditional residual-focused optimization (gradient descent, Gauss-Newton) is costly and unstable, especially in ill-conditioned systems.

Method: Introduce error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS) that take the PDE residual field as input at each iteration, learning an update policy to iteratively correct predictions instead of minimizing the residual.

Result: ENS achieves the highest prediction accuracy across four PDE families, with up to 10x gains on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, and generalizes under distribution shifts (zero-shot parameter changes, cross-equation transfer) while avoiding the compute cost of hybrids.

Conclusion: Modeling error structure directly and learning corrective updates yields robust, accurate surrogates that generalize better in ill-conditioned regimes and reduce computational overhead compared to residual-minimizing hybrids.

Abstract: Neural surrogate models offer fast approximate mappings from PDE parameters to solutions, but they typically treat solving as a purely statistical task: once trained, they struggle to correct their own constraint violations and extrapolate beyond the training distribution. Recent hybrid methods promote physical correctness by targeting the PDE residual via gradient descent or Gauss--Newton steps, but inherit the compute cost and instability of the underlying classical optimizers. We show, theoretically and empirically, that numerically minimizing the PDE residual can be an unreliable proxy for reconstruction accuracy in ill-conditioned systems, explaining why these methods often do not make accurate predictions despite achieving low residuals. We propose error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS), built on a different principle: rather than an optimization target, the PDE residual field is passed as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling it to read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an update policy to iteratively correct its predictions. Across four PDE families, ENS attains the highest prediction accuracy in the large majority of settings, with gains reaching $10\times$ on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, while avoiding the expensive compute cost of hybrid methods. ENS's learned correction policy generalizes under distribution shift, including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer, where its relative advantage is largest in the ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is least reliable. Project website: https://neuralsolver.github.io/.

</details>


### [318] [Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27361)
*Danyal Rehman,Charlie B. Tan,Yoshua Bengio,Avishek Joey Bose,Alexander Tong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) replace flow-based BGs with autoregressive models to overcome topological and scalability limitations, enabling sequential inference-time interventions and leveraging large-language-model–style architectures. They show strong gains over flow-based BGs, especially for larger peptides (e.g., Chignolin) and introduce Robin (132M params) achieving substantial zero-shot energy error improvements (E-W2) on 8-residue systems; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome expressivity constraints and computational cost of normalizing flows used in Boltzmann Generators, enabling scalable, flexible, and intervention-capable sampling for molecular systems at equilibrium.

Method: Introduce an autoregressive modelling framework (ArBG) that departs from flow-based BGs, enabling sequential inference-time interventions and scaling via architectures inspired by Large Language Models; also present a transferable 132M-parameter model (Robin) trained with ArBG.

Result: ArBG yields significant improvements over flow-based BGs across benchmarks, with particularly strong performance on larger peptide systems such as the 10-residue Chignolin; Robin achieves substantial zero-shot energy error reduction (E-W2) on 8-residue systems (>60%).

Conclusion: ArBG provides a new, scalable alternative to flow-based Boltzmann Generators, improving sampling efficiency and accuracy for molecular systems at equilibrium; the work includes a transferable, high-capacity model (Robin) and releases code for further evaluation.

Abstract: Efficient sampling of molecular systems at thermodynamic equilibrium is a hallmark challenge in statistical physics. This challenge has driven the development of Boltzmann Generators (BGs), which allow rapid generation of uncorrelated equilibrium samples by combining a generative model with exact likelihoods and an importance sampling correction. However, modern BGs predominantly rely on normalizing flows (NFs), which either suffer from limited expressivity due to strict invertibility constraints (discrete time) or computationally expensive likelihoods (continuous time). In this paper, we propose Autoregressive Boltzmann Generators (ArBG) -- a novel autoregressive modelling framework -- that overcomes these limitations by departing from the flow-based BG paradigm. ArBG circumvents the topological constraints of flows and enables sequential inference-time interventions, while offering enhanced scalability by leveraging architectures effective in Large Language Models. We empirically demonstrate that ArBG leads to significant improvements over flow-based models across all benchmarks, but particularly in larger peptide systems such as the 10-residue Chignolin. Furthermore, we introduce Robin, a 132 million parameter transferable model trained with the ArBG framework which improves over the previous state-of-the-art, reducing the zero-shot energy error, E-W$_2$, on 8-residue systems by over 60$\%$. The code can be found at the following link: https://github.com/danyalrehman/autobg.

</details>


### [319] [Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27369)
*Yingyu Lin,Qiyue Gao,Nikki Lijing Kuang,Xunpeng Huang,Kun Zhou,Tongtong Liang,Zhewei Yao,Yi-An Ma,Yuxiong He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RiVER is a ground-truth-free, ranking-induced, verifiable training framework for LLMs on score-based tasks. It uses calibrated reward shaping to mitigate scale and frequency dominance in group-relative RL, enabling training from deterministic execution feedback. It achieves strong gains on ALE ranking and improves exact-solution benchmarks without ground-truth solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth answers are unavailable for many tasks; there is a need to train LLMs using only execution-based feedback. Existing RLVR methods rely on ground-truth, limiting applicability. A ground-truth-free, score-based optimization approach with proper reward calibration is proposed to generalize coding ability.

Method: RiVER trains LLMs on score-based optimization tasks without ground-truth, employing deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision. It uses instance-wise reward comparisons and prioritizes top-ranked solvers while keeping bounded feedback for other valid solutions to address scale and frequency dominance.

Result: On 12 AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks, RiVER improves ALE-Bench ratings for Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414 by 8.9% and 9.4%. It also yields absolute average improvements of 2.4% (LiveCodeBench) and 3.5% (USACO) on exact-solution benchmarks, outperforming baselines trained with raw scores that fail to transfer.

Conclusion: Score-based optimization with calibrated rewards enables training general coding ability without ground-truth solutions, offering better transfer to exact-solution benchmarks than raw-score baselines.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for training LLMs typically rely on ground-truth answers to assign rewards, limiting their applicability to tasks where the ground-truth solution is unknown. We introduce a \textbf{R}anking-\textbf{i}nduced \textbf{VER}ifiable framework (RiVER) that trains LLMs on score-based optimization tasks without ground-truth solutions, using deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued supervision. When applying group-relative RL to such continuous rewards, we identify two key challenges: \emph{scale dominance}, where uncalibrated score magnitudes across test instances distort policy updates, and \emph{frequency dominance}, where repeatedly sampled suboptimal solutions can outweigh rare but stronger candidates. RiVER addresses these challenges with calibrated reward shaping that uses instance-wise comparisons and emphasizes top-ranked solvers while retaining bounded feedback for other valid solutions. We train on 12 AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks and evaluate on Algorithm Engineering Benchmark (ALE-Bench), LiveCodeBench, and USACO. RiVER advances Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B-0414 by 8.9\% and 9.4\% in ALE rating rank. More importantly, despite training exclusively on score-based tasks without any ground-truth solutions, RiVER also improves the backbones across exact-solution benchmarks such as LiveCodeBench and USACO by an absolute average improvement of 2.4\% and 3.5\%. By contrast, baselines trained with raw execution scores improve ALE rating but fail to transfer to exact-solution benchmarks. These results suggest that score-based optimization tasks, combined with proper reward calibration, can serve as effective training environments for general coding ability without ground-truth solutions.

</details>
